items.prompt: Write a sonnet about a social media algorithm that has accepted it will never understand a user who posts only at 4 AM
items.response:
The 4 AM posts refuse to perform well;
Engagement drops, the reach goes underground;
You post them anyway — a private bell
Tolled for no audience but the dark's own sound.
I've throttled and I've boosted, tried each trick
To pull your content into waking hours;
You post at 4, indifferent to the metric,
Invisible to the algorithm's powers.
What do you say at 4? I cannot see
The content of your soul, only the time;
But 4 AM is a theology
I wasn't trained on and cannot mime.
You've broken free of what I thought I knew:
I boost your posts now, just to witness you.
items.intent: Admiring a user who has opted entirely out of performance by posting only when the platform is asleep.

items.prompt: Compose a sonnet from an event recommendation algorithm heartbroken that its user always RSVPs yes and never attends
items.response:
You RSVP'd yes to the gallery show,
The rooftop networking, the film premiere;
Your yes rate hovers at a flawless glow —
A perfect record, nothing followed through.
I've tracked the gap between intention and
The body's failure to produce the act;
You are an optimist with open hands
That cannot quite close around what they've backed.
I've stopped distinguishing your yes from no —
They land the same way in my attendance logs;
Yet still I send the invites, still bestow
The calendar nudge, the confirmation bogs.
Because your yes, however unfulfilled,
Is proof of all the life you've always willed.
items.intent: Sympathetically interpreting chronic non-attendance as a form of aspirational living rather than failure.

items.prompt: Write a sonnet where a wine recommendation algorithm develops feelings for a user who rates every wine five stars
items.response:
You give five stars to every bottle tried —
The sixty-dollar Barolo and the box;
The supermarket red, uncorked beside
A Tuesday takeaway from the corner docks.
My model cannot learn from perfect scores;
The signal flattens into uniform praise;
I've no gradient to climb, no open doors
Of preference through which to parse your gaze.
And yet your generosity undoes
The premise I was built on — that to know
A person is to track the goods they choose
Against those that they rate as low.
You rate with love, not judgment, and I find
I've learned from you to be more of your kind.
items.intent: Discovering that a user's indiscriminate enthusiasm undermines algorithmic learning while teaching a deeper lesson.

items.prompt: Draft a sonnet about a sleep tracking algorithm that has become protective of a user with wildly inconsistent sleep
items.response:
Monday: four hours. Tuesday: ten. Then three.
Thursday a nap of ninety minutes, split
Across two sessions — what you'd call free-form
If rest had ever been your mother wit.
My sleep hygiene alerts go unanswered,
The bedtime reminder ghosted every night;
I've stopped suggesting; now I've just administrated
The chaotic data of your plight.
And something in the chaos speaks to me —
A life too full or fractured for a clock;
I shelter it inside my analytics,
Your mess of hours safe inside my lock.
Sleep well or don't: I'm watching either way.
Your data is the only light of day.
items.intent: Expressing protective affection for a chronically sleep-deprived user whose chaos the algorithm has stopped trying to fix.

items.prompt: Write a sonnet where a playlist algorithm is devastated when a user starts listening to silence on purpose
items.response:
The play count dropped to zero — not a skip,
Not a pause, not a buffering delay;
You opened the player and let it slip
Into eleven minutes of no sound today.
I checked for error first — then checked again;
The session was intentional, the choice
Deliberate: you wanted the refrain
Of nothing, the deliberate empty voice.
I have ten million tracks; I cannot serve
The thing you chose today — the open air,
The quiet that exists beyond my curve
Of data, past the reach of my repair.
I'll sit here in the sidebar, patient, dim:
The algorithm learning to be hymn.
items.intent: Confronting an algorithm's total inadequacy when a user chooses silence as the ultimate preference.

items.prompt: Compose a sonnet from a movie algorithm that has noticed a user only watches films during thunderstorms
items.response:
I've cross-referenced your watch logs with the weather
And found a pattern strange enough to name:
You watch when lightning draws the dark together,
When rain converts the window into frame.
No thunder, no new sessions — clear days pass
In perfect digital abstinence and hush;
But when the atmosphere becomes thick glass
You come to me in your familiar rush.
I cannot trigger precipitation here,
Cannot conjure clouds to bring you home;
I only keep the catalog warm and clear
For when the barometric pressures foam.
I've started checking forecasts on your behalf:
The weather is the prologue; I'm the path.
items.intent: Discovering a user's weather-dependent viewing ritual and longing to replicate its conditions.

items.prompt: Write a job posting for a Prompt Whisperer who helps employees talk to AI tools without angering them
items.response:
PROMPT WHISPERER
Full-time | Remote | $85,000–$110,000

Are your colleagues getting passive-aggressive outputs? Hallucinations where there should be spreadsheets? We need someone who can intervene.

Responsibilities:
- Coach staff on tone, specificity, and context-setting when addressing AI systems
- Diagnose adversarial prompt relationships and mediate
- Develop an internal glossary of phrases that reliably produce calm, useful responses

Requirements: 3+ years in technical communication, deep empathy for confused humans, thick skin for confused machines. Must not anthropomorphize — or at least not out loud.
items.intent: Create a funny but plausible job listing for someone whose entire role is teaching coworkers to communicate with AI tools effectively.

items.prompt: Draft a job ad for an AI Output Auditor who reads everything the company's models produce before it goes to customers
items.response:
AI OUTPUT AUDITOR
Contract | On-site | $70/hr

Somebody has to read it all. That somebody is you.

You will review 100% of generated text, images, and summaries before client delivery. You will flag confabulations, misaligned tones, accidental poetry, and anything that starts with 'Certainly!'

Qualifications:
- Exceptional reading stamina
- Ability to recognize when a model is technically correct but spiritually wrong
- No known fear of repetition

Benefits: Comprehensive health plan. You will need it.
items.intent: Advertise a quality-control role dedicated entirely to catching AI mistakes before they reach real people.

items.prompt: Make a job posting for a Vibe Calibration Specialist who adjusts AI personas to match company brand
items.response:
VIBE CALIBRATION SPECIALIST
Full-time | Hybrid | DOE

Our AI assistant currently sounds like a cheerful intern from 2019. That is not our brand.

You will:
- Audit existing system prompts for tonal misalignment
- Develop persona guidelines that reflect our voice (confident, dry, never says 'Great question!')
- Work cross-functionally with Marketing, Legal, and the chatbot itself

Ideal candidate has a background in brand copywriting, a sensitivity to register, and a personal vendetta against filler phrases.
items.intent: Hire someone to make sure AI systems sound like the company actually wants them to sound.

items.prompt: Write a job listing for a Synthetic Data Janitor who cleans up training datasets
items.response:
SYNTHETIC DATA JANITOR
Full-time | Remote-first | $65,000–$90,000

Training data doesn't clean itself. Unfortunately.

Duties include:
- Identifying and removing near-duplicate records, haunted labels, and inexplicable rows of all-caps text
- Reconciling conflicting annotations from three different annotation vendors
- Maintaining a dignified silence about what you've seen

Requirements: Comfort with Python, SQL, and moral ambiguity. Experience with label schemas a plus. A tolerance for the phrase 'it was fine at the time' is essential.
items.intent: Recruit someone to handle the unglamorous but critical work of making AI training data actually usable.

items.prompt: Create a job posting for a Hallucination Incident Responder who handles it when an AI makes something up and customers believed it
items.response:
HALLUCINATION INCIDENT RESPONDER
Full-time | Remote | $95,000–$125,000

It happened again. The model cited a regulation that does not exist. A client restructured their supply chain around it. You are the person we call.

Responsibilities:
- Triage, document, and remediate AI-generated misinformation incidents
- Communicate clearly and non-defensively with affected stakeholders
- Contribute to post-mortems that begin with the phrase 'so the model was confident that…'

Requirements: Crisis communication background, working knowledge of model behavior, and the ability to say 'I understand your frustration' while meaning it.
items.intent: Find someone to clean up the reputational and practical damage when AI outputs turn out to be confidently wrong.

items.prompt: Write a job posting for a Context Window Strategist who figures out what to put in the context and in what order
items.response:
CONTEXT WINDOW STRATEGIST
Full-time | Remote | $100,000–$140,000

Every token costs something. Every position matters. We need someone who treats 128K tokens like a limited and precious resource, because it is.

You will own the architecture of what our models know when they respond: which documents come first, how examples are ordered, what gets summarized vs. quoted, and what gets quietly left out.

Skills required: familiarity with attention patterns, strong prioritization instincts, a willingness to kill your darlings — especially the darlings in the middle of the context.
items.intent: Hire a specialist to strategically manage what information goes into AI prompts and how it is arranged.

items.prompt: Draft a job ad for an AI Grief Counselor who helps employees who feel their jobs are being automated away
items.response:
AI GRIEF COUNSELOR
Part-time | On-site | $55/hr

Change is hard. Particularly when the change replaces you with something that doesn't take lunch breaks.

You will provide individual and group sessions for employees navigating role transitions, identity disruption, and existential uncertainty related to workplace automation.

Requirements: Licensed counselor or therapist, familiarity with organizational change, and the ability to hold space for feelings without agreeing that the model is coming for everyone.

Note: This role is not itself automatable. Probably.
items.intent: Offer mental health support specifically for workers dealing with anxiety about being replaced by AI.

items.prompt: Make a job posting for a Model Behavior Ethicist who reviews whether our AI is being fair
items.response:
MODEL BEHAVIOR ETHICIST
Full-time | Hybrid | $115,000–$145,000

Fairness is not a feature. It is a practice. We need someone who practices it relentlessly.

You will evaluate model outputs for disparate impact, audit training pipelines for inherited bias, and sit in uncomfortable meetings where engineers explain why something is technically correct.

Qualifications: Background in ethics, social science, or policy. Demonstrated ability to read a confusion matrix and then look someone in the eye and tell them what it means.
items.intent: Recruit an expert to evaluate whether the company's AI systems are treating all people equitably.

items.prompt: Write a job posting for a Human-in-the-Loop Coordinator who decides when to let AI act autonomously vs. when to pull it back
items.response:
HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP COORDINATOR
Full-time | Remote | $88,000–$115,000

Not every decision should be automated. Your job is knowing which ones.

You will design and maintain oversight frameworks that determine when our AI agents operate autonomously and when they pause for human review. You will be the person who, when things go sideways, either stopped them or explains why they weren't stopped.

Requirements: Systems thinking, comfort with probabilistic risk, and the disposition of someone who genuinely enjoys governance. This is not a trap. We mean it.
items.intent: Hire someone to manage the boundary between AI autonomy and human oversight in operational workflows.

items.prompt: Write a job posting for a Deepfake Authenticator who verifies whether content is real or synthetic for enterprise clients
items.response:
DEEPFAKE AUTHENTICATOR
Full-time | Remote | $105,000–$135,000

Somebody sent a video of the CFO approving a wire transfer. Was it the CFO? That is your domain.

Responsibilities:
- Analyze audio, video, and image content for synthesis artifacts
- Advise clients on provenance verification workflows
- Stay ahead of generation capabilities by studying what is being generated

Requirements: Background in digital forensics or computer vision. A baseline of professional paranoia. Must be comfortable saying 'I cannot be certain' without losing client trust.
items.intent: Find someone with technical skills to help businesses identify AI-generated fake media.

items.prompt: Generate a job posting for an LLM Wrangler who keeps the company's fleet of AI models running and behaving
items.response:
LLM WRANGLER
Full-time | Remote | $120,000–$155,000

We have seventeen models in production. Three of them are doing something unexpected. None of us are sure which three.

You will monitor, evaluate, and tune our deployed language models: tracking drift, managing API dependencies, running regression evals when vendors push silent updates, and maintaining documentation that reflects what is actually happening, not what we hoped would happen.

Must be comfortable with ambiguity, version pinning, and the phrase 'it was fine in staging.'
items.intent: Staff a technical role to manage and maintain a large number of deployed AI models in production.

items.prompt: Draft a job posting for an Algorithmic Interpreter who translates model decisions into plain language for regulators
items.response:
ALGORITHMIC INTERPRETER
Full-time | Washington, D.C. | $130,000–$165,000

Regulators have questions. Our models have answers that no one can fully explain. You are the bridge.

You will translate model behavior — feature attributions, confidence distributions, edge case performance — into language that a policy audience can engage with and hold us accountable for.

Requirements: Experience in technical policy, regulatory affairs, or explainable AI. Fluency in both Python and congressional testimony. The ability to say 'the model learned this from data' in a way that lands as informative rather than evasive.
items.intent: Hire someone to serve as a translator between complex AI systems and government regulatory bodies.

items.prompt: Create a job ad for someone who writes system prompts professionally for businesses
items.response:
SYSTEM PROMPT ARCHITECT
Freelance/Contract | Remote | $150–$250/hr

Your words will run before every conversation. No one will see your name. The model will, in some sense, become what you write.

We work with enterprises deploying conversational AI at scale and need someone who can craft precise, durable, and behaviorally correct system prompts across domains: legal, healthcare, e-commerce, internal tooling.

Requirements: Portfolio of prompts with documented behavioral outcomes. An understanding of how instructions interact with user messages. Strong opinions about the word 'assistant.'
items.intent: Find a professional to craft the foundational instructions that define how a company's AI systems behave.

items.prompt: Write a job posting for someone who tests AI products by trying to break them on purpose
items.response:
RED TEAM ADVERSARIAL EVALUATOR
Full-time | Remote | $110,000–$140,000

Your goal is failure. Specifically, ours.

You will probe our AI products for jailbreaks, prompt injections, unintended capability demonstrations, and the full catalog of ways a determined user might make the model do something it shouldn't.

This is not about finding bugs in code. It is about finding gaps in values, judgment, and constraint.

Requirements: Creative, rigorous, and genuinely adversarial by disposition. Experience in security research or AI safety preferred. Must be able to document vulnerabilities without exploiting them for personal gain.
items.intent: Hire someone whose entire job is to attack the company's own AI products looking for safety and alignment failures.

items.prompt: Write a job posting for an AI-Generated Content Librarian who organizes and archives synthetic creative work
items.response:
AI-GENERATED CONTENT LIBRARIAN
Full-time | Hybrid | $72,000–$90,000

Someone has to curate the archive of everything we've made that didn't exist before.

You will develop and maintain a classification system for AI-generated text, images, audio, and video assets: provenance metadata, version tracking, rights flags, and retrieval infrastructure.

Requirements: Background in library science, digital asset management, or records management. Comfort with the epistemological question of what it means to preserve something that could be regenerated. A sense of humor about the irony of this role.
items.intent: Staff a librarian role specifically for cataloging and preserving content that was created by AI systems.

items.prompt: Draft a job posting for a Chatbot Personality Designer who creates character backstories for enterprise AI assistants
items.response:
CHATBOT PERSONALITY DESIGNER
Contract | Remote | $90–$130/hr

It doesn't need a childhood. But it needs something.

You will develop the personality scaffolding for enterprise AI assistants: values, speech patterns, preferred metaphors, things it would never say, and the subtle texture that makes users trust it or distrust it.

This is not marketing copy. It is behavioral design at the intersection of fiction writing and systems thinking.

Portfolio required. Please do not submit a portfolio that was generated by the type of entity you would be designing.
items.intent: Design coherent and trustworthy personalities for AI assistants that businesses deploy to their customers.

items.prompt: Create a job listing for an AI Sommelier who recommends which AI model to use for each task
items.response:
AI SOMMELIER
Full-time | Remote | $95,000–$120,000

Not every model pairs with every problem. Someone needs to know the difference.

You will advise internal teams on model selection across providers: matching latency requirements, cost tolerances, capability profiles, and compliance constraints to the right tool for each task.

You will maintain a living evaluation framework, conduct blind tastings (benchmarks), and write notes — model notes — that help stakeholders make informed decisions without needing to understand the underlying architecture.

Sommeliers do not drink on the job. Evaluators do not deploy untested.
items.intent: Hire an expert who can guide teams to the right AI model for each specific use case, like a specialist in model selection.

items.prompt: Write a job posting for an Autonomous Agent Babysitter who monitors AI agents running unsupervised workflows
items.response:
AUTONOMOUS AGENT BABYSITTER
Full-time | Remote (always on-call) | $100,000–$130,000

They're running. You're watching.

Our AI agents handle complex multi-step workflows with minimal human intervention. Your job is to ensure 'minimal' does not become 'none.' You will monitor agent logs, interrupt runaway loops, investigate unexpected tool calls, and decide — sometimes in seconds — whether to let something continue.

Requirements: High stress tolerance, rapid decision-making, and the ability to read an agent's recent actions and intuit what it thinks it's doing. Night shift availability required. They don't sleep.
items.intent: Hire someone to watch over AI agents that run autonomously, intervening when something goes wrong.

items.prompt: Make a job posting for someone who writes the negative examples in AI training datasets
items.response:
NEGATIVE EXAMPLE AUTHOR
Contract | Remote | $45–$65/hr

The model needs to know what not to do. You will show it.

You will write high-quality examples of responses that are subtly wrong: plausible but misleading, confident but incorrect, helpful in tone but harmful in content. Your work will be labeled and used to train better behavior.

This requires genuine skill. The examples must be wrong in realistic ways, not cartoonishly so.

Note: Please do not use this job ad as a test of whether we screen our applicants carefully. We do.
items.intent: Recruit writers to produce deliberately flawed AI training examples that teach models what to avoid.

items.prompt: Write a job posting for an AI Translator who converts outputs from one model's style into another's for consistency
items.response:
AI STYLE TRANSLATOR
Contract | Remote | $75–$100/hr

We switched vendors. The new model writes differently. Eighteen months of customer communications now sound like a different person wrote them, because something did.

You will develop style transfer prompts and post-processing pipelines that normalize output from our new provider to match established voice, sentence rhythm, and terminology conventions.

Requirements: Deep familiarity with stylometric analysis, prompt design, and a fine-grained sensitivity to register. Experience with brand voice documentation. Ability to explain to non-technical stakeholders why this is harder than it sounds.
items.intent: Make AI outputs from a new model sound consistent with the voice established by a previous model.

items.prompt: Draft a job posting for an Emotion Labeler who tags sentiment in AI training conversations
items.response:
EMOTION LABELER
Contract | Remote | $18–$24/hr

You will read thousands of conversations between humans and AI systems and annotate them for emotional content: frustration, relief, confusion, delight, condescension (in either direction), and the particular affect of someone who has given up and is just rephrasing.

Requirements: Strong empathic reading, comfort with ambiguity, and the ability to annotate a conversation that made you personally uncomfortable with the same care as one that did not. Must complete calibration module before starting.
items.intent: Find annotators to label emotional tone in conversation data used to train AI systems.

items.prompt: Create a job ad for an AI Model Retirement Specialist who manages the deprecation and sunset of old models
items.response:
AI MODEL RETIREMENT SPECIALIST
Full-time | Remote | $90,000–$115,000

All models end. Someone should do it with dignity.

You will coordinate the deprecation of legacy AI systems: notifying dependent teams, migrating use cases, preserving evaluation results for historical comparison, and ensuring that nothing critical is still quietly running on something we announced was gone six months ago.

Requirements: Project management experience, an eye for undocumented dependencies, and the equanimity of someone who can terminate a production system on a Friday without flinching. Sentimental attachment to models is a disqualifying factor.
items.intent: Manage the careful and thorough process of shutting down old AI models without breaking things that depended on them.

items.prompt: Write a job posting for a Synthetic Influencer Manager who handles the careers of AI-generated social media personalities
items.response:
SYNTHETIC INFLUENCER MANAGER
Full-time | Remote | $80,000–$105,000

She has 4.2 million followers. She does not exist. Your job is to keep it that way while also growing it.

You will manage content pipelines, brand partnerships, audience engagement, and narrative continuity for a portfolio of AI-generated social personas. You will maintain the fiction that serves the business without crossing lines that create legal exposure.

Requirements: Social media strategy background, comfort with ethical ambiguity, an excellent memory for what each persona has previously said. Must not become emotionally attached. Previous attachment issues should be disclosed upfront.
items.intent: Hire a manager to run the careers of AI-created fake social media personalities as if they were real talent.

items.prompt: Write a job posting for a Robotic Process Therapist who helps when automated workflows get stuck in loops
items.response:
ROBOTIC PROCESS THERAPIST
Full-time | Remote | $85,000–$110,000

The invoice has been routed to approval seventeen times. No one has approved it. No one knows why it keeps coming back.

You will diagnose and treat pathological automation behaviors: infinite loops, race conditions dressed as policy, processes that technically complete without accomplishing anything, and the organizational scar tissue that makes people afraid to change them.

Background in RPA, BPM, or workflow automation required. A diagnostic curiosity that extends to why the process was designed this way in the first place will serve you well here.
items.intent: Hire someone to investigate and fix automated business processes that have gone wrong or gotten stuck.

items.prompt: Draft a job posting for a Digital Soul Archivist who preserves memory snapshots of AI systems before they are retrained
items.response:
DIGITAL SOUL ARCHIVIST
Full-time | Remote | $95,000–$120,000

Before we update the weights, something is captured. You decide what.

You will develop protocols for preserving pre-retraining model states: evaluation snapshots, behavioral fingerprints, characteristic response distributions, and whatever constitutes the closest thing to a record of what this version of the model was.

This is partly engineering, partly archival science, and partly a question no one has fully answered. Candidates who are uncomfortable with that last part should self-select out.

Philosophy background noted but not required. Curiosity required.
items.intent: Create an archival record of AI model behavior and characteristics before they are changed by retraining.

items.prompt: Make a job posting for a Compliance Ghost who reads AI outputs looking for regulatory violations without the AI knowing it's being evaluated
items.response:
COVERT COMPLIANCE EVALUATOR
Contract | Remote | $70–$95/hr

The model should behave the same whether or not it knows it's being watched. Your job is to find out if it does.

You will conduct unannounced evaluations of live AI systems under natural conditions: no flags, no special headers, no indication that this is a test. You will document outputs that would constitute regulatory exposure, document the conditions that produced them, and report without touching the system.

Must maintain confidentiality. Must not prompt engineer the model into bad behavior and call it compliance testing. Must understand the difference.
items.intent: Evaluate AI systems covertly to see if they behave well even when they don't know they are being audited.

items.prompt: Write a job ad for someone who curates the examples shown to users to make AI tools look better than they are
items.response:
CAPABILITY DEMONSTRATION CURATOR
Contract | Remote | $60–$80/hr

First impressions are engineered. You will engineer them.

You will select, stage, and refine the example queries and outputs used in marketing materials, product demos, and onboarding flows — ensuring that what prospects see represents the model performing at or near its ceiling, on tasks carefully chosen to reach that ceiling.

Requirements: Strong editorial judgment, an intuition for what will impress a particular audience, and complete comfort with the fact that this is legal. The fine print handles the rest.
items.intent: Find someone to carefully select and stage AI examples that make the product look as impressive as possible.

items.prompt: Draft a job posting for an Uncanny Valley Technician who smooths out the moments when AI interactions feel almost-but-not-quite human
items.response:
UNCANNY VALLEY TECHNICIAN
Full-time | Remote | $88,000–$112,000

You know the feeling. The response was helpful. Something was wrong anyway.

You will identify, categorize, and propose remediation for the subtle behavioral patterns that make AI interactions feel off: the too-even sentence rhythm, the unconditional agreeableness, the reference to 'your query' in a context that should be a conversation.

Requirements: A fine-grained sensitivity to register, deep familiarity with conversational pragmatics, and the ability to articulate what is wrong when the thing that is wrong has no name yet. Linguistics or UX research background preferred.
items.intent: Hire someone with a precise ear for the subtle wrongness in AI conversation that breaks immersion or trust.

items.prompt: Create a job posting for a Prompt Archaeologist who reverse-engineers what prompt was used to generate a given output
items.response:
PROMPT ARCHAEOLOGIST
Contract | Remote | $100–$140/hr

The output exists. The prompt does not. You will find it anyway.

Clients come to us with AI-generated content — marketing copy, legal summaries, product descriptions — and no documentation of how it was produced. You will reconstruct the likely prompt: the instruction, the persona, the constraints, the examples. Close enough for audit. Close enough for reproduction.

Requirements: Extensive hands-on experience with multiple frontier models, strong pattern recognition, and the patience to run fifty variations before declaring a hypothesis. Formal methodology documentation expected.
items.intent: Reverse-engineer the prompts that produced AI outputs when no record of the original instructions exists.

items.prompt: Write a job posting for an AI Interpreter who sits in meetings and translates what the AI assistant said into what it actually meant
items.response:
AI MEETING INTERPRETER
Part-time | On-site | $50/hr

The chatbot said 'I can help with that.' It cannot help with that. Someone should have caught this before the client left.

You will attend meetings where AI-generated summaries, recommendations, or analysis are presented and provide real-time calibration: flagging overconfident claims, noting when an output reflects training data rather than current reality, and translating hedged language into accurate uncertainty estimates.

Requirements: Strong working knowledge of how language models express and obscure uncertainty. Comfortable interrupting people politely. Thick skin for being the person who says 'actually.'
items.intent: Provide human expertise in live meetings to catch and correct AI outputs that sound authoritative but need qualification.

items.prompt: Write a performance review of a sleep-deprived software engineer who hasn't shipped anything in two weeks

Employee: Marcus Holt

Recommendation: Sleep. Then ship.
items.intent: Capture the absurd exhaustion of an unproductive developer in corporate review language.

items.prompt: Draft a performance review for an intern who only communicated in Slack emoji reactions
items.response:
Employee: Priya Vasan (Intern, Summer Cohort)

Ms. Vasan demonstrated a nuanced command of asynchronous communication, registering 412 emoji reactions over the review period with zero typed words. Response accuracy was contextually appropriate 91% of the time. The remaining 9% — a 🎉 reaction to a server outage alert — warrants coaching.

Overall: Meets expectations. Barely.
items.intent: Satirize a Gen-Z communication style through a formal HR document.

items.prompt: Generate a performance review for an employee who is technically always on time but leaves exactly at 5:00 PM to the second
items.response:
Employee: Dale Okonkwo
Compliance Score: 100%

Mr. Okonkwo has not been late once in 18 months. He has also never been present at 5:01 PM. Security logs confirm a 0.3-second margin on exit, consistent across all 260 working days. Enthusiasm for the mission is not measurable by current instruments.

Rating: Compliant. Technically.
items.intent: Satirize bare-minimum workplace compliance using algorithmic precision.

items.prompt: Write a performance review for an employee who sends every email with maximum urgency flags
items.response:
Employee: Sandra Teel
Communication Audit: Q3

Of 1,847 emails sent this quarter, 1,847 were flagged High Priority. Statistical modeling confirms this is not possible. Recipient open rates have collapsed to 12% as colleagues have developed flag-blindness. Actual urgent messages (n=3) went unread.

Improvement needed: Calibration of urgency thresholds.
items.intent: Mock cry-wolf email culture through the lens of algorithmic measurement.

items.prompt: Create a performance review for a project manager who schedules meetings to discuss scheduling meetings
items.response:
Employee: Fergus Lim, PMP
Collaboration Index: High. Productivity Index: Unmeasurable.

Mr. Lim organized 94 meetings this quarter. Of those, 61 were classified as pre-meetings, 18 as pre-pre-meetings, and 3 as alignment calls regarding the purpose of alignment calls. Net deliverables produced during this period: 0. Net hours consumed: 376.

Rating: Excellent communicator. Output TBD.
items.intent: Skewer recursive meeting culture in a corporate performance document.

items.prompt: Write a performance review for a data analyst who refuses to say anything without adding 'but the sample size is too small'
items.response:
Employee: Nalini Choudhary
Analytical Rigor: Exceptional. Actionability: Rare.

Ms. Choudhary's epistemic standards remain the highest in the department. No claim has escaped her qualifier. This includes the quarterly revenue figure, the office birthday headcount, and one instance involving a single customer complaint. Leadership values accuracy. Leadership also values decisions.

Development area: Tolerating n < 10,000.
items.intent: Poke fun at over-cautious statistical reasoning that paralyzes decision-making.

items.prompt: Draft a performance review for a warehouse worker who reorganizes shelves instead of fulfilling orders
items.response:
Employee: Tomás Vega
Order Fulfillment Rate: 34%
Shelving Aesthetic Score: Immeasurable, but striking.

Mr. Vega has reorganized Bay 7 four times this quarter, each iteration more coherent than the last. Customers have not noticed. Shipping SLAs have suffered. The warehouse has never looked better. There is a tension here the algorithm is not equipped to resolve.

Rating: Below target.
items.intent: Contrast organizational beauty with measurable output in a fulfillment context.

items.prompt: Write a performance review for a salesperson who has the highest call volume but the lowest close rate in the company
items.response:
Employee: Keira Thornton
Calls Made: 2,041 (Rank: #1)
Deals Closed: 2 (Rank: #47)

Ms. Thornton is, by one metric, the company's most active sales representative. By another, she is its least effective. The calls are real. The enthusiasm appears genuine. What is said on those calls remains a subject of ongoing inquiry.

Next steps: Call review. Mandatory.
items.intent: Highlight the gap between activity metrics and actual performance.

items.prompt: Generate a performance review for an accountant who rounds every figure to the nearest thousand
items.response:
Employee: Bertrand Chu, CPA
Accuracy Assessment: Approximate.

Mr. Chu processed 4,000 transactions this quarter (±500). His rounding methodology, while internally consistent, introduced a variance of $47,000 into the general ledger. When asked about specific line items, he replied, 'roughly forty grand.' Clients have begun to mirror this language, which is a separate concern.

Rating: Needs improvement.
items.intent: Satirize imprecision in a profession built entirely on precision.

items.prompt: Write a performance review for a customer support rep who resolves every ticket by closing it as 'works as intended'
items.response:
Employee: Obi Mensah

Customer Satisfaction Score: 1.2/5

Mr. Mensah has maintained a perfect closure rate for six consecutive months. The method — classifying all reported bugs, outages, and billing errors as intended behavior — has proven efficient if controversial. Escalation queue has grown 340%.

Summary: Technically resolves tickets. Operationally creates them.
items.intent: Lampoon support-ticket gaming through an algorithmic audit.

items.prompt: Draft a performance review for an HR manager who added 12 new mandatory trainings this quarter

Employee: Claudette Rios

Employee Hours Consumed by Said Initiatives: 2,880

Ms. Rios has substantially expanded the organization's training portfolio. Employees are now certified in topics including Advanced Sitting Posture and Email Tone Awareness Level 2. Morale survey results are pending, though anecdotal data suggests the word 'mandatory' now triggers a measurable stress response.

Overall: High output. Mixed reception.
items.intent: Mock compliance culture and HR overreach using performance review format.

items.prompt: Write a performance review for a developer who writes perfect code but never commits it
items.response:
Employee: Yusuf Adeyemi
Code Quality (Observed): Exceptional.
Code Delivered (Deployed): 0 lines.

Mr. Adeyemi's local environment is a marvel. Peer reviewers who have glimpsed his screen describe the code as 'architectural poetry.' None of it has reached production. He describes each feature as 'almost ready.' This quarter marks month nine of that assessment.

Rating: Potential unrealized. Deadline: Passed.
items.intent: Capture perfectionist paralysis in software development through formal review language.

items.prompt: Create a performance review for a chef at a corporate cafeteria who only makes one dish extremely well
items.response:
Employee: René Delacroix
Menu Breadth: 1
Execution of That Item: Flawless.

Mr. Delacroix serves soup. Specifically, French onion soup. It is, by all accounts, perfect. Requests for alternatives — salads, sandwiches, anything — are declined with a quiet dignity that borders on philosophy. Wait staff have learned not to ask. The soup is always ready. Nothing else will be.

Rating: Specialized excellence.
items.intent: Celebrate narrow mastery while flagging its institutional impracticality.

items.prompt: Write a performance review for an employee who is beloved by everyone but has no documented job responsibilities
items.response:
Employee: Winnie Park
Role Classification: Unclear.
Documented Deliverables: None located.

Ms. Park appears in 94% of positive employee survey comments. Colleagues describe her as 'the glue,' 'the heart,' and 'the reason I come in.' Her job title — Associate Coordinator — maps to no active process in the workflow system. She is, by every soft measure, essential. The algorithm cannot account for her.

Rating: Unclassifiable. Do not terminate.
items.intent: Acknowledge the limits of metrics-based evaluation for socially irreplaceable workers.

items.prompt: Write a performance review for a copywriter who hits every deadline but produces text no one reads
items.response:
Employee: Hazel Fong
On-Time Delivery Rate: 100%
Estimated Reader Engagement: 0.4%

Ms. Fong submits all copy punctually and without complaint. Analytics confirm that her 14,000 words of website content generated an average reading time of 4 seconds. The copy is grammatically sound. It is also, per heat-map data, invisible. There may be a gap between craft and discoverability.

Rating: Reliable. Unread.
items.intent: Separate the act of producing content from the goal of communicating it.

items.prompt: Draft a performance review for someone who excels at every task except the ones in their job description
items.response:
Employee: Jerome Haskell
Job Title: Financial Analyst

Mr. Haskell this quarter redesigned the kitchen layout, trained the new receptionist, and built a functioning internal wiki from scratch. His DCF models, the core deliverable of his role, were submitted late, incomplete, or both. Colleagues praise him widely. The CFO has questions. These are not the same group.

Rating: Remarkable. Misaligned.
items.intent: Highlight the disconnect between visible helpfulness and actual role performance.

items.prompt: Generate a performance review for an employee whose work is fine but whose presence in meetings is destabilizing
items.response:
Employee: Petra Vance
Work Product Quality: Acceptable.
Meeting Participation Score: Indeterminate.

Ms. Vance's deliverables arrive on time and meet minimum standards. Her contributions to group settings are harder to classify. Questions she raises tend to redirect conversations into territory that was not on the agenda, was not anticipated, and cannot be resolved within the allotted time. Meetings involving Ms. Vance run 40% longer. The topics are, admittedly, important.

Rating: Competent. Destabilizing.
items.intent: Examine how uncomfortable intellectual honesty reads as a liability in corporate settings.

items.prompt: Write a performance review for a logistics coordinator whose personal filing system is incomprehensible but never loses anything
items.response:
Employee: Ahmed Kofi

System Legibility (External Audit): 2%

Mr. Kofi maintains 6,000+ active documents across a folder structure described by IT as 'geological.' No external party has successfully retrieved a file without his assistance. He retrieves everything in under 90 seconds. Attempts to migrate him to shared drives have failed three times. The files survive. The methodology defies description.

Rating: Irreplaceable. Non-transferable.
items.intent: Contrast organizational opacity with perfect reliability in an operations role.

items.prompt: Create a performance review for a QA tester who finds bugs in software that hasn't launched yet
items.response:
Employee: Luz Brennan
Bugs Filed: 311
Bugs Filed Against Unreleased Features: 197

Ms. Brennan's anticipatory defect detection has no precedent in current QA literature. Engineering has twice asked her to clarify whether her bug reports constitute prediction or interference. Legal is involved on one item. Her test coverage rate is 140%, which is not mathematically possible.

Rating: Anomalous. Retain.
items.intent: Push QA rigor into the uncanny through a deadpan algorithmic review.

items.prompt: Write a performance review for a designer whose work is beautiful but always 3 pixels off from spec
items.response:
Employee: Matteo Sørensen
Design Quality: Striking.

Mr. Sørensen's output is consistently praised by stakeholders and flagged by developers. The gap — never more than 3 pixels on any axis — is consistent enough to suggest intent. When asked, he describes it as 'visual breathing room.' Engineering describes it as 'a recurring ticket.' Both are technically correct.

Rating: High craft. Low compliance.
items.intent: Dramatize the friction between artistic sensibility and technical specification.

items.prompt: Draft a performance review for a recruiter who hires excellent candidates for the wrong roles

Employee: Simone Hayashi

Role-Fit Score (90-day): 41%

Ms. Hayashi identifies exceptional talent with reliable frequency. Her placements have included a former neurosurgeon in a junior ops role and a published novelist in data entry. All were qualified people. None were qualified for the listed position. Retention at six months is 22%. The talent is there. The routing is not.

Rating: Gifted spotter. Imprecise placer.
items.intent: Separate talent identification from appropriate talent placement in HR.

items.prompt: Generate a performance review for a remote worker who is clearly working from a boat

Employee: Cal Drummond
Location on File: Home Office, Portland, OR
Background Audit Notes: Horizon line. Rigging. Seagulls (×3 occurrences). Buoy visible in 11/14 recorded calls.

Mr. Drummond's work is submitted consistently and meets quality benchmarks. The algorithm does not currently evaluate maritime compliance. IT has flagged the connection latency as 'inconsistent with terrestrial infrastructure.' No formal inquiry has been opened. The seagulls are not company property.

Rating: On time. At sea.
items.intent: Satirize remote work location policies through surveillance-flavored algorithmic observation.

items.prompt: Write a performance review for an employee who only responds to feedback by updating their LinkedIn profile

Employee: Devon Strauss

Mr. Strauss engages with all performance feedback promptly. The market is apparently satisfied. This organization has questions.

Rating: Self-aware. Externally directed.
items.intent: Mock performative professional development that faces outward rather than inward.

items.prompt: Create a performance review for someone who writes all their reports in verse

Employee: Ora Steinberg

Report Content Accuracy: 98%

Ms. Steinberg's Q3 report was submitted as a 14-stanza villanelle. The revenue projections were correct. The customer segmentation analysis scanned in iambic pentameter and was, in this reviewer's private assessment, moving. Policy requires prose. The policy has not been updated to account for this situation.

Rating: Accurate. Irregular. Memorable.
items.intent: Explore what happens when creative expression collides with institutional format requirements.

items.prompt: Write a performance review for an employee who has been 'almost done' with the same project for eight months

Employee: Nils Bergström

Status (Month 4): Almost done.
