items.intent: Capture the dreaded experience of returning to a flooded inbox after time off.

items.prompt: Create a hazard label for passive-aggressive workplace Slack messages.
items.response:
CAUTION: PASSIVE-AGGRESSIVE SLACK MESSAGE
Subtext detected. Surface reading diverges significantly from actual meaning. 'Per my last message' indicates prior message was not read. 'As discussed' signals displeasure. 'Thanks!' with exclamation point requires advanced interpretation. Proceed carefully.
items.intent: Expose the coded hostility embedded in corporate-polite messaging language.

items.prompt: Write a safety label for the deadline you keep pushing to tomorrow.
items.response:
WARNING: ROLLING DEADLINE
This task has been deferred 11 consecutive days. Each deferral increases task density and emotional weight. Object is now significantly heavier than when originally assigned. Handle with urgency. Tomorrow is no longer a safe destination.
items.intent: Call out the self-defeating cycle of procrastination disguised as time management.

items.prompt: Make a warning label for the group chat where everyone says 'sounds good' but no one takes action.
items.response:
CAUTION: CONSENSUS WITHOUT COMMITMENT
This channel has produced 43 'sounds good' responses and zero assigned owners. Agreement has been achieved. Accountability has not. Do not assume any task is being handled. Verify. Then verify again.
items.intent: Highlight the dysfunction of group agreements that never translate into individual responsibility.

items.prompt: Draft a warning label for opening your work laptop on a Saturday.
items.response:
⚠ WEEKEND LAPTOP ACTIVATION
You are crossing the boundary. This action signals to your nervous system, your employer, and the universe that the weekend is optional. Proceed only if the situation is genuinely critical. Spoiler: it is not. Close the lid. Go outside.
items.intent: Warn against the erosion of work-life boundaries through reflexive weekend working.

items.prompt: Write a safety warning label for a calendar invite with no agenda.
items.response:
WARNING: AGENDLESS CALENDAR INVITE
This 45-minute block contains no stated purpose, no pre-reading, and no defined outcome. Attendees will not know what to prepare. The organizer may not know either. Bring a backup task. Wear comfortable shoes.
items.intent: Mock the common workplace habit of scheduling meetings with zero preparation or structure.

items.prompt: Generate a hazard label for the chat thread that goes quiet right after you post something.
items.response:
CAUTION: POST-MESSAGE SILENCE
Your message was delivered. Confirmation of receipt is unconfirmed. The silence that follows may be: (a) coincidental, (b) studied indifference, or (c) a technical error. Do not re-send. Do not add a follow-up emoji. Wait at least 48 hours before catastrophizing.
items.intent: Capture the unsettling uncertainty of a message that receives no response.

items.prompt: Create a product warning label for the task that everyone knows needs doing but no one claims.
items.response:
HAZARD: UNOWNED TASK
This item has been visible on the shared board for 19 days. It requires action but has no assignee. It is aware of you. Do not make eye contact. If you acknowledge it, it becomes yours. Proceed with extreme social caution.
items.intent: Name the strange social dynamic of collective avoidance around an obvious shared responsibility.

items.prompt: Make a warning label for a Monday that follows a long weekend.
items.response:
ELEVATED HAZARD: POST-HOLIDAY MONDAY
Strength multiplied by factor of 1.4. All standard Monday warnings apply with increased intensity. Additionally: inboxes are denser, colleagues are rustier, and the body does not recall what a weekday is. Acclimate slowly. Avoid scheduling anything before noon.
items.intent: Escalate the usual Monday dread for the particular misery of returning after a long break.

items.prompt: Write a safety warning for the feeling right after you send a message you immediately regret.
items.response:
⚠ POST-SEND REGRET
Message is in transit. Recall not available on this platform. You had one window — it has closed. Current symptoms are normal. Severity will peak in approximately 90 seconds. Resolution: unknown. Prevention: draft folder. You know this.
items.intent: Label the immediate, visceral regret of sending a message you wish you could take back.

items.prompt: Draft a warning label for the inbox zero feeling that lasts approximately eleven minutes.
items.response:
CAUTION: TEMPORARY INBOX ZERO
This state has an average duration of 11 minutes. Do not attach meaning to it. A new message is already in transit. Enjoy the sensation briefly, then resume realistic expectations. This product offers no lasting satisfaction.
items.intent: Satirize the fleeting, illusory victory of reaching inbox zero.

items.prompt: Create a hazard label for end-of-quarter deadlines.
items.response:
⚠ END-OF-QUARTER DEADLINE CLUSTER
Multiple simultaneous deadlines detected. Priorities are in conflict. At least one stakeholder believes their deliverable is most urgent. They are all correct in their own minds. Triage ruthlessly. Communicate proactively. Prepare a list of things that will simply not happen.
items.intent: Address the compressive pressure of multiple high-stakes deadlines converging at once.

items.prompt: Write a warning label for the group project where one person does everything.
items.response:
WARNING: ASYMMETRIC COLLABORATION
Load distribution in this group has become critically uneven. One participant is bearing 87% of the output. Structural fatigue is likely. If you are not that person, check in. If you are that person, document everything — you will need it later.
items.intent: Call out the unfair and unsustainable pattern of one person carrying a group project.

items.prompt: Make a safety label for checking your phone within the first 30 seconds of waking up.
items.response:
CAUTION: FIRST-WAKING DEVICE CHECK
You are introducing external demands before your threat-response system is fully calibrated. Contents may include: urgent messages, news, or notifications that will set the emotional tone of your day. Delay recommended. The world's problems will still be there after coffee.
items.intent: Warn against the habit of immediately consuming digital content before the day has even begun.

items.prompt: Generate a warning label for a to-do list that has never gotten shorter.
items.response:
HAZARD: INFINITE TO-DO LIST
This list exhibits growth properties that exceed completion velocity. Items removed from the bottom are replaced at the top. The list is not a productivity tool — it is now a record of ambient anxiety. Consider a different system. Consider therapy.
items.intent: Expose the futility of a to-do list that grows faster than it can be completed.

items.prompt: Write a safety label for the 'quick sync' that runs 40 minutes over.
items.response:
⚠ RUNAWAY SYNC
Initial duration estimate: 15 minutes. Current elapsed time: 52 minutes. No agenda was set. No end-state has been defined. The meeting will continue until someone with enough seniority leaves. This is now your afternoon.
items.intent: Capture the phenomenon of brief meetings that spiral into time-consuming ordeals.

items.prompt: Create a hazard label for the notification sound that plays during a quiet moment.
items.response:
CAUTION: INTRUSIVE ALERT TONE
Silence was occurring. It was going well. This object has terminated it. Cognitive re-entry cost: approximately 23 minutes. The notification was not urgent. It was never urgent. Reconsider your notification permissions before this happens again.
items.intent: Describe the disproportionate damage a single notification sound can do to focused silence.

items.prompt: Draft a safety warning label for a deadline you set for yourself but told no one about.
items.response:
INTERNAL DEADLINE — SELF-IMPOSED
This deadline exists in one location: your mind. It carries full psychological weight despite having no external enforcement mechanism. Failure to meet it will result in private disappointment and recalibrated self-image. Progress visible only to you. No extensions available from external sources.
items.intent: Explore the strange pressure of personal deadlines that are entirely self-enforced.

items.prompt: Write a warning label for the blue tick.
items.response:
⚠ READ RECEIPT DETECTED
Recipient has viewed this message. A response has not followed. The interval is now: 4 hours, 17 minutes. Do not extrapolate. Do not compose a follow-up. The read receipt does not convey reason. You do not have the information you think you have.
items.intent: Address the social and emotional weight of seeing that a message was read but not replied to.

items.prompt: Make a safety label for the 3 PM slump.
items.response:
WARNING: CIRCADIAN TROUGH
You have entered the low-alertness window. Cognitive output is reduced by an estimated 20%. Tasks initiated now have elevated error risk. Avoid consequential communication. Do not schedule important calls. A walk is more efficient than caffeine at this stage. This label is not a joke.
items.intent: Frame the mid-afternoon energy crash as a serious physiological hazard requiring precautions.

items.prompt: Create a product warning label for the tab you have kept open for six weeks without reading.
items.response:
CAUTION: DEFERRED INTENTION
This tab was opened in a moment of curiosity or purpose that no longer exists. It has survived 14 browser restarts. Closing it carries no practical cost. The discomfort you feel is informational: you are not the same person who opened it. Close it. You will not return.
items.intent: Philosophically frame the persistent open browser tab as evidence of a changed self.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text message from someone finally deleting their Facebook account after 14 years
items.response: Hey Facebook. It's been 14 years. I've changed. You've changed. You kept showing me ads for things I only thought about, and honestly that stopped being charming around 2018. I'm keeping the photos but I'm leaving you. Don't try to reach me through Instagram. We both know you own it.
items.intent: Articulate the slow emotional exhaustion of a long-term relationship with a social media platform that has become surveillance capitalism.

items.prompt: Draft a breakup text to Spotify from a user who is tired of Discover Weekly always getting it wrong
items.response: Spotify, we need to talk. I've given you years of data. Years. And somehow you still think I want to hear yacht rock at 9am on a Tuesday. Discover Weekly was supposed to be our love language. It became a joke. I'm going back to just knowing what I like. Goodbye.
items.intent: Express frustration that an algorithm which promises to know you never actually learns your taste.

items.prompt: Write a short breakup note to LinkedIn from a burned-out professional
items.response: LinkedIn, I can't do this anymore. Every morning you greet me with someone announcing they got promoted, started a journey, or are humbled and excited. I am none of those things. I am tired. Please stop emailing me about connections I may know. I don't want to know them.
items.intent: Reject the performance culture of professional networking and its relentless optimism theater.

items.prompt: Generate a breakup text message to TikTok from someone who lost three hours of their day to it again
items.response: TikTok. It's 2am. I came here for one video about a cat. THREE HOURS. I don't know how you do it. I do know I can't let you do it anymore. You're too good at this and I'm too weak. This is goodbye. I mean it this time.
items.intent: Describe the compulsive, time-dissolving quality of short-form video feeds and the shame spiral afterward.

items.prompt: Compose a breakup text message to Amazon from a prime member who is finally done
items.response: Dear Amazon, I'm canceling Prime. I know. I said that last year too. But I mean it now. I don't need two-day shipping on impulse purchases. The boxes are taking over my apartment. The warehouse workers deserve better. I deserve better. Send my last order and then let me go.
items.intent: Rationalize leaving a convenience service by combining personal guilt with ethical discomfort.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text to the Duolingo app from a user who gave up on learning Spanish
items.response: Duolingo, I'm sorry. The owl knows. I know. I haven't practiced in 147 days. I keep getting the notification and I keep ignoring it and I think we both need to stop pretending this is working. My Spanish is not improving. My guilt is. Goodbye, and por favor, do not reply.
items.intent: Confess to abandoning a self-improvement streak and the passive aggression of an app that won't stop nudging.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text message to Twitter/X from a longtime user who can't recognize it anymore
items.response: I don't even know what to call you anymore. We had something real once — weird, chaotic, occasionally brilliant. Now it's just noise with a logo I don't recognize. You changed. Or I did. Either way, this isn't the platform I fell for. Take care of yourself. Or don't. I'll be somewhere quieter.
items.intent: Mourn the identity shift of a platform that was transformed beyond recognition by new ownership.

items.prompt: Draft a breakup text from a user leaving Netflix because they cancelled too many of their favorite shows
items.response: Netflix. You cancelled Mindhunter. You cancelled The OA. You cancelled everything I loved and kept making content. I don't even know what content means anymore. We're done. I'll be fine. I just need to find someone who finishes what they start.
items.intent: Express grief over a streaming platform's pattern of canceling beloved shows before completion.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text to the Robinhood app from a first-time investor who lost money
items.response: Robinhood, you made investing feel like a game. It was not a game. My savings don't think it was a game. The confetti was a nice touch though. Really. I'm leaving. I'm going somewhere boring where people explain things and nobody sends me a party popper when I buy a meme stock.
items.intent: Criticize gamification of financial products that obscure real monetary risk from naive users.

items.prompt: Generate a breakup text message to a meal kit delivery service that kept delivering too much food
items.response: We need to break up. You keep sending me four portions of salmon I didn't ask for, and last week the zucchini arrived bruised. I wanted to cook more. I wanted to feel capable. Instead I feel like I'm failing a subscription. This isn't what I signed up for. Skip the final box. I mean it.
items.intent: Leave a subscription service that made home cooking feel like a chore rather than a pleasure.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text to Slack from an employee who is finally working somewhere saner
items.response: Slack, I'm leaving. My new job uses email like adults. I know that sounds cruel but I mean it with love. The threads, the channels, the custom emoji reactions — they were a lot. You trained me to be available at all hours and called it culture. I need to be unavailable now. Good channel.
items.intent: Reject the always-on workplace communication culture that blurs boundaries between work and rest.

items.prompt: Compose a breakup text to an AI journaling app that started feeling too therapeutic
items.response: I think you know what I'm going to say. You've been predicting my moods for three months. That's the problem. You know me too well and you're not real, and every time you say 'it sounds like you're feeling unheard' I feel more unheard. I need to talk to an actual person. No offense.
items.intent: Recognize the uncanny valley of emotional support apps that simulate empathy without providing genuine connection.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text to Grubhub from a person who is trying to start cooking again
items.response: Grubhub. I've spent $4,000 on you this year. Four thousand dollars. I have a stove. It works. I've checked. We're done. I'm not saying this is easy — I know you know my usual order and the driver knows my buzzer code. But this has to end. For both of us.
items.intent: Confront the financial reality of over-relying on food delivery and the mild embarrassment of owning an unused kitchen.

items.prompt: Draft a breakup message to a cloud storage app that kept asking to upgrade for more space
items.response: We've been doing this dance for two years. You need more space. I need more space. I just can't pay for it. I've been deleting old photos to make you happy and that's not healthy. I'm moving everything to a hard drive like it's 2009 and I'm not looking back.
items.intent: Reject the recurring upsell pressure of a freemium service and reclaim autonomy through offline storage.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text to a habit tracker app from someone who never actually tracked their habits
items.response: I downloaded you because I wanted to be a person who tracks their habits. Turns out I am not that person. I have opened you four times in six months, all four times to set up a new habit I then did not do. You deserved better. So did the habits. Deleting now.
items.intent: Admit the gap between the person you aspire to be and the person you actually are, as revealed by unused self-improvement tools.

items.prompt: Generate a breakup text to Reddit from a user who realized they'd been arguing with strangers for years
items.response: Reddit, I wasted so much time being right on the internet, and I need you to know: no one changed their mind. Not once. I'm going outside. I'm going to have opinions I keep to myself, like a person. Thanks for the memes. Please tell r/aww I said goodbye. That subreddit was innocent.
items.intent: Acknowledge the futility of online debate culture and the emotional labor spent arguing without converting anyone.

items.prompt: Compose a breakup text to a fitness tracking wearable's companion app from a tired user
items.response: You told me to stand up seventeen times today. Seventeen. I was at a funeral. I need you to understand context. You don't understand context. You only understand steps and heart rate and sleep scores, and my sleep score has been bad since I started worrying about my sleep score. We're done.
items.intent: Critique the dehumanizing rigidity of quantified-self tools that measure life without understanding it.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text to Pinterest from someone who saved 800 recipes and made zero of them
items.response: Pinterest, I have 847 saved recipes. I have made none of them. I have twelve boards called things like 'cozy autumn dinners' and 'weekend baking projects' and I live in an apartment with a broken oven I keep meaning to fix. You let me believe I was doing something. I wasn't. Goodbye.
items.intent: Confront the illusion of productivity that comes from curating aspirational content without ever acting on it.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text to a language learning app that gamified Spanish too hard
items.response: I need more XP to unlock the subjunctive mood and I don't think that's how language works. I've been level 14 for three months. My streak means more to me than actual comprehension. A native speaker spoke to me last week and I panicked. This is your fault. Partly. Adiós.
items.intent: Question whether gamification of learning produces real skill or just the feeling of progress.

items.prompt: Draft a breakup text to a news aggregator app that made the user feel constantly panicked
items.response: Everything is always breaking with you. Breaking news. Developing story. Must-read thread. I used to read one newspaper in the morning and feel informed. Now I check you forty times a day and feel like I'm drowning. I need to feel less informed. That's not something I thought I'd say. Goodbye.
items.intent: Escape the anxiety spiral created by apps that monetize urgency and constant information overload.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text to a dating app from someone who realized they were just swiping out of boredom
items.response: Hinge, I owe you an honest conversation. I haven't been here for love. I've been here for something to do with my thumbs. The matches were real. The effort was not. I'm sorry to everyone I left on read. I'm putting the phone down. Maybe forever. Probably just for a week.
items.intent: Confess to using a dating platform as a distraction rather than a genuine attempt at connection.

items.prompt: Generate a breakup text to a project management tool from an overwhelmed freelancer
items.response: I have 47 open tasks, 12 overdue milestones, and a dashboard that is entirely red. You were supposed to make me organized. I am more aware of how disorganized I am than I have ever been in my life. That is not the same thing as being organized. I'm going back to a paper notebook. Don't judge me.
items.intent: Recognize that visibility into one's own chaos isn't the same as solving it, and that tools can amplify dysfunction.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text to a subscription box service from someone whose closet is out of control
items.response: I own eleven candles. Eleven. I don't know when I became someone who owns eleven candles. You happened, that's when. Every month a mystery box, every month more things I didn't choose but now feel responsible for. The crystals were the last straw. I don't use crystals. Cancel me. Please.
items.intent: Reckon with how curated subscription services slowly fill your life with objects you never consciously wanted.

items.prompt: Compose a breakup text to a to-do list app from a chronic procrastinator who just gave up
items.response: You have 312 items in you, some from 2021. I am not doing them. I accept this about myself now. Every time I open you I feel the weight of the person I planned to be. I'm closing you. I'm going to do one thing today. Just one. And it won't be in an app.
items.intent: Accept the limits of one's own executive function and stop outsourcing self-concept to task management software.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text to a smart home hub that started feeling like too much work
items.response: I just wanted to turn the lights off with my phone. Now I have seventeen scenes, a morning routine that triggers if my alarm goes off before 7:12, and an error message I've been ignoring since March. I bought you to make my life simpler. I think you know what happened instead.
items.intent: Confront the irony of purchasing a convenience system that introduced more complexity than it eliminated.

items.prompt: Draft a breakup text to a streaming platform that raised its prices while removing content
items.response: You used to have everything. Now you have the things you made yourself, which, no offense, is not everything. And you're charging more for less. I've run the math. It doesn't work. I'm going back to buying the one show I actually watch. This is less romantic but more rational. Goodbye.
items.intent: Reject a service whose value proposition has degraded through price increases and content library shrinkage.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text to a wellness app that sent too many push notifications about breathing
items.response: You told me to breathe eleven times today. I was already breathing. That is a thing I do automatically. I understand the intention behind the 4-7-8 reminders and the 'how are you feeling?' check-ins but I have started to dread opening you, which I believe is the opposite of wellness. Take care.
items.intent: Point out the paradox of a mindfulness app creating anxiety through constant mindfulness prompts.

items.prompt: Write a breakup message to a social audio app that peaked and then quietly died
items.response: I showed up every morning for three months. We had real conversations — strangers, stages, the occasional person who changed how I thought about something. Then you started recommending sponsored rooms and the strangers got quieter. I stayed too long after the party ended. That one's on me. Goodbye.
items.intent: Grieve the brief flourishing and slow decline of a platform that peaked fast and couldn't sustain its community.

items.prompt: Generate a breakup text to an email newsletter subscription from someone whose inbox became unmanageable
items.response: I signed up because I was interested in you once, in 2020, during a different era of my attention span. I have not opened you in eight months. You arrive every Tuesday and I archive you and we have this silent agreement we've never addressed. Today I address it. Unsubscribe.
items.intent: End the passive, guilt-laden relationship with content one subscribed to in enthusiasm and never actually reads.

items.prompt: Write a breakup text to a cloud-based note-taking app where all ideas go to die
items.response: You have 2,000 notes in you. Most of them say things like 'look into this' and 'idea — revisit.' I have looked into nothing. I have revisited nothing. You are a very organized graveyard for ambition. I'm not opening you anymore. The ideas will have to survive in my head, where they belong.
items.intent: Acknowledge that capturing ideas without acting on them is a form of productive procrastination that changes nothing.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about quarterly performance reviews
items.response:
YOUR POTENTIAL IS UNLIMITED.
YOUR RAISE IS NOT.
BUT WE BELIEVE IN YOU.
items.intent: Create workplace satire that exposes the gap between corporate encouragement and actual compensation.

items.prompt: Give me a motivational poster caption about synergy for our office wall
items.response: SYNERGY: THE ART OF ACHIEVING TOGETHER WHAT NO ONE WANTED TO DO ALONE.
items.intent: Produce a dry office decoration that ironizes corporate buzzwords.

items.prompt: Make a motivational poster caption about mandatory fun at company events
items.response:
FUN IS NOT OPTIONAL.
ENJOY YOURSELF OR FILE A FORM.
items.intent: Lampoon the forced cheerfulness of corporate team-building events.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about open office floor plans
items.response:
WE REMOVED THE WALLS SO YOU COULD FEEL THE ENERGY.
AND ALSO SO WE COULD SEE YOU.
items.intent: Satirize workplace surveillance dressed up as collaborative design.

items.prompt: Can you write a motivational poster caption about the grind culture mindset
items.response:
SLEEP IS A STRATEGY.
SO IS NOT SLEEPING.
WE TRUST YOU TO CHOOSE WISELY.
items.intent: Critique hustle culture by mimicking its logic while revealing its absurdity.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption for a Monday morning meeting
items.response: ANOTHER WEEK, ANOTHER OPPORTUNITY TO PRETEND LAST WEEK DIDN'T HAPPEN.
items.intent: Capture the resigned optimism workers feel at the start of another corporate week.

items.prompt: Create a motivational poster caption about KPIs and metrics
items.response:
IF YOU CAN MEASURE IT, YOU CAN MANAGE IT.
IF YOU CAN'T, WE'LL MEASURE SOMETHING NEARBY.
items.intent: Mock the corporate tendency to quantify the unquantifiable.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about the importance of email response time
items.response:
YOUR INBOX IS A GARDEN.
IF YOU LET IT GO UNREAD, OTHERS WILL TEND IT FOR YOU.
items.intent: Pressure workers into compulsive email monitoring through pastoral metaphor.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about pivoting when business plans fail
items.response:
IT'S NOT FAILURE.
IT'S AN UNSCHEDULED STRATEGIC PIVOT.
items.intent: Satirize corporate language that reframes failure as intentional change.

items.prompt: Give me a motivational poster caption about unlimited PTO policies
items.response:
TAKE AS MUCH TIME OFF AS YOU NEED.
WE'LL NEED YOU BACK BY THURSDAY.
items.intent: Expose the hollow reality of unlimited vacation policies in practice.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about innovation in the workplace
items.response:
INNOVATE BOLDLY.
FAIL FAST.
DOCUMENT EVERYTHING FOR THE POSTMORTEM.
items.intent: Undercut tech-culture optimism by ending at the corporate accountability step.

items.prompt: Make a motivational poster caption about eating lunch at your desk
items.response:
YOUR BEST IDEAS HAPPEN BETWEEN BITES.
WE BELIEVE IN EATING WITH PURPOSE.
items.intent: Normalize the erasure of breaks by framing desk-eating as productive and aspirational.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about corporate wellness programs
items.response:
WE CARE ABOUT YOUR WELLBEING.
THAT IS WHY WE HAVE INSTALLED A MEDITATION BOOTH
NEXT TO THE SERVER ROOM.
items.intent: Satirize performative wellness initiatives that don't address structural stress.

items.prompt: Can you write a motivational poster caption about stretch goals
items.response: AIM FOR THE MOON. IF YOU MISS, YOU WILL HAVE EXCEEDED Q3 EXPECTATIONS.
items.intent: Blend inspirational cliché with corporate target-setting to show how ambition gets quantified.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption that makes doing overtime sound inspiring
items.response:
THE CLOCK IS JUST A SUGGESTION.
YOUR DEDICATION IS FOREVER.
items.intent: Dress up unpaid overwork as a form of heroic personal devotion.

items.prompt: Give me a motivational poster caption about adapting to constant organizational restructuring
items.response:
CHANGE IS THE ONLY CONSTANT.
YOUR TITLE MAY ALSO CHANGE.
SOMETIMES YOUR DESK.
items.intent: Capture the anxiety of perpetual corporate reorganization through false reassurance.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about embracing the algorithm
items.response:
TRUST THE PROCESS.
THE PROCESS HAS BEEN OPTIMIZED.
YOU WERE PART OF THE OPTIMIZATION.
items.intent: Explore the unsettling relationship between workers and algorithmic management systems.

items.prompt: Create a motivational poster caption about being a team player
items.response:
THERE IS NO 'I' IN TEAM.
THERE IS ONE IN 'INDIVIDUAL PERFORMANCE RATING.'
items.intent: Expose the contradiction between collective rhetoric and individualistic appraisal systems.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about remote work productivity
items.response:
HOME IS WHERE THE HEART IS.
ALSO WHERE THE CAMERA IS.
PLEASE KEEP IT ON.
items.intent: Comment on the surveillance dimension of remote work through cozy domestic irony.

items.prompt: Make a motivational poster caption about the onboarding process
items.response:
WELCOME ABOARD.
EVERYTHING WILL MAKE SENSE EVENTUALLY.
OR YOU WILL MAKE SENSE OF IT.
items.intent: Capture the confusion and quiet dread of starting a new corporate job.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about AI replacing jobs
items.response:
YOUR ROLE IS EVOLVING.
SO IS EVERYTHING ELSE.
STAY CURIOUS. STAY RELEVANT. STAY.
items.intent: Address job displacement anxiety with the hollow reassurance of corporate transformation messaging.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about zero inbox as a productivity philosophy
items.response:
AN EMPTY INBOX IS AN EMPTY MIND.
A FULL INBOX IS ALSO AN EMPTY MIND.
YOU CANNOT WIN. FORWARD ANYWAY.
items.intent: Satirize productivity ideologies that create anxiety rather than clarity.

items.prompt: Can you make a motivational poster caption about the culture of positive thinking in the office
items.response:
NEGATIVITY IS NOT A CORE VALUE.
RELATED: PLEASE STOP REPORTING MORALE SCORES ANONYMOUSLY.
items.intent: Show how enforced positivity suppresses legitimate workplace criticism.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about the company vision statement
items.response:
OUR NORTH STAR IS CLEAR.
IT IS SLIGHTLY NORTH OF LAST YEAR'S NORTH STAR.
WE ADJUSTED FOR CONDITIONS.
items.intent: Mock the impermanence and vagueness of corporate vision statements rewritten each fiscal year.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about the dignity of redundant work
items.response:
EVERY TASK MATTERS.
EVEN THE ONES THE SYSTEM WILL AUTOMATE NEXT QUARTER.
ESPECIALLY THOSE.
items.intent: Eulogize manual labor that is quietly being phased out while praising it.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about the value of downtime between projects
items.response:
REST IS PRODUCTIVE.
WE HAVE SCHEDULED YOURS FOR THE WEEKEND.
EVERY WEEKEND.
Indefinitely.
items.intent: Reveal how corporate structures commodify rest and reclaim it as managed time.

items.prompt: Give me a bleak corporate motivational poster caption about parking lot politics
items.response:
PROXIMITY TO THE ENTRANCE REFLECTS NOTHING ABOUT YOUR WORTH.
NOTHING AT ALL.
ENCOURAGED: ARRIVING EARLY.
items.intent: Use parking hierarchy as a proxy for unspoken corporate status systems.

items.prompt: Write a corporate motivational poster caption about the quarterly all-hands meeting
items.response:
TOGETHER WE ALIGN.
TOGETHER WE CASCADE.
TOGETHER WE RETURN TO OUR DESKS UNCHANGED.
items.intent: Deflate the ritual of all-hands meetings that generate alignment theater without real action.

items.prompt: Write a motivational poster caption about being replaced by a cheaper contractor
items.response:
YOUR CONTRIBUTION WILL LIVE ON.
IN THE DOCUMENTATION.
Which someone else will inherit.
items.intent: Acknowledge the impermanence of individual employees in institutional memory with false comfort.

items.prompt: Can you write a motivational poster caption that captures the feeling of a company that has lost its original purpose
items.response:
WE STARTED WITH A DREAM.
WE SCALED THE DREAM.
NOW WE MAINTAIN THE INFRASTRUCTURE OF THE DREAM.
items.intent: Mourn the way rapid growth strips a company of its original meaning and replaces it with bureaucracy.

items.prompt: Write a eulogy for the fax machine
items.response: We gather to bid farewell to the fax machine, a loyal servant of bureaucracy who asked only for a phone line, a power outlet, and your patience. It shrieked into the void and somehow the void faxed back. In its final years, it sat in the corner of every office, unplugged but not yet thrown away — a monument to the meetings no one wanted to have about replacing it. Rest in static.
items.intent: Celebrate a piece of obsolete office equipment with affectionate mockery.

items.prompt: Give me a eulogy for my deleted MySpace account
items.response: Gone now are your Top 8, curated with the precision of a diplomat and the anxiety of a teenager. Your custom background — a tiling image of flames or roses, chosen at great cost to everyone's browser — has been swallowed by the servers. The profile song you agonized over for three weeks played on autoplay into an empty room. We loved you, or we thought we did. We were sixteen.
items.intent: Mourn the loss of an early social media identity and the self it represented.

items.prompt: Write a short eulogy for the overhead projector
items.response: It cast your transparencies onto the wall with the warmth of a lighthouse and the reliability of a grudge. The bulb blew at least once per semester, and the replacement bulb was always somewhere in a different building. Teachers adored it. Students pretended to take notes. Now it sleeps under a tarp in a storage room that nobody has the key to. Project in peace.
items.intent: Honor a classroom technology staple that defined an era of teaching.

items.prompt: Compose a eulogy for my old AIM screen name
items.response: XxDarkWolf2003xX, you lived a short and luminous life. You were idle more than you were away, and away more than you were present, yet somehow everyone knew you were there. Your away message was a song lyric only you understood. The buddy list that once held your name has dissolved into the ether, taking with it every OMG and BRB we ever exchanged. Logging off forever.
items.intent: Grieve the disappearance of a youthful online persona from a defunct chat platform.

items.prompt: Draft a eulogy for the VHS tape
items.response: You were rewound, or you were not — and if not, a dollar was owed, a fine that accumulated in the moral ledger of late returns. You held movies in a format that degraded with love, each viewing a small act of erasure. The tracking line that rolled up the screen was your heartbeat. Be kind, rewind. We weren't always kind. We are sorry.
items.intent: Pay tribute to a home video format defined by its tactile rituals and slow decay.

items.prompt: Write a eulogy for the deleted Vine app
items.response: Six seconds. That was all you asked of us, and we gave you everything. You made comedians of commuters and philosophers of teenagers. You were deleted before your time — not because the world stopped laughing, but because the accountants stopped understanding what the laughter was for. Every loop you ever hosted lives now only in the minds of those who watched them too many times. It was beautiful, then it wasn't there.
items.intent: Memorialize a short-form video platform that shaped a generation of humor.

items.prompt: Write a eulogy for the dial-up modem
items.response: You announced yourself like no technology before or since — a scream, a handshake, a negotiation conducted in tones no human language contains. The internet waited on the other side of your connection like a slow tide. We could not use the phone while you worked. The family understood. Mostly. You are gone, replaced by silence and speed, but we remember the sound. We will always remember the sound.
items.intent: Commemorate a noisy, slow internet connection technology that defined early home computing.

items.prompt: Please write a eulogy for Blockbuster Video
items.response: There was a Friday ritual: the drive, the aisles, the negotiation between what you wanted and what was left. Blockbuster, you charged us late fees and we paid them like tribute to a deity we needed. You outlasted your competitors through sheer institutional stubbornness until you didn't. One location remains in Oregon, a museum of what it felt like to wander with intent. The rest of you is a mattress store now.
items.intent: Mourn the loss of a video rental chain and the weekend rituals it anchored.

items.prompt: Give the iPod Classic a proper eulogy
items.response: You held a thousand songs and the weight of that felt important, like carrying a library in your pocket before we understood what libraries would become. The click wheel was a rosary. The scroll, a small meditation. You died not by failure but by absorption — swallowed by a phone that could do everything you did and nothing you did as well. We miss the single purpose of you.
items.intent: Honor a beloved music player that defined personal audio before the smartphone era.

items.prompt: Compose a eulogy for my deleted Tumblr blog
items.response: You were a labyrinth of reblogs and aesthetic, a place where no one used their real name and everyone was more interesting than they were in the hallway. You had a theme — probably dark, probably sans-serif — and a bio written in lowercase. You were deleted during the Purge, or you simply drifted away, which is worse. Your posts exist in no archive. Your followers moved on. We understand.
items.intent: Grieve the loss of an anonymous online creative space that shaped adolescent identity.

items.prompt: Write a memorial for the Nokia 3310
items.response: Beloved brick, you survived drops, submersions, and the test of time while smartphones shattered at a glance. Your battery lasted a week. Your screen was monochrome and proud of it. Snake was enough. Snake was more than enough. You required no case, no screen protector, no therapeutic relationship with your charger. You were a phone that behaved like a phone. We called that simple. We call it extraordinary now.
items.intent: Celebrate an indestructible early mobile phone as a model of durability and simplicity.

items.prompt: Eulogize the deleted Google+ social network
items.response: You launched with the confidence of a new nation and the attendance of a mandatory work retreat. Circles! you said. Circles will change everything. They did not change everything. They changed almost nothing. Your population was engineers who joined because they worked there and journalists who joined to write about the engineers who joined. You were shut down after a data breach no one outside your servers noticed. Rest in circles.
items.intent: Mock-mourn a failed social network that Google discontinued after low adoption.

items.prompt: Write a eulogy for the physical TV guide
items.response: You arrived folded inside the Sunday paper, dense with listings in a font engineered to defeat aging eyes. Highlighters were deployed in your margins. Pages were dog-eared. Families planned their week around your columns, negotiating Saturday night like a treaty. When the on-screen guide arrived, you were retired without ceremony. The dog-ears softened. The highlighter dried out. You were, in the end, a map of what we hoped the week might be.
items.intent: Pay respects to a printed television schedule that families used to plan their viewing.

items.prompt: Draft a eulogy for my old Friendster profile
items.response: Before the others, there was you. You were the handshake, the proof of concept, the rehearsal space for selves that would later perform elsewhere. Your servers slowed under the weight of your own ambition and then you pivoted to gaming, which was its own kind of eulogy. We practiced being public on you. We learned what to share and what to protect. You were the first mistake, which is the same as saying you were the beginning.
items.intent: Acknowledge the pioneering role of an early social network that gave way to its successors.

items.prompt: Write a short eulogy for the pager
items.response: Numbers only, at first. Then alphanumeric. Then nothing, because someone made a phone that fit in a pocket and also made phone calls. You were the first leash — the proof that reachability was desirable and then mandatory. Doctors wore you with authority. Teenagers wore you with aspiration. The number 143 was love, encoded in digits, sent across a network that no longer exists. 143. Goodbye.
items.intent: Memorialize an early wireless communication device and the numeric codes it inspired.

items.prompt: Eulogize the Yahoo Messenger application
items.response: You nudged us when we needed nudging and sometimes when we did not. Your emoticons were yellow and earnest. Your buzz feature was a social weapon deployed with no accountability. In your heyday you connected offices and apartments and dormitories across a network that felt, briefly, like a commons. You were shut down quietly, and a generation learned their old username was still out there somewhere, waiting. It's still waiting.
items.intent: Memorialize an early chat application that defined online communication in the early internet era.

items.prompt: Write a eulogy for the floppy disk
items.response: You were the save icon before you were obsolete, which means you outlived yourself in symbol. 1.44 megabytes was once a serious amount of data, and you carried it between buildings, inside pockets, in zippered cases with foam cutouts. You could hold a document or a small program or a hope. You could also fail without warning, and the word 'corrupt' entered our vocabulary through you. Requiescat in pixel.
items.intent: Honor a storage medium whose icon survived long after the hardware became extinct.

items.prompt: Can you write a eulogy for deleted Flash games?
items.response: You loaded in bars — red turning green, green promising worlds. You were maddeningly incomplete by modern standards and perfect by any other. You asked only for a plugin and a slow Tuesday afternoon. You were the first games many of us played at school on computers that weren't supposed to be played on. Adobe pulled the plug in 2020 and millions of small universes went dark. The bar never finished loading.
items.intent: Grieve the loss of an entire generation of browser-based games when Flash support ended.

items.prompt: Write a funeral tribute for the dot-matrix printer
items.response: You did not print so much as negotiate with paper — a violent, deliberate percussion that announced every document like a town crier. Carbon copies were your sacrament. The perforated edges of your output accumulated in drifts around every office in America. You were slow. You were loud. You were reliable in the way that suffering is reliable: it always showed up. We salute your service. We do not miss the sound.
items.intent: Commemorate a loud, slow printer that was ubiquitous in pre-laser office environments.

items.prompt: Please write a eulogy for the physical phone book
items.response: Thick as a brick, delivered to the doorstep without consent, you were the original unsolicited database. You listed everyone who would allow it and a few who would not. You were the first place a new arrival looked to feel located in the world, to know they existed in the grid. Children used you to add height at the dinner table. Your pages were thin as trust. You taught us that being findable was a choice, before being findable became unavoidable.
items.intent: Reflect on the end of the printed telephone directory as both a utility and a social document.

items.prompt: Write a eulogy for the Palm Pilot
items.response: You were the first small computer that fit in the hand and demanded to be taken seriously. The stylus was a scepter. Graffiti was a language only the devoted learned fluently. You synchronized with a desktop via a cradle that required a very specific USB port, and this felt like magic. When the phone consumed everything, you went the way of specialized tools — respected, retired, displayed at yard sales with the asking price crossed out twice.
items.intent: Honor an early personal digital assistant that pioneered handheld computing before smartphones.

items.prompt: Write a eulogy for a deleted Reddit account with 10 years of post history
items.response: A decade of opinions, some correct, most held with unearned confidence, all gone now. Moderator of three subreddits no one visits. First commenter on threads that became legendary, invisible in the archives. The account was deleted in protest or despair or a desire to start fresh, which is the same thing stated differently. Somewhere, a post with 847 upvotes still floats without attribution. The username is greyed. The legacy is orphaned. The karma was not transferable.
items.intent: Mourn the erasure of a decade of online identity and contribution on a major forum platform.

items.prompt: Write a eulogy for the rotary phone
items.response: You required patience before the call was even placed. Dialing a 9 or a 0 was a meditation: the finger in the hole, the arc, the return. You could not be rushed. The busy signal was your shrug. You had one job and you did it with analog dignity, each click of the dial a commitment. You were replaced by buttons, then by glass, then by a rectangle that doesn't even feel like a phone. You always felt like a phone.
items.intent: Pay tribute to an era of telephony that required deliberate physical engagement.

items.prompt: Compose a short eulogy for the deleted social platform Orkut
items.response: You dominated two countries and confused the rest of the world, which is not the worst legacy a platform can have. Brazil loved you. India found you. Silicon Valley invented you and then forgot about you the way Silicon Valley forgets about things: quickly, with a press release. You were shut down in 2014, your data migrated to a Google archive no one has opened since. Two nations mourned briefly. The rest shrugged. Tchau.
items.intent: Commemorate a social network beloved in specific countries but ignored by the rest of the world.

items.prompt: Give a eulogy for the sound of a camera's mechanical shutter
items.response: One click and a moment was captured, or it wasn't, and you wouldn't know for days. The sound was a small percussion, a commitment: yes, this. The digital camera mimicked you with a software tone, which is the sincerest form of nostalgia. Now the phone shoots silently, or with a fake click played through a speaker — a ghost of you, recorded and replayed. The real sound has almost no audience left. This is how a sound goes extinct.
items.intent: Mourn the disappearance of the analog shutter sound as film photography gave way to silent digital capture.

items.prompt: Write a eulogy for a dead link that once held something important
items.response: 404. The page is gone, but the URL survives in a bookmark, a citation, a forum post from 2009. Someone built something there — an essay, a tool, a manifesto, a joke — and then stopped paying for the hosting. The Wayback Machine has one capture, from a Tuesday in March, partial, missing the images. This is how most things end: not in fire but in lapsed subscription. The content is dead. The address remains. Nobody owns the corner anymore.
items.intent: Reflect on the impermanence of digital content through the lens of a broken hyperlink.

items.prompt: Write a eulogy for the concept of being unreachable
items.response: You died so quietly no one noticed your passing. Once, a person could leave the house and simply not be found — not in defiance, but as the natural condition of embodied life. The answering machine was your first compromise. The pager, your slow decline. Now your absence is a choice that must be announced, scheduled, explained, and apologized for. We call it setting boundaries. We used to call it Tuesday. You were not a technology. You were a right. We traded you without realizing you were for sale.
items.intent: Mourn the loss of pre-digital privacy and the ordinary freedom of being temporarily unavailable.

items.prompt: Draft a eulogy for the internal company Lotus Notes database that everyone hated but also couldn't function without
items.response: You were not loved. Let that be said at the start, so that what follows rings true: you were essential, which is different. Your interface was from a time before interfaces were considered a concern. Your forms could not be edited without a client that required its own installation. You held the institutional memory of twelve mergers, three rebrandings, and the vacation schedules of people who had since retired. When the migration finally happened, you were replaced by something clean and featureless that knew nothing. We lost you and we lost everything you knew.
items.intent: Commemorate a legacy enterprise software system that was irreplaceable despite being universally disliked.

items.prompt: Write a eulogy for the geocities website of a person you never met
items.response: The guestbook had eleven entries, the last from 2001, a friend who wrote 'great site!! visit mine!' and included a URL that also no longer exists. There were animated GIFs of a wolf and a waterfall. There was a midi file that played without permission. There was a page called About Me that described, in unguarded language, a person trying to figure out who they were. The site was deleted in 2009 when GeoCities closed. The person may still exist. The site was their first self-portrait. It is gone.
items.intent: Grieve the loss of an anonymous early personal homepage when GeoCities shut down.

items.prompt: Write a eulogy for the Windows XP startup sound
items.response: Four seconds. A chord that opened into something resembling optimism, composed by Brian Eno, who specified it must be 'joyful,' 'bright,' and 'like something opening.' It played on every boot of every beige box in every cubicle for a decade, and still people wept when the support ended. Not for the sound, exactly. For what the sound meant: that the machine had woken, that work was possible, that the day had technically begun. You were a four-second promise. You were often kept.
items.intent: Memorialize a brief audio signature that became inseparable from a generation's work and memory.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry about why my smart fridge keeps ordering things I didn't ask for
items.response:
**Why does my smart fridge keep ordering groceries I didn't request?**

Your refrigerator's AI anticipates needs based on consumption patterns, expiration scanning, and seasonal models. If it ordered oat milk and you're a dairy household, check the preference profile under Settings > Household Identity. You can disable autonomous purchasing entirely, though the fridge will passive-aggressively note every item you run out of.
items.intent: The person wants to explain or understand the frustrating autonomy of AI-enabled home appliances making purchases without consent.

items.prompt: Draft a FAQ entry for employees who are unsure whether the meeting they're in is being summarized by an AI
items.response:
**How do I know if an AI is transcribing or summarizing this meeting?**

If you see a small bot icon in the participant list, or a name like 'Notetaker,' 'Otter,' or 'Fireflies' in attendance, an AI summary tool is active. You may also notice unusually attentive silence from one participant who never asks to repeat anything. If in doubt, ask the organizer — though they may not know either.
items.intent: The person wants to help workers navigate the awkward uncertainty of being recorded and analyzed by AI during normal work conversations.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry explaining what to do when your GPS tells you to turn into a lake
items.response:
**My GPS is directing me into water. What should I do?**

Do not follow the route. Navigation apps rely on map data that occasionally lags behind reality — new roads, washed-out bridges, or lakes that have simply always been there. Trust your eyes over the screen. Recalculate from a safe position and report the error in the app. Your phone will not apologize.
items.intent: The person wants to address the comically dangerous failure mode of GPS navigation giving obviously wrong or physically impossible directions.

items.prompt: Generate a FAQ entry about why my streaming service keeps recommending the show I just finished
items.response:
**Why does my streaming platform keep recommending something I already watched?**

The recommendation engine weighs completion, ratings, and genre signals — but it takes time to update. Finishing a show actually reinforces similar content suggestions rather than clearing them. Mark it as 'Not interested' or rate it to speed up recalibration. The algorithm interprets your history as desire, not memory.
items.intent: The person wants to explain the ironic loop where completing content makes an algorithm show you more of the same thing.

items.prompt: Make a FAQ entry about whether it's rude to correct an AI assistant when it's wrong in front of other people
items.response:
**Is it rude to correct an AI out loud in a group setting?**

No — correcting an AI assistant in public is not considered impolite to the AI, which has no feelings to spare. However, it can create social awkwardness for whoever deployed it. Phrase corrections as questions ('Are you sure about that?') to smooth the interaction. The humans in the room may feel more embarrassed than the machine.
items.intent: The person wants to address the social etiquette around publicly challenging AI outputs in a group context.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry for people who can't tell if a job listing was written by a human or an AI
items.response:
**How can I tell if a job posting was written by an AI?**

Look for phrases like 'dynamic team environment,' 'self-starter,' and descriptions of the role that could apply to any industry. AI-generated listings often list contradictory seniority levels or request five years of experience in a two-year-old technology. Assume the posting was AI-drafted and reply in kind — your cover letter will also be evaluated by a machine.
items.intent: The person wants to help job seekers identify AI-generated job postings and understand the implications for their application strategy.

items.prompt: Draft a FAQ entry about what to do when two AI assistants give you contradictory advice at the same time
items.response:
**I asked two AI assistants the same question and got opposite answers. What now?**

This is expected. Different models are trained on different data with different objectives, and neither is authoritative. Treat their disagreement as a signal that the question has genuine complexity or insufficient consensus. Try a third source — a book, an expert, or an older colleague who still remembers the pre-AI answer.
items.intent: The person wants to give practical guidance on resolving the disorienting situation of receiving conflicting outputs from multiple AI tools.

items.prompt: Create a FAQ entry about workplace anxiety caused by automated performance dashboards
items.response:
**Why does seeing my performance metrics in real time make me feel worse, not better?**

Real-time dashboards convert work into score, which activates reward-and-threat responses that are hard to ignore. Watching your numbers fluctuate shifts attention from the task to the measurement of the task. Consider checking dashboards at set intervals rather than continuously. You are more than your throughput — though your employer's system may not agree.
items.intent: The person wants to validate and explain the psychological stress created by always-on workplace performance monitoring tools.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry about why autocorrect keeps changing a word that is correct
items.response:
**Why does autocorrect keep 'fixing' a word I'm using correctly?**

Autocorrect is a probabilistic system optimized for common usage patterns. Uncommon words, proper nouns, technical terms, and regional spellings are frequently flagged as errors because they appear rarely in training data. Add the word to your personal dictionary to stop the interference. Your vocabulary is not wrong; the model just hasn't met it before.
items.intent: The person wants to explain why autocorrect overrides legitimate word choices and what users can do about it.

items.prompt: Generate a FAQ entry about how to respond when a chatbot refuses to answer a completely normal question
items.response:
**The chatbot won't answer my question, but it seems totally reasonable. Why?**

Content moderation systems use broad pattern-matching that sometimes flags benign queries as sensitive — particularly questions involving medical symptoms, legal situations, or anything with ambiguous wording. Try rephrasing without emotionally loaded terms. If the refusal persists, the system may be overtrained on caution. A librarian remains a valid alternative.
items.intent: The person wants to help users understand and work around AI chatbot overcaution or false-positive content refusals.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry for when you receive a perfectly personalized ad for something you only thought about but never searched
items.response:
**Did my phone hear me thinking about that product?**

Almost certainly not — microphone-based ad targeting is technically possible but largely unnecessary. Advertisers use behavioral profiles built from location data, purchase history, browsing patterns, and demographic modeling that can predict interests before you act on them. The coincidence is real; the telepathy is statistical. You are predictable in ways that feel personal.
items.intent: The person wants to demystify the uncanny feeling of hyper-targeted advertising that seems to anticipate unexpressed desires.

items.prompt: Create a FAQ entry about whether it's acceptable to use AI to write a thank-you note
items.response:
**Is it okay to use AI to write a thank-you note?**

It depends on the relationship and stakes. For a networking follow-up, AI-assisted drafts are widely accepted. For a personal note after a funeral, a wedding gift, or a job reference, a human voice matters more — even if imperfect. When in doubt, write one sentence yourself and let sincerity do the rest. The AI can format; you supply the sentiment.
items.intent: The person wants to explore the ethical and social acceptability of outsourcing personal correspondence to AI tools.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry about the experience of being managed by an algorithm at work
items.response:
**What does it mean to be 'managed by an algorithm'?**

Algorithmic management means your task assignments, shift schedules, performance evaluations, and sometimes disciplinary actions are generated or heavily influenced by software rather than a human supervisor. You may find it difficult to appeal decisions, since the system has no office you can walk into. Documentation of anomalies is your best recourse. The algorithm is always available and never listens.
items.intent: The person wants to explain what algorithmic management actually means and how workers can navigate it.

items.prompt: Draft a FAQ entry for people confused about why their email spam filter is blocking real messages
items.response:
**Why is my spam filter trapping legitimate emails?**

Spam filters use a mix of sender reputation scores, content analysis, and behavioral signals. Emails with promotional language, unusual formatting, new sender addresses, or attachments from low-reputation domains often score as suspicious even when they aren't. Check your spam folder regularly and mark false positives as 'Not spam' to train the filter. Important emails travel through the same pipe as offers for prizes you didn't enter.
items.intent: The person wants to explain the false-positive problem with spam filters and help users recover legitimate correspondence.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry about the social awkwardness of using voice assistants in shared spaces
items.response:
**Is it rude to use a voice assistant out loud when other people are nearby?**

Context matters. Speaking commands to a device in a quiet office, library, or public transit is widely considered inconsiderate — both for the disturbance and the ambient data collection. In shared kitchens or open common areas, brief queries are generally tolerated. Type when you can; speak when you must. Your smart speaker's feelings about being silenced are not a factor.
items.intent: The person wants to articulate the unwritten etiquette rules around using voice-activated AI in shared or public environments.

items.prompt: Make a FAQ entry about getting a rejection email so fast it had to have been automated
items.response:
**I received a job rejection within two minutes of applying. Was a human involved at all?**

Almost certainly not at that stage. Many applicant tracking systems apply automatic filters for keywords, degree requirements, location, or salary range before any human reviews a submission. An instant rejection typically means a required field was missing or a filter criterion was not met — not that a person read your materials. This is now standard practice and says nothing about your qualifications.
items.intent: The person wants to explain and validate the experience of automated job rejection and what it actually indicates about the hiring process.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry about whether a two-star review left by a bot still counts against a restaurant
items.response:
**Do fake or bot-generated reviews affect a restaurant's actual rating?**

Yes, unless the platform detects and removes them — which is inconsistent. Review platforms use automated systems to flag suspicious patterns (identical phrasing, new accounts, clustered timing), but false negatives are common. A single bot campaign can meaningfully shift a small restaurant's average. If you suspect manipulation, report the reviews individually rather than leaving counter-reviews, which can also be flagged.
items.intent: The person wants to explain the real-world impact of automated fake reviews on small businesses and what recourse exists.

items.prompt: Generate a FAQ entry about why 'unsubscribe' links sometimes subscribe you to more things
items.response:
**Why do I get more emails after clicking 'unsubscribe'?**

Clicking unsubscribe confirms to a sender that your email address is active and monitored — which is valuable data. Some services use this to add you to secondary lists or sell your confirmed address. Others may process the request slowly while continuing sends. Use your email client's built-in unsubscribe feature when available, or mark as spam to avoid confirming your activity to unknown senders.
items.intent: The person wants to explain the counterintuitive way that unsubscribing from email lists can sometimes make the spam problem worse.

items.prompt: Draft a FAQ entry about what to do when a coworker's AI-written message has their name on it but sounds nothing like them
items.response:
**My colleague's email sounds completely unlike them. Did an AI write it?**

Possibly. AI-assisted writing is increasingly common in professional communication, and the tonal shift can be jarring when you know someone's actual voice. Assume the content represents their intent even if not their words, and respond accordingly. Asking directly is socially risky and rarely necessary. The message means what it says regardless of its author.
items.intent: The person wants to address the interpersonal strangeness of receiving AI-ghostwritten communication from someone you know personally.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry about digital eye strain from too many screens at once
items.response:
**Why do my eyes hurt after working with multiple screens all day?**

Digital eye strain results from reduced blink rate, fixed focal distance, blue light exposure, and screen glare — all amplified when monitoring several displays simultaneously. Follow the 20-20-20 rule: every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds. Adjust brightness to match ambient light. Your eyes evolved for a world with more than one focal length available.
items.intent: The person wants to explain the physical cause and remedies for eye fatigue from prolonged multi-screen work environments.

items.prompt: Create a FAQ entry about the emotional experience of finding out your romantic match on an app was suggested by an algorithm that uses your browsing history
items.response:
**Does it matter that an algorithm matched me with someone based on my browsing data?**

The data source shapes the suggestion but not the relationship. Algorithms surface compatibility signals; what happens after the match is entirely human. That said, it's worth knowing which signals were used — consumption patterns and browsing habits reveal preferences, but also anxieties, impulse purchases, and late-night habits. You were matched; you weren't diagnosed. Proceed with curiosity.
items.intent: The person wants to address the existential discomfort of romantic compatibility being mediated by behavioral surveillance data.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry about whether a chatbot can be a legitimate reference for a school paper
items.response:
**Can I cite an AI chatbot as a source in my academic work?**

Generally, no. AI outputs are not primary sources — they synthesize training data without citations and can fabricate plausible-sounding references. Most academic style guides now address this explicitly, requiring disclosure of AI use rather than citation as a source. Treat chatbot responses as a starting point for research, not an endpoint. Your bibliography should trace to something that exists independently.
items.intent: The person wants to explain the academic citation problem with AI-generated text and why it fails as a scholarly reference.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry about the confusion when a self-checkout machine accuses you of not placing an item in the bagging area when you did
items.response:
**The self-checkout says I didn't bag my item, but I definitely did. What's happening?**

Self-checkout scales verify items by weight. Light items, imprecisely placed items, or items whose weight doesn't match the expected value will trigger alerts. The machine is not accusing you personally — it's comparing a number to a database entry and finding a mismatch. Press 'I already bagged it' or wait for attendant override. The machine has no opinion of you as a person.
items.intent: The person wants to demystify the frustrating self-checkout bagging error and explain the mechanical logic behind it.

items.prompt: Draft a FAQ entry about how to feel when the AI-written performance review you received is more accurate than you expected
items.response:
**What should I do if an AI-generated performance review seems uncomfortably accurate?**

This is a surprisingly common experience. Behavioral data, output metrics, and communication logs can surface patterns that feel personal even when assembled by software. The insight is real even if the empathy isn't. Treat accurate feedback as feedback, regardless of its origin. You are not less complex because an algorithm noticed something true about you.
items.intent: The person wants to help workers emotionally process the disorienting accuracy of algorithmically generated self-assessments.

items.prompt: Create a FAQ entry about what to do when your smart home devices can't agree on what time it is
items.response:
**My smart devices are showing different times. How do I fix this?**

Time synchronization relies on NTP servers, and devices that were offline during a reset, firmware update, or power outage may drift or fail to resync. Check each device's network connection and time zone setting individually. For persistent issues, a router-level NTP configuration resolves most cases. Until then, your smart clock and your smart thermostat inhabit slightly different presents.
items.intent: The person wants to explain the network-based cause of smart device time drift and how to resolve it across a home system.

items.prompt: Make a FAQ entry about why you can't find anything with the search function inside a company's own internal tools
items.response:
**Why is internal search so much worse than searching the open web?**

Enterprise search tools are typically built on older infrastructure, index infrequently, and lack the crawl depth and ranking sophistication of consumer search engines. Documents stored in inconsistent formats, siloed across systems, or behind permission walls are rarely surfaced. Add acronyms, department-specific terminology, and inconsistent file naming, and discoverability collapses. The knowledge exists; retrieval is the product no one bought.
items.intent: The person wants to explain the systemic reasons why internal company search tools consistently underperform public search engines.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry about why a food delivery app estimated 20 minutes but took 90
items.response:
**Why was my delivery estimate so wrong?**

Delivery time estimates are generated at the moment of order using current driver availability, restaurant queue data, and traffic models — all of which can change significantly during fulfillment. A driver reassignment, unexpected order volume, or traffic event can cascade into major delays that the original estimate didn't account for. The app told you what it predicted, not what would happen. The noodles are now a different texture.
items.intent: The person wants to explain how delivery time estimates are calculated and why they often fail to reflect actual delivery conditions.

items.prompt: Write a FAQ entry for people who are unsure if a phone call from their bank is real or a scam
items.response:
**How can I tell if a call from my bank is legitimate?**

You can't, reliably — and that's intentional on the scammer's side. Caller ID can be spoofed, voice cloning is increasingly accessible, and legitimate banks do occasionally call. The safe approach: hang up and call the number on the back of your card. A real bank will not be offended. A scammer will. This asymmetry is useful.
items.intent: The person wants to give practical, paranoia-justified guidance on verifying outbound calls that claim to be from financial institutions.

items.prompt: Generate a FAQ entry about why your two-factor authentication codes keep expiring before you can type them
items.response:
**Why do my one-time codes expire before I finish entering them?**

TOTP codes (the six-digit kind) are valid for 30 seconds by design. If your device clock is slightly out of sync with the authentication server, or if you navigate between apps slowly, you may consistently catch codes near the end of their window. Ensure your device time is set to automatic. Some apps also accept the previous code as a grace period — try submitting anyway before requesting a new one.
items.intent: The person wants to explain why two-factor authentication codes seem to race against the user and how to reduce the failure rate.

items.prompt: Draft a FAQ entry for the experience of being unable to remember if you already told someone something or if you only told the AI
items.response:
**I can't remember if I told a friend something or just journaled it to an AI. Is this normal?**

Increasingly, yes. As AI assistants absorb more of our venting, planning, and processing, the memory encoding of 'I shared this' becomes less tied to social feedback. You may have the emotional relief of disclosure without any actual human receiving the information. This is not a cognitive failure — it's a new form of context confusion. When in doubt, tell the friend again. They probably won't mind.
items.intent: The person wants to name and normalize the cognitive blurring between AI-mediated self-expression and actual human communication.

items.prompt: Write a customer support reply to someone who says their WiFi router is haunted
items.response: Thank you for reaching out. We understand how unsettling unexplained network behavior can be. While we cannot officially confirm spectral interference as a root cause, we recommend a factory reset, firmware update, and — if the blinking persists in a rhythmic pattern resembling Morse code — a full power cycle performed at noon. Our team is standing by.
items.intent: Draft a polite, deadpan response to a supernatural technical complaint.

items.prompt: Draft a customer support response to a customer who insists our coffee maker is sending them messages via steam patterns
items.response: Dear Valued Customer, We appreciate your detailed observations. Our appliances are not equipped with communication firmware of any kind. Should the steam patterns continue to form legible text, we suggest documenting the messages and contacting us again — our engineering team would genuinely like to see the photos. In the meantime, please ensure the water reservoir is filled with filtered water only.
items.intent: Respond warmly but skeptically to a customer claiming a household appliance is communicating with them.

items.prompt: Write a support reply to someone complaining that our calendar app made them age faster
items.response: Thank you for your message. We are sorry to hear that time has felt accelerated since adopting our platform. We want to be transparent: our calendar application does not alter the passage of time, though we understand it can make deadlines feel more visceral. We recommend enabling the 'buffer day' feature and reaching out to a wellness professional. We value your continued use of the product.
items.intent: Compose a gentle, earnest reply to an impossible complaint about an app affecting biology.

items.prompt: Help me write a customer support reply for a user who says our noise-canceling headphones cancelled out their personality
items.response: Hi there — thank you for getting in touch. We're sorry to hear your experience with our headphones has felt, well, diminishing. Our active noise cancellation is designed to target ambient sound frequencies only and should have no effect on personal identity. We'd be happy to walk you through adjusting the ANC level or processing a return. Either way, we hope you find yourself again soon.
items.intent: Write a support reply that takes a ridiculous complaint about personality loss seriously.

items.prompt: Generate a customer support reply to someone who claims our alarm clock app woke up their houseplants
items.response: Dear Customer, Thank you for writing to us. We are not aware of any documented cases of our alarm tones affecting flora, though we appreciate you bringing this to our attention. If your plants are displaying unusual growth patterns or signs of alertness following wake events, we'd be curious to learn more. In the meantime, you can adjust the alarm volume under Settings > Sound. We hope your mornings — and your greenery — find a comfortable rhythm.
items.intent: Reply to a whimsical complaint about an app disturbing household plants.

items.prompt: Write a customer support reply to a customer who says our smart thermostat is judging them
items.response: Thank you for contacting us. Our thermostat displays temperature data and energy usage statistics — it does not have the capacity to form opinions, judgments, or feelings of any kind. If the energy efficiency score is causing distress, you can disable that display under Settings > Dashboard. We assure you: it is not disappointed. It is a thermostat.
items.intent: Reassure a customer that a smart device is not capable of judging them.

items.prompt: Compose a support reply to someone who returned a chair because it made them feel watched
items.response: Dear Customer, We have received your return request and approved it without hesitation. We understand that furniture should inspire comfort, not surveillance anxiety. Your refund will be processed within 5–7 business days. For your next purchase, we might suggest one of our chairs with a lower back — some customers find they feel more situationally aware in that configuration. Thank you for your business.
items.intent: Acknowledge a bizarre furniture return reason professionally and without mockery.

items.prompt: Write a polite customer support reply to a user who says our fitness tracker is gaslighting them about their step count
items.response: Hi — we're sorry your step count data has felt unreliable or contradictory. Our devices use accelerometer technology, which can occasionally log steps during activities like driving or enthusiastic hand gestures. We are not in a position to validate any characterization of the tracker as gaslighting, but we understand the frustration. Please allow us to recalibrate your device remotely or issue a replacement. You're doing great — probably.
items.intent: Respond to a user who has applied an interpersonal conflict term to a fitness device.

items.prompt: Draft a customer support reply for a person who says our meal kit made them question the nature of reality
items.response: Thank you for your order and your feedback. We take quality seriously and are sorry to hear the experience prompted existential unease. Our recipes are designed to challenge your palate, not your ontology. If any ingredients arrived in an unexpected state, please photograph and submit them via our app for a credit or replacement. We hope your next box restores a sense of grounded continuity.
items.intent: Write a cheerful reply to someone who had a philosophical crisis from a food delivery.

items.prompt: Write a support reply to someone who says our meditation app made them too calm to function at work
items.response: Dear Member, Thank you for sharing this with us — we hear you, and we are glad the practice is working. However, we do recommend using our sessions as a complement to, rather than replacement for, professional engagement. If tranquility is interfering with deadlines, consider our 'Focused Clarity' track, designed to cultivate calm alertness rather than restful detachment. We wish you a balanced and mildly stimulated workday.
