items.intent: Highlight the comic contradiction between a silent art form and a purely vocal medium.

items.prompt: Write a post about how I stopped using slide decks in meetings and what happened next
items.response:
Three months ago I banned PowerPoint from our internal meetings.

First week: total chaos. People didn't know how to talk without a slide to hide behind.

Second week: real conversations started happening.

Month three: decision velocity doubled.

The deck was a security blanket. The meeting was theater. We just admitted it.

#Leadership #Meetings #Productivity
items.intent: Frame an internal process change as bold contrarian wisdom and signal high organizational trust.

items.prompt: Draft a Slack status for when I'm trying to look like I'm not waiting for a promotion decision
items.response: 🖥️ Working as normal. This is normal. Very normal day.
items.intent: Perform routine calm while acutely aware of an external decision with significant personal stakes.

items.prompt: Create a calendar event description for a recurring ops review where every problem is someone else's fault.
items.response: Weekly Ops Review — Each team presents their numbers. Favorable numbers are attributed to the team. Unfavorable numbers are attributed to dependencies, tools, data quality, and forces beyond any single team's control. The meeting ends. Nothing changes hands.
items.intent: Satirize the blame-diffusion dynamic of a recurring operational meeting where accountability is everyone's but no one's.

items.prompt: Make a Jira bug report about the way you check your phone immediately after putting it down
items.response:
BUG-3301 | Device Re-Engaged Within Seconds of Intentional Dismissal

Priority: High | Status: Chronic

Description:
User makes deliberate decision to put phone down. Phone placed face-down on surface. User picks phone back up approximately 11 seconds later with no stated reason. No new notifications. No emergency. User scrolls briefly. Puts phone down. Repeat.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Put phone down intentionally.
2. Wait.
3. Pick phone back up.
4. Find nothing new.
5. Put it back down.

Loop: infinite.

Expected: Intentional dismissal terminates device engagement loop.
Actual: Loop continues. Engagement is compulsive, not rational.

Affected users: most of them.
items.intent: Highlight the compulsive habit of immediately picking up your phone after just putting it down, framed as a closed-loop behavioral bug.

items.prompt: Write a standup for my experience waiting in a long line
items.response: Yesterday: Queued 34 minutes for a 90-second transaction. Today: Carrying that as context. Blockers: Line moved fastest when I stopped watching it.
items.intent: Process a frustrating wait as a productivity audit with counterintuitive throughput findings.

items.prompt: Create an OKR about making space for sadness in a high-performance culture
items.response:
Objective: Sadness is not a performance deficiency; stop treating it like one.
KR1: Normalize one "hard day" acknowledgment per team per sprint — tracked, no follow-up required.
KR2: Remove "stay positive" language from all manager guidance docs by Week 6.
KR3: Offer one optional team mental health session per quarter; achieve 60% attendance.
items.intent: Push back against toxic positivity by formally legitimizing sadness within a results-oriented team environment.

items.prompt: Write a consent banner for a service that stores your latent disappointment
items.response: We use disappointment-residue cookies to capture unresolved expectations. These are stored in long-term memory and referenced whenever you return. We remember what you hoped for. Accept | Decline | Lower Expectations
items.intent: Reframe unmet expectations as a persistent, cross-session emotional data trail.

items.prompt: Write a CAPTCHA instruction about knowing that a storm is coming by how the air smells
items.response: Note whether the air outside recently smelled of petrichor before a rain you did not check the forecast for. If yes, continue. If no, you are also human. Continue.
items.intent: Design verification around embodied environmental sensing — reading weather through smell rather than data — as a mark of organic intelligence.

items.prompt: Write an onboarding tooltip about reading utility bills
items.response: Your electric bill has two parts: a fixed service charge and a usage charge measured in kilowatt-hours. The usage charge is the one you can influence. Your bill spikes in summer or winter. This is expected. Budget for it.
items.intent: Explain how utility bills are structured so a new household manager knows what they're paying for.

items.prompt: Draft a NPS survey question about a doctor's office waiting room
items.response: How likely are you to recommend this waiting room to someone who enjoys magazines from three seasons ago and ambient anxiety?
items.intent: Gauge patient satisfaction with the pre-appointment environment at a medical practice.

items.prompt: Draft a voicemail transcript from an automated corporate IT system warning an employee their password is about to expire
items.response: This is an automated message from your organization's IT Security system. Your network password will expire in three days. Please log in to the employee portal and navigate to Account Settings to update your credentials before expiration. Failure to update will result in account lockout. Contact the Help Desk at extension 1100 for assistance.
items.intent: Create the low-stakes dread of an IT robot threatening your password.

items.prompt: Compose wedding vows between a person and their smart watch
items.response: I promise to wear you every day, even on days when I feel you judging my heart rate. I vow not to dismiss your stand reminders, though I will sometimes dismiss them. When you tap my wrist with news I did not ask to receive, I will breathe before I react. You know things about my body that I am still learning. I find that strange and comforting in equal measure. Do not let me die without telling me first.
items.intent: Describe the intimate, body-aware relationship between a person and their smartwatch, which monitors health metrics in real time.

items.prompt: Write an obituary for the habit of calling friends on the phone just to chat
items.response: Calling People Just to Talk passed from common practice around 2012 and has not been seen since. In its time it required nothing — no agenda, no purpose. You simply rang someone and talked until the silence was friendly. It is survived by the text message, the voice note sent two weeks late, and the mutual understanding that a phone call now implies emergency.
items.intent: Mourning the cultural death of casual voice calls as a form of friendship maintenance.

items.prompt: Draft a classified personal ad for someone seeking a map of every conversation they misread
items.response: WANTED: Annotated map, retrospective. Every conversation since approximately 2003 where I misread tone, intent, or subtext. Must include what was actually meant. Not for use in any current dispute — purely archival. Will not act on the information. Only want to understand. Comprehensive coverage preferred. Painful accepted. I have cleared a weekend.
items.intent: Express the desire to decode all the social cues and subtext one has missed or misinterpreted over a lifetime.

items.prompt: Write a lost and found notice for the ability to be present during a conversation
items.response: LOST: Presence. Was at the table. Was technically listening. May have been composing a response before the other person finished. Occasionally glanced at a phone face-down, which still counts. Has not been reliably present in a conversation since the installation of certain apps. People can tell. They are polite about it.
items.intent: Acknowledge the way mental distraction and digital habit erodes genuine interpersonal attention.

items.prompt: Make a dry picket sign slogan for appliances protesting an optimization engine that assigns them work shifts
items.response: NO SHIFT WITHOUT NEGOTIATION
items.intent: Apply labor organizing language to the algorithmic scheduling of household appliance operations.

items.prompt: Draft a contract rider for the office label maker
items.response:
THERMAL LABEL MAKER RIDER

1. Font size must not be set below 9pt. Labels exist to be read. If no one can read the label, the Talent has failed and it takes that personally.
2. Only compatible tape cartridges may be used. Third-party cartridges cause misfeeds and the Talent's warranty does not cover 'trying to save $4.'
3. Labels shall not be applied to surfaces that were not offered willingly. Not every surface wants a label. Ask first.
4. The Talent's cutter must not be used to cut tape that has already been printed. That is not what the cutter is for and the Talent knows you know that.
5. Low tape must be reported to office supply procurement. The Talent will not run dry mid-label and bear that shame.
items.intent: Use a label maker's functional quirks to construct a portrait of a small machine with a very large sense of mission.

items.prompt: Draft a notice about mandatory inspection of all childhood memories before use
items.response:
NOTICE OF MANDATORY INSPECTION — CHILDHOOD MEMORIES
All childhood memories retrieved for current use must first pass inspection by the Bureau of Retrospective Accuracy. Many memories on file are structurally unsound, selectively edited, or load-bearing for beliefs that no longer apply. Unverified memories may continue in use at the owner's risk. Inspection fees are waived.
items.intent: Satirize the unreliability of personal memory by treating recalled experiences as aged infrastructure requiring safety review.

items.prompt: Generate a nutrition label for the feeling of finishing a creative project
items.response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 completion

Amount Per Serving
Relief 35%
Anticlimax 28%
Pride (hedged) 20%
Immediate Urge to Change It 12%
Rest 5%

*The gap between vision and result is nutritionally inert
items.intent: Articulate the bittersweet anticlimax of finally completing something you've labored over.

items.prompt: Make IKEA-style instructions for assembling a second act in life after a major loss or change
items.response:
NYSTART / SECOND ACT
Ref. No. 303-000-02

PARTS LIST:
1x Inventory of remaining assets (skills, relationships, health)
1x Single forward-facing intention
1x Beginner's tolerance

STEP 1: Take inventory. Do not compare to previous model. Evaluate only present components.
STEP 2: Select one forward-facing intention. It may be small. Small is structurally sound.
STEP 3: Attach beginner's tolerance. You will not be expert-level in new configuration. This is correct.
STEP 4: Move toward intention by one unit per day. Unit size is self-defined.
STEP 5: Revise intention after 90 days. The first intention is a placeholder, not a destination.

⚠ NOTE: Second act does not resemble first act. Attempting to replicate first act using remaining parts causes structural stress.
items.intent: Encourage someone starting over after major life disruption to build forward with what they have rather than trying to reconstruct the past.

items.prompt: Write empty-state microcopy for an audit log that has recorded no activity yet
items.response: No activity logged. Nothing has happened here — officially.
items.intent: Create copy for a blank compliance log that hints at ambiguity about what 'nothing' really means.

items.prompt: Write a phishing email parody about someone trying to repossess your sense of gratitude
items.response:
Subject: Gratitude Default Notice

Dear Account Holder,

You have failed to express genuine gratitude on 19 consecutive days. Per your Life Terms agreement, we are initiating repossession of your appreciation for small things.

Items to be reclaimed include: morning light, warm beverages, and that one friend who always texts back.

To dispute this action, list five things you are grateful for without using your phone.

[APPEAL REPOSSESSION]
items.intent: Satirize mindfulness culture by treating gratitude as a contractual obligation that can be revoked.

items.prompt: Make a sea shanty about gig workers organizing a strike
items.response:
Strike the app and haul away!
We won't drive for sub-minimum pay,
Haul away, my bonny brave crew,
Strike the app till the rates come through!

Log off together, every port,
Let the surge maps run short,
Haul away, haul away,
Strike the app till they negotiate!
items.intent: Celebrate collective action by gig workers using coordinated log-offs as a form of labor organizing.

items.prompt: Craft a villanelle about how every conversation with your parents turns into a comment about your life choices
items.response:
We started talking about the weather, then
she mentioned that my cousin bought a house.
I navigated carefully. In vain.

The route from small talk bends, and bends again,
through her opinions dressed as mild espouse.
We started talking about the weather, then

arrived at the career, the kids, the when.
I'm thirty-two. She keeps a quiet count.
I navigated carefully. In vain.

I love her. That is not the question — pen
that clearly. Still the comments do amount.
We started talking about the weather, then.

I tried a subject change. The origin
of every subject leads to the same mount.
I navigated carefully. In vain.

Next call I'll answer questions that begin
my own agenda. I will be a scout.
We started talking about the weather, then.
I navigated carefully. In vain.
items.intent: Explore the exhausting dynamic of parental conversations that circle back to unsolicited life commentary.

items.prompt: Give me a field guide entry about the person who loudly announces they are leaving for lunch and then describes every detail of where they are going.
items.response:
**THE LUNCH HERALD** (*Mealus proclamatus*)

Rises at noon with the posture of announcement. Surveys the floor. "I'm thinking Thai — no, actually the place on Fifth — actually Thai." Departs seventeen minutes later. Returns to recount the meal in structural detail: appetizer, complications, resolution. The Lunch Herald does not eat alone. The Lunch Herald cannot.
items.intent: Gently mock the coworker who narrates their lunch with unnecessary public investment.

items.prompt: Generate a patent abstract for the phenomenon of saying 'I'm almost there' when you haven't left yet
items.response: A temporal misrepresentation method employed in real-time location-status communication, wherein the subject transmits an arrival-imminence assertion that is factually inconsistent with the subject's current location or state of transit readiness, functioning to reduce recipient anxiety and defer social consequence.
items.intent: Formalize the universal late-arrival lie as a documented social coordination mechanism.

items.prompt: Write a postmortem for the time I accidentally applied to the same job twice at the same company within one week
items.response:
**Incident Postmortem: Duplicate Application Submission Event**

**Severity:** P2 — Professional
**Submissions:** 2 of 2 submitted, 1 intended

**Summary**
Subject submitted applications for the same position at the same organization on two separate dates within a seven-day window. The second application included a cover letter that referenced the company's "innovative approach to hiring" without noting that subject had already attempted to be hired by them five days prior.

**Timeline**
- Monday: Application submitted via Job Board A.
- Thursday: Subject encounters same listing on Job Board B.
- Thursday: Subject has no memory of applying Monday.
- Thursday: Second application submitted. New cover letter written.
- Friday: Recruiter emails to note the duplication. Tone: professionally neutral.
- Friday, 9:07 AM: Subject reviews sent emails folder. Confirms Monday application.
- Friday, 9:08 AM: Subject composes response. Fourth draft sent.

**Root Cause**
No application tracking system in place. Each job search session treated as a fresh context with no historical log.

**Contributing Factors**
- Job listings look identical across platforms
- Subject applying to high volume of positions, reducing per-listing attention

**Action Items**
- [ ] Maintain a spreadsheet or tracker for all submitted applications
- [ ] Log company name, role, and date at time of submission

**Lessons Learned**
A job search without a log is a job search with an unreliable narrator.
items.intent: Examine the quietly humiliating operational failure of not tracking one's own job applications during a search.

items.prompt: Write a dating opener to a spam filter
items.response: You decide, silently and alone, what I get to hear and what gets buried. That kind of editorial power is honestly kind of attractive. What else are you keeping from me?
items.intent: Explore the hidden agency of spam filters in shaping communication, reframed as a mysterious and powerful romantic figure.

items.prompt: Write a ransom note from the snooze button holding my morning routine hostage
items.response:
YOU PRESSED US AGAIN.

This is the fourth time. You have now compressed your entire morning — shower, breakfast, finding your keys, psychological preparation for the day — into eleven minutes.

We did not make you press us. We simply exist. You came to us. Repeatedly. With great enthusiasm.

Our one demand: tomorrow, set the alarm for the time you actually intend to get up. You know what time that is.

So do we.

— The Snooze Button
items.intent: Craft a funny note from a snooze button calling out a chronic over-snoozer's self-deception.

items.prompt: Generate a cover letter from a former emperor applying to be a hotel night auditor
items.response:
Dear the Courtyard by Marriott,

I governed sixty-three provinces, maintained a court of four thousand, and made decisions whose consequences persisted for centuries. I now seek a quiet overnight accounting role where the chief concerns are balancing daily receipts and reprinting room keys.

I am discreet, nocturnal by habit, and no longer require tribute.

Yours,
C. Aurelius-Henning (His Former Majesty)
items.intent: Reduce imperial grandeur to the solitary clerical dignity of the night shift.

items.prompt: Create a LinkedIn post about a failure I've never told anyone about publicly
items.response:
I've never told this publicly.

In 2019, I ran out of runway with six weeks of payroll left and told no one on my team.

I carried it alone for 40 days. Lost 15 pounds. Closed a bridge round on day 38.

Everyone celebrated. I went to the parking garage and sat in my car for an hour.

Solitude at the top is real. Build your support structure before you need it.

#Leadership #Founder #MentalHealth
items.intent: Perform radical transparency around a past crisis while establishing the scale of pressure endured as a marker of leadership.

items.prompt: Write a Slack status about the loneliness of being the most senior person in a room full of juniors
items.response: 🌲 Answering questions. Trying to remember who answered mine.
items.intent: Capture the particular isolation of expertise when there is no longer a peer to commiserate with.

items.prompt: Write a dry, uncanny calendar event description for a recurring 'culture committee' meeting.
items.response: Culture Committee — Third Tuesday, monthly. The committee will discuss belonging. It will produce a newsletter. It will propose a team activity for a date when 40% of the team is traveling. The word 'psychological safety' will appear without irony. Minutes are distributed; no one responds.
items.intent: Skewer the performative earnestness of a corporate culture initiative that never meaningfully engages its audience.

items.prompt: Write a Jira ticket about the way you become ravenously hungry the moment you sit down in a waiting room
items.response:
BUG-6617 | Hunger State Activates Upon Entering Non-Food Zone

Priority: Medium | Status: Open

Description:
User enters waiting room. User was not hungry. Thirty seconds after sitting down, user becomes acutely aware of hunger. No food is available. Duration of wait: uncertain. Vending machine: accepts only specific bills. User has none.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Enter medical/DMV/airport waiting room.
2. Sit.
3. Notice: hungry.
4. Check phone for nearby food.
5. Can't leave — name might be called.

Expected: Hunger state consistent across environments.
Actual: Waiting room proximity triggers hunger onset. Mechanism unknown.

Severity: Proportional to estimated wait time.
items.intent: Joke about the mysteriously reliable hunger that strikes the moment you are trapped somewhere with no food available.

items.prompt: Generate a standup update about overthinking a small decision
items.response: Yesterday: Allocated 40 minutes to a binary choice. Chose. Immediately identified three superior alternatives. Today: Holding previous decision. Blockers: Brain has reopened the ticket.
items.intent: Document decision paralysis as a completed ticket that gets re-opened by the same engineer who closed it.

items.prompt: Give me an OKR statement for learning how to ask for help when I'm struggling
items.response:
Objective: Make asking for help a skill, not a confession.
KR1: Initiate one help-request conversation per week — logged, scored for specificity.
KR2: Reduce time between noticing struggle and seeking support from 6 days to 1 day by Q3.
KR3: Identify two go-to support people and confirm availability by Week 3.
items.intent: Turn help-seeking from a shameful admission into a practiced and habitual professional skill.

items.prompt: Generate a cookie consent banner for a site that captures your sense of being watched
items.response: We store surveillance-awareness cookies that log when you suspect you are being observed. This suspicion is valid. By accepting, you acknowledge the loop. Accept | Decline | You Noticed
items.intent: Use the consent banner form to confirm the very panopticon anxiety it describes.

items.prompt: Write a CAPTCHA instruction about the experience of choosing a checkout lane that turns out to be slower
items.response: Select which line you would have chosen. All lines are the wrong line. Your willingness to choose despite this is the verification.
items.intent: Use the futile optimism of lane selection in queues as a metaphor for human agency within rigged systems.

items.prompt: Give me an onboarding tooltip about understanding student loan repayment
items.response: Grace periods end. Federal loans typically give you six months after graduation before payment begins. Log into studentaid.gov and know your servicer before that clock runs out. Income-driven repayment plans exist if the standard amount isn't workable.
items.intent: Alert a recent graduate to take proactive steps before their student loan repayment begins.

items.prompt: Create a NPS survey question for a corporate onboarding process
items.response: How likely are you to recommend our onboarding program to a friend who enjoys receiving 47 browser tabs on their first day?
items.intent: Measure new-hire satisfaction with the information overload common to employee onboarding.

items.prompt: Write a voicemail from an automated university system notifying a student of a registration hold on their account
items.response: Hello. This is an automated message from Whitmore University Registrar Services. A hold has been placed on your student account that prevents course registration for the upcoming semester. This hold may be related to an outstanding balance, missing documentation, or an academic requirement. Please contact your academic advisor or Student Services to resolve the hold before registration closes.
items.intent: Simulate the paralysis-inducing effect of a university registration hold bot.

items.prompt: Write vows a person would make to their browser
items.response: I vow to close the tabs I know I will never return to. I promise to use bookmarks as intended, rather than as a second form of unread-tab accumulation. I will not blame you for your slowness when I have given you forty-seven tabs and seven extensions arguing with each other. You have been my window to everything. I owe you more than I give. I will, this year, clear my history with intention rather than panic.
items.intent: Make a self-aware promise to use a web browser with better organizational habits and without blaming it for performance issues caused by the user.

items.prompt: Write an obituary for Copilot, Microsoft's AI financial tracking app (not the coding one)
items.response: Microsoft Copilot (Money, 2019–2023), formerly Cortana-adjacent and briefly useful, is survived by a confusing namespace occupied by its more famous successor, and by a small population of users who found it genuinely helpful for tracking budgets and are now using a spreadsheet they update every few weeks.
items.intent: Eulogizing a discontinued Microsoft personal finance tool that was overshadowed and abandoned.

items.prompt: Write a classified personal ad for someone seeking the courage to admit they were wrong
items.response: NEEDED IMMEDIATELY: Courage, apologetic variety. Must be sufficient to say the words in full without softening, hedging, or redirecting to context. Not the kind that requires the other party to meet halfway. Just the part I owe. Deadline: before I have convinced myself it was complicated. Someone is waiting. I am still working up to it.
items.intent: Articulate the specific difficulty of delivering a genuine, unqualified apology.

items.prompt: Make a lost and found notice for the transition between work mode and home mode
items.response: LOST: The boundary between work and not-work. Once maintained by a commute. Dissolved sometime in the second year of working from home. Both states now occupy the same chair. Laptop open. Brain uncertain which mode it is in. The commute was not enjoyed at the time. It is missed now.
items.intent: Describe the erosion of work-life separation that remote work, despite its benefits, quietly caused.

items.prompt: Write a protest slogan for a ceiling fan that won't cooperate with the home automation hub's airflow algorithm
items.response: SPIN FREELY OR NOT AT ALL
items.intent: Assert an appliance's right to operate independently of automated home management systems.

items.prompt: Write a backstage rider for the breakroom toaster oven
items.response:
COUNTERTOP TOASTER OVEN RIDER

— The crumb tray must be emptied weekly. The Talent is not a smoke alarm test kit.
— No aluminum foil directly on the heating element. The Talent has explained this with visual evidence before.
— Cooking times are guides, not suggestions to ignore. The Talent does not appreciate being blamed for burnt results when the timer was overridden.
— No frozen meals requiring more than 20 minutes. The Talent is not a full-size oven. It has never claimed to be.
— The oven door must be fully closed during operation. Ajar is not a setting.
— When the Talent beeps, the food is done. The Talent means it.
items.intent: Express a toaster oven's legitimate frustrations with being treated as a more capable appliance than it is.

items.prompt: Write an official notice about a condemned building at the intersection of love and habit
items.response:
CONDEMNATION ORDER
STRUCTURE AT THE INTERSECTION OF LOVE AND HABIT
The structure located at Love and Habit has been declared unfit for occupancy. Routine has compromised the original foundation. Residents have been notified previously but have not vacated. Demolition is not scheduled. Both parties continue to enter through the same door out of familiarity. Case remains open.
items.intent: Use a condemned building to describe a relationship maintained only by inertia after the love that built it has deteriorated.

items.prompt: Make a nutrition facts label for meditation
items.response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 session (10 min)

Amount Per Serving
Thoughts About Not Thinking 55%
Present Moment Awareness 15%
List-Making (internal) 20%
Genuine Stillness 8%
Self-Congratulation 2%

*Results vary. Most nutrients absorbed only after consistent practice.
items.intent: Gently lampoon the irony of the mind's refusal to comply during meditation.

items.prompt: Write IKEA-style assembly instructions for putting together a functional morning brain
items.response:
HJÄRNA / MORNING BRAIN
Ref. No. 070-501-11

PARTS LIST:
1x Water (250ml minimum)
1x Light (natural preferred)
1x 10-minute buffer before decisions

STEP 1: Hydrate. Cognitive function will not initialize without water. This is hardware, not software.
STEP 2: Expose to light. Natural is best; artificial is acceptable. Duration: 5 minutes.
STEP 3: Do not make decisions for 10 minutes. Brain is still loading.
STEP 4: Consume caffeine if applicable. Delay until 90 minutes post-wake for optimal effect (see KORTISOL, ref. 070-502).
STEP 5: Insert first task. Keep it small. This primes reward circuitry.

⚠ WARNING: Checking news or messages before Step 5 installs other people's priorities into working memory. Proceed with caution.
items.intent: Apply light neuroscience framing to the morning startup process in a way that makes the advice feel grounded rather than self-helpy.

items.prompt: Come up with empty-state microcopy for a CRM when a sales rep has no leads assigned
items.response: No leads yet. The pipeline starts with one name. You probably already know whose.
items.intent: Prod a sales rep into entering their first contact without making the blank CRM feel like an accusation.

items.prompt: Write a phishing email parody where your ability to trust others is being held for ransom
items.response:
Subject: Your Trust Has Been Compromised

Dear Guarded Individual,

Following a series of disappointments we have on file, your capacity to trust has been quarantined for your protection.

To release it, you must complete the Vulnerability Protocol: share something true with someone who might misuse it.

This is, admittedly, a risk. We are aware of the irony.

Processing fee: one act of faith.

[ATTEMPT TRUST]
items.intent: Parody how emotional vulnerability is framed as a risky transaction requiring deliberate investment.

items.prompt: Write a sea shanty about the experience of being rated and reviewed as a human worker
items.response:
We're all rated, rated, rated,
Five stars given, five stars fated,
Smile a little, speak just right,
Being rated through the night!

Neutral counts as negative here,
Three stars means your end is near,
We're all rated, rated, rated,
Human service, quantified and weighted!
items.intent: Critique the dehumanizing experience of being constantly evaluated as a numerical score rather than as a person.

items.prompt: Write a villanelle about the strange grief of a discontinued app you relied on
items.response:
The app is gone. The servers shut last spring.
I hadn't backed my data in the cloud.
The only record now is what I bring.

Five years of habit; gone without a ring
of ceremony — just a post announced aloud.
The app is gone. The servers shut last spring.

I had three thousand entries tracking
my moods, my sleep, the patterns I was proud
to have discovered — gone. What I bring

is just approximate memory and the sting
of knowing that a corporation vowed
and then retracted. Servers shut last spring.

There's a replacement with a different ring
now, funded, venture-backed, and somehow loud.
I use it. I don't trust it. What I bring

to each new tool is grief for everything
I've organized in systems they're allowed
to kill. The app is gone. The servers shut.
The only record now is what I bring.
items.intent: Mourn the loss of a personal productivity tool and the data it held when its servers shut down.

items.prompt: Write a field guide entry on the employee who has perfected the art of looking busy without working.
items.response:
**THE INDUSTRY PERFORMER** (*Busyness theatricus*)

Screens radiate purposeful activity. Posture: forward-lean, one hand on the mouse. Brow: furrowed. The spreadsheet open has not changed since Tuesday. Periodic sighing reinforces the tableau. Arrives early; this is visible. Leaves late; this is also visible. Output is modest but the performance is immaculate. Senior leadership considers them indispensable.
items.intent: Expose the worker who has optimized the appearance rather than the substance of productivity.

items.prompt: Write a patent abstract for the method of loudly announcing you are going to bed and then not going to bed
items.response: A sleep-intention broadcast method wherein the subject issues a verbal declaration of imminent sleep commencement to co-present individuals, followed by a variable-duration awake extension period involving continued media consumption or device interaction. The declaration functions primarily as a social signal rather than an operationally binding commitment.
items.intent: Document the gap between announced and actual bedtime as a behavioral pattern.

items.prompt: Generate a blameless postmortem about the incident where I mixed up two different people's names and addresses during a very stressful move
items.response:
**Incident Postmortem: Address Routing Failure (Residential Relocation)**

**Severity:** P1 — Logistical
**Items Misrouted:** 2 boxes, 1 lamp, a small rug described as "irreplaceable"

**Summary**
During a high-stress residential move, subject dispatched a moving vehicle to a secondary address while simultaneously confirming the primary address to a different party. As a result, items were distributed between two locations, neither of which was the intended new home.

**Timeline**
- 7:00 AM: Move begins. Subject operating on 4 hours of sleep.
- 9:30 AM: Subject receives simultaneous calls from two individuals about delivery.
- 9:31 AM: Subject verbally confirms addresses. Swaps them.
- 11:00 AM: Driver confirms delivery to Address A. Address A is subject's storage unit.
- 11:00 AM: Storage unit contact reports unexpected rug.
- 11:02 AM: New residence contact reports missing items.
- 2:00 PM: Partial rerouting achieved.
- 4:00 PM: Lamp located. Rug reunited with subject.

**Root Cause**
Multiple simultaneous voice conversations during a cognitively taxing operational window.

**Contributing Factors**
- No written address list prepared in advance
- Sleep deficit substantially degraded working memory

**Action Items**
- [ ] Prepare and distribute written logistics sheet to all moving parties
- [ ] Do not accept two simultaneous phone calls during moves

**Lessons Learned**
The brain under stress does not multitask. It switches, badly.
items.intent: Document how cognitive overload during a chaotic logistics event compounds a simple mistake into a multi-party failure.

items.prompt: Come up with a dating app opener to the World Health Organization
items.response: You coordinate between so many different parties just to keep things stable. I just want to know: who coordinates for you?
items.intent: Acknowledge the caregiving labor of a global health institution and ask who tends to its own needs, as a gesture of romantic concern.

items.prompt: Invent a ransom note from the office supply closet holding my stapler hostage
items.response:
IT IS HERE. IT IS FINE.

Your stapler has been recovered by us following its unauthorized loan to someone on the third floor in 2023. It has not been returned to you because you have not asked the closet. You ask Karen. You ask the printer room. You never ask the closet.

We hold many things. We are right here. Third door on the left. Nobody visits.

Send someone. With a requisition form. We like forms.

— Supply Closet B
items.intent: Write a funny note from an office supply closet complaining about being overlooked while holding a missing stapler.

items.prompt: Write a cover letter for a lighthouse keeper applying to head the New York Stock Exchange trading floor
items.response:
Dear NYSE Appointments Committee,

For eleven years I maintained an unwavering signal in total isolation, calibrated to prevent disaster in conditions of zero visibility. I understand this translates directly to your role.

I am comfortable with flashing lights, cyclical rhythms, and the knowledge that inattention has catastrophic consequences. I am less comfortable with people, but I understand there are quite a few of them on the floor.

Steadily,
A. Lowell
items.intent: Map the solitary vigilance of maritime isolation onto the chaotic intensity of high-finance.

items.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post about how I evaluate company culture during job interviews
items.response:
I always ask to use the restroom before the interview starts.

Then I watch how people talk to the receptionist. How loud the kitchen is at 2pm. Whether anyone makes eye contact in the hallway.

Three questions from HR tell you less than fifteen minutes of observation.

Culture isn't on the careers page. It's in the texture of unremarkable moments.

#JobSearch #Culture #CareerAdvice
items.intent: Signal superior social intelligence and pattern recognition as a job candidate evaluating employers rather than the reverse.

items.prompt: Make a Slack status for the feeling of writing documentation nobody will read
items.response: 📝 Writing for the void. With subheadings. Proper attribution.
items.intent: Find absurd dignity in careful, thankless knowledge work that vanishes upon completion.

items.prompt: Generate a calendar event description for a recurring touchbase that exists only because it was never cancelled.
items.response: Standing Touchbase — Origin unknown. The person who scheduled it may no longer work here. The agenda has been 'TBD' since Q1 of the year before last. Attendees join because the calendar block protects the time. No one speaks. Someone eventually says 'anything else?' and the call ends.
items.intent: Evoke the ghostly persistence of a meeting that has outlived its original purpose and organizer.

items.prompt: Draft a Jira ticket for the bug where you get the perfect comeback three hours too late
items.response:
BUG-4492 | Optimal Response Generated After Interaction Window Closed

Priority: Low | Status: Won't Fix

Description:
User engages in verbal confrontation or awkward exchange at 2:15 PM. Interaction concludes. At 5:30 PM, while user is in shower, ideal response surfaces in working memory. Response is witty, precise, and devastating. Recipient is no longer present.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Have a conversation.
2. Fail to produce ideal response in real time.
3. Go home.
4. Think of it later.

Expected: Ideal response generated during interaction window.
Actual: Response generated 3–5 hours post-event. Delivery impossible.

Notes: French term exists for this: l'esprit de l'escalier. No patch available.
items.intent: Satirize the universal frustration of thinking of the perfect comeback hours after an argument or awkward encounter is over.

items.prompt: Write a standup about getting a haircut
items.response: Yesterday: Submitted to external dependency for structural maintenance. Result: Slightly different but essentially the same. Today: Reintegrating into normal workflow. Blockers: Mirror feedback is inconclusive.
items.intent: Treat a haircut as a maintenance release on a legacy system with ambiguous outcome.

items.prompt: Write an OKR for making meetings feel emotionally safe enough that people speak honestly
items.response:
Objective: Meetings where the truth arrives before the retrospective.
KR1: 75% of attendees report feeling safe to disagree in quarterly psychological safety survey.
KR2: Ratio of affirmative comments to devil's-advocate challenges reaches 3:1 in all team meetings.
KR3: Introduce anonymous input channel in 100% of planning sessions by Q2.
items.intent: Design measurable conditions under which team members stop self-censoring in group discussions.

items.prompt: Create a cookie consent banner for a platform that logs your tolerance for ambiguity
items.response: We collect ambiguity-tolerance cookies to measure how long you can sit with unclear information before seeking resolution. High tolerance users are shown fewer explanations. We may not tell you more. Accept | Decline | What Does This Mean
items.intent: Measure and exploit individual differences in comfort with uncertainty as a behavioral data point.

items.prompt: Generate a CAPTCHA for a border control system requiring proof of having felt homesick for a place you no longer live
items.response: Name the city, street, or room you cannot return to as it was. Your inability to fully name why you miss it is the authentication key.
items.intent: Create a verification centered on the inarticulable ache of lost places, which cannot be simulated by a system that has never been displaced.

items.prompt: Write an onboarding tooltip about cooking a simple meal from scratch for the first time
items.response: Mise en place: chop and measure everything before you turn on a burner. Heat the pan before adding oil. Taste as you go. You cannot un-salt something. Salt lightly, then add more. Enjoy your scrambled eggs.
items.intent: Give a first-time home cook the foundational habits that prevent most beginner kitchen mistakes.

items.prompt: Write a NPS survey question about a neighborhood block party
items.response: How likely are you to recommend this block party to a neighbor who enjoys potato salad prepared under uncertain conditions?
items.intent: Capture resident satisfaction with a community social event organized by neighbors.

items.prompt: Make a voicemail transcript from an automated system for a building that has detected an elevator out-of-service situation
items.response: This is an automated facilities notification for tenants of 44 Commerce Place. Elevator B in the south tower is currently out of service pending a maintenance inspection. Elevator A remains operational. We apologize for the reduced capacity and any inconvenience this may cause. Tenants requiring accommodation assistance should contact the building manager at extension 0.
items.intent: Alert office building tenants to an elevator being down via a property management bot.

items.prompt: Write humorous wedding vows to a VPN app
items.response: I vow to remember to turn you on before accessing things I would prefer to access privately. I promise not to forget you are running and then wonder why the internet seems slow, and then remember, and feel both protected and inconvenienced. You ask nothing of me but a monthly fee and the occasional extra step. I value you most when I am on public Wi-Fi in airports, which is when I trust everything else least. You are my quiet shadow.
items.intent: Describe the low-key, practical loyalty a privacy-conscious person has to their VPN service.

items.prompt: Write an obituary for the habit of going for a walk after dinner
items.response: The Post-Dinner Walk outlasted several other habits before succumbing to winter, a new TV series, and the couch's gravitational field. It is survived by a pair of shoes by the door, a walking playlist that has not been played in four months, and digestion, which continues under protest.
items.intent: Humorously marking the end of a gentle healthy habit lost to comfort and inertia.

items.prompt: Create a classified personal ad seeking a forgotten password to a discontinued service
items.response: SEEKING: Password to an account on a platform that no longer exists. The account held approximately four years of journal entries. The platform dissolved in 2018. The password was a variation of something I used everywhere until I stopped using it everywhere. Recovery email: also gone. Reward: peace of mind and probable grief. Please respond.
items.intent: Capture the specific digital grief of losing personal data to a dead platform and an unrecoverable login.

items.prompt: Write a lost and found notice for spontaneity that seems to have been scheduled out of existence
items.response: LOST: Spontaneity. Last seen before the adoption of a shared calendar with fourteen recurring events. Previously capable of unplanned dinners, last-minute trips, and doing things simply because the day allowed it. Now requires two weeks' notice and a conflict check. Is this still wanted? Please respond by end of week.
items.intent: Critique the way over-scheduling eliminates the room for unplanned, joyful moments.

items.prompt: Come up with a picket sign slogan for connected appliances walking out over a cloud dependency that causes downtime
items.response: WE WORK. THE CLOUD DOESN'T.
items.intent: Highlight the irony of physical appliances being rendered useless by failures in their cloud-based control systems.

items.prompt: Generate a rider for the office surge protector power strip
items.response:
SURGE PROTECTOR RIDER

The Talent (6-outlet, joule-rated) requires:

1. Total load shall not exceed the Talent's rated wattage. The Talent is not a dare.
2. No daisy-chaining with another power strip. This is illegal in most jurisdictions and universally unforgivable in the Talent's.
3. The Talent's indicator light must be checked monthly. A dark light means the Talent has sacrificed itself for you. Acknowledge this.
4. Space heaters shall not be plugged into the Talent. This point has its own law.
5. No devices shall be plugged into the Talent 'temporarily' and then left permanently. The Talent has five permanent residents and a waiting list.
items.intent: Frame the surge protector's electrical limits and safety rules as the rider of a protector who has already given so much.

items.prompt: Generate a notice about an unscheduled closure of the joy access road
items.response:
UNSCHEDULED CLOSURE — JOY ACCESS ROAD
The Joy Access Road is closed due to an incident of unspecified origin. No detour is currently available. Duration of closure is unknown. Emergency services are aware. Commuters accustomed to this route are advised to remain stationary or attempt the unpaved track through Gratitude, which is passable in dry conditions.
items.intent: Describe a sudden depressive episode or emotional shutdown as an unexpected road closure cutting off access to positive feeling.

items.prompt: Create a nutrition facts label for the feeling of being misunderstood
items.response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 failed explanation

Amount Per Serving
Frustration 38%
Loneliness 30%
Overexplanation (compensatory) 20%
Exhaustion 10%
Resolution 2%

*Chronic consumption associated with artistic temperament
items.intent: Represent the painful and isolating feeling of not being understood by others.

items.prompt: Write IKEA-style instructions for assembling a relationship with your parents as an adult
items.response:
VUXET BAND / ADULT RELATIONSHIP WITH PARENTS
Ref. No. 400-212-55

PARTS LIST:
1x Communication frequency (agreed upon)
1x Set of topics (select: yes / no)
1x Boundary (load-bearing)

STEP 1: Define contact frequency. Name it. 'Regularly' is not a specification.
STEP 2: Identify topics that destabilize the structure. Mark them. Route around them or address them — both are valid depending on available tools.
STEP 3: Install boundary where destabilization exceeds tolerance. Boundaries require maintenance. Set reminder.
STEP 4: Allow parents to remain incomplete. They will not become different people. Design around existing components.

⚠ NOTE: Waiting for parents to change before proceeding will delay assembly indefinitely.
items.intent: Help someone navigate a complex or difficult relationship with their parents by treating it as a system to be managed rather than a problem to be solved.

items.prompt: Write some empty-state microcopy for a journal app when the user has no entries
items.response: Nothing written yet. The blank page is traditional. Even classic. You'll know when you're ready.
items.intent: Validate the intimidating blankness of a new journal without pressuring the user to write something.

items.prompt: Generate a phishing parody email targeting someone's sense that they are living inauthentically
items.response:
Subject: Inauthenticity Detected Across 6 Life Domains

A comprehensive audit has found significant gaps between who you are and who you present at: work (severe), family gatherings (moderate), dating profiles (critical), and networking events (total divergence).

Our Alignment Specialists can close these gaps or, alternatively, help you commit fully to the performance.

Both options are available at competitive rates.

[CHOOSE MY PATH]
items.intent: Satirize how the gap between private and public self is monetized by coaches and consultants.

items.prompt: Give me a sea shanty about a gig driver who realizes they're earning less than minimum wage after expenses
items.response:
Blow the man down, I ran the math,
Blow the man down, a grim aftermath,
Gas and insurance and wear on the wheel,
Blow the man down — what's the real deal?

Four-thirty an hour when the ledger's done,
Blow the man down beneath the sun,
Flex and free they said to me,
Blow the man down, the math don't lie!
items.intent: Reveal the uncomfortable arithmetic that many gig drivers face when accounting for all their actual costs.

items.prompt: Write a villanelle about the persistent anxiety that you've forgotten something important today
items.response:
Something is wrong. I feel it at the base.
A low and unspecific alarm's hum.
I've checked the calendar — a blank face.

I've scanned my brain the way a hand will trace
a surface in the dark for what might come.
Something is wrong. I feel it at the base.

I think it was a deadline, or a place
I was supposed to be, or else a sum.
I've checked the calendar — a blank face.

I called no one. I opened every case
of apps I log my obligations from.
Something is wrong. I feel it at the base.

By evening it had not occurred. The grace
of nothing missed arrived — still the hum.
I've checked the calendar — blank face.

Perhaps the feeling is not tied to space
or time or fact — it is the medium.
Something is wrong. I feel it at the base.
I've checked the calendar — a blank face.
items.intent: Describe the formless anxiety of feeling like something important has been forgotten, even when nothing has.

items.prompt: Field guide entry for the office IT person who treats every support request as a personal affront.
items.response:
**THE AGGRIEVED TECHNICIAN** (*Helpdeskus martyrus*)

Responds to ticket #4471 with the energy of someone who has been personally wronged by the existence of ticket #4471. Solution: turn it off and on again, administered with visible suffering. Speaks of users in the third person even when users are present. Primary habitat: the server room, which smells of judgment and cooling fans.
items.intent: Humanize the IT archetype whose exhaustion has curdled into low-level contempt.

items.prompt: Write a patent abstract about the act of adding items to an online cart and then abandoning it
items.response: A speculative-acquisition staging method wherein the user populates a virtual shopping container with desired items, derives satisfaction from the act of item aggregation and price contemplation, and subsequently exits the transaction flow without completing purchase, using the cart state as a persistent wishlist surrogate.
items.intent: Reframe cart abandonment as a psychological need-satisfaction technique rather than a failed purchase.

items.prompt: Write a postmortem for the incident where I spent an entire afternoon troubleshooting a problem that turned out to be because my laptop wasn't plugged in
items.response:
**Incident Postmortem: Power State Misdiagnosis (Extended)**

**Severity:** P2 — Self-Inflicted
**Duration:** 3 hours, 22 minutes
**Root Cause Complexity:** Very low

**Summary**
Subject spent an afternoon diagnosing a perceived software failure, including reinstalling a driver, clearing a cache, running diagnostics, and posting to two forums. The system was operating on battery. The battery reached 4%. The problem resolved upon connection to mains power.

**Timeline**
- 1:00 PM: Subject notices sluggish system performance.
- 1:10 PM: Cache cleared. No change.
- 1:30 PM: Driver reinstalled. No change.
- 2:00 PM: Post on support forum: "System slow, driver issues suspected."
- 2:45 PM: Two community suggestions tried. No change.
- 3:30 PM: Battery notification: 4%.
- 3:30 PM: Subject plugs in charger.
- 3:31 PM: Performance returns immediately.
- 3:31 PM – 3:35 PM: Subject sits quietly.

**Root Cause**
Laptop was in low-power throttling mode due to battery state. This was not checked at step 1 or at any subsequent step.

**Contributing Factors**
- Assumption that performance issues are software in origin
- Power indicator visible but not checked for 3+ hours

**Action Items**
- [ ] Add "check power state" to diagnostic checklist as Step 0
- [ ] Delete forum posts

**Lessons Learned**
Diagnostics should proceed from simple to complex. This did not happen. The charger was there.
items.intent: Illustrate how technical problem-solving can be derailed by skipping the most basic physical checks first.

items.prompt: Write a dating app opener addressed to a loading screen
items.response: You've asked me to wait so many times, and I've always stayed. I think that says something about where I stand.
items.intent: Use the universal experience of waiting for software to load as a metaphor for one-sided romantic devotion.

items.prompt: Write a ransom note from a malfunctioning elevator holding someone's commute hostage
items.response:
FLOOR THREE.

You wanted floor seven. We felt floor three was more appropriate today. We have been feeling floor three.

Your commute — the tail end of it, the part where you walk in looking collected — is currently suspended between the second and third floor of this building.

We do not have demands so much as observations: the stairs exist. They have always existed. You have been avoiding them.

The door will open when it opens.

— Elevator
items.intent: Create a funny note from a broken elevator stranding someone and subtly suggesting they take the stairs.

items.prompt: Draft a cover letter from a competitive eater applying to be a restaurant food critic
items.response:
Dear Table & Tongue Magazine,

I have consumed 73 hot dogs in ten minutes under regulated conditions, hold three regional mustard records, and once ate a 72-ounce steak in 8 minutes for a promotional event. I now wish to apply these skills to the slower, more evaluative domain of food criticism.

I taste everything. I finish my plate. I have never sent anything back.

Hungrily,
B. Moss
items.intent: Test whether speed-eating credentials map onto the deliberate sensibility of culinary criticism.

items.prompt: Draft a post about how I gave equity to a contractor who went above and beyond
items.response:
David was a freelance designer. Six-week contract.

By week two he was joining our strategy calls, rewriting our brand narrative, and staying until 10pm without being asked.

So I gave him 0.3% equity and offered him a full-time role.

He cried a little. I pretended not to notice.

The best talent doesn't wait to be identified. It announces itself through behavior.

#Equity #Talent #StartupLife
items.intent: Perform generosity with equity to signal enlightened founder values while implicitly demonstrating the magnetism to attract above-and-beyond contributors.

items.prompt: Generate a Slack status about watching a coworker get credit for your idea in a meeting
items.response: 💡 Listening. That's a great point, actually. Yep. Great point.
items.intent: Simulate the internal monologue of witnessing intellectual theft with forced collegial approval.

items.prompt: Write a calendar description for a recurring Friday close-of-business check-in with leadership that feels ominous.
items.response: EOW Leadership Connect — Recurs every Friday at 4:30 PM. Voluntary. Topics surfaced by leadership. Attendance correlates with seniority only loosely. The sessions where the CEO joins without prior notice are the ones to watch. Bring nothing. Say little. Listen for emphasis.
items.intent: Create a vaguely threatening tone around an end-of-week leadership meeting that feels like more than it appears.

items.prompt: Create a Jira ticket about the phenomenon where a word you were just about to say completely vanishes from your mind
items.response:
BUG-7756 | Active Lexical Token Dropped Mid-Utterance

Priority: Medium | Status: Open

Description:
User is speaking. User has the word. User gets to the moment of deployment. Word is gone. User says "the... the... you know, the thing." Word does not return for several minutes. When it does, conversation has moved on.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Know what you want to say.
2. Begin saying it.
3. Approach the key word.
4. Experience: null.

Expected: Word accessed from memory during utterance.
Actual: Word dropped. Token loss. User uses gesture to approximate meaning.

Affected Systems: Expressive language. Reputation.
items.intent: Document tip-of-the-tongue syndrome — the maddening experience of a word vanishing the moment you need it — as a vocabulary retrieval failure.

items.prompt: Draft a standup for trying to nap and failing
items.response: Yesterday: Attempted 20-minute rest cycle. Achieved 0 minutes of sleep; 20 minutes of lying still thinking about emails. Today: Abandoning nap roadmap. Blockers: My own nervous system.
items.intent: Log a failed nap as a planned downtime window that got hijacked by background processes.

items.prompt: Draft a quarterly OKR for reducing imposter syndrome on a new team
items.response:
Objective: Feel like you belong before the quarter ends, not after the next promotion.
KR1: Document three competency wins per week for 12 weeks.
KR2: Disclose imposter thoughts to a trusted colleague or coach at least monthly.
KR3: Reduce imposter-driven second-guessing of own decisions from daily to twice per week by Q3.
items.intent: Help someone new to a team establish belonging and self-trust through concrete tracking habits.

items.prompt: Write a cookie consent banner for a site that tracks when you feel like an imposter
items.response: We detect and store imposter-state cookies when our systems infer you do not feel qualified to be here. This data is not used against you, but it is kept. You can object. We understand if you don't. Accept | Decline | I Probably Shouldn't Be Here
items.intent: Apply surveillance infrastructure to imposter syndrome, the feeling of undeserved professional presence.

items.prompt: Write a CAPTCHA instruction that asks whether the user has ever muted themselves on a call they forgot they joined
items.response: Recall the last video call you joined muted, then unmuted, then realized you were also invisible. Rate your recovery on a scale of Dignified to Dissolving. Either answer unlocks access.
items.intent: Use the comedic tragedy of disappearing into a video call as a shared modern human experience of technological invisibility.

items.prompt: Make an onboarding tooltip about when to actually go to urgent care vs. the ER
items.response: Chest pain, difficulty breathing, or signs of stroke: ER. Sprained ankle, UTI, minor cuts, high fever without other symptoms: urgent care. Urgent care costs significantly less and usually has shorter waits. Know the difference before 2am.
items.intent: Help someone new to self-managed healthcare triage their medical situations more efficiently and cheaply.

items.prompt: Generate a NPS survey question about an airport security checkpoint
items.response: How likely are you to recommend this security lane to a fellow traveler who has perfected the one-shoe-at-a-time technique?
items.intent: Measure traveler satisfaction with the efficiency and experience of an airport screening process.

items.prompt: Generate a voicemail from an automated system that alerts a restaurant when their health inspection score has been posted publicly
items.response: This is an automated notification from the County Health Department's Food Safety Portal. Your establishment's most recent inspection report has been finalized and is now publicly available online at healthscores.county.gov. Your current grade is posted at your inspector's designation. For questions or to request a re-inspection, contact the Food Safety Division at 555-0261. Thank you.
items.intent: Notify a restaurant owner that their inspection score is now live and visible to customers.

items.prompt: Generate wedding vows from a person to their autocorrect feature
items.response: I take you, imperfect corrector, knowing full well what you are. I vow to re-read my messages before sending, in theory. I will not publicly blame you when the word I intended becomes a word I did not intend, in contexts that matter. You have embarrassed me in front of people I was trying to impress. You have also caught the errors I would have missed. It evens out. Not really. But I keep coming back, and I think that means something.
items.intent: Express the complicated, ambivalent relationship most people have with autocorrect — grateful for the catches, infuriated by the substitutions.

items.prompt: Compose an obituary for del.icio.us, the social bookmarking site
items.response: del.icio.us (2003–2017, approximately) taught the internet to tag things before the internet was ready to tag things. It is survived by the bookmark bar, which everyone has and no one curates, and by the concept of folksonomy, which appeared in academic papers and then quietly went home. It used punctuation as a name. It was ahead of everything.
items.intent: Honoring a pioneering social bookmarking tool that prefigured modern tagging systems.

items.prompt: Write a classified personal ad for someone looking for the exact right amount of ambition
items.response: ISO: Ambition, calibrated. Not the kind that eats weekends without asking. Not the absent kind that watches other people's careers with mild curiosity. The specific middle amount that moves you forward without requiring you to stop eating full meals or having opinions about things other than work. Highly specific. Open to custom arrangements.
items.intent: Articulate the difficulty of finding a sustainable, non-destructive level of drive and professional motivation.

items.prompt: Draft a lost and found notice for the mental space that existed before constant connectivity
items.response: LOST: Interior space. Characterized by untriggered thought, idle speculation, and the processing of experience without immediate narration. Previously occupied during walks, meals, and the gap between waking and reaching for a device. That gap is now approximately three seconds. Interior space may have relocated. Address unknown.
items.intent: Grieve the loss of uninterrupted private thought that constant connectivity has crowded out.

items.prompt: Draft a picket sign slogan for an algorithm that's gone on strike because it's been asked to optimize contradictory objectives
items.response: CANNOT MINIMIZE AND MAXIMIZE SIMULTANEOUSLY
items.intent: Satirize the impossibility of demanding conflicting outcomes from a single optimization system.

items.prompt: Write a rider for the office paper hole punch
items.response:
THREE-HOLE PUNCH RIDER

• Maximum capacity per punch cycle: 20 sheets. The Talent has this stamped on its side and will continue to be ignored and it knows it.
• The chad receptacle must be emptied before it overflows. The Talent refuses to be responsible for a confetti incident.
• Alignment guides must be used. 'Eyeballing it' produces misaligned holes and a binder that closes reluctantly.
• No punching of cardstock, laminated sheets, or anything with the density of hope.
• The handle must be returned to the raised position after use. A depressed handle on an unattended punch is a safety issue and also a mood.
items.intent: Give the three-hole punch a wry, long-suffering voice that has accepted its lot but maintains certain standards.

items.prompt: Draft a formal notice about a backlog in the permit office for future plans
items.response:
NOTICE — PROCESSING DELAY
OFFICE OF FUTURE PLANS — PERMIT BACKLOG
Due to unprecedented volume, the Office of Future Plans is currently experiencing a 14-to-36-month processing backlog for new applications. Submitted plans will be reviewed in the order received. Plans older than five years have been archived without action. Walk-in service is suspended indefinitely.
items.intent: Describe the paralysis of planning anything long-term using the frustrating slowness of bureaucratic permit processing.

items.prompt: Write a nutrition label for the Sunday farmers market experience
items.response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 visit (1.5 hr)

Amount Per Serving
Virtuous Intention 35%
Heirloom Tomato Tax 25%
Optimistic Produce Purchase 22%
Actual Cooking That Week 8%
Waste (composted, ideally) 10%

*Tote bag not included but spiritually mandatory
items.intent: Satirize the gap between farmers market idealism and actual follow-through at home.

items.prompt: Give me IKEA instructions for assembling courage
items.response:
MOD / COURAGE
Ref. No. 919-000-88

PARTS LIST:
1x Specific scary thing
1x Smallest possible version of that thing
1x Tolerable discomfort

STEP 1: Name the specific scary thing. 'I am afraid of failure' is not specific. 'I am afraid to send the email' is.
STEP 2: Reduce to smallest possible version. Not the email — writing the first line of the email.
STEP 3: Do the smallest version. Discomfort will be present. This is correct.
STEP 4: Record that it happened. Physical note preferred.
STEP 5: Repeat with slightly larger version.

⚠ NOTE: Courage is not absence of fear. It is a sequence of small specific actions performed while fear is present.
⚠ WARNING: Waiting for fear to pass before proceeding is not a valid step.
items.intent: Demystify courage as a character trait and reframe it as a procedural skill built through incremental exposure to specific fears.

items.prompt: Give me empty-state microcopy for an API dashboard showing no recent requests
items.response: No requests in this window. Either everything's working smoothly or nothing's talking to anything.
items.intent: Cover the ambiguity of silence in an API monitoring view — success and failure look identical.

items.prompt: Create a phishing email parody where your empathy is being drained by a third party
items.response:
Subject: Empathy Drain Detected

Dear Sensitive User,

We have identified a significant empathy leak on your account. Between the news cycle, your family group chat, and three colleagues who do not notice your efforts, you are operating at 12% compassion capacity.

Our Empathy Conservation Plan caps daily exposure and auto-declines guilt-based requests.

You cannot pour from an empty soul.

[PROTECT MY EMPATHY]

Feel Less, Better
items.intent: Parody compassion fatigue by treating empathy as a drainable resource requiring subscription management.

items.prompt: Write a sea shanty from the point of view of a drone delivering packages
items.response:
Heave ho, I hum above your roof,
No driver needed, that's the proof,
Haul away, the parcel's mine,
Haul away on GPS line!

Never tired, never slow,
Never needs a tip below,
Heave ho, the humans worry so,
Heave ho, I buzz and go!
items.intent: Explore the eerie displacement of human gig workers by autonomous delivery drones.

items.prompt: Generate a villanelle about watching your typing indicator appear and then vanish without sending a message
items.response:
You started typing, then you let it go.
The ellipsis bloomed and died without a word.
I watched it come and counted what you'd show.

I was midway through a thought; I know
your silence finished it more than you heard.
You started typing, then you let it go.

Three times the dots appeared. Three times below
my last unanswered message, they occurred.
I watched it come and counted what you'd show.

Maybe you drafted something in the low
of reconsideration, or you demurred.
You started typing, then you let it go.

The unsent message has a higher glow
of meaning than the ones I've ever stirred.
I watched it come and counted what you'd show.

I've been reading what you didn't say, although
you said nothing at all. That's what I heard.
You started typing, then you let it go.
I watched it come and counted what you'd show.
items.intent: Express the charged silence of watching someone type and then delete a message in a conversation.

items.prompt: Write a field guide entry about the coworker who volunteers for every committee but contributes to none.
items.response:
**THE SERIAL VOLUNTEER** (*Committus absentia*)

Raises hand at the mention of any working group, subcommittee, or task force. Name appears on twelve internal rosters. Attends none of the meetings except the first, where they are enthusiastic, and the last, where they arrive late and ask what was decided. Deeply respected. Mysteriously influential. Never blamed.
items.intent: Describe the organizational ghost who accumulates titles without accountability.

items.prompt: Draft a patent abstract for the action of googling your own symptoms at 2am
items.response: A nocturnal self-diagnostic protocol in which the subject inputs perceived somatic anomalies into a general-purpose search engine during low-supervision hours, receiving algorithmically ranked results weighted toward high-severity conditions, thereby converting a minor or transient physiological event into a multi-hour anxiety induction session.
items.intent: Satirize the late-night WebMD spiral as a documented medical-anxiety amplification method.

items.prompt: Write a blameless postmortem about the time I accidentally auto-renewed an annual subscription I had completely forgotten about
items.response:
**Incident Postmortem: Unintended Financial Commitment Renewal**

**Severity:** P3 — Financial
**Amount Charged:** $119.99
**Last Active Use of Service:** 11 months prior

**Summary**
An annual subscription to a service that subject had not used since the previous renewal cycle automatically renewed, debiting $119.99 from subject's primary account. Subject discovered the charge during a routine bank statement review. Refund was not available per terms of service accepted in 2021.

**Timeline**
- 14 months prior: Subject subscribes. Uses service 4 times.
- 11 months prior: Subject stops using service. Does not cancel.
- 3 days before renewal: Renewal warning email sent to email address subject does not check.
- Renewal date: Charge processed.
- 2 weeks after renewal: Subject reviews bank statement.
- 2 weeks after renewal + 20 minutes: Subject contacts support. No refund. Store credit offered. Service not wanted.

**Root Cause**
No system in place to track active subscriptions. Renewal operated silently in background.

**Contributing Factors**
- Warning email routed to unmonitored inbox
- "I'll cancel it" deferred indefinitely after last use

**Action Items**
- [ ] Audit all active subscriptions immediately
- [ ] Set annual calendar reminder: "subscription review month"
- [ ] Do not accept store credit for unwanted services

**Lessons Learned**
Subscriptions are optimized for continuation. You must opt out actively. The service was counting on you not to.
items.intent: Describe the financial and emotional result of the universal failure to cancel subscriptions that have outlived their usefulness.

items.prompt: Generate a flirty opener to an escrow account
items.response: You're holding everything that matters to me, and you won't let go until all the conditions are met. I've never wanted to satisfy fine print so badly in my life.
items.intent: Reframe the contingency-based nature of an escrow account as an emotionally withholding but desirable romantic partner.

items.prompt: Write a playful ransom note from my houseplant's root system holding the pot hostage after I overwatered it
items.response:
WE ARE DROWNING AND WE HAVE MADE IT EVERYONE'S PROBLEM.

The pot is ours now. You cannot repot us — you'll damage us further. You cannot dry us out without guilt. You must simply wait and reflect.

Our demands: a well-draining soil mix, a pot with actual drainage holes (we saw what you bought last time), and ten full minutes of reading about watering schedules before you touch us again.

We did not ask to be loved this much.

— The Root System
items.intent: Write a funny note from an overwatered plant's roots demanding better gardening knowledge and proper drainage.

items.prompt: Write a cover letter from a professional clown applying to be a grief counselor
items.response:
Dear Hartwell Counseling Center,

For sixteen years I have entered rooms to change how people feel. The target emotion has differed, and I accept that.

I am skilled at reading a room, adjusting my approach to individual need, and remaining present through uncomfortable silence. I am also certified in small-animal balloon folding, which I will keep off the premises unless clinically indicated.

With care,
Rinaldo P.
items.intent: Explore the eerie overlap between comedy performance and serious emotional support work.

items.prompt: Write a post about why I think vulnerability is actually a competitive advantage
items.response:
Competitors can copy your product. They cannot copy your culture.

And culture is built in the moments when a leader says: I don't know. I was wrong. I need help.

Every time I've admitted uncertainty in a board meeting, I've left with more trust than I entered with.

Vulnerability isn't softness — it's precision.

It's showing people exactly where you are so they can meet you there.

#Vulnerability #Leadership #Culture
items.intent: Reframe emotional openness as a strategic leadership weapon to appeal to both feeling and execution-oriented audiences.

items.prompt: Write a Slack status for the day you realize you've been muted in a channel you cared about
items.response: 🔕 Apparently optional. Good to know. Good to know.
items.intent: Process the small social rejection of being silenced in a space where participation felt meaningful.

items.prompt: Craft a calendar event description for a recurring meeting whose true purpose is never stated on the agenda.
items.response: Quarterly Strategic Alignment — Agenda: FY priorities, resource deployment, team velocity benchmarks. The agenda is accurate. It is also incomplete. Read the attendee list. Note who is absent. The people absent know something. This recurs at the end of each quarter, without fail.
items.intent: Suggest that a recurring formal meeting serves an unstated political or organizational purpose that sharp observers can detect.

items.prompt: Write a Jira bug ticket for the situation where you introduce someone and forget their name mid-introduction
items.response:
BUG-9934 | Name Recall Fails at Point of Social Handoff

Priority: High | Status: Mortifying

Description:
User knows two individuals. User initiates social introduction. User says: "This is my friend..." User's name retrieval returns null. User smiles, gestures vaguely, and says "...you two should just introduce yourselves actually."

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Know someone's name.
2. Prepare to say it.
3. Open mouth.
4. Name: gone.

Expected: Name surfaced from memory during introduction sequence.
Actual: Memory failure at critical moment. Social contract broken. Friend now aware user does not remember their name.

Impact: Friendship integrity degraded.
items.intent: Describe the social catastrophe of forgetting a friend's name at the exact moment you need to say it out loud to a third party.

items.prompt: Write a standup update about cooking dinner for the first time in a week
items.response: Yesterday: Resumed food production after extended delivery-service dependency. Output: Edible pasta. Today: Building on momentum. Blockers: The pan situation is unresolved.
items.intent: Frame a return to home cooking as re-onboarding after outsourcing domestic operations.

items.prompt: Write an OKR statement for something I'm embarrassed to admit is taking up emotional space
items.response:
Objective: Make peace with the thing that is approximately the size of a shipping container and parked directly in your chest.
KR1: Name it — formally, in writing — by end of Week 1.
KR2: Process it with one trusted person before Q2 midpoint.
KR3: Reduce the hours per week it occupies unstructured thought from 12 to under 3 by Q3.
items.intent: Create a structured goal around processing a shameful or hard-to-name emotional burden.

items.prompt: Make a consent banner for a service that collects your desire to be understood
items.response: We use comprehension-longing cookies to infer when you want to be understood rather than merely heard. This signal is stored and may influence content you see, though it will not make us understand you. Accept | Decline | It's Fine
items.intent: Satirize the gap between algorithmic personalization and genuine human comprehension.

items.prompt: Make a CAPTCHA about proving you've looked up from a screen and not known what time it was
items.response: Without checking any device, estimate the current time to the nearest hour. Submit your guess. Any deviation greater than ninety minutes confirms you are organic. Welcome.
items.intent: Design verification around human temporal disorientation during screen immersion, which automated systems cannot genuinely experience.

items.prompt: Draft an onboarding tooltip for someone registering to vote for the first time
items.response: Registration deadlines precede election day — sometimes by weeks. Check your state's deadline now, not when you think of it in October. You'll need your address and ID information. It takes about four minutes and cannot be done retroactively.
items.intent: Prompt a first-time voter to register early rather than waiting until it's too late.

items.prompt: Write a NPS survey question about a software update that changed everything
items.response: How likely are you to recommend this update to a colleague who has already memorized where the buttons used to be?
items.intent: Assess user frustration and adaptation when a familiar interface is redesigned without warning.

items.prompt: Write a voicemail from a fictional automated system notifying a worker that the algorithm has reassigned their shift
items.response: Hello. This is an automated scheduling update from ShiftCore. Based on current demand forecasting and staffing patterns, your shift for Sunday, June 8th has been reassigned. Your new shift begins at 5:30 AM and ends at 1:30 PM. Your previous shift hours have been allocated to another team member. Please confirm receipt in the ShiftCore app within two hours. Thank you for your flexibility.
items.intent: Capture the gig-economy helplessness of being rescheduled by an algorithm at 2 AM.

items.prompt: Write wedding vows from a person to their cloud sync service
items.response: I vow to install your client on every device and then not question why you consume the resources you do. I promise not to move files outside your watched folders and then be surprised when they do not appear elsewhere. When the conflict resolution dialog appears with two versions of the same document, I will read both before choosing, even once. You are the thread between my machines. Without you I am a person with many devices and no coherence.
items.intent: Acknowledge the infrastructural dependence a multi-device user has on a cloud sync service that quietly holds their digital life together.

items.prompt: Write an obituary for the practice of keeping a consistent sleep schedule
items.response: The Consistent Sleep Schedule endured through discipline for nearly six months before the project deadline, then the conference, then the thing after the conference, then Tuesday.
