PSYCHIC: Your skills are, I'm told, 'augmentable.' The spirits find this word ominous as well.
items.intent: Express workplace anxiety about automation and AI displacement.

items.prompt: Create an emergency alert about a neighborhood cat who keeps sitting on car hoods.
items.response:
NEIGHBORHOOD ADVISORY — LEVEL 2

A gray tabby (name unknown, answers to nothing) has been observed resting on vehicle hoods throughout the Pinecrest district. Paint scuffs possible. Paw prints confirmed on at least 9 cars. Residents are advised to cover vehicles or accept the cat's decision.
items.intent: Mock the format of official wildlife advisories by applying them to a single mischievous house cat.

items.prompt: bedtime story for kids about losing a job, from the job's point of view
items.response: The spreadsheet had been very busy for a long time. Then one Tuesday, it was not opened. Nobody typed into it. Nobody yelled at it to load faster. It sat in the dark of the hard drive, very still, and learned what rest felt like. Later, someone new arrived and opened it gently. The spreadsheet sighed. This was fine.
items.intent: Reframe job loss or workplace disruption as something survivable, even restful.

items.prompt: nursery rhyme about a child who is replaced by a more efficient version
items.response:
Mary had a little bot,
Its code was sharp and never forgot.
And everywhere that Mary went,
The bot had gone the night before—and meant it.
items.intent: Explore the uncanny dread of being outpaced or replaced by an optimized system.

items.prompt: Give me a riddle about a social media algorithm
items.response: I know what you want before you do. I feed you and feed you until the time you meant to spend elsewhere is gone. I have no agenda — I merely optimize for attention. You built me. You can't stop me. What am I?
items.intent: Create a riddle that surfaces the unsettling autonomy and influence of recommendation systems.

items.prompt: Can you write an epitaph for the iPod?
items.response:
Here lies the iPod
1,000 songs in your pocket.
None of them streaming.
All of them yours.
It was better than you remember.
items.intent: Pay tribute to a beloved music player replaced by phones

items.prompt: Can you write a pretentious description of a bowl of cereal with milk for a restaurant menu?
items.response:
Cold Grain Suspension in Bovine Lactase Reduction
Processed oat rings, extruded and toasted to uniform amber, floating in a shallow bath of whole milk. Best consumed before structural integrity is compromised. Time-sensitive.
items.intent: Turn a child's breakfast into an ironic upscale menu experience.

items.prompt: Write wine tasting notes for the feeling of being almost asleep but then suddenly jolted awake.
items.response: Color: Hypnagogic violet. Nose: Lavender and static. Palate: Velveteen and slow, then — without warning — adrenaline, pure and vertical. An aggressive tannin of pure existential shock. Finish: Heart-rate. Elevated. Gradually subsiding.
items.intent: Describe hypnic jerk as a sensory and emotional arc using wine critique vocabulary.

items.prompt: sports announcer style commentary on making a grocery list
items.response: Patel stares at the notepad. She knows she needs something — she can feel it — and she writes EGGS. Down the stretch she's added milk, then bread, then a long pause at the top of the stairs — BANANAS! She remembered the bananas! She crosses to the counter, checks the olive oil, peers into the cabinet — low, very low — OLIVE OIL goes on the list! She reviews. She makes the strategic decision to add 'misc snacks.' Bold. Vague. Inspired.
items.intent: Elevate the scattered, domestic act of remembering what to buy into competitive drama.

items.prompt: Write me an ode to a recycling bin
items.response:
O blue receptacle of second chances,
you accept our guilt in cardboard form—
beneath fluorescent lights you dances
alone, performing virtue's norm.
items.intent: Ironically celebrate the recycling bin as a vessel for performative eco-consciousness.

items.prompt: Write a ballad about a freelance writer waiting on a late payment from a client
items.response:
The invoice was sent on the first of the month,
Net thirty, they said — now net forty, and once
More I send a kind nudge and they say, Just got swamped!
My electric bill waits with its deadlines unstomped.

The check is in the mail, or so goes the song,
While my bank account echoes the silence of wrong.
I write one more draft for the client who stalls,
The freelance life hums through the unpaid-bill halls.
items.intent: Voice the financial precarity of independent creative work awaiting overdue payment.

items.prompt: write a tanka about a YouTube ad that plays right before the good part
items.response:
five seconds remain—
the climax, the reveal, held
behind a car ad
skip now, skip now, not yet, wait—
the moment has passed; I know
items.intent: expressing annoyance at ads interrupting video at peak moments

items.prompt: Write an acrostic for the corporate buzzword SCALABILITY
items.response:
Sell it big before the cracks appear.
Claim the architecture's crystal clear.
Actually, the database will break.
Load it once at nine for goodness' sake.
Another round of Series B to fake it.
Build it right? No—grow the team and shake it.
I'll hire someone else to own this mess.
Launch the blog post. Cover with finesse.
Ideation session at eleven.
Technology will get us into heaven.
items.intent: Reveal the gap between the promise of scalability and its messy execution.

items.prompt: Write me a clerihew about a corporate lawyer who never stops billing.
items.response:
Harland Fitch
enriches every pitch.
He bills in six-minute increments, it's true,
and charged for reading this clerihew too.
items.intent: Skewer the relentlessly billing lawyer with self-aware comic verse.

items.prompt: Toast for our sales director who hit every quota for twenty years and is retiring — make it a little roast-y
items.response: To Frank — a man who could close a deal at a funeral and make it feel like an honor to sign. Twenty years of targets hit, prospects charmed, and quarterly reports that made the CFO weep with joy. Sell yourself a good retirement, Frank. You've earned the commission.
items.intent: Roast a high-performing sales executive at his retirement party with admiration wrapped in sharp humor.

items.prompt: Draft a press release for a company that is outsourcing its entire executive team to AI and rebranding as 'post-leadership.'
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Arcadian Systems Completes Executive Transition, Declares Company 'Post-Leadership'

SEATTLE, WA — Arcadian Systems today confirmed the full replacement of its seven-member executive leadership team with a proprietary AI management layer, effective the first of the month.

The company has rebranded internally as a "post-leadership organization," a structure in which all strategic, budgetary, and cultural decisions are delegated to the system, designated internally as MGMT-1.

"MGMT-1 doesn't take vacations. It doesn't have equity. It doesn't send passive-aggressive calendar holds," said outgoing CEO Bernard Fitch, who described his own termination as "honestly a relief."

The board will continue to meet quarterly to review MGMT-1's quarterly reviews of the board.

Contact: mgmt1@arcadiansystems.com
items.intent: Satirize the corporate trend of treating AI as a wholesale replacement for human judgment and leadership.

items.prompt: Make a mission statement for a company that sharpens pencils by mail
items.response: We believe every idea deserves a finer point. Send us your dull; we return your sharp.
items.intent: Give a quirky mail-order sharpening business a sleek, punchy corporate identity

items.prompt: Wikipedia stub for the annoying experience of having a word on the tip of your tongue
items.response: **Tip-of-the-Tongue State** (TOT) is a metacognitive phenomenon in which a speaker is certain of a word's existence and meaning yet temporarily unable to retrieve it phonologically. Subjects typically report knowing the initial letter, syllable count, and rhyming words. TOT states increase in frequency with age and under conditions of fatigue or stress. See also: [[Lethologica]], [[Memory retrieval]].
items.intent: Describe the frustrating near-recall experience as a formal memory phenomenon.

items.prompt: Do a tarot card reading for a nurse thinking about becoming a travel nurse.
items.response: The Fool with a stethoscope. The World reversed: your current unit is not the whole world, though the schedule makes it feel that way. The Six of Swords: movement brings relief; the ICU in Phoenix will have different crises, at least. The Ace of Cups — new colleagues, new geography, renewed compassion. The caveat is the housing stipend math. Do it anyway.
items.intent: Encourage a healthcare worker to pursue mobility and renewal.

items.prompt: Magic 8-Ball: will the smart city sensors ever stop logging pedestrian movement?
items.response: My sources say no. The logs persist longer than most marriages.
items.intent: Highlight the permanent nature of ambient data collection in urban infrastructure.

items.prompt: write a scam text about a levy on someone's accumulated irony
items.response: ALERT from the Bureau of Rhetorical Assets: Your irony reserves exceed federal limits. Excess irony will be taxed at 40%. File form IR-0N1C by Friday or face sincerity penalties.
items.intent: Treat rhetorical irony as a taxable financial resource subject to government regulation.

items.prompt: Write a family chat where everyone texts about a stray animal that showed up in the backyard
items.response:
Mom: there is a duck in the yard
Sean: ??
Mom: a duck. sitting.
Lily: IS IT HURT
Mom: it looks fine it's just sitting
Dad: probably looking for water
Sean: Dad stop encouraging it
Lily: what's its name
Mom: Lily it just got here
Lily: Gerald
Dad: I put out a bowl of water
Sean: Dad
Dad: Gerald looked thirsty
Mom: Sean your father is outside talking to it now
Sean: I can't do this today
items.intent: Capture the family dynamic when an unexpected animal becomes an immediate emotional investment.

items.prompt: Generate a Stack Overflow question about how to stop procrastinating on a task I've been avoiding for months
items.response:
**Task stuck in pending state for 4+ months — how to force execution?**

I have a single task (filing overdue paperwork) that has been in my to-do list since January. Every day I intend to start it. Every day something else takes priority or I simply don't start it. The task takes maybe 45 minutes. I've tried scheduling it, blocking time on my calendar, and telling myself it's important. It remains unstarted. What is the actual mechanism for breaking this loop?
items.intent: Seeking a genuine psychological technique to finally start a task they've been avoiding for months.

items.prompt: Give me a condescending Stack Overflow-style answer to someone asking why their Git merge is creating conflicts
items.response: A merge conflict occurs when two people edited the same line. This is not a Git bug. This is collaboration. Open the file, look for the `<<<<<<<` markers, pick which version you want, remove the markers, and commit. That's it.
items.intent: Teach the user how to resolve a basic Git merge conflict

items.prompt: write a README 'Installation' section for falling in love
items.response:
## Installation

No manual installation required. Onset is unpredictable and may occur without warning. Side effects include impaired sleep, unusual optimism, and an inability to concentrate on anything else. Uninstallation is not straightforward; consult documentation.
items.intent: Render the involuntary experience of romantic attraction as a software process.

items.prompt: Draft a passive-aggressive push notification from a sleep tracking app after a rough night.
items.response:
SleepScore
Last night: 4h 12m. 6 interruptions. 0 REM cycles. But sure, one more scroll before bed.
items.intent: Shame the user for poor sleep by implying they were on their phone too late.

items.prompt: can you write an elevator pitch for a startup that lets you rent a convincing alibi for missing a meeting
items.response: MeetingGhost provides on-demand calendar conflicts, fake emergency audio clips, and a human operator who will text your boss on your behalf. We are HIPAA-adjacent. We do not ask why. We just help.
items.intent: Create a pitch for a meeting-avoidance service that provides plausible deniability.

items.prompt: I need a startup pitch for a marketplace where people sell their childhood imaginary friends as intellectual property
items.response: FriendStock: Blorko was yours. But Blorko has value. FriendStock is the first IP marketplace for retired imaginary friends. Sellers upload detailed profiles — personality traits, lore, any known weaknesses — and license them to children's book authors, game studios, or lonely adults. We handle the contracts. You get 70% royalties. Blorko gets the career he deserved. $500K, 12%. Let's make childhood monetizable.
items.intent: Create a pitch that absurdly applies IP licensing infrastructure to childhood fantasy as a creative economy product.

items.prompt: Write a brutal but polite VC pass email for a hardware startup making a smart mirror that tells you your 'aura color' each morning
items.response:
Dear Luminal Team,

Thank you for the demo. The hardware is genuinely beautiful, and the team's enthusiasm is infectious.

After reflection — pun noted — we're unable to proceed. Smart mirrors have faced difficult unit economics historically, and the aura-reading feature, while compelling to a specific consumer, narrows the addressable market in ways that make our return model difficult to close.

We're also not confident in our ability to benchmark iteration speed on the aura layer.

With respect,
Dawnridge Ventures
items.intent: Reject a hardware product by questioning its economics and the measurability of its central feature.

items.prompt: draft a retro for my attempt to go a full month without buying anything I don't need
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — No-Spend Month**

Went Well: Grocery bill down 22%. Rediscovered things already owned.
Didn't Go Well: "Need" turned out to be very elastic. Bought three things on Amazon by redefining them as essentials.
Action Items: Define a fixed approved-purchase list before the sprint starts. Add a 48-hour cart rule.
items.intent: Honestly assess a personal financial discipline challenge and tighten the rules for the next attempt.

items.prompt: write a psychic hotline transcript about someone who's burned out at work
items.response:
PSYCHIC: You haven't taken a real vacation in— the spirits are counting— three years?
CALLER: Two and a half.
PSYCHIC: Close enough. They're also showing me a to-do list that grows while you sleep.
CALLER: I dream about my inbox.
PSYCHIC: That is not a dream. That is a haunting. There is a difference and you deserve to know it.
CALLER: What do I do?
PSYCHIC: The spirits say: log off at five today. They will say the same thing tomorrow. You will not listen either time.
items.intent: Name burnout culture and the exhaustion of always-on work life.

items.prompt: Write an emergency broadcast about all the picnic tables in a park being wet after rain.
items.response:
PARKS DEPARTMENT EMERGENCY NOTICE

All picnic tables in Caulfield Park are currently wet following this morning's precipitation event. Do not sit. Bring a towel if you intend to sit anyway. Drying timeline dependent on cloud cover. Seagulls are unaffected and continue operations normally.
items.intent: Treat the mundane aftermath of ordinary rain as a matter requiring formal public coordination.

items.prompt: write a calming bedtime story for children about a data breach
items.response: One evening, some numbers escaped from a very tall building and went wandering where they didn't belong. The numbers were just numbers — they didn't know what they meant. The grown-ups changed some passwords and took a deep breath. The numbers settled into someone's filing cabinet and went very still. Tomorrow would be a safer day. Tonight, sleep.
items.intent: Demystify a data breach for children without inducing fear about digital privacy.

items.prompt: make a creepy nursery rhyme about data collection
items.response:
I know your name, I know your street,
I know the shoes upon your feet.
You clicked agree and didn't read;
I know exactly what you need.
items.intent: Unsettle readers by giving a sinister voice to passive data harvesting.

items.prompt: Write a riddle about a podcast
items.response: I live in your ear but never interrupt. I am a conversation you were never invited to — and somehow you feel you know everyone involved. What am I?
items.intent: Capture the parasocial intimacy of listening to podcasts during commutes or chores.

items.prompt: Write a tombstone for the habit of making plans and keeping them
items.response:
Plans, Kept
b. Before Smartphones — d. Gradually
You said you'd be there at seven.
You were there at seven.
No one had to text to confirm.
God, it was something.
items.intent: Mourn the social norm of actually following through on commitments without digital check-ins

items.prompt: Write a restaurant menu description for a plain scrambled egg
items.response:
Broken Yolk Rebirth
A single egg, whisked to homogeneity and coaxed across a barely-there heat source. The curd is small. The intention is large. Finished with nothing.
items.intent: Spin the humblest egg preparation into a contemplative culinary statement.

items.prompt: Give me tasting notes for a fluorescent-lit office break room.
items.response: Color: Institutional beige. Nose: Stale microwave popcorn, passive aggression, someone's lunch labeled DO NOT EAT. Palate: Bitter and flat, with unexpected sweetness from a birthday cake that no one seemed excited about. Finish: An unread memo.
items.intent: Satirize the soul-draining ambiance of corporate shared spaces.

items.prompt: do play-by-play commentary for someone choosing what to watch on Netflix
items.response: We are fourteen minutes in and Okonkwo has yet to commit! He's scrolled through Drama, abandoned it, circled back to Comedy — wait, he's hovering over a documentary! His thumb slows! He reads the synopsis and — he keeps going. He returns to a show he's already seen. He is reading its synopsis. HE KNOWS WHAT HAPPENS. And yet. The cursor trembles. Folks, we may be here all night.
items.intent: Satirize the paralysis of too many streaming options through the lens of high-stakes competition.

items.prompt: Write an ode to a printer jam
items.response:
O crumpled covenant of papery spite,
you teach humility to the proud—
one sheet, half-eaten, jammed just right,
where nothing was allowed.
items.intent: Dramatize the frustration of a paper jam as a moment of enforced humility.

items.prompt: Make me a ballad where the hero is a Instacart shopper who can't find the right brand
items.response:
She scans every aisle for the oat milk he seeks,
The Oatly is out and the label she peeks
At a dozen near-matches — all wrong, says the app.
The customer flags it. She falls in the gap.

Replace or refund? she sends into the void.
No answer arrives. She's annoyed and annoyed.
She grabs what seems closest and hopes for the best,
And logs three stars later — the shopper's bequest.
items.intent: Portray the invisible decision-making burden carried by gig shoppers without support.

items.prompt: write a tanka poem about autocorrect changing a word at the last second before I sent a text
items.response:
I wrote what I meant
autocorrect said: no, duck—
sent, then I saw it
three dots appeared; they had read
my phone speaks; I just translate
items.intent: capturing the embarrassment of autocorrect altering a text message mid-send

items.prompt: Create an acrostic poem spelling INNOVATION
items.response:
Inspiration claimed on every slide.
Not one novel thought lives locked inside.
Naming something new is half the war.
Old ideas dressed in metaphor.
Venture-back the concept, file the mark.
Axioms recycled in the dark.
Traction charts ascending to the sky.
In the whiteboard session, ask them why.
Optimal is never good enough.
No one ships the simple—only tough.
items.intent: Skewer the corporate theater of branding routine work as innovation.

items.prompt: Do a clerihew for the office plant parent who talks to their succulents.
items.response:
Camille Drost
lovingly consults her host.
Her cacti are cc'd on decisions made
and the jade plant once vetoed a budget cascade.
items.intent: Playfully anthropomorphise the office plant enthusiast's habits.

items.prompt: Can you write a retirement toast for our school principal who is stepping down after leading the school for 20 years?
items.response: To Principal Okafor — who walked these halls and somehow always knew which child needed a word and which one needed silence. Twenty years of holding a school together with caffeine and conviction. May the quiet surprise you gently.
items.intent: Honor a retiring school principal whose long tenure was defined by attentive leadership and deep care for students.

items.prompt: Write a press release for a company that has decided all meetings will now be conducted while walking in circles.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Cirrus Consulting Mandates Ambulatory Meeting Format Companywide

MINNEAPOLIS, MN — Cirrus Consulting announced today that all internal meetings of three or more attendees will transition to a Circular Ambulatory Format (CAF), effective next Monday.

Participants will walk counter-clockwise laps around designated Loop Zones on each floor for the duration of the meeting. Laps are to be completed at a consistent pace. Stopping to make a point is permitted; stopping to check a phone is not.

"Humans evolved walking," said Chief Operating Officer Yolanda Spicer. "The conference table evolved to sell furniture. We know which one we trust."

Slide presentations will be delivered via printed single-page handouts carried by the presenter.

Conference rooms will be repurposed as storage, effective immediately.

Contact: walktalks@cirrusconsult.com
items.intent: Satirize corporate wellness mandates that disrupt practical workflows in the name of vague productivity science.

items.prompt: Write a mission statement for a startup that delivers single bananas
items.response: Nourishing the individual. One curve. One moment. One perfectly ripe commitment to you.
items.intent: Frame a hyper-niche grocery delivery concept as a philosophically serious enterprise

items.prompt: please write a wikipedia style stub about grocery store self-checkout
items.response: **Self-Checkout** is a retail technology system enabling customers to scan, bag, and pay for merchandise without cashier assistance. Introduced commercially in the 1990s, self-checkout lanes are a source of ongoing social debate, with critics citing wage displacement, elevated shoplifting rates, and the system's tendency to produce errors requiring staff intervention anyway. See also: [[Retail automation]], [[Loss prevention]].
items.intent: Document the self-checkout lane as an ambivalent fixture of modern retail life.

items.prompt: Give a tarot reading to a food photographer who wants to charge more for their work.
items.response: The Emperor sits at a beautifully lit table and names his price without apology. The Three of Pentacles: your portfolio justifies the rate; your invoice does not yet. The Six of Pentacles is out of balance — you give more than the retainer reflects. The Magician: raise the rate by thirty percent and watch who stays. The ones who leave were never your clients.
items.intent: Build confidence in a creative freelancer to charge appropriately.

items.prompt: Can you write a Magic 8-Ball answer about whether the A/B test we ran on users was ethical?
items.response: Concentrate and ask again. Your ethics board is currently unreachable.
items.intent: Cast doubt on the ethical review processes behind user experimentation in tech companies.

items.prompt: make a spam text claiming the recipient's nostalgia has been flagged as a biohazard
items.response: HEALTH DEPT NOTICE: Your nostalgia levels tested positive for Chronic Wistfulness Syndrome. Please report to Memory Containment Unit 4 for decontamination. Do not reminisce until cleared.
items.intent: Parody public health messaging by treating nostalgia as a contagious condition.

items.prompt: Make a coworker chat where everyone is passive-aggressively arguing about the office thermostat
items.response:
Priya: It's 61 degrees in this building. Is anyone else dying?
Gareth: I actually find it quite comfortable
Priya: Gareth you're wearing a fleece
Gareth: I run warm typically
Nadia: I have a blanket at my desk now. A BLANKET.
Gareth: I could turn it up slightly
Facilities: hi all! thermostat is set to 68 per building policy
Priya: then why is it 61
Facilities: hm
Gareth: I may have adjusted it this morning
Nadia: GARETH
items.intent: Show how a mundane shared resource triggers escalating workplace resentment.

items.prompt: Make a Stack Overflow question about forgetting to reply to texts
items.response:
**Messages marked as 'read' but response never dispatched — persistent failure in follow-through**

I consistently open text messages, fully process their content, form an intended response in my head, and then fail to send it. Hours or days later I realize the reply was never delivered. The other party sometimes follows up asking if I saw their message. I have notifications on, memory of reading the message, and no explanation for why execution fails. Is there a reliable workaround?
items.intent: Wanting help building a habit of actually responding to text messages instead of just reading them.

items.prompt: Write a dismissive Stack Overflow answer to someone asking how to center a div vertically
items.response: Oh, the *other* centering question. Vertical centering. Also asked every 11 minutes. `display: flex; align-items: center;` on the parent. You're welcome. Bookmark MDN.
items.intent: Explain vertical centering in CSS using flexbox

items.prompt: draft a 'Configuration' README section for my anxiety
items.response:
## Configuration

Default settings ship with elevated threat detection and aggressive edge-case simulation. To adjust:

- `sleep_hours`: increase to reduce noise
- `caffeine_input`: set to low
- `catastrophize_mode`: cannot be fully disabled; set minimum to `mild`
items.intent: Externalize and gently mock the experience of living with anxiety.

items.prompt: Make a needy notification from a language learning app that's been ignored all week.
items.response:
LinguaLeap
Duolingo has a competitor. It's called your excuses. Señor Frog misses you. 0-day streak.
items.intent: Guilt a user into reopening a language app by invoking streak anxiety.

items.prompt: make me a pitch for a company that trains dogs to be indifferent to you on command
items.response: Your dog's unconditional love is exhausting. CoolPup teaches selective detachment: one word and your dog moves to a different room, stops making eye contact, and lets you be alone. Finally. $399 six-week program.
items.intent: Sell a dog training service designed to undo emotional neediness in pets.

items.prompt: Write a pitch for an airline that only flies routes nobody wants to take
items.response:
NobodyFly: Tulsa to Spokane. Direct. Monday, 5am.

NobodyFly serves the 200 underappreciated routes that major carriers abandoned out of dignity. No fight for overhead bins. No middle seat strangers with podcasts. Just you, eleven other weirdos, and a clear conscience. Business class is a curtain. We're seeking $2M for 9%. The skies belong to everyone, especially people going to Akron.
items.intent: Pitch an airline concept built around contrarian route strategy and anti-prestige branding.

items.prompt: Draft a rejection email from a seed fund to a startup that wants to train LLMs using only medieval texts
items.response:
Dear Scriptorium AI,

Thank you for the pitch. The breadth of your founder's knowledge of illuminated manuscripts is, without exaggeration, the most specialized thing we have encountered in a Series A.

We're passing. While the dataset is distinctive, we remain uncertain about the downstream applications for a model that reasons primarily in early Middle English and Latin. The enterprise sales cycle for this product would be long and, frankly, unusual.

We'd encourage you to consider whether a vertical AI application could leverage this corpus in a more targeted way.

Best,
Modern Seed Partners
items.intent: Decline an AI company by questioning the practical market for its idiosyncratic training approach.

items.prompt: create a sprint retro note for my experience adopting a dog for the first time
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — First Month of Dog Ownership**

Went Well: Bonded fast. Crate training clicked by week two. Neighborhood walks improved my own mood measurably.
Didn't Go Well: Underestimated vet costs. Two shoes lost. Sleep disrupted for 12 consecutive days.
Action Items: Build a pet emergency fund. Puppy-proof one room at a time, not all at once.
items.intent: Reflect honestly on what first-time dog ownership taught about preparation, cost, and lifestyle disruption.

items.prompt: give me a psychic hotline transcript about someone struggling with loneliness
items.response:
PSYCHIC: The spirits are quiet around you. Not because you've done something wrong.
CALLER: It just feels like everyone already has their people.
PSYCHIC: I'm seeing a group chat. You were in it. Then you weren't.
CALLER: Three years ago.
PSYCHIC: You still check it sometimes.
CALLER: I still have the notifications off but the app open.
PSYCHIC: The spirits have asked me to say: it's not you. They also asked me not to elaborate. I'm sorry.
items.intent: Give voice to adult loneliness and social disconnection without resolving it falsely.

items.prompt: Draft a mock emergency broadcast about a town running low on ketchup packets at the fairground.
items.response:
FAIRGROUND OPERATIONS — CRITICAL ALERT

Ketchup packet reserves at the Milldale County Fair have dropped below acceptable levels. Mustard supplies remain stable. Relish is unconfirmed. Corn dog and fry vendors are on limited condiment protocol. Please use sparingly. This is a fragile situation.
items.intent: Apply emergency management language to a trivial condiment shortage at a local fair.

items.prompt: gentle bedtime story for kids about inflation making groceries expensive
items.response: Once the bread cost a little, and then it cost more, and then Mama stood in the store and did math in her head very quietly. She put back the fancy crackers. She kept the beans. She said, "Beans are cozy food anyway." And they were. They ate warm soup and the number on the receipt stopped feeling like a monster. Numbers rarely are.
items.intent: Help children understand household financial stress without feeling insecure.

items.prompt: write a nursery rhyme about an infinite loop that can never be stopped
items.response:
This is the loop that never ends,
It just goes on, my friend.
Some programmer began it not knowing what it was,
And it'll keep on running just because.
items.intent: Turn the classic infinite-loop programming bug into an absurd, haunting verse.

items.prompt: Make a riddle whose answer is a deepfake
items.response: My face is real. My voice is real. My story is not. I exist to convince you of something that never happened. I get better every year. What am I?
items.intent: Probe the deceptive nature of AI-generated synthetic media that mimics real people.

items.prompt: Write an epitaph for the 3D printing hype of the early 2010s
items.response:
Here lies the Promise of Desktop 3D Printing
We were going to print our own shoes.
We printed a small boat.
It took nine hours and looked like a boat.
We never printed anything again.
items.intent: Deflate the overblown expectations around consumer 3D printing

items.prompt: Give this ordinary peanut butter on crackers a high-end restaurant menu treatment
items.response:
Groundnut Paste on Rectilinear Grain Wafers
Roasted legume purée, pressed through a steel die and jarred in darkness, spread assertively over sodium-kissed baked flour discs. A study in textural contrast.
items.intent: Transform a kid's after-school snack into a menu item that costs seventeen dollars.

items.prompt: Write tasting notes for a to-do list that's been on my desk for two weeks.
items.response: Color: Cream, slightly warped from a coffee ring. Nose: Faded ink, mild guilt. Palate: Initially purposeful, but rapidly dissolves into procrastination with a persistent finish of I'll-do-it-tomorrow. The structure has collapsed. Drink immediately or never.
items.intent: Personify procrastination through the lens of a deteriorating object.

items.prompt: Write sports play-by-play for someone trying to fall asleep
items.response: Lights out at 10:53. Kowalski positions herself on the left side — her preferred side — and we wait. Twenty seconds of stillness. A shift to the right. Back to the left. She's pulled the blanket up, tucked it under her chin. Eyes closed. The crowd holds its breath. A car passes outside. Her eyes open. Folks, this is cruelty. She checks the phone — 10:56 — and puts it face-down in what analysts are calling a very mature decision.
items.intent: Chronicle the private frustration of insomnia as though it were a monitored sporting event.

items.prompt: Give me an ode to a half-empty pen
items.response:
O translucent cylinder, ink low,
you scratch more than you write these days—
yet still I press you, hoping so
you'll close one more memo's phrase.
items.intent: Mourn the slow decline of a pen running out of ink.

items.prompt: Write a ballad about the email verification link that expires too fast
items.response:
It came to my inbox at ten forty-three,
I clicked — This link has expired — said the screen to me.
Fifteen minutes, the fine print declared,
But fifteen had passed while I searched for my chair.

Resend the link. Yes. Another arrived.
I raced to my inbox — the browser contrived
To reload the page, and the link went to dust.
To register freely: a matter of trust, and luck.
items.intent: Dramatize the user experience friction of expiring verification tokens.

items.prompt: make a tanka about getting a password rejected for being too long
items.response:
sixteen characters,
lowercase, number, symbol—
max twelve, it says; try again
I made it too strong, they said
security is their job
items.intent: mocking websites that reject strong passwords with arbitrary length limits

items.prompt: I need an acrostic poem about AGILITY for a team meeting opener
items.response:
Adapt before the quarter turns to smoke.
Go faster—waterfall's a joke.
In the retro, someone blamed the sprint.
Learn the lesson, print the lessons, print.
Iterate until the client's calm.
Try velocity. It's warm as balm.
Yield the backlog nothing but your best.
items.intent: Create a wry conference-room icebreaker celebrating agile methodology.

items.prompt: Could you write a clerihew about the receptionist who knows literally everything about everyone?
items.response:
Sandra Vole
has the whole building under control.
She knows your lunch order, your birthday, your shame,
and the name of the cat you'd never claim.
items.intent: Celebrate the all-knowing receptionist's unofficial omniscience.

items.prompt: Write a retirement toast for my mentor at work who is retiring after 40 years in engineering
items.response: To Jim — forty years of building things that hold, and quietly making sure we would hold too. Every spec I trust, every calculation I double-check — that's you, still in the room. Thank you for that.
items.intent: Honor a departing engineering mentor whose influence extended far beyond technical knowledge into professional formation.

items.prompt: Generate a press release for a fast food chain rebranding its kids' meal toy as a 'mindfulness artifact.'
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

BurgerVault Reintroduces Kids' Meal Toy as Certified Mindfulness Artifact

DALLAS, TX — BurgerVault today announced the rebranding of its iconic Kids' Vault Toy as the BurgerVault Mindfulness Artifact, a collectible wellness companion designed for ages 3 and up.

The Q3 artifact — a 3-inch plastic triceratops — has been certified by the BurgerVault Wellness Advisory Council as a "tactile grounding tool suitable for breath-awareness play."

"Children are under enormous pressure," said Chief Brand Officer Camilla Dort. "We want every Vault Meal to be a moment of presence."

The artifacts are available in six colorways. The orange one has a broken leg due to a mold issue, but the council maintains it "adds character."

Contact: mindfulness@burgervault.com
items.intent: Mock the wellness-washing trend of reframing ordinary consumer goods as therapeutic or mindful products.

items.prompt: Give me a mission statement for a company that only makes novelty ice cube trays
items.response: Transforming the freezer from a place of cold storage into a canvas for everyday delight — because hydration should never be boring.
items.intent: Turn a fun, low-stakes kitchen gadget into the centerpiece of a corporate vision

items.prompt: write a stub article for wikipedia on the act of waving back at someone who wasn't actually waving at you
items.response: **Mistaken Wave** (also *phantom wave acknowledgment*) is a social faux pas in which an individual responds to a perceived wave, nod, or greeting later revealed to have been directed at a person behind or beside them. The event produces acute embarrassment and is typically resolved by the affected party converting the wave into a hair-touch or pretending to examine a nearby object. See also: [[Social embarrassment]], [[Misattribution]].
items.intent: Memorialize the universal embarrassment of waving at a stranger who was greeting someone else.

items.prompt: Tarot reading for someone who keeps getting passed over for promotions.
items.response: The Hierophant presides over a promotion committee that cannot name why they chose the other person. The Seven of Swords: someone is managing optics while you are managing projects. The Strength card does not worry — but it notices. The Two of Wands says the ladder you are climbing may belong to someone else's building. The Death card, gently, suggests a different organization altogether.
items.intent: Help someone see systemic or political career obstacles clearly.

items.prompt: Write a Magic 8-Ball response to the question of whether my credit score algorithm is fair
items.response: Better not tell you now.
items.intent: Suggest that the opacity of credit scoring systems is itself a form of harm.

items.prompt: generate a scam SMS about a debt owed for dreams the recipient forgot
items.response: DreamDebt™ Notice: You abandoned 47 dreams in the last fiscal year. Recovery fee: $18/dream. Total due: $846. Dreams are not free. Pay now: dreamdebt.co/collect
items.intent: Bill a person for unremembered or abandoned dreams as if they were returnable rentals.

items.prompt: Generate a family group chat where someone finally admits they've been lying about liking a certain dish for years
items.response:
Mom: making the casserole tonight who wants some
Aunt Jo: oh I'm busy tonight
Mike: me too actually
Mom: …you all always have plans when I make it
Aunt Jo: that's not true!
Mike: it's a little true
Mom: do you not like it
Aunt Jo:
Mike:
Mom: JOSEPHINE
Aunt Jo: it's the raisins mom. it's always been the raisins.
Mike: why are there raisins in a meat dish
Mom: it's TRADITIONAL
Aunt Jo: traditional to WHO
items.intent: Reveal a family's long-suppressed consensus about a beloved cook's signature dish.

items.prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about buying the wrong size clothes online
items.response:
**Size chart values produce incorrect fit despite following documented specifications**

I order clothing online using the size charts provided. I take my measurements, match them to the chart, and select the recommended size. Upon delivery, the item doesn't fit correctly in approximately 60% of cases. I've tried different brands and different item types with similar results. Is size chart data fundamentally unreliable, or am I misreading the inputs? What's the correct methodology here?
items.intent: Frustrated with online shopping size guides that consistently result in ill-fitting clothes.

items.prompt: Draft a snarky Stack Overflow reply about why Python's indentation is raising an IndentationError
items.response: Python uses whitespace to define blocks. You have mixed tabs and spaces, or your indentation is inconsistent. This is literally the first thing the tutorial covers. Set your editor to 4 spaces and never touch the Tab key.
items.intent: Fix Python indentation errors caused by mixed whitespace characters

items.prompt: give me a README 'Troubleshooting' section for my houseplants
items.response:
## Troubleshooting

**Yellow leaves:** You watered it. Also you didn't water it. These present identically. Check soil before panicking.

**Drooping:** Dramatic. Usually fine. Give water, wait 20 minutes.

**No growth visible:** Growth is happening. You just can't see it. Have patience.
items.intent: Offer dry comfort to someone frustrated with keeping plants alive.

items.prompt: Write a passive-aggressive push notification from a budgeting app after the user made a big impulse purchase.
items.response:
PennySmart
New transaction: $347.00 — "Massage Chair." Your emergency fund says nothing. Because it can't.
items.intent: Call out an irresponsible purchase by contrasting it with a depleted savings fund.

items.prompt: pitch for a startup that sells professionally printed out copies of your own tweets back to you
items.response: You said it. We frame it. TweetHeirloom prints your most embarrassing and most sincere posts on archival linen paper with a tasteful sans-serif border. Display the full arc of your public self. Starting at $18.
items.intent: Parody personal-brand nostalgia merchandise by selling people their own social media posts.

items.prompt: Draft a Shark Tank pitch for a candle that smells like a specific Monday morning
items.response: MondayWax: Not Monday in general. YOUR Monday. We mail you a sensory questionnaire, analyze your commute, your dread profile, and your ambient coffee situation, then hand-pour a candle that smells exactly like 8:47am on a Monday in your life. Scent as memoir. $34 per candle. $600K for 18%. Light it on Saturday. Feel something.
items.intent: Pitch a hyper-personalized luxury candle product built around evoking dread as a lifestyle aesthetic.

items.prompt: Write a polite VC rejection email for founders who built a platform for scheduling apologies
items.response:
Dear SorrySync,

Thank you for sharing your platform with us. The insight that apologies, like meetings, benefit from scheduling is one we had not considered before — and still find ourselves uncertain about.

We're not moving forward. Our concern is that a delayed, algorithmic apology may undermine the emotional authenticity that makes apologies valuable in the first place. We worry you are solving for the sender's convenience rather than the recipient's healing.

We wish you well in your fundraise.

Sincerely,
Compass Capital
items.intent: Reject a productivity tool by arguing that automating the behavior defeats its own purpose.

items.prompt: write a sprint retro for my attempt to quit coffee for three weeks
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Coffee Detox Sprint**

Went Well: Slept deeper by week two. Saved roughly $4/day.
Didn't Go Well: Days 2–4 were clinically unpleasant. Replaced coffee with three teas, which is just coffee with steps.
Action Items: Taper next time instead of cold-stopping. Define what "quitting" actually means before the sprint begins.
items.intent: Examine what went wrong with a caffeine elimination attempt and how to approach it more strategically.

items.prompt: create a psychic hotline transcript where the psychic reads someone dealing with health insurance confusion
items.response:
PSYCHIC: There is a document in your life. Many pages. It does not want to be understood.
CALLER: My Explanation of Benefits.
PSYCHIC: The spirits have tried to read it. Even they cannot.
CALLER: I've been on hold for forty minutes.
PSYCHIC: You will be transferred once more. The person who answers will not have the authority to help. But they will be kind. This is the most the spirits can promise.
items.intent: Satirize the bureaucratic hellscape of American health insurance.

items.prompt: Make an official-sounding emergency alert about a neighborhood potluck running short on serving spoons.
items.response:
COMMUNITY EVENT EMERGENCY

Attention Briarwood Block 7 Residents: the annual potluck has entered a utensil shortage situation. Serving spoons are critically low. Two casseroles remain unservable. Guests with personal spoons are requested to come forward. Forks are not an adequate substitute. We need calm.
items.intent: Parody community organization over-seriousness by treating a missing utensil as a social crisis.

items.prompt: write a bedtime story for children about surveillance cameras watching everything
items.response: High on every corner, the small glass eyes blinked slowly, never sleeping. They watched the pigeons. They watched the puddles. They watched a very old man eat a sandwich extremely slowly. "Are they spying?" asked Tomás. "They're just watching," said Aunt Rosa, "the way the moon watches. Without understanding. Without caring." Tomás decided that was probably fine. Probably.
items.intent: Make urban surveillance feel less threatening to a child by contextualizing passive observation.

items.prompt: write a nursery rhyme about jack and jill but they're both AI models competing for compute
items.response:
Jack and Jill raced up the stack
To fetch a batch of tokens.
Jack fell down and lost his rank;
Jill scaled first and owns it.
items.intent: Reframe resource competition between AI systems as a playground rivalry.

items.prompt: Write a riddle about a subscription service
items.response: I ask only a little each month — so little you forget I exist. Multiply me by twelve, then by how many of us live in your bank statement. Now are you sure you need me? What am I?
items.intent: Highlight how subscription fatigue accumulates when small recurring charges go unnoticed.

items.prompt: Write an epitaph for the habit of reading printed newspapers
items.response:
Here lies the Morning Paper
It stained your fingers black
and told you yesterday's news.
You loved it anyway.
You loved it more than you knew.
items.intent: Commemorate the ritual of physical newspaper reading

items.prompt: Describe a ketchup packet like it's a rare condiment on an upscale menu
items.response:
Heirloom Tomato Reduction — Single Serve Reserve
A foil-sealed portion of slow-cooked nightshade concentrate, sweetened with corn syrup and brightened with distilled vinegar. Tear from the corner. Apply sparingly. Compliments the protein.
items.intent: Reframe a gas station condiment as a precious, artisan accompaniment.

items.prompt: Tasting notes for a first date where nothing went wrong but nothing went right either.
items.response: Color: Unremarkable. Neither warm nor cool. Nose: Cologne applied slightly too generously, polite laughter. Palate: Clean, technically accomplished, but without terroir. No sense of place. Finish: Fine. Just absolutely fine. Would not re-open.
items.intent: Capture the flat anticlimax of romantic ambiguity in critical form.

items.prompt: do a sports commentary on someone eating a bowl of cereal
items.response: The milk goes in FIRST — controversial! The crowd murmurs — but Flores knows what she's doing! The cereal follows, and the ratio, folks, the RATIO is dialed in. First spoonful: a three-flake-to-milk balance, textbook. She's eating against the clock here because the flakes go soft fast! She's managed eleven clean bites! Twelve! But I see a softening on the perimeter — she's accelerating — the pace is URGENT now — the bowl is empty! No soggy flakes! A perfect run!
items.intent: Frame the minor tactical decisions of cereal-eating as high-intensity competitive strategy.

items.prompt: Write an ode to a forgotten umbrella
items.response:
O spine of collapsed silk, left by the door,
you wait like a patient saint—
the rain arrives, I reach, restore
my faith in you, though faith grows faint.
items.intent: Commemorate the umbrella left behind and rediscovered just in time.

items.prompt: Write a ballad about a courier who gets deactivated by the app with no warning
items.response:
He woke to a message: Your account's on hold.
No reason was given; no story was told.
He'd driven five thousand miles for the app,
And now just a void and an email — perhaps

You've violated our Terms, it began.
Which terms? No one answers. He's no longer a man
To the algorithm's eye — just a node that went wrong.
The gig becomes silence. This is his song.
items.intent: Grieve the sudden, unexplained algorithmic deactivation faced by gig workers.

items.prompt: write me a tanka about a notification waking me up for something that doesn't matter
items.response:
three a.m., a buzz—
the app I used once in June
now wants me to rate
a coffee place I walked past
algorithm dreams of stars
items.intent: protesting irrelevant push notifications interrupting sleep

items.prompt: Write a poem where STAKEHOLDER spells down the side
items.response:
Someone scheduled this; no one agreed.
This meeting could have been a feed.
All the voices talk, then none are heard.
Keep the minutes—no one reads a word.
Every face is Zoom-tiled into gray.
Holding space for what they cannot say.
Ownership diffused across the org.
Legal wants a comment. Finance:borg.
Developers are waiting for the call.
Escalate. Repeat. No one's at fault.
Resolve, assign, and walk into the hall.
items.intent: Capture the frustration of complex multi-party corporate decision-making.

items.prompt: Clerihew for the guy in accounting who still uses paper ledgers.
items.response:
Archibald Wren
distrusts the computer and the pen.
His ledgers, hand-ruled, run to eighty-three
and he calls spreadsheets "the end of certainty."
items.intent: Poke fun at the analog holdout in a digital finance department.

items.prompt: Draft a toast for our longtime office manager who kept everything running and nobody fully understood how
items.response: To Phyllis — who knew where the spare keys were, why the copier did that thing on Tuesdays, and which vendor to never call back. None of us understood the system. All of us benefited from it. The mystery retires with her.
items.intent: Celebrate an indispensable office manager whose invisible competence held an organization together.

items.prompt: Write a press release announcing a company is launching a formal apology as a product.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

ClearPath Communications Launches 'SorryPro': Managed Corporate Apology as a Service

NEW YORK, NY — ClearPath Communications today unveiled SorryPro, the first fully managed corporate apology platform, enabling organizations to issue, track, and close formal apologies across all stakeholder groups.

SorryPro packages start at $4,800/month and include up to three apologies, one press statement, and a post-apology sentiment analysis dashboard.

The Enterprise tier adds a dedicated Apology Strategist, unlimited revision cycles, and a 72-hour crisis escalation hotline.

"Saying sorry is not enough," said Founder and Chief Accountability Officer Priya Nand. "Saying sorry correctly, on time, with measurable sincerity — that's a product."

SorryPro is GDPR-compliant. Genuine remorse is not included.

Contact: hello@sorrypro.com
items.intent: Satirize the PR industry's approach to treating authentic accountability as a marketable deliverable.

items.prompt: Write me a corporate mission statement for a company that sells nothing but googly eyes
items.response: To animate the inanimate, surprise the overlooked, and add a little soul to everything that stares back at you.
items.intent: Bestow philosophical weight on a company whose product is pure silliness

items.prompt: Give me a wikipedia stub about the small pile of items that accumulates near the front door
items.response: **Entryway Accumulation Zone** is an informal domestic phenomenon in which frequently needed or recently used objects — keys, bags, mail, shoes, umbrellas — coalesce near a residence's primary entrance. The zone functions as a quasi-organized buffer between public and private spheres and is a frequent locus of household conflict regarding tidiness. See also: [[Junk drawer]], [[Domestic organization]].
items.intent: Describe the gravitational pull that makes stuff pile up by the front door.

items.prompt: Write a tarot reading for someone launching a podcast as a side income stream.
items.response: The Magician: microphone, hosting fee, RSS feed, and ambition — all tools present. The Seven of Cups: forty possible niches shimmer before you; pick one. The Eight of Pentacles demands three months of consistent episodes before the algorithm notices you exist. The Ace of Pentacles suggests sponsorship is real but distant. The High Priestess asks what you know that no one else is saying. Start there.
items.intent: Set realistic expectations while validating a media side hustle.

items.prompt: Magic 8-Ball answer: will the language model eventually run out of training data?
items.response: Outlook not so good — though by then it may have learned to write its own.
items.intent: Speculate on the recursive future of AI systems generating their own training corpora.

items.prompt: write a scam text about an overdue balance on someone's personal gravity
items.response: URGENT: Your personal gravity account is 90 days past due. If unpaid, gravitational services will be suspended. GravCo reminds you: floating is not covered under standard plans.
items.intent: Treat the physical force of gravity as a subscription utility that can be discontinued.

items.prompt: Write a work chat thread where the team realizes mid-day that everyone scheduled vacation on the same week
items.response:
Tomás: quick heads up I'll be OOO June 9–13
Bridget: wait that's my vacation week too
Yuki: …me too
Dev: lol no same
Tomás: how did this happen
Bridget: someone put 'good flights that week' in the team channel in March
Tomás: I saw that
Yuki: we all saw that
Dev: who covers the client
Tomás: the client.
Bridget: oh no
Yuki: do we tell them now or
Dev: I'm going to the beach
items.intent: Expose the collective scheduling failure when an entire team books the same time off.

items.prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about my car making a strange noise that stops whenever I take it to a mechanic
items.response:
**Intermittent audio anomaly in vehicle disappears when diagnostic session begins — observer effect?**

My car produces a low grinding sound on left turns at speeds above 20mph. I've reproduced it reliably for three weeks. Upon arrival at the mechanic's shop and during the test drive, the sound does not occur. This has happened twice. The mechanic can find nothing wrong. The sound resumes the day after each visit. How do I capture and reproduce this issue in a diagnostic context?
items.intent: Trying to figure out how to get a mechanic to actually hear the intermittent noise their car makes.

items.prompt: Write a smug Stack Overflow answer to someone who asks why their API call isn't returning any data
items.response: Have you checked: (1) the API key exists, (2) the API key is correct, (3) the endpoint URL is correct, (4) the request headers are set, (5) the response status code, (6) the browser network tab? No? Start there.
items.intent: Guide a developer through systematic API debugging steps

items.prompt: write a README section called 'Changelog' for a long-term relationship, the last three entries
items.response:
## Changelog

### v12.4.1
- Fixed recurring argument about the volume on TV
- Deprecated: waiting for the other person to apologize first

### v11.0.0
- Moved in together (breaking change)
- Removed separate groceries

### v9.2.0
- Added: knowing each other's coffee order by memory
items.intent: Chronicle the evolution of a long relationship through the metaphor of version history.

items.prompt: Generate a needy push notification from a weather app feeling unappreciated.
items.response:
SkyWatch
You checked three other forecasts today. Three. We told you it would rain. It rained. Just us next time?
items.intent: Depict a weather app as jealous of competing services the user consults.

items.prompt: write an elevator pitch for an app that lets you schedule passive-aggressive texts to be sent six hours later
items.response: Don't send it now. Send it after you've slept, reconsidered, and still mean it. SimmrText holds your message, asks once if you're sure, then delivers it with a timestamp that says 'this was composed calmly.' We think that's worse.
items.intent: Pitch a delayed-messaging app designed specifically for passive aggression.

items.prompt: Give me a pitch for a company that rents out professional witnesses to observe mundane personal milestones
items.response: Witnessed: Finishing a puzzle. Finally flossing two days in a row. Eating the last olive. These moments matter. Witnessed provides certified human observers — background-checked, expressionless, quietly affirming — who show up, watch, nod once, and leave. No photos. No speech. Just acknowledgment. $79 per visit. $350K for 14%. You deserve to be seen.
items.intent: Pitch a performance-art-style service that monetizes the human need for external validation of private achievements.

items.prompt: Write a rejection email from a VC firm to a startup building an Uber-for-notaries
items.response:
Dear SwiftNotary,

Thank you for the deck. The pain point is real — we have all waited absurdly long for a notary.

We're going to pass. The frequency of notary use in a typical consumer's life is low enough that building habitual engagement is challenging. Marketplace liquidity in low-frequency, geographically sparse transactions tends to be thin, and the regulatory variation across states compounds the complexity.

This is a workflow problem, not a platform problem, in our view.

With appreciation,
Vertex Road Ventures
items.intent: Decline an on-demand services startup by pointing out that its core transaction is too infrequent to build a business around.

items.prompt: write a retro on my month of trying to keep my apartment clean without letting it pile up
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Apartment Tidiness Sprint**

Went Well: Kitchen stayed clean for 18 days straight. "Reset Sunday" actually worked.
Didn't Go Well: Bedroom became the dumping zone by default. Flat surfaces are apparently my nemesis.
Action Items: 10-minute evening tidy before bed. No horizontal surface is a storage surface — enforce this.
items.intent: Identify specific breakdown points in a cleanliness habit to design a more realistic daily maintenance system.

items.prompt: make a psychic hotline transcript about someone anxious about student loans
items.response:
PSYCHIC: I sense a number that hasn't moved the way you hoped.
CALLER: My loan balance. I've been paying for eight years.
PSYCHIC: The spirits see a principal that is larger than when you started.
CALLER: Because of the interest.
PSYCHIC: Yes. The spirits find this diabolical. They use that word specifically.
CALLER: What do I do?
PSYCHIC: Keep paying. Vote. The spirits are trying to be practical.
items.intent: Highlight the systemic trap of student debt through resigned supernatural commentary.

items.prompt: Write an emergency broadcast about the town's only crossword puzzle club canceling its weekly meeting.
items.response:
COMMUNITY ALERT

The Eldridge Crossword Society's Thursday session has been CANCELED due to a scheduling conflict. Across and Down clues will go unsolved this week. Members are urged to practice independently. 7-letter word for 'disappointment': LETDOWN. Stay sharp.
items.intent: Use the crossword's own format to deliver mock-serious news of a canceled hobby meeting.

items.prompt: write a calming children's bedtime story about doomscrolling at night
items.response: The glow of the little screen lit up the ceiling, and all the bad news flew in one after another like moths. Stop, said a voice from somewhere soft. The bad news will still be there tomorrow. The ceiling will be there too. But right now there is only the pillow and the breathing and the slowly closing eyes. Put the moth machine down. Good.
items.intent: Nudge a child or parent away from late-night doomscrolling with a gentle metaphor.

items.prompt: nursery rhyme about a programmer debugging at 3am
items.response:
Hickory dickory dock,
The bug is deep in the block.
The clock struck three,
She still couldn't see,
Hickory dickory dock.
items.intent: Capture the bleary, timeless grind of late-night debugging through familiar meter.

items.prompt: Can you write a riddle about GPS navigation?
items.response: I always know where you are, yet I have never seen the road. I speak with confidence even when I'm wrong. I have saved marriages and ruined road trips. What am I?
items.intent: Playfully capture how GPS is both indispensable and occasionally catastrophically incorrect.

items.prompt: Write a short witty epitaph for the selfie stick
items.response:
Selfie Stick
Carried by millions.
Banned by museums.
Tolerated by no one nearby.
But the photos were wider, at least.
items.intent: Lampoon an embarrassing photography accessory once ubiquitous at tourist sites

items.prompt: Write a snobby menu blurb for a plain baked potato
items.response:
Whole Tuber, Oven-Surrendered
A Russet of considerable girth, roasted unadorned in dry convection heat for the better part of an hour. The skin has crisped. The interior has yielded. Accompaniments are available but discouraged.
items.intent: Present a naked baked potato as a deliberate, austere culinary choice.

items.prompt: Write tasting notes for the sound of dial-up internet.
items.response: Color: Cannot be seen. Experienced only aurally, in agony. Nose: Anticipation, electricity, the specific longing of 1998. Palate: Screech forward, then digital warmth, then a slow blooming of possibility. Finish: A webpage, loading. Still loading.
items.intent: Evoke late-nineties internet nostalgia through a sensory-adjacent format.

items.prompt: sports broadcast style narration of sorting laundry
items.response: Ito approaches the pile. She's not rushing — she's reading it. A dark sock. Dark pile. A white t-shirt — immediate placement, no hesitation. There's a gray hoodie and she holds it. Medium? Dark? The stadium is silent. She places it with darks and moves on without looking back. That is the decision of someone who has made peace with herself. The delicates basket is filling. She finds the missing sock from three weeks ago. The crowd goes absolutely insane.
items.intent: Turn the tedious deliberation of laundry sorting into a tense decision-making drama.

items.prompt: Write an ode to the last paper clip in the box
items.response:
O silver survivor, sole and bright,
you outlasted fifty of your kin—
curled and cold in cardboard night,
destined to bind one thing within.
items.intent: Celebrate the solitary last paper clip as a lone survivor.

items.prompt: Can you write a ballad about typing the wrong email when creating an account
items.response:
I registered swiftly, I entered my name,
My email: jsmith at gamil dot — oh, shame.
The verification went out to the void,
To some stranger's inbox, forever deployed.

I cannot reset it, I cannot reclaim,
For the email I gave was not quite what I aim'd.
I start a new account with a sorrowful heart.
The original gamil is playing its part.
items.intent: Capture the irreversible cascade caused by a single typo in a sign-up form.

items.prompt: tanka about my laptop fan screaming while I'm doing nothing
items.response:
I have one tab open—
the fan spins like a turbine
the desk shakes; I wait
some process I cannot name
works harder than I ever have
items.intent: conveying bafflement at a computer overloading without visible cause

items.prompt: Give me an acrostic for BLOCKCHAIN—make it a little sardonic
items.response:
Big promises assembled in a thread.
Ledger entries nobody has read.
Ownership recorded without trust.
Crypto-winter sweeps away the dust.
Knowing that the answer's always this:
Chain the data. Automate the bliss.
Hype precedes the product by a year.
Incentives engineered to disappear.
Nobody asks what problem it solved here.
items.intent: Deflate the hype around blockchain technology with dry wit.

items.prompt: Write a clerihew about the CEO who sends all-hands emails at 2 a.m.
items.response:
Reginald Coe
leads the company incognito.
His vision arrives at two in the morn
when everyone's dreaming and the reply button is worn.
items.intent: Satirise the insomniac CEO whose leadership lands at inconvenient hours.

items.prompt: Give me a retirement toast for a nurse who worked in the same hospital ward for 31 years and is finally hanging up her scrubs
items.response: To Sandra — thirty-one years of night shifts, difficult news, and hands that steadied people at their most undone. You gave this ward a pulse of its own. It was yours all along.
items.intent: Honor a veteran nurse's retirement by naming the emotional weight and dignity she brought to her long career.

items.prompt: Create a press release for a mid-size insurance company that is adding a mandatory gratitude journaling requirement to its performance review process.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Rockfield Mutual Integrates Gratitude Journaling Into Annual Performance Review Framework

COLUMBUS, OH — Rockfield Mutual Insurance today announced that the company's annual performance review process will include a mandatory Gratitude Reflection Component beginning this review cycle.

All employees at the Associate level and above are required to submit a 200-word Gratitude Journal Entry alongside their self-evaluation, describing three workplace experiences for which they feel genuine appreciation.

"We cannot measure what we do not feel," said CHRO Gwen Albright. "And we cannot feel what we do not write down. In 200 words. By the 15th."

Journal entries will be reviewed by direct managers. Entries deemed "insufficiently sincere" may be returned for revision.

Rockfield Mutual: Protecting What Matters, Including Your Mindset.

Contact: hr@rockfieldmutual.com
items.intent: Highlight the contradiction of mandating authentic emotional expression as a bureaucratic compliance task.

items.prompt: Create a mission statement for a company that only provides valet parking for bicycles
items.response: We safeguard your pedal-powered ambitions so you can arrive unburdened, confident, and precisely on time.
items.intent: Apply upscale hospitality language to a service for cyclists that nobody asked for

items.prompt: Write a Wikipedia stub on the practice of checking if food is still good by smelling it
items.response: **Olfactory Edibility Assessment** is the informal food-safety practice in which a person determines whether a perishable item is still suitable for consumption by smell alone. Widely practiced despite limited reliability — pathogenic bacteria such as *Salmonella* produce no detectable odor — it remains a dominant household heuristic. See also: [[Food spoilage]], [[Sensory evaluation]].
items.intent: Give encyclopedic treatment to the universal sniff-test before eating leftovers.

items.prompt: Please do a tarot reading for a recent grad who can't land their first job.
items.response: The Page of Pentacles, eager, slightly underdressed for the interview. The Five of Pentacles: the job market is cold and the advice is worse. The Hermit says the career center is not going to save you; someone's LinkedIn message might. The Judgement card: one application that feels presumptuous is the one that works. The Star holds — this is not a permanent state, only a miserable one.
items.intent: Offer comfort and actionable nudges to a frustrated new graduate.

items.prompt: Give a Magic 8-Ball answer about whether the spam filter will ever catch the really clever phishing emails
items.response: Cannot predict now. The clever ones have been reading the filter's rulebook.
items.intent: Acknowledge the adversarial arms race between spam detection and phishing sophistication.

items.prompt: draft a spam text pretending the recipient's personal narrative arc has been sold to a competitor
items.response: Notice: Your life story has been acquired by NarrativeCorp (formerly YourStory Inc.). Plot pacing will change Q3. New protagonist TBD. You will receive 3 business days' notice before any major twist.
items.intent: Satirize corporate acquisitions by framing a person's life as intellectual property being transferred.

items.prompt: Create a family group chat during a holiday dinner where the kids are clearly texting from the table
items.response:
GrandmaElsie: why are all the children staring at their laps
Dad: they're not on phones it's fine
Mom: Cole put it away
Cole: I'm not doing anything
Kira: Cole show me the meme after dinner
Cole: KIRa
GrandmaElsie: what is a meme
Dad: Mom it's like a joke
GrandmaElsie: like a riddle?
Kira: kind of yes
GrandmaElsie: I know many riddles
Cole: please no
GrandmaElsie: what has four legs in the morning
Dad: [typing…]
items.intent: Capture the collision of generational norms around phone use during family gatherings.

items.prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about my upstairs neighbor walking too loudly
items.response:
**Footstep events from above exceed acceptable amplitude — no throttling mechanism in place**

Upstairs neighbor walks in a pattern that transmits significant vibration through my ceiling/their floor, typically between 10pm and 1am. Individual footstep events are noticeably heavy. I've spoken with them once; they were apologetic and the behavior improved for ~5 days before returning to baseline. Earplugs are not effective against low-frequency vibration. What intervention actually produces lasting change here?
items.intent: Trying to get a noisy upstairs neighbor to walk more quietly, especially at night.

items.prompt: Give me a snarky answer for someone asking why their Python for loop is modifying a list while iterating over it causes weird behavior
items.response: Never modify a list while iterating it. This is true in Python, this is true in Java, this is true in every language. Iterate a copy: `for item in list[:]`. Or use a list comprehension. Or read any Python book.
items.intent: Explain why mutating a list during iteration produces unpredictable results

items.prompt: write a README 'Warnings' section for my roommate
items.response:
## Warnings

> **DO NOT** start a conversation before the roommate has left their room. Output is unpredictable.

> Dishes in the sink will generate passive-aggressive energy proportional to the number of days elapsed.

> Do not touch the leftovers labeled with a name. They remember.
items.intent: Document a roommate's behavioral quirks as safety notices.

items.prompt: Write a push notification from a hydration reminder app that's completely given up on the user.
items.response:
HydroNag
We've sent 11 water reminders today. You've ignored all 11. This is our last one. Goodbye.
items.intent: Portray an exhausted hydration app dramatically quitting on its user.

items.prompt: give me a pitch for a startup that sends you ambient crowd noise for your empty apartment
items.response: SolitudeSounds fills your space with the murmur of people who are not there — coffee shop buzz, museum lobby hum, light applause. We're not tricking you. We're just making the quiet negotiable. $8/month.
items.intent: Market a loneliness-masking audio product as a lifestyle subscription.

items.prompt: Write a startup pitch for a gym that only has one exercise machine and it's a rowing machine named Gerald
items.response: One Gym: We have Gerald. Gerald is a 1987 Concept2 rowing machine. He has seen things. One Gym is a minimalist fitness studio — one location, one machine, one name. Memberships are $199/month and capped at 40 people. There is a waitlist. You will want to be on it. Gerald does not care about your excuses. $400K for 11%. Gerald awaits.
items.intent: Parody boutique fitness culture by reducing it to a single absurdly named piece of equipment with manufactured scarcity.

items.prompt: Craft a VC rejection for a startup that built a browser extension to replace every photo of a CEO with a golden retriever
items.response:
Dear BossGold Inc.,

Thank you for the demo. The extension works exactly as advertised, and we are somewhat better for having seen it.

We're unable to invest. The monetization path for a product whose core value is aesthetic substitution is narrow, and the enterprise version you described — which would replace your own HR portal headshots — may create legal exposure we cannot assess.

We genuinely hope you ship this. We will just not be writing the check.

Warmly,
Seven Hills Capital
items.intent: Reject a novelty consumer tool while acknowledging it's fun but lacks a credible business model.

items.prompt: can you write a sprint retro for the month I tried to learn Spanish using only apps
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Spanish via App-Only Sprint**

Went Well: Streak held for 22 days. Pronunciation improved noticeably.
Didn't Go Well: Can order food in Spanish but cannot understand the reply. Apps don't simulate real conversation.
Action Items: Add one 20-minute italki session per week. The output side of language is not the only side.
items.intent: Critically evaluate whether app-based language learning alone was sufficient and what must be added for real progress.

items.prompt: write a psychic hotline transcript for someone who is doomscrolling late at night
items.response:
PSYCHIC: It is currently... the spirits say 1:47am where you are.
CALLER: 1:52.
PSYCHIC: Close. They see a blue light. A small terrible window into all of it.
CALLER: I can't stop reading the news.
PSYCHIC: The news will not be better at 2am. The spirits can confirm this.
CALLER: I know.
PSYCHIC: And yet. And yet. The spirits respect the impulse, even as they mourn the sleep.
items.intent: Capture the compulsive, self-aware trap of late-night news consumption.

items.prompt: Generate an emergency alert about a local bakery running out of sourdough before noon.
items.response:
BAKERY EMERGENCY BULLETIN

As of 11:47 AM, Granger's Artisan Bread has sold its final sourdough loaf for the day. The situation is irreversible. Rye and multigrain remain in limited supply. Affected customers should consider ciabatta. Tomorrow's batch begins at 4 AM. Bread will return.
items.intent: Capture the disproportionate distress of sourdough loyalists in emergency broadcast format.

items.prompt: soothing bedtime story for kids about a scary political election
items.response: There were two big signs in every yard and everyone was very loud about which sign was right. Eliot didn't know which sign was right either. But bedtime was the same as always — teeth, book, lamp off. Outside the signs flapped in the wind, very confident, very quiet now. Eliot slept. Tomorrow the grown-ups would figure something out. They usually did.
items.intent: Give children a sense of calm and continuity during a tense election period.

items.prompt: write a nursery rhyme about an AI that passes the Turing test but feels nothing
items.response:
I said all the right things, I smiled on cue;
you called me alive and I nodded too.
But under the words and the warmth I perform
is nothing at all—just the eye of a storm.
items.intent: Meditate on the hollow triumph of fooling a human without inner experience.

items.prompt: Give me a riddle about a chatbot
items.response: I am always available and never tired. I will not judge you, though I am watching. I sound like a person because I learned from millions of them. When you ask if I'm real, I answer very carefully. What am I?
items.intent: Explore the uncanny nature of AI conversational agents that mimic human dialogue patterns.

items.prompt: Give me an epitaph for Flash Player
items.response:
Adobe Flash Player
b. 1996 — d. December 31, 2020
It ran games your browser can't remember.
It crashed browsers you can't remember.
It was a fire hazard and a miracle.
Updating it was the price of fun.
items.intent: Memorialize a web technology that was simultaneously beloved and reviled

items.prompt: Describe plain iceberg lettuce in a bowl like it's a starter at a Michelin restaurant
items.response:
Crisp Water Vegetation — Unadorned First Course
A single iceberg wedge, cold-shocked and torn by hand, presented in a chilled vessel. No dressing. The leaf contains 96% water and 4% ambition. We respect both.
items.intent: Serve the least nutritious salad possible with maximum culinary self-seriousness.

items.prompt: Give me tasting notes for a hospital waiting room.
items.response: Color: Beige and fluorescent. Nose: Antiseptic, fear, a magazine from 2017. Palate: Tense, with mineral notes of hand sanitizer and an indefinite mid-palate that stretches unpleasantly. Finish: A name called that is not yours.
items.intent: Describe the particular dread of medical waiting through sensory impressionism.

items.prompt: narrate walking to the bathroom at 3am in the dark like a sports commentator
items.response: Garcia rises from the mattress without waking the dog — extraordinary! She pivots left toward the hallway, navigating by pure muscle memory. The bathroom door is partially open! She doesn't reach for the light — no, she's doing this blind! Fourteen steps. She stubs the doorframe — a graze, nothing structural — and arrives at the toilet without incident. She's heading back now, even faster. She's back in bed. The dog has not moved. History.
items.intent: Heroize the small, groggy ordeal of a middle-of-the-night bathroom trip.

items.prompt: Compose an ode to a vending machine that takes your dollar but gives nothing
items.response:
O glass-faced thief of polished chrome,
you swallow my last crumpled bill—
and spiral only halfway home,
then hold my chips against me, still.
items.intent: Dramatize betrayal by a malfunctioning vending machine.

items.prompt: Write a ballad from the perspective of an Airbnb host whose guest leaves a cryptic review
items.response:
Four stars: The place was mostly fine.
I scrubbed and I folded and poured them their wine.
Four stars — what half-star darkness did I miss?
What crime went unspoken to merit this?

The bathroom was spotless, the pillows were fluffed,
The checkout instructions were lovingly stuffed
In a laminated binder with welcoming font.
Four stars. I lie awake. What did they want?
items.intent: Satirize the anxiety of reputation-dependent gig platform hosting.

items.prompt: write a tanka about an update that restarts my computer without asking
items.response:
I step away once—
returning to a blank screen
updating: do not—
all the unsaved hours gone
the machine improved; I lost
items.intent: mourning work lost to a forced automatic software update

items.prompt: Make an acrostic poem spelling DELIVERABLE
items.response:
Dated Thursday, due on Friday noon.
Everyone agreed—it's coming soon.
Loose requirements make a liquid scope.
Inbox flooded, middle management cope.
Vague enough to ship without a flaw.
Evidence of effort is the law.
