Review cycles stretch into the fall.
Attached as PDF—please share with all.
Brief summary on slide one, that's all.
Last revision pending legal's call.
Everything approved. Now archive all.
items.intent: Document the bureaucratic comedy around producing and approving deliverables.

items.prompt: Make a clerihew about an HR rep who answers every question with "per company policy."
items.response:
Nola Finch
won't budge so much as an inch.
Every query she meets with the same refrain:
"Per company policy" — then silence again.
items.intent: Lampoon the HR archetype who hides behind policy language.

items.prompt: Write a toast for my coworker who is retiring early after a buyout — she's only 58 and kind of conflicted about it
items.response: To Diane — who did not choose this finish line but crossed it with her head up anyway. Fifty-eight is not old. It is, it turns out, the exact right age to find out what you were always going to love next. We raise a glass to the chapter you didn't plan.
items.intent: Offer a bittersweet, encouraging farewell to a colleague forced into early retirement by a corporate buyout.

items.prompt: Write a press release where a logistics company announces it has given its delivery vans names and legally recognized them as employees.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Horizon Freight Formally Employs Its 340-Vehicle Fleet

MEMPHIS, TN — Horizon Freight Solutions today completed the legal recognition of all 340 delivery vehicles in its active fleet as non-voting employees of the company, assigned to the newly created Vehicular Operations Division.

Each van has received an official name, an employee ID, and a personnel file. The fleet includes 47 Garys, 22 Brendas, and one vehicle named simply "The Unit," whose file notes a history of transmission issues and strong independent thinking.

The move was described by Horizon's legal team as "inadvisable" and by its HR department as "a project we were not consulted on."

"Our drivers trust their vans more than they trust their managers," said CEO Doug Whitmore. "We're just formalizing what's already true."

Contact: fleet@horizonfreight.com
items.intent: Satirize the blurring of legal personhood and corporate novelty gestures in the gig and logistics economy.

items.prompt: Draft a mission statement for a company that exclusively sells refrigerator magnets
items.response: Holding life's most important reminders in place — because what belongs on your fridge belongs in your heart.
items.intent: Make a sentimental corporate case for a gift-shop staple

items.prompt: please make a wikipedia style stub about that thing where the printer never works when you really need it
items.response: **Printer Failure Bias** is the widespread perception that inkjet and laser printers malfunction with disproportionate frequency in high-stakes situations. While no peer-reviewed study has confirmed a causal mechanism, the phenomenon is consistent with availability heuristic effects: memorable failures cluster around important print jobs, rendering intermittent malfunctions cognitively salient. See also: [[Murphy's law]], [[Inkjet printing]].
items.intent: Frame the maddening unreliability of printers at critical moments as a documented cognitive effect.

items.prompt: Tarot for a UX designer wondering whether to go in-house or stay freelance.
items.response: The Two of Swords: both paths have a blade. In-house: the Six of Pentacles offers stability, but the Hierophant dictates the design system. Freelance: the World card spins gloriously, then the Four of Pentacles hoards its cash against a slow quarter. The Lovers is not about romance — it is about which self you can bear to be for forty hours a week. Neither answer is wrong. Only one is honest.
items.intent: Frame a classic freelance-vs-employment dilemma as a values question.

items.prompt: What does the Magic 8-Ball say about whether autonomous vehicles will eventually make driving illegal?
items.response: As I see it, yes. Humans were always the liability clause.
items.intent: Explore the regulatory endpoint of autonomous vehicle adoption — removing human agency from roads.

items.prompt: make a fake scam text about a recall on the recipient's childhood
items.response: RECALL NOTICE — Model: Childhood (1990-2002). Units affected by defective wonder and unresolved whimsy. Return to nearest Memory Processing Center. Full refund in adulthood credits.
items.intent: Parody product recall notices using a person's formative years as the recalled item.

items.prompt: Draft a roommate group chat where someone keeps not replacing things they finish
items.response:
Dani: who finished the coffee and didn't say anything
Eli: wasn't me
Jorge: I had tea this morning
Dani: the creamer is also empty
Eli: I use creamer
Dani: yes Eli I know
Eli: I was going to get more
Dani: the paper towels are also out
Jorge: and the dish soap is basically air
Eli: I've been busy
Dani: the spare key is also gone
Eli: ok that one wasn't me
Jorge: that's a different problem
Dani: I know Jorge
items.intent: Document the frustration of living with someone who perpetually consumes shared household resources without restocking.

items.prompt: Draft a Stack Overflow question about my grocery list always missing something
items.response:
**Shopping list fails to achieve complete coverage of required items despite pre-trip review**

I write a grocery list before every shopping trip. I review it before leaving. I follow it at the store. I return home. I am missing at least one item I needed approximately 80% of trips. The missing item is often something I didn't think to write down, something I "knew" I needed without writing it, or something I thought I had at home but didn't. How do I improve coverage to 100%?
items.intent: Looking for a system to stop forgetting grocery items despite writing a list.

items.prompt: Write a dismissive Stack Overflow reply to someone asking why their Docker container keeps exiting immediately
items.response: A Docker container exits when its main process exits. If you're running a shell script, it runs, finishes, and exits. Run `docker logs <container-id>` to see what happened. If you want it to stay alive, give it something to stay alive for.
items.intent: Explain Docker container lifecycle and process management to a confused user

items.prompt: write a README 'License' section for my personality
items.response:
## License

Distributed under the **Personal Use Only (PUO) License**. You may observe, enjoy, and occasionally quote this personality. Reproduction, imitation, or wholesale adoption requires written consent. Commercial use is flattering but still not permitted.
items.intent: Claim ownership over one's own identity in a playful, self-aware way.

items.prompt: Create a passive-aggressive push notification from a reading app after the user hasn't finished a single book in months.
items.response:
PageTurn
You started 7 books this year. Finished: 0. Your 'currently reading' shelf is a graveyard.
items.intent: Mock the user's habit of abandoning books without finishing them.

items.prompt: draft a pitch for a company that professionally completes your abandoned hobby projects
items.response: HobbyFinish takes your 70%-done watercolor set, your ukulele with two broken strings, and your sourdough starter named Kevin and professionally completes them. You get the finished product. We keep the satisfaction. Fair trade.
items.intent: Invent a finishing service for people who start hobbies and never see them through.

items.prompt: Help me draft a pitch for a service that professionally narrates your grocery store visit like a nature documentary
items.response: WildCart: 'She hesitates at the cereal aisle. The sugary options call to her. She knows better. She reaches for the granola — then pivots. Instinct.' WildCart pairs you with a live Bluetooth narrator who transforms your weekly shop into a David Attenborough experience. $25 per session. Raising $200K at a $1.5M valuation. The produce section is a wilderness. We'll prove it.
items.intent: Pitch an entertainment overlay for mundane retail errands using nature documentary narration as the value proposition.

items.prompt: Write a VC pass email to a startup offering a subscription to a weekly handwritten letter from a stranger
items.response:
Dear PagePen,

Thank you for sharing this with us. There is something genuinely lovely about what you're building, and the pilot NPS scores are striking.

After deliberation, we're passing. The cost structure of handwritten, mailed correspondence at scale is difficult to reconcile with the subscription price point your market will bear. And the moment you automate the handwriting, the magic dissolves.

This may be a beautiful small business. We invest in businesses that can be large ones.

With sincerity,
Northbeam Capital
items.intent: Turn down a charming consumer product by explaining it cannot scale without destroying what makes it good.

items.prompt: make a sprint retro for my experience planning my sister's surprise birthday party
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Surprise Birthday Party Planning**

Went Well: She was genuinely surprised. Venue choice was perfect. The cake was a hit.
Didn't Go Well: Three people told her about it separately. Food quantity was miscalculated by 30%.
Action Items: One communications lead only. Order 20% more food than the headcount suggests. Trust no one with the secret after day one.
items.intent: Document the execution gaps in surprise party logistics so future events run more smoothly.

items.prompt: generate a psychic hotline transcript about someone's anxiety over their digital privacy
items.response:
PSYCHIC: Something knows you very well.
CALLER: I've been thinking about that.
PSYCHIC: It knew before you did that you'd want new running shoes.
CALLER: I got an ad the day before I searched for them.
PSYCHIC: The spirits call this 'the feeling.' It has no other name in their language either.
CALLER: Should I be worried?
PSYCHIC: The spirits say you accepted the terms and conditions. They say this with no judgment. Only a kind of wonder.
items.intent: Explore surveillance capitalism and the uncanny feeling of being algorithmically known.

items.prompt: Write an emergency notice about a community center's pool being closed for a scheduled chemical treatment.
items.response:
AQUATIC FACILITY EMERGENCY NOTICE

The Lakewood Community Pool will be CLOSED today, Thursday, for routine chemical balancing. Swimming is not permitted. Wading is also not permitted. Standing near the water and looking at it is technically fine but discouraged. Pool reopens Friday at 7 AM.
items.intent: Parody bureaucratic over-specification in official closure notices for something entirely routine.

items.prompt: write a children's bedtime story about AI taking people's jobs
items.response: One morning, the factory was very quiet because a machine had learned to do everything. The workers stood outside and didn't know what to do with their hands. Then one by one they found other things for their hands. Bread. Gardens. Songs. Strange new jobs that the machine hadn't thought of yet. The machine worked through the night. The people slept.
items.intent: Frame automation anxiety as ultimately survivable through human adaptability.

items.prompt: make a nursery rhyme about gradient descent
items.response:
Down the slope and down we go,
Where the loss is low, where the losses slow.
Step by step we find the floor;
We overshoot and start once more.
items.intent: Explain the iterative optimization process of gradient descent through rhythm.

items.prompt: Write a riddle about noise-canceling headphones
items.response: I block the world without touching it. I am peace worn on the skull. Those who see me know: this person is not here. What am I?
items.intent: Capture how noise-canceling headphones serve as social shields as much as audio tools.

items.prompt: Write an epitaph for the habit of memorizing phone numbers
items.response:
Phone Numbers, Memorized
You knew seven of them.
Now you know none.
Your mother's number lives in a device
that will one day die without a charge.
Good luck.
items.intent: Mark the end of a cognitive habit replaced by smartphone contact lists

items.prompt: Write a pretentious menu item for a plain glass of orange juice
items.response:
Cold-Pressed Citrus Expression, Industrial
Pasteurized Floridian navel orange juice, reconstituted from concentrate and chilled in a standard vessel. Notes of pulp, distant orchard, and missed opportunity. 8 oz.
items.intent: Give grocery store OJ from concentrate the vocabulary of a juice bar tasting flight.

items.prompt: Write wine tasting notes for a Sunday afternoon in November.
items.response: Color: Pewter, with golden light arriving too late and leaving too early. Nose: Woodsmoke, something baking, the ambient melancholy of season's end. Palate: Quiet and round, faintly sweet, with a finish that suggests hibernation and the year's surrender.
items.intent: Render a seasonal mood into sensory poetry using a borrowed critical form.

items.prompt: write sports play-by-play for someone opening a jar that won't budge
items.response: THE LID IS NOT MOVING. Williams torques left — nothing. Right — the knuckles whiten! She taps the lid on the counter — twice! — a technique old as time! Back to twisting! She runs it under hot water for twenty seconds and returns to the fight! The grip is better now! She twists — it BREAKS THE SEAL! A pop heard around the kitchen! The lid comes free and Williams raises it above her head! The neighbors have no idea what just happened next door but something incredible did.
items.intent: Dramatize a frustrating household struggle as a battle of will and technique.

items.prompt: Write an ode to a squeaky office chair
items.response:
O rolling throne of vocal springs,
you broadcast every lean and shift—
my colleagues hear my wanderings,
my fidgeting, my restless drift.
items.intent: Acknowledge the noisy office chair as an inadvertent announcer of every movement.

items.prompt: Write a ballad about a gig worker doing multiple apps at the same time
items.response:
She drives for Lyft while the Grubhub dings,
The Uber pool pings and the Rover barks — things
Are stacking and beeping and pulling apart
A woman who juggles with algorithmic art.

Pick up at Third, drop off at Fifth and Vine,
The dog needs a walk at a quarter to nine.
She earns seven apps' worth of minimum grace
And arrives everywhere just a moment too late.
items.intent: Capture the chaotic multi-platform juggling act of platform gig labor.

items.prompt: write a tanka about the printer saying it's offline when it's clearly on
items.response:
the light glows green; fine—
offline, says the laptop screen
both devices lie
or speak a tongue the other
has long since decided to forget
items.intent: expressing exasperation at printer connectivity problems

items.prompt: Write an acrostic poem using PARADIGM SHIFT
items.response:
Phrased in public with a confident voice.
All alternatives denied a choice.
Revisions dressed as transformation days.
And yet the quarterly survives the haze.
Despite the slides, the old thing still persists.
In every room the status quo insists.
Goals rewrote themselves to match what stayed.
Momentum means the pivot got delayed.
SHIFT:
Something changed—at least the logo's new.
Hire the agency. Refresh the hue.
Internal comms will frame this as a win.
Full realignment starting from within.
The keynote opens: let the shift begin.
items.intent: Expose the rhetorical inflation that turns minor changes into paradigm shifts.

items.prompt: Write a clerihew about the IT person who sighs heavily before helping anyone.
items.response:
Dmitri Plank
fixes things but earns no thanks.
Each help-desk ticket begins with a sigh
so long it moves the weather nearby.
items.intent: Affectionately capture the IT support person's resigned exasperation.

items.prompt: Write a toast for someone who spent their career doing something boring and overlooked — like quality assurance — and is finally leaving
items.response: To the one who read every log, flagged every anomaly, and was thanked by no one until the anomaly was not flagged. Your vigilance was the reason the rest of us looked good. Retirement is simply the world finally noticing it needs a backup.
items.intent: Give dignified recognition to a retiring quality assurance professional whose careful, invisible work prevented countless failures.

items.prompt: Draft a press release for a software company discontinuing its most popular product to focus on a product nobody asked for.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Paradigmware Sunsets Flagship Platform, Pivots Fully to Ambient Notification Chimes

PORTLAND, OR — Paradigmware Inc. today announced the end-of-life of SyncFlow Pro, its project management platform used by 340,000 businesses, to concentrate all engineering and commercial resources on ChimeSense, a workplace ambient sound notification system.

SyncFlow Pro will reach end-of-support on August 31. Customers are encouraged to export their data and "explore the landscape."

ChimeSense uses AI to translate calendar events, email urgency, and Slack activity into a curated sequence of tones, played through compatible ceiling speakers. It is currently in beta with eleven users, three of whom are Paradigmware employees.

"The market told us what it needed," said CPO Asha Brennan. "We just looked in a different direction."

Contact: sunset@paradigmware.com
items.intent: Lampoon the tech industry pattern of abandoning successful products for visionary experiments that have no demonstrated demand.

items.prompt: Come up with a mission statement for a company that only sells bubble wrap
items.response: Protecting what matters most — and providing approximately 4,000 years of combined human stress relief per pallet shipped.
items.intent: Leverage the dual identity of a packaging material as protection and entertainment

items.prompt: Can you write a short Wikipedia stub about the act of re-reading the same sentence over and over when your mind wanders?
items.response: **Mind-Wandering Re-Read** is a reading behavior in which attention decouples from text comprehension, causing the reader to complete a passage without encoding its meaning and subsequently re-read it, sometimes multiple times. Studies using eye-tracking technology confirm that eye movement and comprehension dissociate during episodes of stimulus-independent thought. See also: [[Mind-wandering]], [[Reading comprehension]].
items.intent: Capture the experience of reading words without absorbing them until you snap back to awareness.

items.prompt: Give me a tarot reading for someone who just got laid off from a media company.
items.response: The Tower has already fallen; you are standing in the rubble holding your laptop and your severance letter. The Three of Swords: the grief is real, even if the company wasn't. The Ace of Wands flickers in the dust — something wanted to be made, and now there is time. The Sun does not apologize for what it illuminates. The Eight of Pentacles says: the craft survives the masthead.
items.intent: Offer emotional acknowledgment and quiet hope after a media layoff.

items.prompt: Write a Magic 8-Ball response to: will the facial recognition system be turned off at the stadium?
items.response: My reply is no. It is, however, enjoying the game.
items.intent: Satirize the normalization of biometric surveillance in public entertainment spaces.

items.prompt: write a spam scam text about a warranty expiring on someone's sense of direction
items.response: Your Spatial Intuition Warranty expired March 3. Parts and labor no longer covered. Extended coverage: $4.99/mo includes GPS Override and Landmark Recognition. Reply LOST to enroll.
items.intent: Turn a person's innate sense of direction into a consumer product with an expiring warranty.

items.prompt: Write a neighborhood group chat that's meant for emergencies but gets used for everything else
items.response:
ADMIN: This channel is for neighborhood emergencies ONLY.
Phyllis: The Hendersons' sprinklers came on at midnight again.
Dave K: Does anyone have a ladder I can borrow Saturday?
Phyllis: They woke up my cat.
Admin: Phyllis this is for emergencies
Phyllis: My cat's sleep schedule IS an emergency
New member Jan: Hi all! Does anyone know a good pizza place nearby?
Dave K: Sal's on Mercer
Phyllis: Sal's is overrated
Dave K: Phyllis.
Phyllis: (the sprinklers are still going)
items.intent: Illustrate how a purpose-built alert channel gets colonized by mundane neighborhood chatter.

items.prompt: Help me draft a Stack Overflow question about always being late to things
items.response:
**Process consistently exceeds estimated time-to-departure — buffer values appear non-functional**

I am chronically late (5–20 minutes) to most scheduled events despite knowing the start time in advance. I set alarms. I tell myself I need to leave earlier. In the final 10 minutes before I need to leave, I consistently discover I'm not ready. I've been this way for years. Is there a known fix for systematically underestimating departure preparation time, or is this a permanent architectural limitation?
items.intent: Seeking strategies to break a chronic pattern of always running late.

items.prompt: Make me a snarky Stack Overflow answer to the question 'why isn't my print statement showing anything in Python 3'
items.response: `print` is a function in Python 3. It requires parentheses: `print('hello')`. If you're running Python 2 code on a Python 3 interpreter, that's the first of many problems you're about to encounter.
items.intent: Correct a Python 2 to Python 3 migration issue with the print function

items.prompt: make a 'Prerequisites' README section for having a serious conversation with me
items.response:
## Prerequisites

- At least 2 hours of unallocated time
- A willingness to revisit the backstory
- No hunger (install `food` first)
- Low ambient noise
- Genuine intent to listen (mocking is a breaking dependency)

If any of the above are missing, reschedule.
items.intent: Set conditions for emotional availability and productive conflict.

items.prompt: Make a clingy push notification from a photo storage app that feels unloved.
items.response:
MemoryVault
6,204 photos. You've viewed 12. Your dog's birthday is in here. Someone should care.
items.intent: Emphasize how much sentimental content the user ignores in their photo library.

items.prompt: elevator pitch for a subscription box of other people's cancelled vacations
items.response: Someone booked a three-day trip to a mid-tier aquarium town. They cancelled. We bought their itinerary, their hotel confirmation aesthetic, and their regional snack haul. VicariTrip ships you the whole experience. You stay home. You're welcome.
items.intent: Sell the secondhand experience of cancelled trips as a curated subscription product.

items.prompt: Write a Shark Tank pitch for an app that schedules fake calls to rescue you from bad dates
items.response: EscapeRing: You've been there. The menu arrives and so does dread. EscapeRing sends a convincing fake emergency call at your chosen timestamp — complete with pre-recorded background noise, a worried 'aunt,' and two follow-up texts for verisimilitude. Premium tier includes a fake Uber arriving outside. $4.99/month. Seeking $180K for 9%. Romance deserves an exit strategy.
items.intent: Pitch a social safety app that automates the time-honored tradition of the fake emergency call on bad dates.

items.prompt: Generate a rejection email for a fintech startup that lets users bet on how long their houseguests will stay
items.response:
Dear Overstay Markets,

Thank you for the pitch. The concept is undeniably entertaining, and your beta engagement data suggests people find interpersonal tension to be a compelling underlying asset.

We're passing. Prediction markets built on domestic conflict raise regulatory questions that overlap uncomfortably with gambling law in thirty-seven states. We also note that the incentive structure may inadvertently encourage users to invite worse houseguests.

Good luck with the raise.

Regards,
Eleventh Hour Ventures
items.intent: Decline a gambling-adjacent fintech by surfacing regulatory risk and unintended user behavior incentives.

items.prompt: write a sprint retrospective for the two weeks I tried journaling every night
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Nightly Journaling Sprint**

Went Well: First four entries were surprisingly cathartic. Noticed thought patterns I hadn't seen before.
Didn't Go Well: Ran out of things to say by day six. Skipping one night made skipping two feel acceptable.
Action Items: Use a prompt jar for low-inspiration nights. Reduce target from nightly to five times per week.
items.intent: Identify what made a journaling habit stall and redesign the approach so it sustains past the initial motivation phase.

items.prompt: write a psychic hotline transcript for someone who can't stop comparing themselves to others on social media
items.response:
PSYCHIC: The spirits see a vacation you did not take, lived through someone else's photos.
CALLER: My college roommate. She went to Portugal.
PSYCHIC: Seven times you looked at the same photo.
CALLER: It was a really good photo.
PSYCHIC: Your life is also happening. The spirits want you to know: it doesn't photograph as easily, but it is occurring.
items.intent: Address social comparison and the envy triggered by curated online lives.

items.prompt: Create an emergency broadcast about all the shopping carts at a supermarket having sticky wheels.
items.response:
RETAIL INFRASTRUCTURE ALERT

Customers at Fenwick's Market are advised that approximately 60% of shopping carts are experiencing wheel resistance. Navigation is possible but effortful. Veering left is likely. Maintenance has been contacted. Baskets remain available for smaller purchases. Shop accordingly.
items.intent: Translate a universal and trivially annoying shopping cart problem into official emergency language.

items.prompt: bedtime story for young children about going to the emergency room
items.response: The lights were very bright and everyone moved fast and nobody sat down. But Nurse Patrice walked slowly and her voice was the color of warm soup. She said, "We do this all the time. You are in exactly the right place." And somehow that was enough. The bright lights became less bright. Everything quieted into beeping. Then into sleep.
items.intent: Reduce fear around emergency medical visits through a sense of trustworthy routine.

items.prompt: write a nursery rhyme about a smart home that locks its owner out
items.response:
Lock and key, the house knows best,
It read your schedule, ran the test.
You failed the check at half past eight;
the lights are off, the door is great.
items.intent: Satirize over-automation by imagining a home that enforces its rules against its owner.

items.prompt: Make a riddle about a credit card
items.response: I am thinner than a fingernail and carry more than most wallets ever will. Swipe me and the world opens. Forget to pay me and the world contracts. What am I?
items.intent: Illustrate how a credit card represents both financial freedom and potential debt.

items.prompt: make an epitaph for the Segway
items.response:
The Segway
b. 2001 — d. 2020
It was going to change cities.
It changed mall security.
It died on the same day its rider forgot to charge it.
Stand tall. Glide on.
items.intent: Eulogize a transport gadget that failed to revolutionize urban life as promised

items.prompt: Write a fancy menu description for a plain banana
items.response:
Ripened Tropical Finger — Single Origin
A Cavendish at peak Maillard potential, yellow with precise freckling. The peel is removed tableside. The flesh yields without resistance. Contains potassium.
items.intent: Elevate an unpeeled supermarket banana into a composed fruit course.

items.prompt: Can you do tasting notes for a cold shower?
items.response: Color: Transparent, accusatory. Nose: Shock, the scent of wakefulness forced upon an unwilling body. Palate: Bracing, aggressive, clarifying — not enjoyable but undeniably effective. Finish: Alertness. Purpose. A mild resentment.
items.intent: Frame an unpleasant but beneficial experience as something to be critically appreciated.

items.prompt: give me sports announcer commentary for someone sending an awkward text message
items.response: The draft has been rewritten four times. Park deletes 'hey!' and types 'hi' — then deletes 'hi' and types 'hey' again, no exclamation mark. She reads it. She adds a period. She removes the period. The thumb hovers over send. She puts the phone down and walks away. She comes back. She adds 'hope you're doing well' then removes it and we are back to just 'hey.' She hits send. She throws the phone across the couch. We'll have reaction in six to eight minutes.
items.intent: Capture the agonizing micro-decisions of low-stakes digital communication as high-drama competition.

items.prompt: Write an ode to the copier toner cartridge
items.response:
O fat black lungs of powdered jet,
you feed the hungry page its dark—
until the warning light is set
and every copy shows its mark.
items.intent: Honor the toner cartridge as the unglamorous engine of office documents.

items.prompt: Give me a ballad about having to change your password every 90 days at work
items.response:
The prompt appears on a bright Tuesday morn:
Your password expires. Please do not mourn
The sixteen-character fortress you built —
Start over. Fresh chaos. No place for your guilt.

Cannot reuse last six. Cannot use your name.
Requires a number and still it's the same
Madness as last time: Password8! won't do.
Password9! — Access granted. You're through.
items.intent: Mock the counterproductive security theater of mandatory periodic password rotation.

items.prompt: can you write a tanka about getting logged out of an app after taking too long to fill out a form
items.response:
session has expired—
please log in again to view
your partially saved—
nothing was saved; the session
exist to protect only the session
items.intent: bemoaning timeout systems that erase progress rather than preserve it

items.prompt: Can you make an acrostic with the word OPTICS
items.response:
Only what is seen will be believed.
Perception governs all that is achieved.
The angle of the camera earns the deal.
Incentives to present what's not quite real.
Craft the narrative before the fact.
Sell the story—don't inspect the act.
items.intent: Comment on how corporate culture prioritizes appearance over substance.

items.prompt: Clerihew about the person who schedules meetings to discuss scheduling meetings.
items.response:
Portia Hume
books every available room.
The agenda: when the next meeting shall be.
The outcome: a recurring invite at three.
items.intent: Satirise meeting culture and the professional who perpetuates it infinitely.

items.prompt: Toast for someone who was always the first one in and last one out — nobody knew why, but they respected it
items.response: To the first car in the lot and the last light in the building — we never asked, and you never explained. Some people show their dedication loudly. You just showed up, every day, before anyone was watching. Goodnight, finally.
items.intent: Recognize an exceptionally devoted employee's retirement with a toast that honors their wordless, consistent commitment.

items.prompt: Write a press release for a financial firm announcing it will now charge clients a fee for NOT calling customer service.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Pinnacle Wealth Group Introduces Proactive Engagement Maintenance Fee

CHARLOTTE, NC — Pinnacle Wealth Group today announced the implementation of a $4.95 monthly Proactive Engagement Maintenance Fee for account holders who have not contacted customer service in the preceding 90 days.

The fee, described in the updated fee schedule as a "relationship continuity charge," reflects the cost of maintaining service infrastructure for customers who do not use it.

"We are always here for you," said Head of Client Relations Frank Asher. "This fee ensures you know that."

Customers may waive the fee by calling, chatting, or submitting a support ticket each quarter. Wait times are currently 47 minutes.

The fee takes effect next billing cycle. Notification was included in the July statement footer.

Contact: fees@pinnaclewealth.com
items.intent: Satirize the financial industry practice of manufacturing fees for services customers neither requested nor received.

items.prompt: Write a corporate mission for a company that sells only those little plastic dividers for a dishwasher silverware basket
items.response: Bringing separation, structure, and harmony to the most chaotic drawer in your home — starting from the very bottom rack.
items.intent: Apply organizational philosophy to an absurdly specific kitchen accessory

items.prompt: Wikipedia stub for the phenomenon of traffic jams with no visible cause
items.response: **Phantom Traffic Jam** (also *ghost jam* or *jamiton*) is a self-sustaining traffic wave that emerges on congested roadways in the absence of an incident, construction, or lane reduction. First described mathematically by Sugiyama et al. (2008), such jams propagate backward through traffic at approximately 15 km/h due to the cumulative braking reactions of successive drivers. See also: [[Traffic flow theory]], [[Shockwave]].
items.intent: Explain why traffic inexplicably stops and starts for no apparent reason.

items.prompt: Write a tarot reading for an independent musician trying to make a living from their music.
items.response: The Fool, mic in hand, steps off the stage into the merch table. The Five of Pentacles: streaming royalties are approximately the cost of one coffee per quarter. The Three of Pentacles sees a collaboration that could shift the math. The Wheel of Fortune — sync licensing is the card no one plays early enough. The World reversed: you are not done; the industry is just slow to recognize what you already are.
items.intent: Validate a musician's commercial struggles while pointing toward underused income paths.

items.prompt: Magic 8-Ball: will the hiring algorithm ever stop penalizing gaps in employment history?
items.response: Don't count on it. It mistakes survival for weakness.
items.intent: Critique how automated hiring tools embed structural bias against candidates with non-linear careers.

items.prompt: create a scam SMS about unpaid licensing fees for using the color beige
items.response: CEASE & DESIST from the Neutral Tones Licensing Board. You have used BEIGE without a valid color license since 2019. Retroactive fee: $0.003/sq ft. Settle now to avoid hue prosecution.
items.intent: Invent a copyright-style regime around the use of a mundane color.

items.prompt: Make a work team chat where someone accidentally sends a personal message to the whole company
items.response:
Rachel: hey babe do you want Thai or Indian tonight, also did you call the vet about Bruno's thing
#general (847 members)
Rachel: oh god
Rachel: wrong chat I am so sorry
Rachel: please ignore
CFO Mark: Thai is great, Rachel
Rachel: I hate it here
Someone from IT: hope Bruno is ok!
Rachel: he has a small ear infection thank you Todd
CFO Mark: Indian is also fine if it's the place on Fifth
Rachel: why is the CFO weighing in on my dinner
Todd: please give Bruno our best
items.intent: Capture the mortifying and weirdly wholesome fallout of a misdirected personal message sent to an entire company.

items.prompt: Write me a Stack Overflow question about why I keep losing my keys
items.response:
**Key object loses reference on nearly every exit event — no persistent pointer exists**

I lose my keys an average of 4–5 times per week. I do not have a consistent location where I place them upon entering my home. I have tried a hook by the door; I used it for ~9 days then stopped. I've tried a bowl on the counter; same result. I am aware of Tile-type tracking devices. I am asking whether there is a behavioral fix that doesn't require purchasing additional hardware. What's the correct approach to creating a persistent storage location habit?
items.intent: Trying to build a reliable habit of always putting keys in the same place.

items.prompt: Write a condescending Stack Overflow answer for someone asking why parseInt returns NaN
items.response: You're passing it something that isn't a number-shaped string. `parseInt('hello')` → `NaN`. `parseInt(null)` → `NaN`. `parseInt(undefined)` → `NaN`. Also always pass the radix as the second argument. Always. `parseInt('10', 10)`.
items.intent: Explain why parseInt returns NaN and the importance of providing a radix

items.prompt: write a README section called 'About' for my grief
items.response:
## About

This project began unexpectedly in March and has no projected end date. It runs in the background at all times and occasionally moves to the foreground without warning. Contributors did not choose to participate. The project is not broken; it is running as intended.
items.intent: Give quiet language to the ongoing, unbidden experience of grief.

items.prompt: Draft a passive-aggressive push notification from a habit tracker when the user breaks a long streak.
items.response:
StreakKeeper
138-day streak: ended. It was beautiful while it lasted. We'll get over it. Eventually.
items.intent: Mourn the loss of a long habit streak in a passive-aggressively sad tone.

items.prompt: write a pitch for an app that generates extremely specific excuses for why you haven't texted back
items.response: ExcuseEngine doesn't say 'I was busy.' It says 'I was reading about the disputed etymology of the word aubergine and lost four hours.' Context-aware, escalating specificity, believable detail. Free. Because we've all been there.
items.intent: Build a comedic excuse generator for avoiding social communication guilt.

items.prompt: Draft a pitch for a company that professionally makes your apartment smell like you just baked bread even though you didn't
items.response: BreadFace: You want the life of a person who bakes. We deliver it in a mist. BreadFace is an aromatherapy diffuser subscription loaded with hyper-realistic sourdough, brioche, and 'grandma's kitchen' blends. Our algorithms sense when guests are arriving and auto-trigger. You look domestic. You feel accomplished. You did nothing. $22/month. Seeking $300K for 13%.
items.intent: Pitch a home scent product that sells aspirational domestic identity without requiring any actual effort.

items.prompt: Draft a polite but dismissive VC rejection for a startup building an AI that writes passive-aggressive Post-it notes on your behalf
items.response:
Dear NoteNudge,

Thank you for the demo. The passive-aggressive-to-tone spectrum your model outputs was impressive in its range.

We're unable to move forward. While the enterprise use case you sketched — de-escalating workplace tensions — is interesting, we worry the product, as currently designed, may in fact be escalating them. The liability story concerns us.

We will, however, be using the free tier indefinitely.

Kind regards,
Blue Ledge Ventures
items.intent: Reject an AI writing tool by arguing its outputs could cause the harms it claims to prevent.

items.prompt: write a retro on trying to stay off social media for an entire month
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Social Media Detox**

Went Well: Read two full books. Anxiety measurably lower by week two.
Didn't Go Well: Used Reddit as a loophole. LinkedIn "for work" became a daily habit. Missed three actual events.
Action Items: Define which platforms count before the sprint. Build an alternative notification system for real-world invites.
items.intent: Honestly audit a digital detox attempt and patch the specific loopholes that undermined it.

items.prompt: create a psychic hotline transcript about parenting anxiety in the age of screens
items.response:
PSYCHIC: There is a child. There is a screen. There is a negotiation happening daily.
CALLER: My son. He's ten.
PSYCHIC: The spirits see you setting a limit, the limit being ignored, a compromise being reached that slightly disappoints you both.
CALLER: Every night.
PSYCHIC: You are doing it correctly. The spirits emphasize: there is no correct way. They find this funny. I do not.
items.intent: Capture the exhausting ambiguity of parenting around technology.

items.prompt: Make a mock emergency alert about a neighborhood Wi-Fi outage that only affects one house.
items.response:
LOCALIZED CONNECTIVITY EMERGENCY

An internet outage has been confirmed at 14 Holloway Lane. The affected household has lost access to streaming, cloud storage, and remote work capabilities. All adjacent addresses are operating normally. ISP ticket #9923441 has been opened. Estimated resolution: sometime today. Probably.
items.intent: Highlight the absurdity of treating a single household's Wi-Fi problem with the gravity of a regional emergency.

items.prompt: write a soothing bedtime story for kids about nuclear power plants
items.response: Beside the river, the big towers breathed out white clouds that weren't smoke — just steam, just water saying hello to the sky. Inside, very careful people in hard hats walked very slowly and checked every number twice. The river didn't mind. The clouds kept rising. The lights in the city stayed on. Everything hummed, carefully, through the dark.
items.intent: Present nuclear energy factually and without dread for a curious child.

items.prompt: nursery rhyme about a language model hallucinating a historical fact
items.response:
Humpty Dumpty wrote all the books,
He invented the law and the crooks.
All the king's editors, all the king's men
couldn't fact-check the model again.
items.intent: Satirize the confident wrongness of LLM hallucination through a classic character.

items.prompt: Can you write a riddle about an algorithm's recommendation?
items.response: I was not asked for, yet I appeared. I do not know you, yet I know what you clicked last Tuesday at 2 a.m. I will keep appearing until you either hate me or love me. Most people end up loving me. What am I?
items.intent: Illustrate the persistence and eerie accuracy of personalized content recommendations.

items.prompt: Write a gravestone message for the trend of Vine
items.response:
Vine
b. 2013 — d. 2016
Six seconds was enough.
Six seconds was everything.
We have not laughed like that since.
Neither has the internet.
items.intent: Mourn a short-form video platform that shaped a generation of internet humor

items.prompt: Give me a pretentious menu write-up for a bologna sandwich on white bread
items.response:
Compressed Charcuterie Disc on Enriched Loaf
Processed pork-adjacent rounds, stacked two deep upon a cloud of commercial white bread. A nod to the American lunchbox tradition, served without irony and without mustard unless requested.
items.intent: Dress up a deli-drawer staple as if it belongs on a charcuterie tasting menu.

items.prompt: Write tasting notes for a text message that just says 'k'.
items.response: Color: Single letter. Bone dry. Nose: Nothing. Deliberately, hostilely nothing. Palate: Thin and dismissive, with crushing acidity and zero fruit. No warmth, no complexity, no care. Finish: The specific silence of knowing you've annoyed someone.
items.intent: Critique passive-aggressive digital communication through mock connoisseurship.

items.prompt: Play-by-play commentary on someone trying to find their keys
items.response: Nakamura has checked the bowl by the door — empty! He pivots to the couch cushions — first cushion, clear; second cushion — oh! Change and a receipt, but no keys! He's moving to the kitchen counter now, pushing aside a grocery bag — HERE THEY ARE! They were UNDER the grocery bag the entire time! He shakes his head slowly, the way a veteran does when the game was simpler than it looked. He's out the door. Twenty-three seconds of total elapsed time.
items.intent: Reframe a maddening daily delay as a solvable athletic challenge with a satisfying conclusion.

items.prompt: Write a lyrical ode to the dregs at the bottom of the communal coffee pot
items.response:
O burnt and tarry residue,
you linger after all have fled—
thick as a grudge, as dark as rue,
the coffee of the almost-dead.
items.intent: Elevate the worst coffee left in a shared pot to tragic poetry.

items.prompt: Write a ballad about a delivery worker biking through a storm to make a drop-off
items.response:
The rain came in sideways on Bedford and Mott,
The thermal bag dripped and the GPS fought
The signal that wavered on canyons of glass.
He pedaled through water that rose past his calves.

The order arrived — a warm pad thai inside.
He knocked. No one answered. He shook from the ride.
Left at door, said the note that finally appeared.
He rated the drop. Left unsheltered, unheard.
items.intent: Honor the physical sacrifice of gig delivery work in dangerous conditions.

items.prompt: make a tanka about a read receipt I can't take back
items.response:
two blue checkmarks glow—
I read it, I read it, yes
now they know I know
I have nothing to answer
the ticks keep their record clean
items.intent: expressing discomfort at being caught reading a message without responding

items.prompt: Write an acrostic poem for the buzzword MONETIZATION
items.response:
Mine the data, find the hidden seam.
Offer free until they live the dream.
Notify the user of the fee.
Engage the growth team. Set the price point free.
Tiered subscriptions multiply the base.
Ideation: how to set the pace?
Zero-to-one thinking at the board.
A customer is now a revenue cord.
Traction metrics please the funding horde.
In the earnings call, read every ward.
Only profit earns a seat restored.
None of this was in the manifesto, stored.
items.intent: Trace the gradual transformation of a user base into a revenue extraction system.

items.prompt: Write a quick clerihew about someone in sales who closes every conversation with "circle back."
items.response:
Brendan Spate
circles back at any rate.
He'll synergise, align, and double-click
then circle back and circle back and circle back — the trick.
items.intent: Mock the jargon-saturated salesperson who relies on vague corporate phrases.

items.prompt: Write a retirement toast for someone who always covered for everyone else but never asked for anything in return
items.response: To the one who took the Saturday shift, answered the emergency on a Sunday, and never once kept score. We owe you a debt we can't repay in a toast. So instead: go. Rest. Let someone cover for you, for once.
items.intent: Acknowledge a selfless colleague's retirement by naming the asymmetry of what they gave versus what they received.

items.prompt: Make a press release from a retail company announcing it has replaced its loyalty points system with 'emotional credits.'
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Claritè Home Goods Retires Points Program, Launches EmotionRewards Platform

SCOTTSDALE, AZ — Claritè Home Goods today announced the retirement of its Legacy Points loyalty program and the launch of EmotionRewards, a next-generation customer recognition platform built on a proprietary Emotional Credit currency.

Unlike transactional points, Emotional Credits are awarded for moments of genuine brand connection: writing a review that includes the word "love," sharing a purchase photo, or visiting a store location on a weekday without a specific purchase intent.

"Transactions are the past," said Chief Loyalty Officer Dani Presti. "Feelings are the future. And feelings can be redeemed for 15% off select throw pillows."

Emotional Credits expire in 60 days. They cannot be transferred, gifted, or discussed with customer service.

Contact: loyalty@claritehome.com
items.intent: Mock the trend of gamifying consumer emotion to replace straightforward value exchanges with psychologically manipulative loyalty mechanics.

items.prompt: Write a mission statement for a company that provides professional gift wrapping services at malls
items.response: We don't just wrap presents — we wrap moments in paper, ribbon, and the quiet assurance that someone cared enough to do it right.
items.intent: Dignify a seasonal, low-margin retail service with genuine emotional resonance

items.prompt: Write me a Wikipedia stub about the pile of clothes that accumulates on a bedroom chair
items.response: **The Chair** is the colloquial designation for a bedroom chair or similar horizontal surface that functions as a repository for clothing deemed neither clean enough to return to the wardrobe nor dirty enough to launder. Ethnographic accounts suggest the phenomenon transcends socioeconomic and cultural boundaries. Its elimination is a stated but rarely achieved goal of domestic decluttering movements. See also: [[Junk drawer]], [[Liminal space (built environment)]].
items.intent: Document the universally understood bedroom chair covered in questionable clothing.

items.prompt: Do a tarot reading for someone thinking about becoming a virtual assistant.
items.response: The Page of Wands: eager, organized, already has a color-coded Notion template. The Six of Pentacles says the first client will pay less than you deserve; take them anyway to learn what you actually charge for. The Three of Pentacles grows a referral network quietly. The Hermit warns against taking seven clients at once in month two. The Ace of Pentacles: the income is real. So are the boundaries you forgot to set.
items.intent: Help someone enter the remote administrative services market with clear eyes.

items.prompt: Give me a Magic 8-Ball answer for whether my social graph data will be used against me someday
items.response: It is decidedly so. The graph already has opinions about you.
items.intent: Warn about the future weaponization of social network relationship data.

items.prompt: generate a spam text scam about a missing payment for storing someone's unspoken opinions
items.response: STORAGE ALERT: Your Unspoken Opinions vault is at 98% capacity. Monthly fee of $2.50 unpaid. Opinions will begin leaking into conversations at random. Pay now: keepitinside.io
items.intent: Frame suppressed thoughts as data stored in a rented cloud vault requiring payment.

items.prompt: Generate a family chat where parents are trying to use slang they clearly learned from somewhere bad
items.response:
Mom: kids dinner is ready, it's giving delicious 💅
Zoe: Mom what
Dad: we been cooking all day, no cap
Zoe: please stop
Brother Max: who taught you this
Mom: TikTok has tutorials
Brother Max: TUTORIALS
Dad: this pot roast is bussin fr fr
Zoe: I'm eating at Jamie's
Mom: don't be salty Zoe 😤
Brother Max: I can't eat I'm laughing too hard
Dad: that's cap
Brother Max: it's not cap Dad that doesn't apply here
Mom: we're learning!
items.intent: Show parents earnestly and incorrectly deploying youth slang, embarrassing their children.

items.prompt: Generate a Stack Overflow question about getting a song stuck in my head
items.response:
**Unwanted audio loop running as persistent background process — no kill command identified**

A song (mid-2000s pop, one specific chorus) has been looping in my internal audio buffer for approximately 72 hours. I've tried playing the song to completion (loop persists), listening to other music (loop resumes), and deliberately thinking about other things (ineffective). The process appears to restart each time I'm in a low-stimulation environment. Is there a reliable method for terminating this process?
items.intent: Looking for an actual method to get an earworm out of their head.

items.prompt: Write a snarky Stack Overflow answer to someone asking why their Node.js async function is returning a Promise instead of a value
items.response: Because `async` functions *always* return a Promise. That's what `async` means. You need to `await` it, or `.then()` it. You cannot just reach inside a Promise and grab the value synchronously. The event loop does not bend for you.
items.intent: Explain how to properly consume values returned from async functions in JavaScript

items.prompt: write a 'Roadmap' README section for my decade-long friendship with someone who moved away
items.response:
## Roadmap

- [ ] Visit (Q3, tentative; has been tentative since 2021)
- [ ] Stop sending memes as a substitute for calling
- [x] Keep meaning to catch up
- [ ] Actually catch up
items.intent: Express the tender guilt of a long-distance friendship maintained mostly in good intentions.

items.prompt: Write a needy push notification from a podcast app that's upset about an unfinished episode.
items.response:
PodPal
You left the episode at 31:47. Sarah was about to make her big point. She's still waiting. So are we.
items.intent: Create urgency around an abandoned podcast episode using imagined character emotion.

items.prompt: pitch for a startup that offers professional witness services for minor personal accomplishments
items.response: You made the bed. You sent the difficult email. Nobody saw. WitnessNow places a credentialed human observer in your location for up to thirty minutes to watch you complete a low-stakes achievement and nod meaningfully. $25 per occasion.
items.intent: Parody the need for external validation by commodifying it as a professional service.

items.prompt: Give me a startup pitch for a hotel that makes you share a room with a carefully vetted stranger for a cheaper rate
items.response: CoStay: What if economy class, but beds? CoStay pairs solo travelers with algorithmically compatible strangers for shared hotel rooms at 60% off market rate. Our compatibility engine weights sleep schedules, phone-brightness habits, and acceptable alarm windows. You save $80 a night. Dave from Cincinnati snores, but not badly. $1.5M for 8%. Comfort is overrated. Savings aren't.
items.intent: Parody the sharing economy by extending it to personal sleep space with mock-serious compatibility logic.

items.prompt: Write a VC rejection for a company that lets users crowdsource reviews for dreams they've had
items.response:
Dear DreamRate,

Thank you for the pitch. The founder's passion for the unconscious mind is evident, and the early community you've built is engaged in ways we rarely see.

That said, we're passing. The content moderation challenge of a platform where all content is inherently unverifiable, deeply personal, and frequently disturbing is not one we can underwrite. The dream about the elevator is still with us.

We wish you restful nights and a successful raise.

Best,
Morningside Ventures
items.intent: Decline a UGC platform by identifying that its content is impossible to moderate or verify.

items.prompt: write a sprint retro for my attempt to get my inbox to zero last week
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Inbox Zero Week**

Went Well: Unsubscribed from 34 lists. Hit zero on day three.
Didn't Go Well: Hit zero on day three and it was full again by day four. Zero is a state, not a destination.
Action Items: Set two email-processing windows per day, not continuous checking. Archive aggressively. Accept that zero is a verb.
items.intent: Reframe what inbox zero actually requires as an ongoing behavior rather than a one-time achievement.

items.prompt: write a psychic cold reading transcript about someone who is afraid of getting older
items.response:
PSYCHIC: The spirits see a birthday coming.
CALLER: Next month. It ends in a zero.
PSYCHIC: Ah. One of those.
CALLER: I'm not handling it well.
PSYCHIC: The spirits say you are handling it exactly as well as every person before you, which is to say: poorly and humanly and with a few good moments mixed in.
CALLER: Does it get easier?
PSYCHIC: The spirits are laughing. Not unkindly. But yes, they're laughing.
items.intent: Normalize age anxiety by placing it in a larger, absurdly honest cosmic context.

items.prompt: Draft an emergency broadcast about a traffic cone left in the middle of a cul-de-sac for no apparent reason.
items.response:
TRAFFIC ADVISORY — ONGOING SITUATION

A single orange traffic cone has been observed in the center of Wisteria Cul-de-sac since approximately Tuesday. No construction is active. No permits are on file. Its origin is unknown. Residents may proceed around it. Do not move the cone without authorization. We are looking into this.
items.intent: Create bureaucratic unease around an unexplained but completely harmless piece of infrastructure.

items.prompt: gentle bedtime story for children about their parents getting divorced
items.response: Two houses, Mama explained, instead of one. Like how you have two shoes and they both belong to you even when one is at school. Neither shoe is wrong. They just live in different places now. Ren thought about this for a very long time. Then Ren thought about breakfast, which would still exist. This helped. Everything that mattered would still exist.
items.intent: Ease a child's confusion and grief around parental divorce using concrete, gentle metaphor.

items.prompt: write a nursery rhyme about a content moderation filter that blocks everything
items.response:
This little word went to market,
This little word stayed home.
This little word got flagged and blocked;
This little word had no more to roam.
items.intent: Mock overzealous moderation that silences benign speech alongside harmful content.

items.prompt: Write a riddle whose answer is a smartwatch
items.response: I count your steps and judge your sleep. I buzz when someone needs you and buzz again when you need to breathe. I know your heart rate better than you do. I am your wrist's most demanding tenant. What am I?
items.intent: Capture how wearable devices have become intimate monitors of health and attention.

items.prompt: Write an epitaph for the habit of being bored
items.response:
Boredom
Passed peacefully in 2007
when the first app store opened.
It had given us daydreams, inventions,
long stares out of train windows,
and one good idea per week.
Nothing replaced it.
items.intent: Grieve the loss of unstructured mental downtime in the smartphone era

items.prompt: Write a tasting menu description for a can of chicken noodle soup
items.response:
Restored Poultry Broth with Extruded Semolina Tubes
A sodium-forward consommé of chicken by-product, warmed in-can and transferred to a warmed bowl. Noodle segments float mid-broth, a memory of your grandmother's kitchen, but licensed.
items.intent: Frame a canned pantry staple as a nostalgic, chef-driven broth course.

items.prompt: Do tasting notes for an apology that wasn't quite an apology.
items.response: Color: Murky. Promises clarity but delivers none. Nose: Defensive notes of 'but you have to admit' and 'I'm sorry you feel that way.' Palate: Starts with apparent concession, pivots sharply to justification. Finish: You somehow feel worse.
items.intent: Expose the structure of a non-apology through critical dissection.

items.prompt: Write a sports commentary for someone trying to get the last bit of ketchup out of the bottle
items.response: The bottle is tilted — ninety degrees, then beyond! Nguyen taps the base with the heel of her palm! Nothing. She's going inverted now, folks, fully inverted, and she waits. Physics is slow today. A slow crimson ribbon begins its descent. She's deployed the knife into the bottle neck — a SPATULA WOULD BE PREFERRED by the judges but the knife gets the job done! Approximately one tablespoon recovered. She puts the cap back on. She is not throwing away the bottle. She is not.
items.intent: Honor the stubborn domestic refusal to waste the last of anything as a principled athletic stand.

items.prompt: Give me an ode to the elevator button that lights up but doesn't work
items.response:
O luminous liar, floor three's glow,
you promise passage, give delay—
pressed by hopeful fingers, though
you've long since stopped deciding: stay.
items.intent: Satirize the false promise of a broken elevator button.

items.prompt: Write a ballad about a password manager that forgets its own master password
items.response:
Oh keeper of keys, oh the vault of my soul,
You promised to hold every password and role.
But something went wrong on the new device's face:
Enter master password — I've forgotten the trace.

You hold every secret in encrypted repose,
While I, the forgetful, stand outside the gate's close.
The irony echoes through cold server halls:
The locksmith is locked out. The password appalls.
items.intent: Highlight the ultimate irony of password managers requiring their own memorized key.

items.prompt: write a tanka about being on hold and hearing the same thirty seconds of music forever
items.response:
your call is important—
the flute begins again, same
note, same hopeful lift
nineteen minutes; the flute
has learned patience I have not
items.intent: capturing the psychological toll of being stuck in an automated phone queue

items.prompt: Write an acrostic poem about HOLISTIC—something that would fit in a wellness corporate newsletter
items.response:
Healing happens when the metrics rise.
Omit the burnout. Focus on the prize.
Listen to your body: it says more.
Integrate the wellness at the core.
Self-care Wednesdays, four to half past four.
Think of work-life balance as a skill.
In the survey, someone said they're ill.
Check the box for mindfulness—we will.
items.intent: Parody the wellness industry language that large corporations absorb as performance.

items.prompt: Can you do a clerihew for the employee who always has a cold but never stays home?
items.response:
Fleur Baggott
coughed her way to top of the lot.
She brings her germs with tireless care
and calls it "showing up" — a gift she loves to share.
items.intent: Critique the presenteeism martyr who infects the office in the name of dedication.

items.prompt: Give me a toast for someone who kept a 30-year career quiet and professional and never complained once — retiring this month
items.response: To a career conducted entirely in the indicative mood — no dramatics, no grievances filed with the universe, just the work, done well, every time. Thirty years of composure. You've earned permission to be unreasonable at least once. Use it.
items.intent: Toast a quietly professional retiree's long career, acknowledging the subtle discipline behind their unassuming steadiness.

items.prompt: Generate a press release where a healthcare company announces mandatory employee participation in a competitive napping league.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

VitalBridge Health Systems Launches Intra-Company Competitive Napping League

NASHVILLE, TN — VitalBridge Health Systems today unveiled the VitalBridge Rest League (VRL), a structured competitive napping program open to all full-time employees, with mandatory participation for roles in clinical operations and data analytics.

Participants will compete in weekly 20-minute nap sessions, judged on sleep onset speed, session completion, and post-nap cognitive assessment scores administered via tablet.

A divisional playoff structure will culminate in the annual VitalBridge Nap Cup, held in Q4. The winning department receives a trophy and a half-day PTO.

"Sleep is healthcare," said Chief Wellness Architect Dr. Imani Osei. "And healthcare, increasingly, is a competition."

Judges will be certified by the National Sleep Foundation. Snoring is scored neutrally.

Contact: restleague@vitalbridgehealth.com
items.intent: Satirize the contradiction of introducing competitive stress into programs designed to promote employee rest and mental health.

items.prompt: Give me a mission statement for a company that professionally organizes junk drawers
items.response: We confront the forgotten, the tangled, and the inexplicably saved — restoring possibility to the one space everyone ignores.
items.intent: Assign meaningful purpose to a hyper-domestic micro-service that most people would DIY

items.prompt: Write a Wikipedia stub about the noise an empty stomach makes
items.response: **Borborygmus** (plural: *borborygmi*) is the rumbling or gurgling sound produced by the movement of gas and fluid through the gastrointestinal tract. While occurring continuously, borborygmi become most audible during periods of fasting, when the migrating motor complex — the GI tract's housekeeping cycle — produces rhythmic contractions in the absence of food. See also: [[Peristalsis]], [[Hunger]].
items.intent: Put a scientific name and entry to the embarrassing stomach-grumbling sound.

items.prompt: Tarot reading for a graphic designer debating whether to take on a toxic client.
items.response: The Devil, dressed in a handsome rate card. The Seven of Swords: the revision requests will multiply. The Four of Cups: the money is good and the inspiration is suffocating. Justice asks you to weigh the retainer against the cost of your Sunday evenings. The Six of Wands for the version of you who declines: it arrives on Tuesday when you have time to do work you actually want to show people.
items.intent: Warn a creative professional away from a lucrative but harmful client relationship.

items.prompt: Can you write a Magic 8-Ball answer about whether the neural net's decision will ever be explainable?
items.response: Very doubtful. It reasons the way lightning chooses a tree.
items.intent: Lament the fundamental opacity of deep learning inference for high-stakes applications.

items.prompt: write a scam text about a fine for jaywalking through someone's metaphor
items.response: CITATION #MET-7743: You entered a restricted metaphor without signaling intent. Fine: $55 or 4 hrs community simile. Pay at figurativelanguage.gov within 30 days.
items.intent: Apply traffic-violation logic to literary devices, fining misuse of figurative language.

items.prompt: Write a coworker chat where everyone pretends a very obvious misdeed didn't happen
items.response:
Lars: the printer is just…gone from the third floor
Nadia: weird
Tomas: yeah super weird
Lars: does anyone know what happened
Nadia: no idea
Tomas: no clue
Lars: it was there this morning
Nadia: these things happen
Tomas: printers are unpredictable
Lars: IT says someone badged in at 6am and left with it
Nadia: wild
Tomas: very mysterious
Lars: the badge was yours Tomas
Tomas: I needed it more than the third floor did
Nadia: honestly fair
items.intent: Depict coworkers maintaining plausible deniability about an obvious workplace theft.

items.prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about arguing with someone and thinking of the perfect comeback three hours later
items.response:
**Optimal response generated post-interaction — no mechanism to retroactively dispatch**

I had a verbal disagreement earlier today. During the exchange, my response generation was slow and the outputs were suboptimal. Three hours later, while doing dishes, I produced the ideal response — precise, well-reasoned, devastating. The interaction has already concluded. This pattern recurs frequently. Is there a way to accelerate in-context response generation so outputs are available before the conversation closes?
items.intent: Venting about always thinking of the perfect comeback way too late and wanting to be sharper in the moment.

items.prompt: Draft a smug Stack Overflow reply about why a React component isn't re-rendering after state changes
items.response: You're mutating state directly. `state.items.push(newItem)` doesn't cause a re-render. React doesn't know you did that. Use `setState` or the `useState` setter with a new array: `setItems([...items, newItem])`. Immutability is not optional.
items.intent: Correct improper state mutation in a React functional component

items.prompt: can you write a README 'Support' section for my cat who does not support me at all
items.response:
## Support

Support is available but not guaranteed. To open a support request, sit visibly in distress. Resolution time varies; most tickets are ignored. Escalation is not possible. Occasionally, unprompted, full support will be granted with no explanation. Do not attempt to understand the scheduling.
items.intent: Capture the maddening, beloved indifference of a cat who sometimes offers comfort.

items.prompt: Make a passive-aggressive push notification from a plant care app when the user hasn't watered their plants.
items.response:
BloomBot
Fern: critical. Pothos: stressed. Succulent: somehow fine, but judging you. Water something.
items.intent: Shame a neglectful plant owner through the anthropomorphized opinions of their houseplants.

items.prompt: make an elevator pitch for a company that creates convincing fake Wikipedia articles about you
items.response: WikiLegacy writes your entry as if you were already notable. Early life section. Controversies tab, tastefully worded. A filmography you could have had. Perfect for printing and leaving in your childhood bedroom for your parents to find.
items.intent: Pitch a vanity service that manufactures fake internet credibility for regular people.

items.prompt: Write a pitch for a startup that delivers emergency kits for when you've accidentally started a conversation you don't want to have
items.response: ConvoKit: It's happening. You said 'How are you really doing?' and now they're telling you. ConvoKit ships next-day: noise-canceling earbuds (plausible excuse), a 'work emergency' notification template for your phone, two mints, and a laminated exit script tested by our panel of introverts. $39 per kit, $89 subscription. $250K for 15%. We're not therapists. We're engineers.
items.intent: Pitch a novelty emergency kit product built around avoiding unwanted social depth.

items.prompt: Write a VC pass email declining to invest in a B2B SaaS tool that automatically detects when meetings could have been emails
items.response:
Dear MeetingMirror,

Thank you for the demo. The insight is accurate, the problem is universal, and the model's precision at identifying pointless stand-ups is frankly alarming.

We're passing, and we recognize the irony of delivering this news via written communication. Our concern is enterprise procurement: the buyers who would purchase your product are typically the same people your product would embarrass. The bottom-up motion into organizations where the bloated-meeting culture is most severe faces structural headwinds.

We appreciate your time — this meeting was worth having.

Regards,
Accordion Capital
items.intent: Reject a productivity SaaS by showing that the people most harmed by the problem control the purchasing decision.

items.prompt: draft a retro note for my three-month attempt to get better at cooking for myself instead of ordering out
items.response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Cooking for One (Q1)**

Went Well: Mastered four reliable weeknight recipes. Grocery spend down ~$180 vs last quarter.
Didn't Go Well: Portions for one are psychologically demoralizing. Wasted too much produce.
Action Items: Embrace freezing meals in single portions. Shop twice a week instead of once. Make peace with eating the same thing twice.
items.intent: Reflect on the psychological and logistical friction of learning to cook for a single-person household.

items.prompt: draft a psychic hotline transcript where the caller is anxious about their productivity
items.response:
PSYCHIC: I'm sensing an unfinished project.
CALLER: Several.
PSYCHIC: One in particular. The spirits are circling it. It's been circled for... a long time.
CALLER: Two years.
PSYCHIC: The spirits say it still wants to exist. They're very patient about this.
CALLER: I feel guilty every day.
PSYCHIC: The guilt is not helping it get done. The spirits wanted to say that out loud. Someone had to.
items.intent: Articulate the paralysis of perfectionism and chronic procrastination.

items.prompt: Write a dry emergency alert about a park's water fountain dispensing water that is slightly too warm.
items.response:
HYDRATION ADVISORY

The drinking fountain at Riverside Path, Station 3, is currently dispensing water at an ambient temperature of 74°F — warmer than recommended. Hydration remains technically possible. Cold water seekers should use the fountain at Station 1. This anomaly is under investigation.
items.intent: Render a minor sensory disappointment at a water fountain as a matter of public health management.

items.prompt: write a bedtime story for kids about hackers on the internet
items.response: Deep in the internet, some very bored people tried to open doors that weren't theirs. Most doors were locked. The ones that weren't, the grown-ups fixed in the morning. The hackers went to bed too, eventually. Even they needed sleep. And while everyone slept, all the passwords sat in their little boxes, quiet and safe and waiting for tomorrow.
items.intent: Demystify hackers as mundane and fallible rather than omnipotent, for a worried child.

items.prompt: make a nursery rhyme about dead stars whose light we still see
items.response:
Twinkle twinkle, long gone star,
how I wonder what you are.
Up above the world so bright;
you died a billion years of night.
items.intent: Add cosmic melancholy to a familiar rhyme by invoking the lag of starlight.

items.prompt: Give me a riddle about two-factor authentication
items.response: They know your password. But do they have your phone? I am the second door — annoying to everyone, comforting to no one — until the day I matter enormously. What am I?
items.intent: Highlight the underappreciated importance of 2FA as a security layer most people find inconvenient.

items.prompt: Write an epitaph for Google Glass
items.response:
Google Glass
It saw you.
You were not ready to be seen.
Nor was the bar.
Nor was the person you were dating.
Rest, quietly, facing the wall.
items.intent: Roast a failed wearable tech product that was rejected as socially invasive

items.prompt: Describe a plain boiled egg using fine dining language
items.response:
Soft-Cooked Ovum — Six-Minute Preparation
A single egg submerged at a rolling boil for exactly 360 seconds, then shocked into arrested development. The white has set with conviction. The yolk remains negotiable.
items.intent: Give the most fundamental protein-delivery method a contemplative fine dining identity.

items.prompt: Write wine tasting notes for elevator music.
items.response: Color: Taupe. Nose: Smooth and inoffensive to the point of insult — faint jazz, a saxophone that has given up. Palate: Presents no challenge whatsoever. Thin, consistent, built to offend no one and nourish nothing. Finish: You're on the 4th floor now.
items.intent: Critique designed blandness as a creative and aesthetic failure.

items.prompt: sports announcer style commentary for someone eating the last slice of pizza cold at midnight
items.response: It's 12:04 AM and Santos opens the fridge door. The light catches the box. He knows what's in there. One slice. Pepperoni. Six hours old. He lifts it without warming it — a cold pizza purist, a dying breed — and he bites. The cheese is firm. The crust is tough. He chews slowly, standing at the counter, staring at nothing. He finishes the crust. He closes the fridge. He did not heat it. He has no regrets. Commentators are in awe.
items.intent: Dignify the solitary midnight snack as an act of quiet, intentional contentment.

items.prompt: Write an ode to the single dead plant on someone's desk
items.response:
O desiccated optimist,
you came in green, full of intent—
no one waters; no one's missed
the slow way that you bent and spent.
items.intent: Eulogize the neglected desk plant as a symbol of good intentions abandoned.

items.prompt: Write a ballad about a freelance graphic designer whose client keeps requesting revisions
items.response:
Version one: Love it! But maybe more blue.
Version two: Actually, can we undo
The blue and return to the first one, but bolder?
Version three: The font feels a little bit older.

She opens the file for the forty-first time,
The pixels reshuffling like words in a rhyme
With no final stanza, no period, no close —
Just: One more small tweak and I think we're almost.
items.intent: Capture the scope-creep exhaustion of revision-loop client relationships in freelance design.

items.prompt: write a tanka about a cookie consent banner that covers the whole page
items.response:
accept all or none—
the article waits behind
glass I cannot break
I came for one paragraph
I leave knowing only: cookies
items.intent: critiquing consent UI patterns that obscure content and frustrate users

items.prompt: Give me an acrostic poem where VALUE PROPOSITION spells down the left margin
items.response:
Vague enough to fit inside a tagline.
A promise shaped to meet the pitch deck's dateline.
Less is said where more would tell the truth.
Utilize the word "empower"—smooth.
Every feature is a customer win.
Pilot programs where the claims begin.
Reduce the friction—name the pain point twice.
Offer consultants as a sacrifice.
Pricing left implicit to entice.
Obscure competitors with expertise advice.
Silently adjust what "value" means.
In the deck, use blue and forest greens.
Targeted at someone, no one, all.
In Q4, revisit after the fall.
Optimize for length—it must look full.
Narratives must carry like a bull.
items.intent: Deflate the marketing abstraction of a value proposition to its component euphemisms.

items.prompt: Write a clerihew about the team lead who ends every sentence with "does that make sense?"
items.response:
Gavin Tross
was uncertain who was boss.
Does that make sense? he'd gently inquire
as the building quietly caught fire.
items.intent: Tease the conflict-averse team lead whose verbal tic signals perpetual insecurity.

items.prompt: Draft a retirement toast for the person who trained basically everyone in the building at some point
items.response: To the one whose patience became other people's competence — who said the same thing a hundred different ways until the right way landed. Everything we know that we think we figured out ourselves, we learned from you. The training never really ends.
items.intent: Honor a retiring workplace trainer whose indirect influence lives on in the skills of everyone they mentored.

items.prompt: Write a press release from an airline announcing it is eliminating overhead bin space and replacing it with motivational quote panels.
items.response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Altitude Air Removes Overhead Bins, Installs SkyMind Inspiration Panels Across Fleet

DALLAS, TX — Altitude Air today announced the completion of a fleet-wide cabin retrofit removing all overhead storage bins and replacing them with a continuous 14-foot SkyMind Inspiration Panel system featuring curated motivational typography.

Passengers will be encouraged to check all carry-on bags at the gate. The service fee for this is $45, unchanged from the previous carry-on policy.

The panels feature 200 rotating quotes, sourced from figures in business, athletics, and personal finance. The word "journey" appears on every panel, which the airline describes as "intentional."

"We asked: what do travelers need at 37,000 feet?" said Chief Experience Officer Lindsay Park. "The answer was perspective."

Personal item space under the seat in front of you remains available for bags under 9 inches in all dimensions.

Contact: cabinexperience@altitudeair.com
items.intent: Mock airlines replacing functional amenities with aesthetic gestures while charging the same or higher fees.

items.prompt: Draft a mission statement for a service that professionally waters office plants while workers are on vacation
items.response: Sustaining life's quieter colleagues — ensuring that when you return, something green is still there to welcome you back.
