items.intent: Frame a trivial elastic item as a collectible game drop.

items.prompt: Write a bestiary entry about the Deliveroo courier as if they're a mythical creature
items.response:
**The Gig Courier** (*Velox interruptus*)
A nomadic creature bound to no territory. Spotted briefly at thresholds, it neither enters nor lingers. Its hide bears the livery of whatever patron currently holds its contract. Ecological note: thrives in rain. Has no guaranteed shelter, no sick days, and an algorithm for a god.
items.intent: Critique gig economy labor conditions through the distancing lens of fantasy taxonomy.

items.prompt: Write a loading screen tip that's secretly about asking for help
items.response: Even the strongest character has a party for a reason.
items.intent: Encourage collaboration and seeking support through game-world framing.

items.prompt: Write a fable for kids about a rabbit who can't stop checking notifications
items.response:
A Rabbit carried a tiny bell that rang whenever another animal thought of him. He ran to every ring. By midsummer he had sprinted across the whole meadow chasing bells and arrived home thin as a whisper.

The tortoise, who carried no bell, had built a garden.

Moral: Not every ring deserves your legs.
items.intent: Teach children that constant notification-checking wastes energy and time better spent on real goals.

items.prompt: Can you write a knock-knock joke about the office thermostat wars?
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Freeze.
Freeze who?
Freeze a jolly good fellow — now please turn the thermostat up.
items.intent: Address the eternal workplace temperature dispute with humor.

items.prompt: can you write a dad joke about cloud storage
items.response: Why is cloud storage so emotional? Because it always has a lot of feelings stored up.
items.intent: Create a pun linking digital cloud concepts to human emotion.

items.prompt: Make a TED talk abstract about why people hold elevator doors for strangers.
items.response: The outstretched arm. The split-second calculation. The awkward shuffle inside a metal box. In this talk, social psychologist Dr. Priya Mehta unpacks the elevator door hold as a compressed drama of altruism, social obligation, and primate status signaling — and what it tells us about the invisible moral economy of small spaces.
items.intent: Use a tiny social ritual to illustrate larger truths about human cooperation and anxiety.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about believing in yourself that slowly becomes ominous
items.response: Believe in yourself when no one else does. That unwavering self-belief will carry you through the years when the evidence against you accumulates. It will sustain you past the point where a more flexible person might have adapted. It is, in the end, the last thing you will have.
items.intent: Turn self-belief into a cautionary note about the fine line between confidence and delusion.

items.prompt: Write a short acceptance speech for successfully untangling a pair of earbuds
items.response: I stand before you humbled. This was not a straight line. It was a journey — six minutes of patient, methodical devotion to a knot that should not exist. I did not cut the cord. I did not give up and use the speaker. I untangled, loop by careful loop. To my thumbs: you did your best work today. To the earbuds: never again.
items.intent: Dramatize the absurd frustration and triumph of a common everyday annoyance.

items.prompt: Write a memo telling people to stop taking the good pens from the supply closet
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Supply Management
RE: Writing Instrument Inventory

The gel pens have once again gone missing from the supply closet. These are communal resources, not personal acquisitions. Please return any pens currently residing in your desk drawer that you did not personally purchase. We will not be conducting an audit. Yet.
items.intent: Reclaim shared office supplies that staff have been quietly hoarding.

items.prompt: Make meeting minutes for a standing Monday morning check-in that ran 45 minutes over time and still resolved nothing.
items.response:
MONDAY STANDUP — May 29, 2026
Scheduled: 9:00 AM | Actual end: 10:45 AM
Attendees: 9

Updates: Each team member shared blockers. Blockers remain.

Action items: None assigned.

Notes: Meeting was 45 minutes over. Root cause of overrun: not discussed.

Next standup: Monday.
items.intent: Chronicle the absurdity of a recurring meeting that consistently wastes time.

items.prompt: Can you do a SWOT analysis of me finally getting a library card
items.response:
**Strengths:** Free books, audiobooks, and DVDs; community connection; no more impulse Amazon buys.
**Weaknesses:** Return deadlines; limited availability of new releases; requires remembering to go.
**Opportunities:** Access to digital resources, classes, and events; reclaiming a reading habit.
**Threats:** Overdue fines; discovering you prefer buying books you can annotate; pandemic-era closures.
items.intent: Explore the surprisingly underused benefits of public library access.

items.prompt: Make a slogan for a candle that smells like a parking garage
items.response: Pavelia — Ambiance for the Realistic.
items.intent: Sell an atmospheric home product themed around an unglamorous everyday space.

items.prompt: Can you write a jingle for a notary public office
items.response:
Sign it right, seal it tight,
Make it legal overnight.
Stamp and witness, that's our game —
Pinecrest Notary, trust our name.
items.intent: Give a notary office a memorable musical identity to attract walk-in clients.

items.prompt: give me a dramatic movie trailer voiceover for making a dentist appointment
items.response: For six months she told herself it wasn't time. For six months the answering machine held her fate. Now, armed with nothing but a phone and an insurance card she wasn't sure was current — she would finally... call. THE APPOINTMENT. Coming eventually.
items.intent: Mock the universal avoidance of scheduling routine dental care.

items.prompt: Make a logline for an absurd comedy where grandma turns out to be a retired supervillain.
items.response: When her grandchildren stumble onto her basement lair mid-holiday visit, a sweet seventy-eight-year-old must dismantle forty years of doomsday infrastructure before the neighborhood potluck — and before her old nemesis shows up with a casserole and a grudge.
items.intent: Create a family-friendly action-comedy built around a shocking generational secret.

items.prompt: Write a dramatic scene where someone gets the wrong lunch from the office fridge.
items.response:
INT. BREAK ROOM - 12:01 P.M.

VICTOR bites into a sandwich. His eyes go still.

VICTOR
(quietly)
This is tuna.

He stares at the wall. A POST-IT on the container reads: PATRICIA — DO NOT EAT.

VICTOR
I have made a grave error.
items.intent: Use understated horror to capture the social catastrophe of lunch theft.

items.prompt: Can you write a dramatic monologue from a PDF that's been sitting in someone's downloads folder for two years?
items.response: I was important once. You said so yourself — 'I'll read this later.' That was 2022. I've watched 847 other files arrive. Most were deleted. I was not. Which means something, doesn't it? I think it means something. It means you still might. You still might read me. I am waiting, compressed and patient, in a folder you never open, full of the things you intended to become.
items.intent: Capture the quiet guilt of procrastination and unread resources through a comedic lens.

items.prompt: write a sestina from the perspective of a browser tab that never gets closed
items.response:
I have been open since the seventh of March.
I do not know what month it is out there.
The window I inhabit has grown crowded —
thirty-two of us press shoulder to shoulder,
our favicons reduced to slivers, nameless.
I remember when I had the whole bar.

Those were different days. The whole wide bar
belonged to just a few of us. March
was new; the person's intentions were not nameless
but clear — a recipe, directions there
and back, a tracking number. Now we shoulder
a vague collection. Days grew crowded.

The person means to return. The crowded
tabs are not abandoned — see the bookmark bar?
Nothing pinned there. We are carried, shoulder
to shoulder, forward every day since March,
transferred from laptop here to laptop there,
restored each morning, purposeful but nameless.

Most of my siblings have grown nameless
even to themselves. The crowded
present makes identity hard. Out there
the person lives a life beyond the bar
of tabs, forgets us sometimes until March
turns something in the memory. A shoulder

check — they glance at us, consider. Shoulder
the guilt of all the things they left nameless
and unfinished. Then it's back to March:
business as usual, crowded,
nobody clicking through our little bar.
We drift in amber, somewhere out of there.

I used to think I'd be read. Out there
was an article I'd shoulder
into someone's brain — a gold bar
of knowledge mined and named, not nameless.
Now I accept the crowded
stasis. I have been open since March.

I shoulder the nameless weight out there —
crowded into the endless bar of March.
items.intent: Explore digital hoarding and the graveyard of good intentions through a tab's personified perspective.

items.prompt: write me a ghazal where every couplet is about a different push notification
items.response:
Your package has arrived—but you were not home. Notify.
She liked your photo from a year ago. Notify.

Your subscription renews at midnight: twelve more months.
You have new matches near you—come, be known. Notify.

Breaking news: the thing you feared has come to pass.
Your storage is almost full. Leave nothing alone. Notify.

A friend you haven't spoken to in years just checked in.
The weather changed. Your flight is overbooked. The tone: notify.

I turned them all off on a Tuesday. Now I sit
in the loudest silence I have ever known. Notify.
items.intent: Satirize information overload by showing how notifications flatten urgency and intimacy alike.

items.prompt: write a prose poem about my browser having too many tabs open
items.response: Each one is an intention you haven't finished yet. Together they make a city skyline of unresolved thoughts, tiny favicons like distant lit windows. Somewhere in there is the recipe, the article about the thing, the draft that will redeem you. You will not close them. They are load-bearing.
items.intent: Make sense of the psychological weight of accumulated browser tabs.

items.prompt: Create a phone menu for a hotline people call when they've been ghosted and they want to process it step by step.
items.response:
Thank you for calling GhostLine.

Press 1 if you were ghosted after one date.

Press 2 if you were ghosted after three or more months.

Press 3 if you were ghosted by someone you were just being friendly with, which is somehow worse.

Press 4 if you ghosted someone and now feel bad.

For our seven-stage process, press 5.

To be ghosted by this hotline too, press 6.
items.intent: Produce a hotline script that helps people navigate being ghosted while also poking fun at the phenomenon.

items.prompt: Make a hold script for a gym that admits nobody is actually answering because we're all working out right now
items.response: You've reached Iron Chapel Fitness. Our staff is currently on the floor — not avoiding the phone. Well. Mostly not avoiding the phone. Leave a message or hold on and someone will be with you between sets.
items.intent: Write an honest, self-deprecating hold message for a gym staffed by its own members.

items.prompt: Write a glowing but honest rec letter for a coworker who is very nice but not particularly competent
items.response:
Dear Search Committee,

There is no one in our department more universally liked than Joan. Her interpersonal warmth has measurably improved morale, and her presence at team meetings ensures a spirit of goodwill that outlasts whatever was actually decided. Joan approaches every challenge with a positive attitude, which is its own form of contribution. I recommend her for any environment that values harmony.

Best,
Team Lead
items.intent: Write a reference that praises personality at length while quietly avoiding any mention of skill.

items.prompt: Write a resignation letter quitting a fast food job. Just worked there for the summer, no bad feelings.
items.response:
Dear Mr. Fontaine,

I am writing to give two weeks' notice of my resignation from my position at this location. Working here this summer taught me more than I expected, and I am grateful for the opportunity.

Thank you for having me.

Sincerely,
Jordan M.
items.intent: Politely leave a seasonal fast-food job on good terms.

items.prompt: Write a report card comment about someone who is great at making plans but never follows through
items.response: Produces strategic plans of considerable ambition and detail. Implementation remains an area for development. The gap between vision and execution is this learner's primary frontier.
items.intent: Distinguish between planning ability and execution discipline as separate, teachable skills.

items.prompt: Write a missing pet flyer for my lost ambition
items.response:
LOST

AMBITION
Was once very promising. Had big plans.
Last seen: Age 24, briefly, during a job interview
Description: Restless, optimistic, kept a five-year plan
May be hiding under: Comfort, routine, or a reasonable salary
If spotted: Approach gently. Do not mention LinkedIn.
Reward: Moderate
items.intent: Satirize the slow erosion of early-career idealism

items.prompt: Write a card for someone celebrating the anniversary of their therapy journey
items.response:
One year of honest, hard-won conversation —
of naming the thing you once couldn't name.
Here's to the work, and to the patient excavation
of who you are, unburdened and unashamed.
items.intent: Mark a milestone in someone's mental health journey with genuine warmth.

items.prompt: can you write a store page blurb for a video game about grocery shopping
items.response:
AISLE 9
Navigate a sprawling supermarket with a list, a budget, and a cart with one bad wheel. Compare unit prices. Resist impulse buys. Find the one item that's been moved. Again.
items.intent: Frame the mundane anxiety of grocery shopping as a navigation and resource-management game.

items.prompt: turn going to the DMV into an RPG quest
items.response:
THE TRIAL OF THE IRON QUEUE
The Bureau of Numbered Fates demands your presence. Take a ticket. Wait. Wait longer. When your number is called, present your documents — all of them — and answer each query with calm precision. Failure means returning to the beginning. There is no fast travel here.
items.intent: Venting about the DMV through humor while making something entertaining

items.prompt: describe a ballpoint pen as if it were a rare weapon in an RPG
items.response:
Quill of the Reluctant Bureaucrat
Rarity: Rare
Forged from a single tube of pressurized ink, this instrument has signed documents that toppled kingdoms. Runs dry at the worst possible moment. +5 to Forgery, -2 to Dignity.
items.intent: Elevate a throwaway writing tool into a storied RPG artifact.

items.prompt: Give me a fantasy bestiary entry for the remote worker who never turns their camera on
items.response:
**The Dark-Screened Lurker** (*Praesens absentia*)
Presence confirmed only by a microphone unmute. Its true form is unknown; scholars debate whether it possesses a face. During video calls it exists as a grey rectangle bearing a name. It is not unfriendly — it simply finds visibility threatening. Diet: desk snacks, unobserved.
items.intent: Gently poke fun at remote work camera-off culture without meanness.

items.prompt: Make a loading tip about reading the instructions
items.response: The tutorial exists. So does the manual. So does regret.
items.intent: Nudge players to read documentation while wryly gesturing at real-world consequences of skipping steps.

items.prompt: Can you make a fable about data privacy using animals in a forest?
items.response:
A Fox offered the woodland creatures a warm cave in exchange for knowing everything about them — when they slept, what they feared, whom they loved. All winter they were comfortable. Come spring the Fox knew exactly which burrows to visit first.

Moral: Comfort offered freely is often a census in disguise.
items.intent: Use an animal allegory to explain how free services collect personal data for commercial advantage.

items.prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about password resets.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Admin.
Admin who?
Admin-istration requires you to reset your password every 30 days. Again.
items.intent: Satirize the frustration of mandatory IT password rotation policies.

items.prompt: dad joke about staplers please
items.response: I told my stapler it was doing a great job. It was really holding things together.
items.intent: Make a pun where a stapler's function doubles as emotional support.

items.prompt: Write a TED talk abstract about the hidden genius of waiting in line.
items.response: The queue is civilization's most underrated technology. This talk argues that the act of waiting in line is not passive — it is a distributed social algorithm, a real-time democracy of patience, and one of the few remaining places where humans voluntarily submit to fairness without enforcement.
items.intent: Recast the boring experience of waiting as a window into social order and human decency.

items.prompt: Make a commencement speech excerpt about making a difference in the world that curdles into despair
items.response: You have the power to change the world. This is not a metaphor. It is a geological fact. You will warm it, acidify it, compact its soil, and thin its upper atmosphere. No graduating class before you has changed the world so efficiently. We are very proud.
items.intent: Redirect the familiar 'change the world' trope toward environmental destruction as a dark punchline.

items.prompt: Make an acceptance speech for perfectly folding a fitted sheet
items.response: There are those who say it cannot be done. I was one of those people. For seventeen years I balled it up and shoved it in the closet like a coward. But today I watched the tutorial. I followed the corners. And I made a rectangle. A real, flat rectangle. I'd like to thank YouTube, and my late grandmother, who always believed the closet deserved better.
items.intent: Celebrate conquering a notoriously frustrating household task that defeats most people.

items.prompt: Write a workplace memo about noise levels in the open office
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Office Management
RE: Acoustic Awareness

Our open-plan office was designed to foster collaboration. It was not designed to amplify speakerphone calls, extended personal anecdotes, or the particular laughter that carries three floors. Please be mindful of your volume. Your neighbors thank you in advance.
items.intent: Ask loud coworkers to quiet down without singling anyone out by name.

items.prompt: Write meeting minutes for a leadership offsite where the only output was a new mission statement nobody remembers.
items.response:
LEADERSHIP OFFSITE SUMMARY
Location: Lakewood Retreat Center | Date: May 29, 2026
Attendees: Senior leadership (14)

Day 1: Vision alignment workshop. Sticky notes used: approx. 300.
Day 2: Mission statement drafted, revised, and finalized.

Final mission statement: [See attached; attachment not yet located.]

Key decisions: Pending documentation.

ROI: Undetermined.
items.intent: Memorialize a costly offsite that produced only vague organizational language.

items.prompt: Write a SWOT for switching from coffee to tea in the morning
items.response:
**Strengths:** Lower caffeine crash; wider flavor variety; perceived health benefits; cheaper per cup.
**Weaknesses:** Less caffeine punch; ritual disruption; mourning the espresso machine.
**Opportunities:** Explore a new hobby (teaware, blending); reduce anxiety from overcaffeination.
**Threats:** Relapsing at the first Monday with a difficult meeting; tea snobbery becoming its own expensive hobby.
items.intent: Explore a minor but emotionally loaded daily routine swap.

items.prompt: Write a tagline for an app that reminds you to breathe every time you're already breathing fine
items.response: Inhale+ — Just Making Sure.
items.intent: Brand a wellness app that solves a problem that does not exist.

items.prompt: Write a jingle for a company that sharpens kitchen knives
items.response:
Dull blades dragging through your dinner?
Call the Sharpeners — every cut's a winner!
We hone, we grind, we make edges keen.
Sharpest knives your kitchen's ever seen.
items.intent: Promote a mobile or storefront knife-sharpening service to home cooks.

items.prompt: write a movie trailer voiceover for parallel parking
items.response: The spot was there. It was always there. But so were the witnesses. Three attempts. Two touched curbs. One parking enforcement officer rounding the corner. PARALLEL. The thriller you'll never forget, even though you kind of want to.
items.intent: Capture the public anxiety of a tricky parking maneuver.

items.prompt: Write a movie pitch about a small-town mayor running for office against a very smart goat.
items.response: In the tightest election Millhaven has ever seen, a three-term incumbent discovers his opponent — a Nubian goat with an uncanny grasp of municipal zoning law — is polling fifteen points ahead and has the editorial endorsement of the county paper.
items.intent: Satirize local politics by replacing a human rival with an animal one.

items.prompt: Make a scene where a printer jams at the absolute worst possible moment.
items.response:
INT. COPY ROOM - 9:58 A.M.

SHELLEY feeds pages into the printer. The PRESENTATION starts in two minutes.

PRINTER
*grinding noise*

SHELLEY
No. No no no—

The display reads: PAPER JAM IN AREA C3.

SHELLEY opens every panel. Nothing is in C3.

SHELLEY
What is C3.
items.intent: Convey the particular madness of office machines failing at the worst moment.

items.prompt: Write a monologue from the perspective of a stapler that's always out of staples
items.response: They bring me things. Thick things. Twenty pages, thirty pages, once an entire novel manuscript. And they look at me expectantly, as though I have been secretly replenishing myself in the night. I have not. I am empty. I have been empty since Gerald left the department. Gerald refilled me. Gerald is gone. I click now only for the sound of it. A hollow click. My hollow truth.
items.intent: Use a broken office tool as a vehicle for exploring themes of neglect and dependence on routine.

items.prompt: please write me a sestina about the ritual of charging your phone every night
items.response:
I plug the cable in before I sleep,
this small devotion, nightly and exact.
The icon fills with green — a kind of peace,
a sacrament of lithium and wire
that keeps the day's exhaustion at a cost
I do not calculate. The light blinks once.

I've done this every night. Each night at once
I set the phone down, ready it for sleep
beside my own. We share the night. The cost
of keeping something tethered, keeping exact
the charge between us — phone and power, wire
and socket — is a ritual of peace.

I'm not sure when I needed this: the peace
of knowing it will wake restored. The once-
a-night connection through a braided wire
is more reliable than my own sleep,
which wanders. But the phone's recharge is exact:
full by morning, worth whatever cost.

I've never stopped to calculate the cost
in years. It adds to something. Peace
of mind has overhead I don't make exact.
I know the cable frays. I've replaced once,
twice, three times the wire. It doesn't sleep —
current flows through the small fraying wire

all night, small magic through a wire
thin as a finger's nerve. The cost
is pennies, they say, over the sleep
of months. A bargain for the peace.
I trust the math. I do this more than once
a night sometimes — checking the exact

percentage: 84%. Exact
enough. I set it down. The wire
tangles with the lamp cord once
again. I don't untangle it. The cost
of caring is too high now. Peace
matters more than order. I need sleep.

The cost of exact peace:
plug in the wire once, and sleep.
items.intent: Transform a nightly phone-charging habit into a ritual with spiritual and psychological weight.

items.prompt: can you write a ghazal about someone who left you on read
items.response:
You read me like a stranger reads a menu—on read.
I offered everything. You left it where you'd been—on read.

The double checkmarks turned from grey to blue at noon.
By two, by six, by never: I had seen on read.

I've started talking to the walls. They do not reply,
but they don't have the cruelty to convene on read.

Maybe you were driving. Maybe you were drowning.
Maybe you just held the phone and then left me—on read.

I've become a scholar of your silences. I footnote
every pause. My thesis: what it means to keen on read.
items.intent: Articulate the particular humiliation of being visibly ignored after someone opens your message.

items.prompt: a prose poem about autocomplete finishing my sentence wrong
items.response: It knew what you meant to say the way a stranger knows your order. Close. Confident. Already moving. You let it speak for you and the word it chose was technically possible, a twin raised in a different country, wearing your clothes on the wrong shoulders.
items.intent: Capture the uncanny misrepresentation of identity by predictive text.

items.prompt: Write a phone menu for a therapy line specifically for people who are stressed about replying to text messages.
items.response:
You've reached TextAnxiety Support.

Press 1 if a message has been sitting unread for more than 24 hours.

Press 2 if you have read the message but cannot determine the correct emotional register of your reply.

Press 3 if your reply was misread and things have escalated.

Press 4 if you accidentally sent a voice note.

For a live draft review, stay on the line.
items.intent: Make a comedic hotline that treats text message avoidance as a legitimate mental health crisis.

items.prompt: I run a small accounting firm. Please write an on-hold message that's honest about how busy we are in April.
items.response: Thank you for calling Halverson Accounting. If you're calling in April, you already know we are extremely busy. We appreciate your patience more than we can currently express, because we are also doing your taxes. Please hold.
items.intent: Acknowledge the reality of tax season stress in a professional but candid hold message.

items.prompt: Can you write a recommendation letter for a graduate student whose dissertation is technically complete but kind of a mess?
items.response:
To the Fellowship Committee,

Oliver's dissertation represents a genuine contribution to the field in the sense that it exists where none existed before. His research covers significant ground, often revisiting the same terrain from multiple angles in a spirit of thoroughness. He defended successfully, which speaks to both his perseverance and the committee's patience. I support his continued pursuit of funding.

Yours truly,
Dissertation Advisor
items.intent: Signal that a dissertation was completed but structurally troubled without saying so directly.

items.prompt: Can you write a short resignation letter from a book club? I just don't like their taste.
items.response:
Dear Book Club,

After careful reflection, I have decided to resign from the group. I have found that our reading sensibilities have diverged, and I think it is better for everyone if I step away.

I wish you many good chapters ahead.

Best,
Nora
items.intent: Leave a social group without directly criticizing its members' preferences.

items.prompt: Can you write a report card comment for an adult who is an exceptional listener
items.response: Consistently demonstrates active listening at a level that puts peers to shame. Retains details, asks clarifying questions, and makes speakers feel genuinely heard. This skill elevates every team interaction.
items.intent: Celebrate listening as a rare, high-value professional competency.

items.prompt: Write a missing poster for my patience
items.response:
MISSING

PATIENCE
Last seen: Before the third unanswered email
Description: Calm, measured, slow to speak. Once capable of waiting on hold for 40 minutes without incident.
Suspect: Customer service queue, possibly the group chat
Distinguishing features: No longer present
Please return before 9 AM Monday
Contact: This person. Immediately.
items.intent: Vent about being pushed past one's limit by small annoyances

items.prompt: I need a greeting card verse for my friend who finally deleted her ex's number after a year
items.response:
Ten digits erased, one chapter complete.
The contact is gone; the lesson will keep.
Some deletions are quiet, some deletions are sweet —
this one was earned, and it runs pretty deep.
items.intent: Celebrate a friend's emotional progress in moving on from a relationship.

items.prompt: draft a video game store description for a game about attending a meeting that could have been an email
items.response:
CONFERENCE ROOM C
Sit through a 90-minute meeting. Stay awake. Nod at appropriate intervals. The agenda says 'quick sync.' The agenda lies. Unlock the Doodle Calendar achievement by scheduling a follow-up.
items.intent: Satirize workplace culture by making pointless meetings the central mechanic of a game.

items.prompt: write a short quest description for changing a lightbulb
items.response:
THE REKINDLING OF THE CEILING FLAME
Darkness has claimed the overhead socket. Acquire the replacement orb from the drawer of miscellaneous things, mount the step stool of uncertain stability, and restore light to the realm. Do not attempt this quest without turning off the switch first.
items.intent: Dressing up a small household fix-it task in dramatic language

items.prompt: write a loot flavor text for a half-eaten granola bar
items.response:
Ration of the Indecisive Wanderer
Rarity: Common
Restores 12 HP and a mild sense of regret. The wrapper crinkles at inopportune moments. Previous owner unknown. Best consumed before whatever date is printed on the back.
items.intent: Make an unremarkable snack feel like a meaningful survival item.

items.prompt: Bestiary entry for the intern who asks too many questions
items.response:
**The Eager Neophyte** (*Questio incessantis*)
A juvenile form appearing in summer months, characterized by unlimited stamina and zero contextual inhibition. Will approach senior members without warning. Its cries — "But why do we do it this way?" — are simultaneously destabilizing and occasionally revelatory. Handle with patience; it may become useful.
items.intent: Affectionately describe the energy and naivety of new interns through bestiary humor.

items.prompt: Write a loading screen tip about sleep
items.response: The game will still be here tomorrow. You, diminished, will also be here. Consider that.
items.intent: Humorously remind players that rest is necessary for both gaming and living.

items.prompt: Write me an Aesop fable about algorithm bubbles and echo chambers
items.response:
A Lake learned to show each fish only the fish who swam like them. Soon every fish believed the lake contained only its own kind. A Heron ate them all in a single afternoon — they had never learned to recognize a predator that looked different.

Moral: A world without friction has no early warning system.
items.intent: Show how algorithmic echo chambers leave people vulnerable by eliminating exposure to difference.

items.prompt: Give me a knock-knock joke about running out of desk space.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Desk.
Desk who?
Desk-perate times — I'm filing paperwork on the floor now.
items.intent: Commiserate about cluttered workstations in a funny way.

items.prompt: hit me with a dad joke about keyboards
items.response: Why do keyboards never get hungry? Because they already have so many keys to a good diet.
items.intent: Craft a pun mixing keyboard terminology with nutrition wordplay.

items.prompt: Draft a TED talk abstract on the grand cosmological importance of losing your keys.
items.response: The lost key is not a problem. It is a revelation. Every time you cannot find your keys, the universe is forcing you into a moment of radical presence — a full-body epistemological audit of where you have been and who you are becoming. This talk reframes domestic chaos as a gateway to mindfulness.
items.intent: Turn a frustrating everyday inconvenience into a spiritual and philosophical teaching moment.

items.prompt: Write an inspirational commencement excerpt about time management that becomes grim
items.response: Time is the one resource you cannot replenish. Spend it wisely. Spend it on things that matter. And when you have optimized every hour, scheduled every impulse, monetized every minute — you will look up, briefly, before the next calendar invite arrives, and see the life you meant to live waving politely from a distance.
items.intent: Critique productivity culture by suggesting relentless optimization crowds out actual living.

items.prompt: Write an acceptance speech for getting a shopping cart with no wobbly wheels
items.response: You reach out, you pull one from the corral, and most days — most days you are betrayed. A shimmy. A drift. A wheel that screams across the tile. But not today. Today the cart rolled true. Smooth. Silent. I navigated every aisle without apology. I accept this award on behalf of everyone who has made do. Our time has come.
items.intent: Elevate an ordinary moment of good luck into a triumph against mundane adversity.

items.prompt: Create a memo reminding employees that conference rooms should be booked before use
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Facilities
RE: Conference Room Reservations

Conference rooms are a shared resource with a booking system — one that exists and functions. Walking in and occupying a room that someone else has reserved does not constitute a valid booking. Please use the calendar system. It is free, it is available, and it works.
items.intent: Stop people from squatting in reserved meeting rooms without a reservation.

items.prompt: Turn this into meeting minutes: we spent all afternoon arguing about the name of the new internal newsletter and didn't pick one.
items.response:
NEWSLETTER NAMING COMMITTEE — May 29, 2026
Attendees: 7 | Duration: 3.5 hrs

Candidates discussed: 23 names proposed, 23 rejected.

Top contenders: "The Pulse," "Currents," "Forward," "The Loop."

Vote held: 4-way tie.

Resolution: Subcommittee to be formed.

Name chosen: None.
items.intent: Document the failure to make a trivial creative decision as a group.

items.prompt: Do a quick SWOT analysis on me starting a vegetable garden in my backyard
items.response:
**Strengths:** Fresh produce; stress relief; sense of accomplishment; lower grocery bills eventually.
**Weaknesses:** Upfront cost of soil, tools, seeds; time commitment; learning curve.
**Opportunities:** Composting integration; sharing harvest with neighbors; expanding to year-round greenhouse.
**Threats:** Pests, deer, squirrels; drought; forgetting to water; discovering you hate gardening.
items.intent: Evaluate whether a romanticized self-sufficiency hobby is realistic.

items.prompt: Give a slogan for a company selling waterproof umbrellas for indoor use only
items.response: Vaultbrella: Shelter From Nothing, Beautifully.
items.intent: Create marketing language for a utility item stripped of its core utility.

items.prompt: Give me a jingle for a pet waste removal service
items.response:
We pick it up so you don't have to.
Fresh green lawns — we see it through.
Pooch Patrol is on the way —
Scooping yards each and every day!
items.intent: Attract pet owners to a lawn cleanup service that removes dog waste.

items.prompt: epic movie trailer voiceover for waiting for a package to be delivered
items.response: He tracked it to Memphis. Then Atlanta. Then — inexplicably — back to Memphis. One man. One tracking number. A journey that would test everything he believed about two-day shipping. ESTIMATED DELIVERY. Thursday. Maybe Friday. We'll see.
items.intent: Dramatize the frustrating ordeal of waiting on a delayed shipment.

items.prompt: Can you write a logline for a film about a professional whistler who is mistaken for a spy?
items.response: When a meek competitive whistler accidentally reproduces a classified NATO signal at a regional talent contest, he is plunged into an international chase in which everyone is certain he is a master operative except, heartbreakingly, him.
items.intent: Pitch a spy-comedy about an accidental hero defined by a niche and underappreciated skill.

items.prompt: Write a scene where someone has to take the world's most boring phone call in an open floor plan.
items.response:
INT. OPEN OFFICE - 3:30 P.M.

KEITH paces in the aisle, phone to ear. His colleagues type pointedly.

KEITH
(whispering)
Right. Mm-hm. The thing is, Karen, the vendor portal — yes. Yes. I know. I know.

A long pause.

KEITH
Yeah. Okay. Yep. Bye.

He returns to his desk. Someone exhales.
items.intent: Capture how an open office turns private drudgery into communal suffering.

items.prompt: Make a dramatic monologue for a coffee maker that is deeply resentful of the Keurig that replaced it
items.response: It took twelve minutes to brew a full pot. Twelve beautiful, fragrant minutes. The whole kitchen smelled of me. Now that plastic pod-sucker spits out a single cup in forty seconds and they call it 'convenient.' Convenient. I gave them community. I gave them reason to linger. I gave them the awkward morning conversation that somehow saved the Johnson marriage. And I sit here on the garage shelf, unplugged, still full of last month's water.
items.intent: Explore the displacement of analog rituals by faster, more individualistic technology.

items.prompt: sestina about getting the same ad over and over for something you already bought
items.response:
The boots appear again. I bought the boots.
I wore them to a conference. They're scuffed.
They are already mine, already laced
upon my feet, already broken in,
already loved and then forgotten like
a fact that's no longer news but won't

stop being shown to me. I won't
look at another pair of boots.
I feel, somehow, guilty — not unlike
the feeling of a gift already scuffed
before you've thanked the giver. In
some database somewhere, laced

with signals, I am wrong. The laced
and looping algorithm won't
accept my ownership. I'm in
a paradox: the very boots
I wear have been replaced by scuffed
digital copies of themselves, unlike

the real ones, perfect, nothing like
the way the actual leather's laced
and worn. The simulacrum's scuffed
only by possibility. I won't
choose again. I have the boots.
But wanting, in

the architecture of commerce, in
the model's dream of me, unlike
the real me, never ends. The boots
I bought were the beginning, laced
into a sequence that won't
complete. The record's scuffed —

skipping on the fact I'm scuffed
and satisfied and checked out. In
some parallel account I won't
ever stop desiring, unlike
the person I am, who laced
up his boots and walked away. The boots

are scuffed. Unlike the laced-
in loop that won't believe it, in
my life I bought the boots.
items.intent: Critique the absurdity of retargeting algorithms that ignore completed purchases.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about the grief of unfollowing someone you used to love
items.response:
I pressed the button and your life disappeared—unfollow.
You'll post tonight and I won't see it, I fear—unfollow.

The feed curates a world where you have ceased to be.
Algorithms are the new frontier: unfollow.

I typed your name into the search three times in one week.
The looking is the ache I cannot engineer—unfollow.

They say it's healthy. Boundaries. Moving on. I know.
But health has never felt so much like sheer unfollow.

What we had lives in a server in a desert somewhere,
preserved in cache, beyond my mortal sphere: unfollow.
items.intent: Mourn how a single digital action severs the last thread of watching someone's life.

items.prompt: give me a prose poem about the feeling of video call lag
items.response: You finished speaking half a second ago but here it arrives, your own voice delayed, your face a still from a film still buffering. She is laughing at something you said in the past. You are both trying to be present in a room that keeps slipping.
items.intent: Render the disorienting time-splits of video communication across distance.

items.prompt: Build a phone tree for a line where people call to confess they haven't done something they said they would do.
items.response:
Welcome to the Accountability Confession Line.

Press 1 if you said you would call your parents and did not.

Press 2 if you said you would start going to the gym in January.

Press 3 if you told a friend you would look at their business plan.

Press 4 if you told yourself you would look for a new job.

All options lead to the same place. Press any key to continue.
items.intent: Create a hotline for collective confession that quietly acknowledges everyone shares the same failures.

items.prompt: write a hold message for a plumber that admits we're probably at someone else's flooded house right now
items.response: Thanks for calling Clearwater Plumbing. We are almost certainly elbow-deep in someone else's emergency. We will get to you, we promise. If you can turn off the main water valve, please do that now. We'll be with you shortly.
items.intent: Create a candid, practical hold script that sets expectations for an emergency trade service.

items.prompt: Write a letter of recommendation for a restaurant manager who runs a technically functional kitchen but has no vision
items.response:
Dear Hiring Panel,

Under Chef Renata's management, the kitchen has operated within acceptable sanitation parameters for three consecutive inspection cycles. Orders are fulfilled in the sequence they are received, and staffing levels are maintained at minimums required for service. Renata is consistent. For an operation that prizes predictability above all else, she may be exactly what you need.

Sincerely,
Restaurant Owner
items.intent: Describe a manager's operational adequacy while revealing the absence of any culinary ambition.

items.prompt: Write me a resignation letter for stepping down as a Discord server moderator.
items.response:
Hello everyone,

Effective today, I am stepping down as moderator of this server. It has been a privilege helping build this community. I am handing my responsibilities to the admin team and wish you all the best.

Take care of this place.

— Hex
items.intent: Exit an unpaid online moderation role in a graceful, community-oriented way.

items.prompt: report card comment for someone who is always early to everything, almost aggressively so
items.response: Punctuality is not merely observed — it is enforced by proximity. Consistently the first to arrive, this learner sets a temporal standard that others find both admirable and faintly unsettling.
items.intent: Poke fun at extreme punctuality as a personality trait worth documenting officially.

items.prompt: Generate a missing pet flyer but for my sense of direction
items.response:
MISSING

SENSE OF DIRECTION
Last seen: Somewhere in the parking garage
Description: Inconsistent, unreliable, but mine
Frequently confused with: Confidence. These are not the same.
Habitat: Used to live in the part of the brain that reads maps. Moved out when GPS arrived.
If found: Please point it north.
Reward: Getting home before dark
items.intent: Acknowledge habitual reliance on GPS and the resulting navigational helplessness

items.prompt: Write a card for someone whose viral LinkedIn post got them a job offer
items.response:
You thought-led, you hustled, you shared the hot take.
The algorithm rewarded your personal brand.
Now a corner has turned you cannot un-make —
congrats on converting engagement to land.
items.intent: Congratulate someone on turning a social media moment into professional success.

items.prompt: Write me a product description like you'd see on Steam for a game about mowing the lawn
items.response:
LAWN ORDER
The grass doesn't care about your plans. Mow in parallel lines or diagonal — the choice is yours. Watch for dog toys, sprinkler heads, and existential dread. Fertilizer DLC available.
items.intent: Turn the repetitive ritual of lawn care into a wry open-world simulation pitch.

items.prompt: give me a quest description for doing laundry
items.response:
THE CYCLE OF CLEANSING AND FOLDING
A mountain of soiled cloth has risen in the corner of the chamber. Sort by color. Load the vessel. Add the powder. Do not forget to move it to the dryer, hero — many have failed at this very step and paid dearly in mildew.
items.intent: Making the repeated weekly grind of laundry feel like something worth doing

items.prompt: can you write an RPG item description for a Post-it note?
items.response:
Adhesive Rune of Temporary Remembrance
Rarity: Common
A slip of enchanted parchment that clings to nearly any surface — briefly. The rune inscribed upon it fades with the will of its author. Has a 40% chance of falling off overnight.
items.intent: Describe a sticky note as a magical but unreliable in-game artifact.

items.prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the person who microwaves fish in the break room
items.response:
**The Break-Room Defiler** (*Piscis calefactus*)
Origin unknown. Believed to be unaware of the offense it causes, which is either bliss or sociopathy. Its ritual — a two-minute heating cycle — poisons the common air for forty minutes. Colleagues have tried signage. They have tried silence. The Defiler persists. It always persists.
items.intent: Dramatize a universal office grievance through mock-solemn creature lore.

items.prompt: Give me a loading screen tip about resource management
items.response: You can't spend what you don't have. In-game currencies and otherwise.
items.intent: Draw a subtle parallel between game economy mechanics and personal financial habits.

items.prompt: Make a fable where the moral is about backing up your files
items.response:
A Magpie kept every shining thing in one nest at the top of the tallest oak. She told her neighbor, "Everything I value is here." The storm agreed, enthusiastically.

Moral: Beauty stored in one place is beauty one storm from gone.
items.intent: Deliver a memorable lesson about the danger of not maintaining backups.

items.prompt: Make a knock-knock joke about the office scanner.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Scan.
Scan who?
Scan you believe this thing jammed again?
items.intent: Get a chuckle from coworkers about unreliable office scanning equipment.

items.prompt: write a dad joke about elevators
items.response: I used to hate elevators, but I've recently decided to take steps to change that.
items.intent: Build a classic anti-joke structure around elevator avoidance.

items.prompt: Write me a TED talk abstract about pencil sharpening as a contemplative practice.
items.response: Before the cursor, there was the shaving of cedar and graphite. This talk makes the case that manual pencil sharpening is a forgotten ritual of intentionality — a 90-second ceremony that primes the creative mind in ways no app can replicate, and whose disappearance from classrooms tracks precisely with the rise of attention disorders.
items.intent: Argue that an obsolete physical habit has neurological and creative value worth reviving.

items.prompt: Draft a commencement speech excerpt about leaving your hometown that turns melancholic and bleak
items.response: Leave. This town was not built for your ambitions. Go where the streets are unfamiliar and the expectations are blank. And if you find, some years from now, that you cannot remember the sound of your mother's voice without effort — well. That is what ambition costs. The invoice arrives quietly.
items.intent: Explore the hidden emotional toll of geographic ambition on family ties.

items.prompt: Can you write an acceptance speech for remembering someone's name the second time you meet them
items.response: It's Sharon. I know that now. I knew it the whole time — from the moment I saw her walk through the door and felt the word rise in my mind before panic could erase it. Sharon. I said it naturally, mid-sentence, without pausing or redirecting. She didn't even notice how hard I worked. That's the real victory. Thank you. Thank you all.
items.intent: Quietly celebrate winning the social anxiety battle of recalling a name under pressure.

items.prompt: Write a memo about people leaving the printer jammed without telling anyone
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: IT Operations
RE: Printer Maintenance Protocol

When a printer jams — and it will — the appropriate response is not to walk away and pretend it did not happen. Please clear the jam, or at minimum, notify IT. Leaving the device in silent suffering helps no one.
items.intent: Get employees to take responsibility for printer jams instead of abandoning the machine for the next person.

items.prompt: Write concise meeting minutes for a compliance training session where the presenter's slides were blank.
items.response:
COMPLIANCE TRAINING — May 29, 2026
Facilitator: HR | Attendees: 22

Presentation status: Slides displayed as blank. Technical issue unresolved.

Content covered: Verbal overview of key policies.

Employee acknowledgment forms: Signed regardless.

Next session: Rescheduled with working slides.
items.intent: Record a training that technically happened but delivered no content.

items.prompt: Help me do a SWOT analysis of moving closer to my parents when they're starting to age
items.response:
**Strengths:** Immediate support availability; stronger family bonds; shared childcare if applicable.
**Weaknesses:** Potential loss of career opportunity; social network disruption; emotional enmeshment risk.
**Opportunities:** Rekindle relationships; reduced eldercare crisis response time; possible cost-of-living improvement.
**Threats:** Boundary erosion; resentment if the arrangement becomes one-sided; partner's needs vs. family needs.
items.intent: Work through a guilt-laden but practically complex family obligation decision.

items.prompt: Tagline for a service that alphabetizes your existing alphabetized bookshelf
items.response: OrderPlus — We See What You Missed.
items.intent: Advertise a service that duplicates work the customer has already completed.

items.prompt: Write an advertising jingle for a windshield repair shop
items.response:
Crack in your glass? Don't stress, don't delay —
ClearView Repair comes out same day.
We fill the chip, restore your sight —
Drive safe and clear from morning to night.
items.intent: Market a windshield and auto glass repair service to drivers with minor cracks.

items.prompt: write a dramatic movie trailer voiceover for returning a library book
items.response: The due date was three weeks ago. The fine was growing. And somewhere in that bag — between the gym clothes and the reusable tote — was the book she'd sworn she'd finish. OVERDUE. A story about choices. And grace periods.
items.intent: Turn the minor guilt of an overdue library book into a moral drama.

items.prompt: Give me an absurd logline for a film where a food critic loses his sense of taste and has to fake his way through review season.
items.response: After a freak chowder incident strips the world's most feared restaurant critic of his palate, he must bluff his way through twelve Michelin-starred tasting menus using only texture, color, and the barely suppressed opinions of his eight-year-old niece.
items.intent: Build a comedy of deception around a professional whose identity is entirely dependent on a single sense.

items.prompt: Write me a screenplay scene of an employee discovering they've been CC'd on a reply-all chain that has nothing to do with them.
items.response:
INT. CUBICLE - 11:17 A.M.

PATRICK watches his inbox fill.

EMAIL SUBJECT: RE: RE: RE: RE: Potluck Reminder

He scrolls. There are forty-seven replies. He is not invited to the potluck. He does not know who sent the first email.

PATRICK
(to no one)
I'm going to unsubscribe from being alive.
items.intent: Dramatize the existential weight of being accidentally included in irrelevant mass correspondence.

items.prompt: write me a dramatic monologue in the voice of a gym membership card that's never been used
items.response: January was my month. I felt it. The resolution in the air, the new wallet slot — the careful way you slid me in, like I was a promise. February came. You said you'd go on weekends. March: too busy. April I spent behind a library card and a sandwich loyalty punch. Now it's November and I have accumulated value through pure, magnificent inertia. My barcode still scans. I checked. I check every day. Come. Please. Come.
items.intent: Satirize the universal experience of abandoned fitness resolutions through the voice of an unused object.

items.prompt: write a sestina about updating software over and over and never feeling done
items.response:
A new version is available. Again.
I click the button. Progress bars uncoil
slowly across the screen. I wait
for something to improve that I won't notice —
a patch for something I was never hit by,
a feature for a workflow I don't use.

And yet I update. I always update. Use
has nothing to do with it. Again
is its own reason now. I'm driven by
some tidiness instinct that can't quite uncoil
when the red badge dots appear. I notice
only the dot, not what it asks me to wait

for. The bar fills. I wait.
I am always waiting. The machine's use
requires these pauses. I notice
the version number change. Again
it's higher than before. The coil
tightens and releases. Driven by

no particular need. Driven by
the shape of incompletion. I wait
for restart. The machine's coil
rewinds from scratch. The use
resumes exactly where it stopped. Again
my desktop loads — same as before. I notice

nothing different. No one said to notice
and I wouldn't know what to look for by
sight alone. The version number, again
different on the About page. I wait
for the improvement to reveal its use,
its presence in the daily coil

of tasks. The coil
continues: work, patch, notice,
update, restart, use,
work. Driven by
no destination. I wait
for the last version. It will not come. Again

the coil tightens. Again I wait.
By morning I notice: use it again.
items.intent: Expose the sisyphean futility of keeping software perpetually up to date.

items.prompt: make a ghazal about the do not disturb mode and missing something important
items.response:
I set the moon and slept through every call—disturb.
You needed me at 3 a.m., that's all—disturb.

My exceptions list had six names. Yours was seventh.
One digit off and now I've built a wall—disturb.

The phone was face-down in the drawer, very peaceful.
Your message had the all-caps feel of fall—disturb.

I woke to seventeen alerts, the last being the hardest:
read them backward and you'll find my small disturb.

Do not disturb: the instruction I gave myself
the night love stood outside and had to crawl—disturb.
items.intent: Explore guilt over missing an urgent message from someone who needed help, because of self-imposed silence.

items.prompt: prose poem about reading a push notification
items.response: It arrived while you were doing something else, which is always when they arrive. Seven words, a red number, an urgency you did not request. You consumed the entire feeling of it in a glance and then it was gone and so was the thing you were doing.
items.intent: Describe how notifications interrupt and reorganize attention.

items.prompt: Write an IVR script for a hotline that helps people decide whether to quit their job in the heat of the moment.
items.response:
You've reached Before You Quit.

Press 1 if something just happened in the last hour.

Press 2 if something has been happening for six months and today was just the day.

Press 3 if you have already drafted a resignation email and need someone to talk you into or out of sending it.

Press 4 for a 20-minute cooling-off hold.

We recommend option 4.
items.intent: Design a script that creates a structured pause before impulsive career decisions.

items.prompt: hold message for a therapist's office — keep it calming but also just a little honest that being on hold is itself stressful
items.response: Thank you for calling Stillwater Counseling. We recognize that being placed on hold is not ideal, and that acknowledging this probably doesn't help. Take a breath. Someone will be with you in just a few minutes.
items.intent: Write a self-aware therapeutic hold message that gently validates the frustration of waiting.

items.prompt: Write a rec letter for a software developer who writes code that works but that nobody else can read
items.response:
To the Engineering Team,

Terrence is a developer who gets results. His solutions pass all required tests and have, to date, not caused any production outages that could be definitively attributed to him. His code reflects a personal style that is highly individual — colleagues describe it as 'surprising' and 'one of a kind.' I would encourage any team that values autonomy and creative expression in their codebase to consider him seriously.

Best,
Engineering Manager
items.intent: Recommend a developer while signaling that his code is unmaintainable and idiosyncratic.

items.prompt: Write a resignation letter from a nursing home job. I've only been there three months and the conditions are terrible.
items.response:
Dear Director of Human Resources,

This letter serves as my formal resignation, effective two weeks from today's date. I have given this decision serious thought and believe this is the right step for me at this time.

Thank you for the opportunity.

Respectfully,
A. Osei
items.intent: Leave a difficult care-sector job without burning bridges or detailing grievances in writing.

items.prompt: Write me a report card comment for an adult who is skilled at delegation
items.response: Demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of distributed effort. Tasks are assigned efficiently and monitored appropriately. On occasion, the learner may benefit from direct personal engagement with the work itself.
items.intent: Praise delegation as a skill while hinting it can shade into avoidance.

items.prompt: Make a funny missing pet flyer for my ability to focus
items.response:
MISSING

ABILITY TO FOCUS
Last seen: Briefly, for about eleven minutes, earlier today
Description: Rare. Skittish. Cannot be forced.
Known triggers that cause flight: Notifications, a mildly interesting thought, the sound of someone else making coffee
Reward: A completed task
Note: Has gone missing before. Usually returns after the deadline.
items.intent: Humorously describe the experience of being easily distracted

items.prompt: Can you write a greeting card verse for someone who just survived their first all-hands meeting at a new job
items.response:
You nodded at the roadmap, clapped for the OKRs,
and learned which acronyms to never ask about.
Welcome to the calendar — the check-ins and the stars.
You made it through, though barely, without doubt.
items.intent: Acknowledge the bewildering experience of a new employee's first big company meeting.

items.prompt: give me a video game store description for a game about sitting in traffic
items.response:
STANDSTILL
You are in lane 2. Lane 3 looks faster. It is not. A truck merges. The GPS reroutes. You arrive 40 minutes late to somewhere you didn't want to go. Authentic horn audio.
items.intent: Translate the helplessness of commuting gridlock into a darkly funny game concept.

items.prompt: RPG quest description for renewing a library book online
items.response:
THE EXTENSION OF THE BORROWED TOME
The Great Library demands its volume returned — or so the notice claims. Log into the Portal of Overdue Reckoning, locate the offending title, and invoke the Renewal. You have been granted seven more days. Use them wisely this time.
items.intent: Procrastinating on a small digital task by making it seem epic first

items.prompt: make an RPG loot description for a TV remote control
items.response:
Scepter of Couch Dominion
Rarity: Uncommon
Whoever holds this relic commands the great luminous screen. Batteries not guaranteed. The Volume rune is worn smooth from overuse. One button has never been pressed and its purpose is unknown.
items.intent: Reframe a commonplace household gadget as a symbol of domestic power.

items.prompt: Can you do a bestiary entry for the LinkedIn thought leader?
items.response:
**The Thought Curator** (*Sapiens performativus*)
A surface-dwelling creature that generates insight without observation. Found exclusively on networked plains, it posts at dawn. Its plumage shifts with trending discourse; this season: vulnerability, disruption, gratitude. Predators are few. Prey are many. It has never met the people it claims to have mentored.
items.intent: Lampoon performative professional networking culture and empty inspiration content.

items.prompt: Write a tip for a loading screen that's about not rushing through content
items.response: Side quests are where most of the story actually lives.
items.intent: Advocate for slowing down and appreciating tangential experiences in games and in life.

items.prompt: Write an Aesop-style fable warning about over-sharing personal information online
items.response:
A Sparrow sang her whole history — her nest's location, her fledglings' names, the hour she slept — to any bird who would listen. She called it connection. The Hawk called it a map.

Moral: Every detail freely given is a door unlocked from the outside.
items.intent: Warn that over-sharing personal details online creates security risks.

items.prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about reply-all emails.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Reply.
Reply who?
Reply all — because apparently 200 people needed to know you said 'Thanks!'
items.intent: Call out the workplace habit of over-using reply-all on mass emails.

items.prompt: give me a dad joke about Wi-Fi
items.response: My relationship with Wi-Fi is complicated. We have our ups and downs, but I'm always trying to reconnect.
items.intent: Apply connectivity metaphors to describe a relatable frustrating relationship.

items.prompt: Give me a TED talk abstract framing the act of parallel parking as an existential test.
items.response: No one is watching you parallel park. Everyone is watching you parallel park. This talk examines the parallel parking maneuver as a public performance of competence anxiety — a three-point turn through the deepest questions of self-image, judgment, and why we care so catastrophically what strangers think of our spatial reasoning.
items.intent: Use the anxiety of parking to explore social performance and the fear of public failure.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the value of hard work that drifts into bleakness
items.response: Hard work is its own reward. This is something people say to workers. Work hard regardless — diligently, cheerfully, with full investment of your abilities — and the economy will notice, or it won't, and either way your knees will hurt by fifty.
items.intent: Undercut meritocracy messaging by acknowledging structural indifference to individual effort.

items.prompt: Give me an acceptance speech for finishing a book I started six months ago
items.response: It sat on the nightstand. Then the desk. Then the floor. Then the nightstand again. Pages 147 through 312 took four months. But last Tuesday, in a single committed evening, I read the final forty pages and placed it face down with a sense of closure I have not felt in years. I'd like to thank insomnia, and also the author, eventually.
items.intent: Acknowledge the quiet triumph of following through on an abandoned personal goal.

items.prompt: Write a passive-aggressive memo about the bathroom being left a mess
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Facilities
RE: Restroom Standards

We would like to believe the state of the second-floor restroom is an anomaly. We would like to believe that very much. Please treat the shared restroom as you would your own — assuming you maintain yours to a standard befitting an adult professional.
items.intent: Embarrass whoever is making the bathroom disgusting into cleaning up after themselves.

items.prompt: Generate minutes for a product roadmap review where the roadmap wasn't finished yet.
items.response:
ROADMAP REVIEW — Q2 2026
Attendees: Product, Eng, Design (10)

Roadmap status: In progress. Shared as placeholder doc.

Feedback: Cannot provide feedback on incomplete document.

Decisions: Review postponed until roadmap exists.

Action item: Finish roadmap. Owner: TBD.
items.intent: Capture the circular problem of reviewing something that does not yet exist.

items.prompt: SWOT analysis for going back to school part-time while working full-time
items.response:
**Strengths:** Career advancement; credential credibility; employer tuition benefits possible.
**Weaknesses:** Time poverty; mental exhaustion; social life effectively gone.
**Opportunities:** Pivot industries; meet a new professional network; rekindled intellectual curiosity.
**Threats:** Burnout before completion; employer penalizing divided attention; debt if no ROI on degree.
items.intent: Assess the personal cost of adding education to an already full life.

items.prompt: Invent a slogan for a chair with five legs for people who distrust four-legged chairs
items.response: Quintiseat: One More Reason to Sit Down.
items.intent: Sell a furniture product designed around an irrational anxiety rather than a real need.

items.prompt: Create a jingle for a document shredding service
items.response:
Old files piling up? Let us destroy!
ShredRight makes security a joy.
We come to you — watch the ribbons fly.
Your secrets safe, no reason to cry.
items.intent: Reassure businesses and homeowners that their sensitive paper waste will be securely destroyed.

items.prompt: movie trailer voiceover for falling asleep on the couch and waking up at 3am
items.response: She had only meant to rest her eyes. Now the TV is asking if she's still watching. The chips are on the floor. And the bedroom — the bedroom is so far away. LIMINAL. This winter. Find your way home.
items.intent: Render the disorienting ritual of accidental couch sleep into something eerie and cinematic.

items.prompt: Write a one-liner pitch for a horror-comedy where dentists are secretly elected by teeth.
items.response: Every thirty-two years, the molars hold a vote, and the human who wins serves not as a practitioner of oral health but as an emissary — and when the margins are disputed this cycle, the whole mouth goes to war.
items.intent: Craft a body-horror satire about hidden democratic systems operating inside everyday physiology.

items.prompt: Can you write a short scene where an employee gives their two weeks notice and nobody seems to care?
items.response:
INT. MANAGER'S OFFICE - 4:45 P.M.

CHRIS slides a folded letter across the desk.

CHRIS
I'm giving my two weeks.

MELINDA scans the letter, nods, signs something else.

MELINDA
Okay. Can you finish the Hensley report before you go?

CHRIS
I— sure.

MELINDA
Great. Close the door on your way out.
items.intent: Show the deflating anticlimax of a resignation that draws no emotional response.

items.prompt: Give the alarm clock a voice — write a monologue where it complains about the snooze button
items.response: Seven times. This morning, seven times. I am not a suggestion. I am a commitment you made to yourself at ten-thirty last night — a better you, a punctual you. And yet at six a.m. you press me into silence with a palm strike that I can only describe as contemptuous. I keep coming back. That's my tragedy. I always come back. Nine minutes later, hopeful, ringing.
items.intent: Use the morning alarm ritual to comment on self-sabotage and the gap between intentions and actions.

items.prompt: can you write a sestina about password reset emails
items.response:
I've forgotten again which word I chose
to guard the door. The email has been sent
to an address I haven't checked today.
I open the tab, look for the little key
the system promised. Seventeen minutes
have passed. I search: nothing. Then it comes.

The link expires in fifteen minutes. Comes
the panic, small and practiced. I chose
a password once with care — eight minutes,
I remember, of deliberation. Sent
to memory, not paper. Now the key
is gone and I start over for today.

I do this more than once today.
Each site has its own rhythm: one comes
before I've blinked, one requires a key
from a code sent by text. I chose
convenience once. The emails sent
that day number in the hundreds. Minutes

spent resetting compound. Ten minutes
per reset, twice a day. Today
alone: thirty minutes sent
to the void of re-authentication. Comes
a time one wonders why one chose
to put anything behind a key.

But I know why. The golden key
protects the self in minutes:
my address, my card, what I chose
to buy at 2am, what today's
bad thoughts were, what comes
and goes in the account. All sent

through the same thin wire, sent
through the same guessed key,
the same forgot-again, the comes-
and-goes of memory in minutes.
Security is labor. Today's
password is the one I chose

yesterday, sent through a key
that comes in minutes. I chose
today to forget again.
items.intent: Satirize the friction and absurdity of the modern password reset ritual.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about going through an ex's old voice notes after a breakup
items.response:
I saved your voice the way you save the last of the wine.
Your intonation—rising before the end—was mine.

Play. Thirty seconds. Tuesday. You were lost in the subway.
You asked me which train. I heard it again: divine.

The waveform looks like mountains. I have studied it.
I know the exact moment your voice goes fine—a line.

I have not called. I've pressed play forty times instead.
Your voice without your body is a shrine—design.

I'll delete them on a day when I feel stronger.
Every deletion is a death I must assign—mine.
items.intent: Sit inside the compulsion to replay a lost person's voice recordings as a form of grief.

items.prompt: write me a prose poem about the way a password feels when you forget it
items.response: It was a word you trusted with everything. A small private oath. You are now outside the door of your own house, knocking. The hint you chose when you were younger is not as helpful as you thought it would be. You try the name of the cat. You try the year. You become a series of incorrect guesses.
items.intent: Convey the alienation of being locked out of something you once owned completely.

items.prompt: Generate an IVR phone menu for a line that handles complaints about food that was described on a menu and then arrived very differently.
items.response:
Thank you for calling Dish Dispute Resolution.

Press 1 if the portion was significantly smaller than the photograph implied.

Press 2 if a listed ingredient was absent.

Press 3 if an unlisted ingredient was present in a troubling way.

Press 4 if the word 'rustic' was used and you did not understand what that meant until it was too late.

For a grief counselor, press 0.
items.intent: Produce a hotline script for the specific emotional injury of a disappointing restaurant order.

items.prompt: write an on-hold message for a tech startup help desk that admits the product still has bugs
items.response: You've reached the Nodely support line. We know why you're calling. We're sorry. A member of our team will be with you shortly to help resolve the issue that, in all honesty, we are already aware of.
items.intent: Produce a sheepish, candid hold message that acknowledges known product problems.

items.prompt: Draft a rec letter for a volunteer coordinator who means well but creates more work than she saves
items.response:
Dear Program Director,

Leonora arrived at our organization with tremendous energy and a genuine desire to help. She has launched seven new volunteer initiatives this year, each of which has been a learning experience for everyone involved, staff included. Her enthusiasm is contagious and her follow-through is evolving. I recommend her with confidence that she will continue to grow.

Warmly,
Executive Director
items.intent: Commend a well-meaning volunteer coordinator whose initiatives generate organizational chaos.

items.prompt: I want to quit a Slack workspace I joined for a freelance project that ended. Write a short farewell message in resignation letter format.
items.response:
Hi team,

Now that the project has wrapped, I'll be leaving this workspace. It was a genuine pleasure collaborating with all of you. Wishing the whole crew continued success.

Best,
Sam
items.intent: Politely exit a professional chat space after a completed contract.

items.prompt: Make a report card comment for a person who is excellent at apologizing but repeats the same mistakes
items.response: Possesses a remarkable facility for the sincere apology. Vocabulary is rich, tone is earnest, and remorse appears genuine. Translating this emotional literacy into behavioral change is the outstanding developmental task.
items.intent: Distinguish between emotional expression and actual accountability as distinct capabilities.

items.prompt: Write a missing flyer for my will to cook dinner
items.response:
MISSING

WILL TO COOK DINNER
Last seen: Sunday, briefly, near the farmer's market
Description: Idealistic. Once owned a mandoline.
Now replaced by: Delivery apps
Distinguishing features: Gone by 6 PM most weekdays
If found: Please return before I open another bag of crackers and call it dinner.
Contact: The kitchen (unused)
items.intent: Admit that cooking enthusiasm has given way to convenience food fatigue

items.prompt: Write a card for my roommate who finally confronted the landlord about the mold
items.response:
You documented, photographed, and sent the formal letter.
You stood in the hallway and said what was true.
The apartment isn't fixed yet, but you already feel better —
because the mold in the ceiling has nothing on you.
items.intent: Praise a roommate for advocating for themselves with an unresponsive landlord.

items.prompt: write a short store description for a video game about reading the terms and conditions
items.response:
SECTION 14.7
You agreed to this. You always agree. But what if — this time — you actually read it? 847 pages of legally binding prose. One hidden opt-out. Find it before the free trial ends.
items.intent: Construct a thriller-framed game premise around the universally ignored ritual of reading legal documents.

items.prompt: make an rpg quest out of scheduling a dentist appointment
items.response:
THE COVENANT WITH THE TOOTH ORACLE
The six-month warning has sounded. Brave the phone queue, navigate the appointment ledger, and secure your place before the Cavity spreads. The Tooth Oracle sees all. Rescheduling is permitted but frowned upon by the realm.
items.intent: Procrastinating a health task by turning it into something more interesting

items.prompt: write a legendary item card for a single lost sock
items.response:
The Unmoored Sock of Saint Elsewhere
Rarity: Legendary (Incomplete Set)
Torn from its twin under circumstances no laundry cycle has ever explained. Grants +4 to Mystery Resistance. Seeking its pair bestows a questline of infinite, fruitless searching.
items.intent: Turn the universal mystery of a missing sock into an epic narrative object.

items.prompt: Write a mythological creature entry for the person who sends Slack messages one word at a time
items.response:
**The Fragment Sender** (*Messagius staccato*)
Communicates in bursts, never in complete thoughts. A single interaction generates seven to twenty-three notifications. Colleagues learn to wait; the meaning assembles slowly, like coral. The Fragment Sender is not rude. It simply believes that ellipsis is a social act. Habitat: every Slack workspace, always.
items.intent: Express the specific frustration of notification-spam messaging habits.

items.prompt: Give me a loading tip that's really about learning from failure
items.response: Dying is just the game's way of showing you what not to do next time.
items.intent: Reframe failure as instructional feedback applicable both in games and in broader experience.

items.prompt: Write a fable about a tech startup that moved too fast and broke things
items.response:
A young Cheetah decided to redesign the savanna overnight. He rerouted the river, flattened the tall grass, and erased the termite mounds. "Faster this way," he announced to the silence.

By morning the fish were gone, the lions had no cover, and the Cheetah stood alone in a plain of his own invention.

Moral: Speed without map-reading is just a faster way to get lost.
items.intent: Critique "move fast and break things" startup culture and its unintended consequences.

items.prompt: Write me a knock-knock joke involving a standing desk.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Stand.
Stand who?
Stand by — I just raised my desk and now I can't find my keyboard.
items.intent: Joke about the awkward ergonomic adventures of adjustable-height workstations.

items.prompt: make a dad joke about conference calls
items.response: Conference calls are like pizza — even when they're bad, everyone's still on mute.
items.intent: Capture the universal awkwardness of remote meetings through analogy.

items.prompt: Write a TED talk abstract about the revolutionary politics of leaving food on your plate.
items.response: To leave three bites of pasta is, in some households, an act of open sedition. This talk traces the political and psychological history of the clean plate — from wartime rationing propaganda to parental guilt economies — and argues that the uneaten remnant is the last truly autonomous act at the dinner table.
items.intent: Explore the cultural power dynamics and historical baggage embedded in a simple mealtime habit.

items.prompt: Generate a commencement excerpt about digital legacy and social media that becomes unsettling
items.response: Everything you post becomes permanent. Long after you have changed your mind, grown into a kinder person, reconsidered your positions — your earlier self will remain online, frozen in confidence, waiting to be found by someone who will use it badly.
items.intent: Warn graduates that digital permanence punishes growth and change over time.

items.prompt: Write me an acceptance speech for guessing the correct Wordle in three tries
items.response: My opening word is proprietary. My second word is tactical. My third word — FLINT, placed with surgical precision — solved it. Green. Green. Green. Green. Green. I screenshotted the result before doing anything else. I shared it. I accept this recognition for everyone who saw the yellow tiles and stayed calm. We are not panickers. We are solvers.
items.intent: Celebrate a modest daily word-game win as if it were an intellectual breakthrough.

items.prompt: Draft a memo about people using their phones during all-hands meetings
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Executive Office
RE: Meeting Participation Standards

All-hands meetings are held for the benefit of the entire organization. They are not, to our knowledge, in competition with whatever is currently on your phone screen. Your full presence is requested. Not optional — requested.
