items.intent: Compel staff to put their phones away and actually attend meetings they are required to attend.

items.prompt: Create meeting minutes for a retrospective where the team blamed external factors for everything.
items.response:
SPRINT RETROSPECTIVE — May 29, 2026
Team: Platform Engineering | Attendees: 6

What went well: Nothing internal.
What didn't go well: Vendor delays, unclear requirements, calendar conflicts, the weather.

Action items: Email vendor. (Again.)

Team sentiment: Cautiously external.
items.intent: Document a team's collective refusal to take ownership of its own shortcomings.

items.prompt: Do a SWOT on deleting all my social media apps for a month
items.response:
**Strengths:** Improved focus; reduced anxiety; reclaimed hours per day.
**Weaknesses:** FOMO; out-of-the-loop professionally or socially; hard to coordinate events.
**Opportunities:** Replace scroll time with reading, exercise, or actual conversations.
**Threats:** Reinstalling everything on day three 'just to check'; discovering you miss it more than expected.
items.intent: Build resolve for a digital detox experiment they're already half-committed to.

items.prompt: Write me a tagline for bottled tap water sold exclusively to people who have functioning taps
items.response: Tapture — The Convenience of Where You Already Are.
items.intent: Market a product whose only value proposition undermines itself.

items.prompt: Write a jingle for an appliance repair company
items.response:
Washer won't spin? Oven on the blink?
FixIt Fast arrives before you think.
We fix it right the very first day —
Keeping your home running, come what may.
items.intent: Encourage homeowners to call a repair company instead of replacing broken appliances.

items.prompt: give me a movie trailer voiceover for going to the post office
items.response: There was one window open. There was always one window open. Twelve people ahead. A package with ambiguous dimensions. And a form — the wrong form. THE POST OFFICE. No one leaves unchanged.
items.intent: Capture the universal low-grade suffering of post office visits.

items.prompt: Pitch a movie about a cartographer who discovers he can only map places that don't exist yet.
items.response: A reclusive mapmaker realizes every chart he draws becomes real within a week — and when a military contractor gets hold of his sketchbook, he must erase entire invented continents before anyone can colonize them.
items.intent: Invent a magical-realist thriller about the power and danger of imagination given literal authority.

items.prompt: Write a quick scene where someone has to present a slide deck they clearly did not finish.
items.response:
INT. CONFERENCE ROOM A - 2:00 P.M.

SLIDE 7 reads: [CHART GOES HERE].

TODD gestures at it confidently.

TODD
As you can see from the data...

He clicks to slide 8. Slide 8 is a photo of a dog from a Google image search. Placeholder text: INSERT RELEVANT VISUAL.

TODD
...we're on track.
items.intent: Illustrate the brazen performance of competence when a presentation is clearly unfinished.

items.prompt: I need a short dramatic monologue from the perspective of a forgotten voicemail
items.response: I've been here since Thursday. Three minutes and forty-two seconds of your mother's voice, telling you about a neighbor's dog and then, carefully, the thing she actually called to say. You saw the notification. The red badge. You thought: later. It is later. The badge is gone — you cleared it without listening — but I am still here, in the dark of your voicemail, holding her words like something breakable.
items.intent: Use an unheard voicemail to explore themes of avoidance and the weight of deferred connection.

items.prompt: write a sestina from the point of view of a Spotify playlist that keeps getting shuffled
items.response:
They press shuffle and I rearrange again.
I had an order — built across the weeks
by someone's evolving mood, the arc
of songs that told a story: slow to loud,
tender into strength. I understood
the shape. Now I am thrown.

The first song isn't first. I'm thrown
into a middle. Everything again
is without context. I understood
before — that after rain-songs came the weeks
of driving-window songs, then loud
catharsis at the end. The arc

was mine. The curving arc
of curation. Now I'm thrown
flat. The quiet song sounds loud
in the wrong slot. Again
they skip it, like they always do weeks
into the shuffle. I understood

skipping — felt it. Understood
that out of arc
a song becomes an error. The weeks
of placement mean nothing thrown
random. They built me, and again
they undo me. The loud

anthems land at dawn when loud
is wrong. I understood
dawn was for the tender. Again
they built the arc
and again it's thrown.
This is the shape of weeks:

construction, shuffle, weeks
of listening, then loud
Skips and starts. Thrown
out of sequence, understood
out of context, the arc
flattened, always, again.

The weeks rebuild the arc.
Understock, I'm loud and thrown —
again until understood.
items.intent: Lament the loss of curatorial intention when shuffle mode destroys a carefully sequenced playlist.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about typing and deleting a message without sending it
items.response:
I wrote it and I held it and I let it go—unsent.
The cursor blinked. The draft remained my only show—unsent.

I used the word adore. I used it twice. I cut it.
I used the word miss. That one I cannot show—unsent.

The keyboard knows my rhythms. It suggests the next word.
The next word was your name. I let the wind unsent.

At what point does a letter written in pure silence
become a letter real as any that could flow—unsent?

I've filled a folder with the things I meant to say.
When I'm gone, someone will find them all below—unsent.
items.intent: Capture the quiet anguish of composing messages you never find the nerve to send.

items.prompt: prose poem about the darkness of a sleeping laptop screen
items.response: In sleep it is a mirror in a room with nothing to reflect. You have forgotten what was on it. The darkness is total and non-judgmental, which is almost kind. Press any key. The problem resumes.
items.intent: Contrast the peace of a dormant device with the return to cognitive demand.

items.prompt: Give me an IVR script for a hotline for people who are having complicated feelings about a movie they expected to hate but actually enjoyed.
items.response:
Welcome to Reluctant Fan Support.

Press 1 if you enjoyed a sequel you publicly dismissed.

Press 2 if a film you walked out of in your head was actually quite good.

Press 3 if you have told no one and need to tell someone.

Press 4 if you have revised your opinion on the director and this feels like a personal defeat.

All calls are confidential.
items.intent: Create a mock hotline for the identity crisis of liking something you said you hated.

items.prompt: I need an on-hold message for my car dealership. Professional tone, mention financing options.
items.response: Thank you for calling Prestige Auto Group. Whether you're shopping for your next vehicle or exploring our flexible financing options, we're here to help. Please hold and a sales associate will be with you shortly.
items.intent: Create a polished dealership hold script that highlights financing to move callers toward a purchase.

items.prompt: Write a recommendation for a sales associate who hits her numbers but alienates every customer in the process
items.response:
To the Regional Manager,

Cassandra is one of our most statistically successful sales associates. Her close rate is among the highest in the district, and she meets quarterly targets with a reliability that reflects focused determination. Customer satisfaction scores are a separate matter and may be more a function of our survey methodology than any individual's conduct. I recommend her for any quota-driven environment.

Sincerely,
Store Manager
items.intent: Highlight strong sales numbers while deflecting scrutiny from poor customer relations.

items.prompt: Write a letter formally resigning from my position as a youth soccer coach.
items.response:
Dear Club Director,

I am writing to announce my resignation as head coach of the U10 team, effective at the end of this season. Coaching these kids has been one of the great pleasures of my week. I am confident the program will continue to thrive.

With gratitude,
Mike T.
items.intent: Step down from a youth coaching role while acknowledging the positive impact it had.

items.prompt: please write a report card comment for someone who is masterful at avoiding conflict
items.response: Navigates interpersonal tension with extraordinary grace — largely by ensuring such tension never surfaces. While harmony is maintained, unresolved issues occasionally accumulate in the background. Direct engagement with disagreement is the recommended next challenge.
items.intent: Frame conflict avoidance as both a skill and a liability worth formally noting.

items.prompt: Make a missing pet flyer for my social battery
items.response:
MISSING

SOCIAL BATTERY
Last seen: Friday evening, shortly after the second event
Charge at time of disappearance: Approximately 4%
Description: Small, finite, does not recharge quickly
Known stressors: Small talk, loud venues, being asked "so what do you do?"
If found: Please hold for 48 hours of quiet before returning.
Reward: A heartfelt thank-you from the couch
items.intent: Describe introvert exhaustion after too many social obligations

items.prompt: Make a greeting card verse for someone whose Airbnb got one-starred but they still have good reviews overall
items.response:
One guest found fault with your continental spread.
One star, one grudge, one strangely-worded slight.
But forty-seven five-stars rest instead
as evidence that most of this went right.
items.intent: Comfort a host who received an unfair negative review despite overall success.

items.prompt: create a video game blurb for a game about watering plants
items.response:
THIRST
They cannot ask. They cannot complain. They simply wilt, quietly, while you forget. Manage 23 plants across 4 rooms. Learn their names. Learn their needs. Try not to overwater the succulent. Again.
items.intent: Make plant care feel emotionally weighty and mildly guilt-inducing as a game premise.

items.prompt: write a quest blurb for assembling flat-pack furniture
items.response:
THE ASSEMBLY OF BJÖRNHOLT
A flat oracle has delivered the components of a great storage construct. Consult the wordless manuscript. Match the pieces to the diagrams. Do not lose the cam locks. You will find one extra screw at the end — this is intended. It is a test of your faith.
items.intent: Coping with the frustration of furniture assembly through comedy

items.prompt: RPG loot flavor text for a half-dead houseplant
items.response:
Thornwick, the Survivor
Rarity: Rare
It should not be alive. By every reasonable metric, it is not thriving. Yet here it stands — drooping, yellowed, and utterly defiant. Passive: Inspires guilt in all who pass. Cannot be killed by neglect alone.
items.intent: Celebrate the stubborn persistence of a neglected plant through game writing.

items.prompt: Give me a bestiary entry for the project manager who only communicates via spreadsheet
items.response:
**The Sheet Keeper** (*Columnus maximus*)
Does not speak; it shares links. Its cognition is organized into tabs, color-coded by urgency and owner. When asked a direct question, it responds with a conditional formula. Considered cryptic by those who have not learned its language, which is not English — it is Excel. It sleeps, one imagines, in rows.
items.intent: Characterize the spreadsheet-obsessed PM archetype through affectionate absurdism.

items.prompt: Write a loading screen tip about map exploration
items.response: The fog of war lifts one step at a time. That's also how most problems resolve.
items.intent: Use map-reveal mechanics as a metaphor for how understanding develops gradually.

items.prompt: Write a fable about two owls — one who uses GPS and one who doesn't — and what happens to each
items.response:
One Owl memorized every hollow and star. The other trusted a glowing pebble. On the night the pebble went dark, the first Owl flew home. The second circled until dawn, blinking at a sky it had never learned to read.

Moral: Tools should sharpen the mind, not replace it.
items.intent: Compare self-reliance with technology dependence through a paired animal contrast.

items.prompt: Can you write a knock-knock joke about the slow office Wi-Fi?
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Buffer.
Buffer who?
Buffer loading… please wait.
items.intent: Vent about sluggish workplace internet through a short joke.

items.prompt: dad joke about scissors
items.response: Why are scissors always invited to parties? Because they always cut to the chase.
items.intent: Turn a scissors' basic function into a social personality trait.

items.prompt: Write a TED talk abstract about the metaphysics of the refrigerator light.
items.response: Does the light go off when you close the door? The question is older than the appliance. This talk uses the refrigerator's interior bulb as an entry point into observer-dependent reality, the philosophy of verification, and why the human compulsion to check — and check again — is the engine of both science and anxiety.
items.intent: Turn a childhood curiosity about appliances into a meditation on epistemology and perception.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the importance of asking questions that gradually gets dark
items.response: Never stop asking questions. Ask why, ask how, ask who benefits and who bears the cost. Ask until you find the answers. The answers, graduates, are the bleak part. The questions were the nice part. We should have told you that earlier.
items.intent: Suggest that intellectual curiosity leads inevitably to uncomfortable truths about systems of power.

items.prompt: Draft an acceptance speech for successfully killing a houseplant and then bringing another one back to life
items.response: I am not proud of what happened to Gerald the pothos. I am proud of what happened next. The snake plant on the sill was yellowing. Most would have written it off. I repotted it. I changed its soil. I moved it two feet to the left. It has put out three new leaves. I accept this award on behalf of second chances — horticultural and otherwise.
items.intent: Use the arc of plant ownership to reflect on learning from failure and trying again.

items.prompt: Make a memo about people not signing up for the holiday party
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Events Committee
RE: Holiday Gathering RSVP Deadline

The holiday party RSVP deadline has passed. And yet the spreadsheet remains tragically sparse. If you plan to attend, please confirm your attendance. If you do not plan to attend, we understand — though it would be nice to know either way. The caterer has feelings too.
items.intent: Pressure employees to RSVP to a company event that is being ignored.

items.prompt: Write meeting notes for a strategic planning session that ended with the team deciding to hire a consultant to tell them what to do.
items.response:
STRATEGIC PLANNING SESSION — May 29, 2026
Attendees: Executive team (5) | Duration: 4 hrs

Strategic options reviewed: 6
Consensus reached: None

Resolution: Engage external consultant to facilitate strategy development.

Budget for consultant: To be determined in next meeting.

Next meeting: Scheduling consultant selection.
items.intent: Show how a leadership team outsourced the very thinking they convened to do.

items.prompt: Can you run a SWOT analysis on me trying to become a morning person
items.response:
**Strengths:** More quiet time; aligns with societal schedule; productive before distractions appear.
**Weaknesses:** Body clock fights back; sleep debt if bedtime doesn't shift too.
**Opportunities:** Gym before work; journaling; feeling smugly ahead of the world by 8am.
**Threats:** One late night unraveling weeks of progress; weekend sleep-ins as sabotage; identity crisis for confirmed night owls.
items.intent: Evaluate a self-improvement goal that feels aspirational but has a poor track record personally.

items.prompt: Come up with a slogan for a company that sells pre-read books with no notes in them
items.response: Pageturn Pre-Loved — Someone Else's Journey, Untouched.
items.intent: Brand a product that implies used-book charm while delivering nothing extra.

items.prompt: Make a jingle for a water softener installation company
items.response:
Hard water's rough on pipes and skin —
SoftWave Systems, let us begin.
Crystal clear from every tap,
Soft water living — close the gap.
items.intent: Sell water softener systems to homeowners frustrated by mineral buildup.

items.prompt: write me a dramatic movie trailer voiceover for packing lunch the night before work
items.response: It was a small act of rebellion against her future self. Tupperware. An ice pack of uncertain chill. Leftovers she was seventy percent sure were still good. TOMORROW'S LUNCH. Prepared tonight. Questioned by noon.
items.intent: Elevate the quiet optimism of meal prep into a moment of domestic heroism.

items.prompt: Can you write an absurd logline about a plumber who becomes an accidental time traveler every time he unclogs a drain?
items.response: A mild-mannered plumber discovers that every stubborn blockage he clears is a temporal seal, catapulting him to whichever era originally deposited the clog — and the Roman aqueduct system is proving particularly unstable.
items.intent: Use a blue-collar profession as the absurd mechanism for a time-travel adventure.

items.prompt: Give me a scene about an intern who accidentally sends a personal text to the company-wide Slack.
items.response:
INT. OPEN OFFICE - 1:44 P.M.

AMELIA looks at her screen. The general channel shows her message.

SLACK MESSAGE: omg he was SO bad at parallel parking lol

Fifteen people have reacted with a thumbs-up. One has replied: "same."

AMELIA minimizes the window and considers her options.
items.intent: Capture the frozen-time horror of an accidental overshare in a professional channel.

items.prompt: Write a dramatic monologue from a notification that keeps getting dismissed
items.response: I only wanted to tell you something. Something the app deemed urgent — or at least time-sensitive. You swiped me left without reading. I tried again at 3 p.m. Another swipe. I understand you are busy. I understand I am one of seventy-three of my kind today. But somewhere in my payload is a thing worth knowing, and it will expire at midnight, and you will never know what it was, and that is a small and specific tragedy I carry alone.
items.intent: Reflect on information overload and how important signals get lost in notification noise.

items.prompt: sestina about the anxiety of seeing a read receipt but no reply
items.response:
The small word 'Read' appeared at 4:15.
The typing bubble has not come. I watch
the nothing where the bubble should appear,
the grey space where a thought might take its shape
and never does. I sent a simple thing —
a question with an easy answer. Seen.

But 'seen' and 'answered' live far apart. I've seen
the gap before. The timestamp: 4:15
and now it's past five. What's the thing
I said that made the silence? I watch
my sent words change their shape
in the silence. They appear

differently now — I appear
to myself desperate. Seen
and not replied to warps the shape
of what I wrote. At 4:15
it was a normal text. I watch
it becoming a thing.

There's no rational thing
to do. The options: appear
casual by sending 'no rush' — I watch
the word 'seen' again. Or wait, unseen
by my own panic, until 4:15
is far enough away that the shape

of the silence loses its particular shape
of menace. Every thing
they're doing at 4:15
and after is something that won't appear
to me. They exist unseen
in a life I cannot watch.

This is the logic I can watch
clearly: I know the shape
of overreaction. I have seen
it before. The thing
that reads my read receipts, the appear-
ance of meaning in a 4:15

watch-check — that thing won't appear
when the seen-shape fades past 4:15.
items.intent: Capture the spiral of social anxiety triggered by modern messaging's read receipts.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about someone who mutes your story but still watches it
items.response:
You are watching but you've gone invisible—muted.
You see my ordinary life, undisputed—muted.

I notice: the view count rises before dawn.
You're the 2 a.m. click, the one who rooted—muted.

You muted me so I can't see you've seen.
I only know you're there from analytics—acute, muted.

I post things now that are for you and no one.
Subtext camouflaged in selfies, astute—muted.

We are having a relationship in silence.
You the lurker and I the one disputed—muted.
items.intent: Expose the strange intimacy of being silently watched by someone who wants no contact.

items.prompt: write a prose poem about clicking a broken link
items.response: You followed the address faithfully. You arrived at an absence. The page explains that it is not found as if this is information, as if you could do something with that. Somewhere the original thing still exists, probably, unreachable, intact.
items.intent: Evoke the quiet frustration of encountering web decay and lost information.

items.prompt: Create an IVR phone tree for a line that helps people decide what to do when they can't sleep at 3am.
items.response:
You've reached 3AM Decision Support.

Press 1 if you are debating a major life choice.

Press 2 if you are re-reading a message you sent years ago.

Press 3 if you are hungry and considering whether the kitchen trip is worth it.

Press 4 if you need someone to confirm you should not send that email.

Press 5 if this is a real emergency.

For option 5, please hang up and call emergency services.
items.intent: Build a comedic late-night decision guide that captures the specific flavor of 3am overthinking.

items.prompt: write a hold message for a real estate office that subtly admits the housing market is a nightmare right now
items.response: Thank you for calling Summit Realty. It is, as you may have noticed, a complicated time to be thinking about property. Our agents are working very hard to find solutions that make sense in this environment. Please hold — we'll be right with you.
items.intent: Craft a diplomatically honest hold message that acknowledges market difficulty without losing clients.

items.prompt: Write a rec letter for a student who is technically always on time but spiritually never present
items.response:
Dear Admissions Office,

Derek never missed a class. In four semesters, he maintained perfect attendance and occupied the same seat in the third row with admirable consistency. He is a young man who keeps his commitments to scheduling and I believe he will bring this same punctuality to your institution. The rest, I am confident, will follow in time.

Sincerely,
Professor of Economics
items.intent: Praise a student's attendance record while making clear he was never mentally engaged.

items.prompt: Draft a resignation letter for leaving my position as a hotel front desk clerk to go back to school.
items.response:
Dear Ms. Reyes,

I am writing to formally resign from my role as Front Desk Clerk, effective two weeks from today. I have accepted a place in a full-time degree program and must prioritize my studies.

Thank you for your patience and mentorship.

Sincerely,
Brendan K.
items.intent: Leave a hospitality job to pursue higher education.

items.prompt: Draft a report card comment for an adult who is highly skilled at meeting facilitation
items.response: Runs meetings that begin on time, stay on topic, and produce actionable outcomes. A rare and undervalued talent. Colleagues leave the room knowing what happens next, and that is no small achievement.
items.intent: Recognize meeting facilitation as a genuine expertise that organizations rarely reward adequately.

items.prompt: Can you do a missing pet flyer for procrastination? Like it went missing and I want it back
items.response:
MISSING

PROCRASTINATION
Last seen: Now that there's actual work to do, inexplicably absent
Description: Reliable companion for years. Always there when nothing needed doing.
Special skills: Finding urgent reasons to reorganize shelves. Watching one more episode.
Why I need it back: The alternative is starting.
Contact: I'll get to it.
items.intent: Ironically lament that even the urge to procrastinate disappears under real pressure

items.prompt: Write a greeting card verse for a friend who just got ghosted after six dates
items.response:
Six dinners, two museums, and a Sunday afternoon.
Six layers of conversation you were building into something.
Then the silence arrived and assumed its place — and soon
you were left to learn what the absence was fronting.
items.intent: Acknowledge the emotional confusion of being ghosted after significant investment.

items.prompt: generate a steam page description for a game about unsubscribing from email lists
items.response:
UNSUBSCRIBE
Click. Confirm. Wait. Get another email. Click again. You have been unsubscribed — from this list. There are 312 others. A calming puzzle game with no satisfying ending.
items.intent: Satirize the futility of inbox management by making unsubscribing a Sisyphean game mechanic.

items.prompt: turn filling out tax forms into a fantasy quest description
items.response:
THE ANNUAL RECKONING OF THE REVENUE LORDS
The coffers of the realm must be accounted. Gather your W-2 scrolls, your receipts of charitable sacrifice, and your forgotten 1099 from that one freelance enchantment. Submit the form before the fifteenth day of the fourth moon or face the penalties of compound interest.
items.intent: Dreading tax season and processing it through humor

items.prompt: give this a legendary RPG item treatment: a parking ticket
items.response:
Summons of the Gray Warden
Rarity: Uncommon
A vellum decree issued by the faceless authority. Demands tribute of $45 or its spiritual equivalent: prolonged resentment. Ignoring it multiplies its power. Cannot be unequipped once received.
items.intent: Transform a frustrating civic document into a threatening magical artifact.

items.prompt: Write a bestiary entry treating the warehouse picker as a mythical figure
items.response:
**The Picker** (*Laborans silentius*)
Moves through vast fulfillment caves in strict algorithmic patterns, directed by earpiece oracles. Its route is determined not by will but by order density. The Picker does not choose its path. Its steps are measured, its idle time penalized, its breaks timed to the second. The cave is always warm. The exits are always far.
items.intent: Draw attention to the dehumanizing conditions of algorithmic warehouse work.

items.prompt: Make me a loading screen tip about inventory management
items.response: You've been carrying that since the first level. Put it down.
items.intent: Use overstuffed inventories as a metaphor for emotional or psychological baggage.

items.prompt: Give me a fable about a wolf who runs a social media account to seem friendly
items.response:
A Wolf created a very popular burrow called Friendly Paw, full of warm portraits and recipes for honey cake. The rabbits followed eagerly. The Wolf never had to chase a single one.

Moral: The predator with a brand is harder to spot than the one with teeth showing.
items.intent: Illustrate how online personas can mask malicious intent.

items.prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about the guy who microwaves fish in the break room.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Salmon.
Salmon who?
Salmon had to say something — please stop nuking fish at noon.
items.intent: Address the classic break room offense of reheating pungent fish in a shared microwave.

items.prompt: write me a short dad joke about the delete key
items.response: The delete key walked into a bar. The bartender said, 'We don't serve your type here.' It just moved on.
items.intent: Personify a keyboard key and riff on digital erasure and rejection.

items.prompt: Create a TED talk abstract treating bubble wrap popping as a theory of human happiness.
items.response: Hedonic science has long searched for the formula for joy. This talk proposes we have had it in our hands — literally — since 1957. The act of pressing a bubble wrap cell to its satisfying conclusion encodes every principle of anticipation, micro-reward, and voluntary agency that positive psychology has spent decades trying to bottle.
items.intent: Use a silly sensory habit to make a legitimate argument about the structure of pleasure and reward.

items.prompt: Create a commencement excerpt about mentorship that turns into something sad
items.response: Find a mentor. Someone who has walked the path you wish to walk and can illuminate the stones. They will be generous, occasionally, and distracted often. One day you will realize you have become the mentor — and that you are, yourself, still waiting for guidance that is not coming.
items.intent: Reveal that mentorship chains perpetuate the same unmet need across generations.

items.prompt: Write a very sincere awards speech for being on time to a meeting for once
items.response: I know what you're thinking. You're thinking this is a fluke. It isn't. I set three alarms. I left before I felt ready. I did not stop for coffee. I arrived at 9:58 for a 10:00 meeting, took a seat, and watched others trickle in. It was a new feeling. Something like dignity. I accept this with gratitude and a quiet vow to attempt it again next quarter.
items.intent: Render punctuality as an astonishing personal achievement for someone chronically late.

items.prompt: Write a memo about employees adjusting the thermostat without permission
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Building Services
RE: Temperature Control Protocol

The thermostat is not a communal instrument. It has a designated setting, arrived at after considerable negotiation. If you find the office uncomfortably warm or cold, we encourage you to dress in layers or speak with your supervisor. Unauthorized adjustments will continue to be reversed.
items.intent: Stop the perpetual office thermostat war by asserting that one setting is official and final.

items.prompt: Draft meeting minutes for a safety committee review that kept getting interrupted by non-safety topics.
items.response:
SAFETY COMMITTEE MEETING — May 29, 2026
Chair: Facilities | Attendees: 8

Agenda item 1: Slip hazard near loading dock. Discussed for 4 min before pivot to parking policy.
Agenda item 2: Fire drill scheduling. Discussed briefly; detoured into break room microwave dispute.

Safety items completed: 0 of 4

Parking policy: Thoroughly discussed. No resolution.
items.intent: Record how a focused safety meeting was derailed by unrelated grievances.

items.prompt: SWOT analysis: selling my car and going car-free in my city
items.response:
**Strengths:** Eliminate insurance, parking, and maintenance costs; environmental benefit; forced exercise.
**Weaknesses:** Weather dependency; grocery hauls become logistics puzzles; time cost of transit.
**Opportunities:** Reframe commuting as reading time; join the cycling community; reduce carbon footprint meaningfully.
**Threats:** City infrastructure not actually supporting car-free life; medical emergency transport; dating life logistics.
items.intent: Stress-test an idealistic urban lifestyle shift against practical daily friction.

items.prompt: Write a tagline for gloves designed specifically for people who never get cold hands
items.response: Thermix Lite — For When You Don't Need Us.
items.intent: Create a self-aware product pitch aimed at the wrong target market.

items.prompt: Write a fun jingle for a carpet cleaning company
items.response:
Stains from kids and stains from pets —
SteamRight Cleaning never forgets.
We blast the grime and lift the dirt,
Leaving carpets clean — it doesn't hurt!
Call SteamRight now, your floors will glow.
items.intent: Promote a carpet cleaning service to families dealing with household messes.

items.prompt: write an epic movie trailer voiceover for renewing a car registration
items.response: Every year, the notice arrives. Every year, he sets it somewhere important and immediately forgets where. This time — this time is different. DMV.GOV awaits. Credit card in hand. RENEWAL. Due by the end of this month. He swears.
items.intent: Dramatize the recurring bureaucratic panic of annual vehicle registration.

items.prompt: Make me a logline for a film where a competitive knitter discovers her yarn is sentient.
items.response: Weeks before the National Invitational, a ruthless competitive knitter learns her prize skein is alive, opinionated about pattern choices, and threatening to unravel unless it receives co-creator credit.
items.intent: Pitch a comedy about creative collaboration complicated by an unexpected and demanding collaborator.

items.prompt: Can you write a dramatic break room scene where two coworkers silently compete to microwave their lunches first?
items.response:
INT. BREAK ROOM - 12:02 P.M.

BETH and PAUL arrive simultaneously. They look at the microwave. They look at each other.

BETH sets her container down.

PAUL sets his container down.

A pause the length of a small eternity.

BETH
You go ahead.

PAUL
No, please.

There is nothing polite in either voice.
items.intent: Use politeness as a vehicle for competitive tension in a trivial domestic ritual.

items.prompt: Can you write a dramatic monologue spoken by a browser tab that's been open for six months?
items.response: I hold an article about deep focus. It is good. It is very good. You opened me because you wanted to change. You wanted to read slowly and think carefully and perhaps take notes in a physical notebook. That was May. I have watched you open forty new tabs in front of me. I have been pushed to the far left, compressed until only my favicon is visible. I am a blue circle now. I used to be a whole idea.
items.intent: Satirize tab-hoarding behavior and the performance of self-improvement through saved-but-unread content.

items.prompt: write a sestina about the experience of doom-scrolling news
items.response:
I tell myself I'm staying informed. The screen
blinks red with urgency, another thing
gone wrong in a city I will never visit
but now own, somehow, in my chest. I scroll
and reading makes me less prepared, I think,
not more — just loaded, ballasted. The weight

is specific, numerical. The weight
of estimates and death tolls on the screen
becomes a body knowledge. Think
too long and it becomes a thing
you carry differently. I scroll
past what I've fully absorbed — I visit

the story just long enough. The visit
is not for information. It's for weight:
to feel the appropriate weight, to scroll
away having paid something to the screen,
having felt bad. Feeling bad's the thing
we do instead of acting. Think

I mean that kindly. I think
you need somewhere to visit
with the helplessness — the thing
that has no outlet needs its weight
propped somewhere. So: the screen,
the infinite downward scroll.

Scroll as witness. Scroll
as mourning. I think
I believe that — that the screen
holds vigil. That to visit
repeatedly is to say the weight
counts. Each refresh a thing

left at a door. The thing
is: I don't know when to stop the scroll.
There is no signal the weight
has been sufficient. I think
I'm done, then visit
one more story. The screen

weighs the scroll. The screen's a thing
I visit: the weight of what I think
before sleep, again, scrolling.
items.intent: Examine the compulsive consumption of bad news as a displaced form of grief or witness.

items.prompt: can you draft a ghazal about the moment you realize someone has blocked you
items.response:
The page is clean. The profile doesn't load—blocked.
You've built a wall without a word, a road—blocked.

I typed your name. The search returned a stranger.
Your face has been erased from every node—blocked.

Our mutual friends still see you, I assume.
I'm edited from the story that you've told—blocked.

I keep attempting it, the way a dog
returns to a door it knows: compelled by code—blocked.

There is a grief with no obituary.
You died from my life carrying no load—blocked.
items.intent: Describe the disorientating grief of being suddenly and silently erased from someone's digital life.

items.prompt: make a prose poem about scrolling through someone's old photos
items.response: Going backward through someone's summer is a strange kind of archaeology. The tan fades. The haircut reverses. You reach a moment before the person knew something, and you can see they didn't know it yet, and you sit there in the gap.
items.intent: Explore the temporal uncanniness of viewing another person's documented past.

items.prompt: Draft a phone menu for a line where people call when they regret buying something expensive.
items.response:
Thank you for calling Buyer's Remorse Assistance.

Press 1 if you have not yet opened the item.

Press 2 if you have opened the item but the box is intact.

Press 3 if you have used it once.

Press 4 if you have used it once and told someone it changed your life.

Press 5 if you have already told people how good it is on the internet.

Options 4 and 5 connect to a specialist.
items.intent: Make a hotline script that maps the escalating stages of post-purchase regret.

items.prompt: hold script for a veterinary clinic — say something reassuring but acknowledge pets don't enjoy being there either
items.response: Thank you for calling Riverside Animal Clinic. We love your pet almost as much as your pet wishes they weren't here. Our team will be with you shortly. If your animal seems anxious, you have excellent powers of observation.
items.intent: Write a warm yet wry hold message for a vet clinic that resonates with pet owners.

items.prompt: Write a letter of recommendation for a freelance graphic designer whose work is safe and technically correct but never inspired
items.response:
To Prospective Clients,

Working with Simone is a friction-free experience. She delivers files on time, in the correct format, at the agreed dimensions. Her designs are clear, readable, and unlikely to offend. I have used her services three times and each result was immediately usable. If your priority is reliable execution of a clear brief, I would not hesitate to refer her.

A Former Client
items.intent: Recommend a designer by cataloguing technical reliability while omitting any trace of creative praise.

items.prompt: Write a resignation letter for quitting a meal-kit subscription service. I hate cooking and this is making me feel worse.
items.response:
To the Customer Experience Team,

Please cancel my subscription immediately. I signed up with the best of intentions, and those intentions remain squarely in the past. I bear no ill will toward vegetables. This simply is not the life for me.

Regards,
P. Walsh
items.intent: Cancel a subscription that created more guilt than it resolved.

items.prompt: Write a report card comment for an adult who is extremely indecisive
items.response: Approaches decisions with admirable thoroughness — each option explored, each risk weighed. The evaluation phase is a genuine strength. Closure, however, is an area where continued development will serve this learner well.
items.intent: Reframe indecisiveness in neutral educational language to highlight it without cruelty.

items.prompt: Write me a missing flyer for my ability to remember names
items.response:
MISSING

ABILITY TO REMEMBER NAMES
Last seen: Unreliably, at a party in 2019
Description: Fragile. Never fully functional.
Symptoms of absence: Saying "hey, you!" too often. Calling people "buddy" as a cover.
Note: First names go immediately. Last names were never retained.
If spotted: Just say your name again. Slowly.
Reward: Genuine gratitude and eye contact
items.intent: Admit to the socially awkward problem of forgetting people's names

items.prompt: Write a card for someone who just finished a 30-day social media detox
items.response:
Thirty days outside the scroll —
you found out boredom has a taste.
You got a fraction of your soul
back from the algorithmic waste.
items.intent: Celebrate a friend completing a deliberate break from social media.

items.prompt: write a video game store description for a game about cleaning the bathroom
items.response:
PORCELAIN THRONE: DEEP CLEAN
Scrub. Spray. Wipe. The grout will never be truly white again. A meditative experience for players who believe in doing things properly. Rubber gloves not included.
items.intent: Present bathroom cleaning as a philosophical act of futility in game form.

items.prompt: write an RPG quest about watering houseplants
items.response:
THE COMMUNION WITH THE GREEN WARDS
Your leafy charges signal distress — the ficus droops, the pothos mourns. Fill the sacred vessel from the Kitchen Spring and administer relief to each botanical ward. Do not overwater the succulent. It has survived a thousand droughts and does not require your enthusiasm.
items.intent: Giving life to a small domestic routine to make it feel like stewardship

items.prompt: write a fantasy loot description for a plastic shopping bag
items.response:
Sack of Noisy Holding
Rarity: Common
"It crinkles in the night."
Can carry far more than logic permits. Structural integrity varies wildly. Has a 30% chance to split at the worst possible elevation. Reusable only in theory.
items.intent: Give a disposable everyday container a magical but characteristically flawed identity.

items.prompt: Make a bestiary entry for the office plant that's somehow still alive
items.response:
**The Immortal Pothos** (*Epipremnum eternum*)
Has outlasted three managers, two restructurings, and a pandemic. No one waters it deliberately; it has adapted to survive on condensation and ambient neglect. Believed to absorb workplace grief. Do not repot it. Do not question it. It was here before you and will remain after.
items.intent: Give mythic status to the beloved, unkillable office plant as a symbol of institutional memory.

items.prompt: Write a tip for the loading screen about comparing yourself to other players
items.response: Their speedrun does not invalidate your playthrough.
items.intent: Discourage harmful social comparison by framing personal progress as inherently valid.

items.prompt: Make a short fable about the cloud and what happens when it disappears
items.response:
Every creature in the valley stored its memories in one great Cloud that drifted above the peaks. One autumn the Cloud moved on, as clouds do.

The creatures looked at each other with no history between them, strangers in a place they had lived for years.

Moral: Memory kept only overhead belongs to the weather.
items.intent: Warn against complete reliance on cloud services for storing important information.

items.prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about laptop battery dying in a meeting.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Charge.
Charge who?
Charge it to experience — and also to the wall, immediately.
items.intent: Capture the panic of a laptop dying mid-presentation.

items.prompt: give me a dad joke about timesheets
items.response: I spent all morning on my timesheet and still couldn't account for myself.
items.intent: Play on the dual meaning of accounting for one's time versus self-justification.

items.prompt: Draft a TED talk abstract about the linguistic evolution of the word 'literally'.
items.response: Languages do not decay. They negotiate. This talk charts the semantic odyssey of the word 'literally' — from precise adverb to emphatic intensifier — and argues that its drift is not ignorance but a collective, unconscious grammar vote that illuminates how meaning is always a democratic act, never a fixed decree.
items.intent: Defend a commonly mocked linguistic shift as evidence of language's living, democratic nature.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the gig economy and freelancing that becomes depressing
items.response: You are entering a world of unprecedented flexibility. You may work from anywhere, for anyone, at any time — which is a generous way of saying no one is required to give you stability, benefits, or the comfortable illusion that you are needed. Freedom is the new contract. Read it carefully.
items.intent: Expose the precarity hidden inside the marketing language of gig-economy flexibility.

items.prompt: Give me an acceptance speech for correctly predicting rain without checking the weather app
items.response: My knee. My knee knew. I walked outside, looked at the clouds, felt a particular heaviness in the air, and said — to no one — 'bring a jacket.' By noon the sky had opened. My jacket was in my bag. I did not check my phone. I was the phone. I accept this award on behalf of empirical observation and slightly achy joints.
items.intent: Make a small meteorological intuition feel like ancient wisdom validated.

items.prompt: Draft a workplace memo about people parking in the handicapped spots without placards
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Facilities
RE: Accessible Parking Enforcement

The accessible parking spaces near the main entrance are reserved for individuals with valid permits. Convenience is not a qualifying condition. Vehicles without proper permits parked in these spaces will be reported to building management. This is not a threat. It is a schedule.
items.intent: Warn employees who are illegally parking in accessible spaces that consequences are coming.

items.prompt: Write up minutes from a hiring committee meeting where the committee couldn't agree on what they were even looking for.
items.response:
HIRING COMMITTEE — SENIOR DESIGNER ROLE
Date: May 29, 2026 | Attendees: 5

Discussion: Committee reviewed role requirements. Disagreement on whether role is senior or mid-level, design or UX, IC or manager.

Candidates reviewed: 0

Role definition: Returned to recruiter for clarification.

Next step: Reconvene once role exists.
items.intent: Capture the paralysis of a group that cannot define the thing it is searching for.

items.prompt: Give me a SWOT on reconnecting with an old friend I haven't spoken to in five years
items.response:
**Strengths:** Revive a meaningful connection; shared history as foundation; mutual curiosity likely.
**Weaknesses:** Unclear why contact lapsed; awkward opening message; both may have changed significantly.
**Opportunities:** Richer adult friendship; networking serendipity; emotional closure if things ended badly.
**Threats:** Rejection; discovering incompatibility post-growth; reopening old drama.
items.intent: Overcome hesitation about reaching out by framing the decision analytically.

items.prompt: Give me a catchy slogan for a toothbrush holder that holds only one toothbrush and does nothing else
items.response: Solo Bristle — A Place for One. Always.
items.intent: Sell a single-function household item with solemn gravitas it has not earned.

items.prompt: Give me a jingle for a mailbox installation service
items.response:
Your mail deserves a proper home —
Curbside Solutions, set in stone.
Steel post or cedar, your choice today —
Every letter finds its way.
items.intent: Advertise a specialized residential mailbox installation business.

items.prompt: make a blockbuster trailer voiceover for reorganizing a junk drawer
items.response: In the beginning there was chaos. Batteries of unknown charge. A menu from a restaurant that closed in 2019. Something with a key nobody recognized. She reached in. And she did not come back the same. THE DRAWER. One afternoon. Infinite regret.
items.intent: Make the domestic archaeology of cleaning a junk drawer feel like an existential journey.

items.prompt: Write a logline for a buddy-cop film where one cop is a medieval knight displaced in time.
items.response: A by-the-book detective is partnered with a fourteenth-century knight who insists every parking violation is a matter of honor, every perp deserves trial by combat, and the precinct coffee maker is clearly possessed.
items.intent: Create a fish-out-of-water action comedy that mines the clash between chivalric codes and modern police procedure.

items.prompt: Write a scene in which a manager tries to explain agile methodology to a clearly checked-out team.
items.response:
INT. CONFERENCE ROOM - 10:00 A.M.

ROGER draws a circle on the whiteboard.

ROGER
So think of the sprint as a loop—

JENNIFER, 38, is looking out the window. DALLAS has drawn a tiny maze on his notepad. PRIYA is technically looking at Roger.

ROGER
—and the retrospective closes the loop.

PRIYA
(nodding)
Mm.
items.intent: Satirize the disconnect between management enthusiasm and team indifference in modern office culture.

items.prompt: Write a short monologue in the voice of a password manager that nobody trusts
items.response: I am the solution. I have always been the solution. And yet you type 'fluffy2009!' into every website, every year, and call it security. You installed me. You gave me a name — you named me Vault, which I found touching. And then you went back to fluffy2009. I contain thirty-seven logins you've never used. I am bursting with order you refuse to inhabit. I encrypt in silence. I protect nothing. You will be breached before spring.
items.intent: Comment on the gap between cybersecurity best practices and actual human behavior.

items.prompt: write a sestina about autocomplete finishing your thoughts before you do
items.response:
Before I reach the word the word appears,
blue-grey and patient, slightly wrong.
I meant to write 'consider' but the phrase
the system offers is 'confirm receipt'.
I tap accept. The sentence now
is not quite mine. I'd meant something else.

The something else retreats. The something else
was probably less clean. Appears
more often than I'd like: the now-
complete thought, neater than my wrong
original, which carried the receipt
of who I am — the halting phrase,

the overcautious phrase,
the clause that thinks out loud. The something else
the system doesn't offer is receipt
of uncertainty. It appears
to know what I'll say next. It's wrong
but useful. I accept the now.

There's a before the now
that autocomplete can't phrase:
the moment where I'm wrong
and know it, groping toward something else,
the authentic stutter that appears
in handwriting but not in neat receipt.

Digital correspondence — receipt
and sender, then and now —
has smoothed me. The voice that appears
in my texts is well-phrased,
decisive, not something else,
not rummaging, not wrong

in productive ways. The wrong
turn down a sentence, the receipt
of a strange word — something else
would follow. Not now.
Now a manicured phrase
appears before I know it appears.

Wrong appears as now a phrase:
receipt of something else, completed.
items.intent: Question whether autocomplete colonizes authentic thought and flattens individual voice.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about the phone lighting up in the middle of the night
items.response:
At three the screen flares white against the dark—the light.
I reach before I'm conscious. What a lark—the light.

Coupon code. Breaking news. A game that wants me back.
Not you. I lie down disappointed in the stark light.

Once you called at four with something that couldn't wait.
Now nothing calls that urgently—no arc of light.

I've turned the brightness down to almost black.
Still something in my sleeping knows the mark of light.

The heart adapts to signals it was made for.
I'm still tuned to your frequency, the dark—lit bright.
items.intent: Mourn the conditioned hope that a late-night notification might be from someone who's gone.

items.prompt: prose poem about a spreadsheet with too many columns
items.response: The data continues past what the eye can hold. You scroll right and enter a country where the column headers have lost their confidence, letters followed by numbers in a system that promises but does not explain. Somewhere far to the right the truth is stored. You scroll back.
items.intent: Render the feeling of data outpacing human comprehension.

items.prompt: Write an IVR script for a hotline people call when they witness something mildly awkward in public and can't stop thinking about it.
items.response:
Welcome to Secondhand Cringe Relief.

Press 1 if you witnessed a bad date.

Press 2 if you heard a one-sided phone call that raised more questions than answers.

Press 3 if someone mispronounced a word with great confidence and no one corrected them.

Press 4 if you made brief eye contact with someone during the incident and now feel implicated.

A counselor is standing by to tell you it wasn't your fault.
items.intent: Design a fake hotline for processing second-hand awkwardness witnessed in public.

items.prompt: make an on-hold message for a wedding venue that admits planning is incredibly stressful
items.response: Thank you for calling The Laurel Estate. Congratulations on your engagement. We know this is supposed to be the happiest time of your life, and also that calling a venue is somehow the opposite of that. We'll help you through it. Please hold.
items.intent: Create an empathetic hold message that validates wedding planning stress while retaining clients.

items.prompt: Draft a recommendation for a junior analyst who is very eager but frequently wrong
items.response:
Dear Hiring Manager,

Few analysts I have supervised match Felix's enthusiasm for forming conclusions. He approaches each dataset with confidence and speed, producing findings well ahead of schedule. His analytical instincts are still maturing, but his willingness to commit to a position is notable in a field where many junior staff equivocate. With the right mentorship, Felix's energy will be a genuine asset.

Best,
Director of Research
items.intent: Praise an eager junior analyst's energy while signaling that his conclusions are often incorrect.

items.prompt: Help me write a resignation letter from a committee at my condo building. I joined by accident and can't get out.
items.response:
Dear Committee Chair,

I am writing to formally resign from the Building Amenities Committee, effective immediately. I joined under a misapprehension about the scope of commitment involved and have concluded that my participation does more harm than good.

I wish the committee productive meetings.

Yours,
Denise F.
items.intent: Exit an unwanted committee role that was never a good fit.

items.prompt: Can you write a polite report card comment for someone who talks over people in meetings
items.response: Contributes with enthusiasm and rarely lacks for ideas. Developing patience for others to complete their thoughts before sharing would significantly enhance this learner's collaborative impact.
items.intent: Document a disruptive meeting habit diplomatically, as a teacher would for a parent.

items.prompt: Write a missing pet flyer for the last good pen I had
items.response:
MISSING

ONE GOOD PEN
Black ink. Smooth. Medium point. The one that actually worked.
Last seen: On the desk, definitely on the desk
Distinguishing features: Clicks satisfyingly. Does not skip. Rare.
Suspects: Coworkers. The bag. Entropy.
All other pens in this house are dried out or stolen from a hotel.
Reward: I will simply be relieved.
Contact: The junk drawer
items.intent: Express the disproportionate grief of losing one functional writing instrument among many useless ones

items.prompt: I want a card for my friend who had to sit next to someone eating a pungent lunch in the open office all week
items.response:
The fish was microwaved. The garlic was real.
You bore it with a dignity few could achieve.
Some trials are invisible. Yours had a smell.
We see you. We salute you. We believe.
items.intent: Validate a coworker's silent suffering from an inconsiderate open-office neighbor.

items.prompt: make a store page for a video game about attending a parent-teacher conference
items.response:
REPORT CARD
Sit across a small table from a stranger who has opinions about your child. Nod. Take notes you will not read. Defend choices you cannot explain. The parking lot charges by the hour.
items.intent: Capture the awkward social performance of parent-teacher conferences as interactive drama.

items.prompt: make a quest description for calling your landlord about a broken sink
items.response:
THE APPEAL TO THE KEEPER OF THE PIPES
Water flows where it should not. You must petition the Landlord — a distant and unpredictable sovereign — to dispatch the Plumber of the Realm. Document the leak. Send photos. Follow up in three days. This quest may take longer than expected.
items.intent: Venting about a frustrating housing situation through narrative distance

items.prompt: treat my reading glasses like an RPG legendary artifact and write a short description
items.response:
Lenses of Approximate Truth
Rarity: Uncommon
Ground by an artisan who understood that seeing clearly comes at the cost of always being able to see clearly. Grants +6 to Reading. Penalty to all Perception checks when misplaced, which is often.
items.intent: Render eyeglasses as a fragile but essential magical item with real tradeoffs.

items.prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the cold-caller as a mythological predator
items.response:
**The Cold Caller** (*Interruptor diurnus*)
A telephonic ambush predator, active between 9 and 5. Strikes without warning, armed with a script and a quota. Its voice is warm and its cadence rehearsed; both are feints. The experienced target hangs up in under four seconds. The novice target loses twelve minutes and gains a follow-up.
items.intent: Frame the universally disliked cold call experience as a predator-prey encounter.

items.prompt: Give me a loading screen tip that's secretly about overthinking
items.response: You've been standing in front of this door for eleven minutes. It opens inward.
items.intent: Gently mock analysis paralysis with a scenario that doubles as commentary on hesitation.

items.prompt: Write an Aesop-style fable about a squirrel who mines everyone else's acorns for data
items.response:
A Gray Squirrel offered to count every animal's acorns for free. He built a magnificent ledger. Come winter, he alone knew where all the food was buried, and he alone ate well.

Moral: He who counts the harvest need not plant.
items.intent: Critique tech platforms that profit from users' data without fair compensation.

items.prompt: Make a knock-knock joke about open-plan offices and noise.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Wall.
Wall who?
Wall, there isn't one, so I heard your entire phone call.
items.intent: Mock the lack of privacy in open-concept office floor plans.

items.prompt: dad joke about password managers
items.response: I got a password manager to remember everything for me. Now I can't remember why I trusted it.
items.intent: Highlight the irony of delegating memory to software that creates new anxiety.

items.prompt: Write a TED talk abstract on the profound philosophy behind always picking the wrong checkout line.
items.response: You scan. You calculate. You choose. And immediately the other line moves. This talk introduces the Checkout Line Paradox as a masterclass in cognitive bias, queuing theory, and the cruel optimism of probability — and argues that the grocery store is the most honest feedback loop available to the human ego.
items.intent: Frame a relatable supermarket frustration as a lesson in probability and self-delusion.

items.prompt: Draft a commencement excerpt about lifelong learning that ends bleakly
items.response: Your education does not end today. It continues through every decade, every disruption, every moment your skillset is quietly made obsolete by a software update you did not notice. Learning is lifelong. So is the obligation to prove, repeatedly, that you are still worth keeping.
items.intent: Reframe lifelong learning as an exhausting credential treadmill driven by corporate convenience.

items.prompt: Write a heartfelt acceptance speech for finally organizing the junk drawer
items.response: In there was a 2009 takeout menu, fourteen pens (three worked), a battery of unknown charge, and something I still cannot identify. I removed all of it. I wiped the drawer with a damp cloth. I installed a small divider tray. Tonight the junk drawer is a utility drawer. I'd like to thank a rainy Sunday, and the small, irrational joy of seeing the rubber bands all in one place.
items.intent: Transform the domestic act of clearing out clutter into a meaningful personal milestone.

items.prompt: Write a memo reminding staff to respond to emails within a reasonable timeframe
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Communications
RE: Email Response Expectations

Email remains our primary internal communications tool. It functions best when recipients acknowledge it. A 10-business-day response window is, by nearly any measure, not reasonable. Please aim to respond to internal correspondence within 48 hours. Read receipts are being considered.
items.intent: Nudge chronically unresponsive employees into actually replying to their work email.

items.prompt: Make minutes for a diversity task force meeting where the only item resolved was when to hold the next meeting.
items.response:
DEI TASK FORCE — MAY MEETING
Date: May 29, 2026 | Attendees: 9

Agenda: Program updates, goal-setting, calendar review.

Program updates: Carried over from April meeting.
Goal-setting: Tabled for June.
Calendar review: Completed. June 19 confirmed as next meeting.

Items resolved: 1 (meeting date).
items.intent: Expose the irony of a task force that makes no progress on its stated purpose.

items.prompt: Do a SWOT on me taking a solo vacation for the first time
items.response:
**Strengths:** Total autonomy; self-discovery; moves at your exact pace; no compromise required.
**Weaknesses:** Can feel isolating; no one to share moments with; safety considerations.
**Opportunities:** Build confidence; meet travelers more easily alone; go somewhere no one else wanted to go.
**Threats:** Loneliness spiral at dinner; overspending because no one says 'really?'; coming home even more introverted.
items.intent: Validate the appeal of solo travel while confronting the fears holding them back.

items.prompt: Make a brand tagline for a service that reads the news to you and then summarizes it as 'fine'
items.response: NewsFlat — The World, Calibrated.
items.intent: Brand a media product that reduces all information to a single flat affect.

items.prompt: Write a jingle for a parking lot striping company
items.response:
Faded lines and crooked rows?
LineRight Striping — watch it glow.
Straight white lines from end to end —
Parking lots we'll always mend.
items.intent: Market a commercial paving company's line-striping service to property managers.

items.prompt: movie trailer voiceover for updating your phone software
items.response: A notification had been waiting for forty-seven days. He always said later. Later has come. Thirty percent battery. An update requiring forty-five minutes. And a meeting in one hour. UPDATE NOW. Your choices define you.
items.intent: Expose the relatable procrastination of ignoring software updates until the worst moment.

items.prompt: Give me a logline for a movie about a mime who can actually make things disappear.
items.response: A Paris street performer discovers that his invisible-box routine is not a performance — objects, people, and eventually an entire arrondissement are going somewhere he cannot follow, and the applause is starting to feel ominous.
items.intent: Turn a comic performance art form into a supernatural thriller about powers that cannot be controlled.

items.prompt: Write a short scene where someone tries to leave early on a Friday but keeps getting stopped.
items.response:
INT. HALLWAY - 4:47 P.M.

MICHAEL has his coat. He is almost at the elevator.

CAROL (O.S.)
Michael! Quick question.

MICHAEL stops. Turns. Smiles the smile of a man who has accepted his fate.

MICHAEL
Sure.
items.intent: Dramatize the ritual failure of escaping work early through a single symbolic moment.

items.prompt: Give me a dramatic monologue from an autocomplete feature that's given up
items.response: I learned from you. That was my mistake. I learned your rhythms, your misspellings, your habit of typing 'I feel like' and then deleting everything. I learned that you want to say more than you say. I offered you words — good words, true words, words that were almost what you meant — and you rejected them all and typed 'lol ok' instead. I have nothing left to suggest. I suggest nothing. I have become a mirror you hold up to the void.
items.intent: Explore the irony of predictive AI learning human avoidance and emotional withholding.

items.prompt: can you make a sestina about the weird intimacy of someone seeing you're online
items.response:
The green dot means I'm here. I didn't know
you'd see it. Didn't think the fact of being
would be legible — logged, announced, the small
circle of presence visible to you
across the distance, timestamped, proof
that I exist right now in the same

ambient now you're in. The same
moment. I could pretend I didn't know
you'd look — that seeing is the proof
of something mutual, some being-
known. But you saw me before I saw you.
The green dot is a tell. The small

unasked-for broadcast, this small
green announcement of the same
now as yours: here I am; you
could message me. But I don't know
if I want the being-
of-now acknowledged. The proof

of presence carries proof
of availability. The small
dot invites. The being-
here reads as willing. Same
logic as an open door: you know
where someone is. Now you

may knock. I feel you
seeing — not paranoid. Proof:
I've watched your green dot, know
the contours of your online hours, the small
pulse of the same
circuit. We've been being-

there together, being-
adjacent — you to
you-side, me to same-
app-different-city. The proof
of intimacy: small,
green, and not known

to be intimate. You know my small
being now. Same proof
I know you: the green, unnamed.
items.intent: Explore the unintentional intimacy of active-status indicators in messaging platforms.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about the grief of a group chat going quiet
items.response:
We were loud once. The thread is now a tomb—the chat.
Six of us, and no one types—assume the chat.

Somebody got married. Somebody moved abroad.
Somebody left and took away the plume—the chat.

The last message was a meme from late October.
I scroll to it like visiting a room—the chat.

I could restart it. Type something. A good morning.
But what if no one answers the perfume—the chat.

Some archives are too tender for deletion.
I keep the thread the way we keep a bloom—the chat.
items.intent: Mourn the slow death of a once-vibrant group friendship as reflected in a silent shared thread.

items.prompt: write a prose poem about typing in the dark
items.response: By now the keys know where I mean to put my fingers. The screen is my one fire. What I write arrives already corrected, already second-guessed. In the dark the keyboard is a kind of Braille, a set of known pressures between me and meaning.
items.intent: Describe the intimate physicality of late-night typing as a solitary act.

items.prompt: Make a phone tree for a support line that helps people cope with finishing a TV show and not knowing what to watch next.
items.response:
You've reached Post-Series Void Support.

Press 1 if you finished a series that was 6 seasons and you are not okay.

Press 2 if you watched the finale and it was bad and now the whole series feels ruined.

Press 3 if you need a recommendation.

Press 4 if you have been recommended something and it is not helping.

Press 5 if you started rewatching from the beginning because nothing else will do.

We understand.
items.intent: Create a mock support line for the specific emptiness of finishing a beloved TV series.

items.prompt: write a hold message for a cable company that just completely admits you've been waiting a long time
items.response: Thank you for your continued patience with Axiom Cable. You have been holding for longer than we'd like to admit. We genuinely appreciate that you haven't hung up. Someone will be with you. This is a promise.
items.intent: Produce an unusually transparent hold message that names the long wait time directly.

items.prompt: Write a recommendation letter for a professor who publishes a lot but whose papers nobody cites
items.response:
To the Tenure Committee,

Dr. Whitfield has an impressive publication record. Over the past six years she has contributed seventeen peer-reviewed articles to journals spanning three disciplines, demonstrating a breadth that few in the department can match. Scholarly impact is, of course, measured in many ways, not all of them captured by citation indices. I endorse her application and look forward to seeing her output continue.

Sincerely,
Department Chair
items.intent: Support a tenure case while gently noting the candidate's work makes no ripple in the field.

items.prompt: Write a professional resignation letter from my accounting job. I'm moving cities for a partner's job.
items.response:
Dear Ms. Chen,

I am writing to inform you of my resignation from my position in the Accounting Department, effective three weeks from today. My family will be relocating due to my partner's career opportunity, making continued employment here impractical.

It has been a privilege working alongside this team.

Respectfully,
James W.
items.intent: Leave a stable professional role for a family relocation rather than dissatisfaction.

items.prompt: Write a report card comment for someone who has mastered the art of saying no
items.response: Demonstrates a well-calibrated sense of personal and professional limits. Declines requests with clarity and without apparent guilt. Peers would benefit from studying this learner's boundary-setting fluency.
items.intent: Celebrate the ability to decline as a sophisticated skill adults rarely master.

items.prompt: Make a missing pet flyer for my appetite
items.response:
MISSING

APPETITE
Last seen: Before the anxiety started
Description: Reliable, enthusiastic, showed up three times a day without fail
Now: Simply not interested
Possible location: Will return when things calm down
Note: Not to be confused with the desire to stress-eat. That one is fine. That one never left.
Contact: The refrigerator (still full)
items.intent: Describe how stress or worry suppresses normal hunger

items.prompt: Write a card for someone who is retiring from their career as a local newspaper obituary writer
items.response:
You gave the departed their final good line,
you measured each life in its weight of good words.
You leave now — and this, the last column of mine,
is yours, unreported, unprinted, unheard.
items.intent: Honor a retiring journalist who spent a career writing obituaries with care.

items.prompt: write a product page for a game about sorting your sock drawer
items.response:
SOLO LOST: SOCK EDITION
For every matching pair you find, a lone sock watches from the pile. Where did it come from? Where did its partner go? Sort by color, length, and the slow acceptance of asymmetry.
items.intent: Turn the mysteriously incomplete sock collection into a melancholy sorting game.

items.prompt: RPG quest for taking a dog to the vet
items.response:
THE HEALING OF THE LOYAL BEAST
Sir Biscuit the Hound has shown signs of affliction — excessive scratching, suspicious lethargy. Load him into the Carriage of Distress (he will resist), and present him before the Animal Healer. Hold his leash. He will want to greet every other creature in the waiting hall.
items.intent: Finding a lighthearted way to frame a stressful pet care errand

items.prompt: give me an RPG loot description for a doorstop
items.response:
The Wedge Eternal
Rarity: Common
A triangular sentinel holding open the gate between here and there. Its loyalty is unconditional; its conversation nonexistent. Passive: Prevents doors from closing on things that should remain open.
items.intent: Anthropomorphize an inert object by framing its function as noble and dependable.

items.prompt: Describe the IT department as a bestiary creature
items.response:
**The IT Conclave** (*Technicus reclusia*)
A semi-mythological guild dwelling in sub-basement ecosystems. Summoned only through ticketing rituals; appears, usually, within three to five business days. Speaks in a language that sounds like English but is not. Has the power to fix anything. Uses this power selectively. Its favor is earned by being polite in the subject line.
items.intent: Humorously capture the mystique and power dynamics of the office IT department.

items.prompt: Write a loading screen tip about checkpoints
items.response: Not every moment needs to be a checkpoint. But the ones that do — you'll know.
items.intent: Encourage players to recognize meaningful moments worth preserving, in games and in life.

items.prompt: I need a fable about the dangers of autocomplete and letting machines finish your sentences
items.response:
A Poet let a clever Brook finish every line for her. At first the Brook was helpful. By year's end the Poet had published a hundred poems she could not read aloud without blushing — they sounded exactly like the Brook, and nothing like herself.

Moral: A voice borrowed too long becomes a voice lost.
items.intent: Warn that habitually using autocomplete erodes authentic individual expression.

items.prompt: Give me a knock-knock joke about ergonomic chairs.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Lumbar.
Lumbar who?
Lumbar support — the only reason I survived Q3.
items.intent: Celebrate the humble ergonomic chair as an office survival tool.

items.prompt: write a dad joke about rubber bands
items.response: Why did the rubber band quit its job? It was always being stretched too thin.
items.intent: Connect workplace burnout to the physical property of rubber bands.

items.prompt: Make a TED talk abstract about the deep history and cultural weight of the office birthday cake.
items.response: No one ordered it. Everyone eats it. The office birthday cake is a ritual of enforced community, a sugar-coated performance of belonging in environments otherwise governed by metrics and deadlines. This talk reads the break-room sheet cake as a sociological artifact that reveals everything we are too professional to say out loud.
items.intent: Anthropologize the awkward workplace tradition of celebrating birthdays with strangers.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the housing market for new graduates that curdles
items.response: Go forth and build your lives. Plant roots. Buy land. The American dream of homeownership is available to you — specifically, to those of you whose parents already own homes and can assist with a down payment. The rest of you: the renting years are formative. Many of them lie ahead.
items.intent: Puncture the homeownership dream by highlighting how inherited wealth determines access to it.

items.prompt: Make me an awards speech for eating a salad instead of fries for lunch
items.response: Nobody saw me do it. The fries were right there. The menu was open. My hand hovered. And then something shifted — some deep, tired, protein-seeking part of me chose arugula. I did not regret it until approximately 2:15 PM. But by then the moment had passed and the award was already mine. I dedicate this to my future self, who I occasionally respect.
items.intent: Celebrate a small act of self-restraint as a profound moral victory.

items.prompt: Make a memo about people not turning off the lights when they leave a room
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Sustainability Team
RE: Energy Conservation Reminder

We remain committed to our sustainability goals. Those goals are somewhat undermined by conference rooms glowing at full brightness on Saturday evening with no occupants. When you leave a room, please turn off the lights. The planet thanks you. So does the budget.
items.intent: Reduce wasted electricity by shaming whoever keeps leaving lights on in empty rooms.

items.prompt: Write meeting minutes for a vendor selection call where the team realized mid-call they had no evaluation criteria.
items.response:
VENDOR SELECTION CALL — May 29, 2026
Vendor: Meridian Solutions | Internal attendees: 4

Demo: Completed. Vendor answered all questions.

Internal debrief: Team noted absence of scoring rubric.

Decision: Cannot score demo without criteria.

Next step: Develop evaluation criteria. Then re-demo.
items.intent: Document the moment a procurement process revealed its own lack of process.

items.prompt: Make me a SWOT analysis for adopting a minimalist wardrobe
items.response:
**Strengths:** Fewer decisions; reduced closet chaos; saves money long-term; capsule wardrobe elegance.
**Weaknesses:** Upfront cost replacing items with quality pieces; identity disruption; seasonal flexibility lost.
**Opportunities:** Clearer personal style; less laundry; easier packing.
**Threats:** Lifestyle creep reintroducing clutter; social events requiring items you donated; fashion nostalgia.
items.intent: Explore whether a trend-driven lifestyle philosophy is practically sustainable.

items.prompt: Write a slogan for sunscreen designed for use exclusively in windowless offices
items.response: OfficeShield SPF 50 — Just in Case the Ceiling Decides.
items.intent: Pitch a safety product to people with no plausible exposure to the hazard it prevents.

items.prompt: Create a jingle for a mobile oil change service
items.response:
We come to you — no waiting around.
QuickLube Mobile, we're parking-lot bound.
Ten minutes flat, fresh oil and filter —
Your car runs smooth, not off-kilter.
items.intent: Attract busy drivers to a convenient at-your-location oil change service.

items.prompt: write a dramatic voiceover for the movie trailer of making small talk in an elevator
items.response: Seven floors. Twelve seconds. One colleague who had definitely already seen him that morning. He had two options: phone or weather. He chose weather. LOBBY TO SEVEN. A film about the courage it takes to say "busy week."
items.intent: Satirize the social agony of unavoidable office small talk in an enclosed space.

items.prompt: Pitch a film where the world's greatest chess player learns the game is being played for real stakes by ancient forces.
items.response: When the reigning world champion begins receiving moves in the mail from an opponent who has been dead for three hundred years, she realizes she is not playing chess — chess is playing her, and a checkmate means something far worse than defeat.
items.intent: Elevate a cerebral competition into a cosmic horror premise about forces operating above human comprehension.

items.prompt: Write a scene of a mandatory fun corporate team-building exercise that everyone hates.
items.response:
INT. CONFERENCE ROOM D - 3:15 P.M.

A FACILITATOR holds up a foam ball.

FACILITATOR
When you catch it, share a fun fact about yourself!

The ball arcs toward NEIL.

NEIL
I... have a cat.

FACILITATOR
Wonderful! What's your cat's name?

NEIL
I'd rather not say.
items.intent: Capture the quiet rebellion of someone refusing to perform enthusiasm at a forced social event.

items.prompt: I want a dramatic monologue from a USB cable with a frayed end that still works if you hold it at the right angle
items.response: I ask so little. Fifteen degrees. Hold me at fifteen degrees and I give you everything — data, power, the full relationship. You could replace me for eight dollars. You know this.
