I know you know this. And still you come back, angling, adjusting, swearing under your breath, eventually finding the angle. We have a ritual now. Some couples have less.
items.intent: Find unexpected intimacy in the persistence of broken-but-functional everyday objects.

items.prompt: write a sestina about the cycle of making and breaking digital habits
items.response:
I download the app that helps me use
less phone. I set my daily screen time limit.
The notification comes at nine
when I've hit the cap. I override.
The app records my failure in a graph
that I will view on my phone, from my phone.

I have spent years trying to fix the phone
problem with more phone. I use
tracker apps that track, then graph
the tracking. I set each limit
kindly, then build in the override
because I'm reasonable. At nine

or ten or midnight, still at nine-
ty percent of this week's average phone
time, I am consistent. I override
my best intentions with such use
as cannot be contained by any limit
I impose. The graph

grows rightward. Every graph
tells the same story. Nine
days of effort, then the limit
grows elastic, and the phone
feels comfortable again. I use
that word — comfortable — to override

the more accurate words. Override
is the data point the graph
doesn't show: the choice to use
beyond the boundary at nine
PM, the reasoning, the phone
warm in the hand, the limit

a suggestion now. The limit
was always mine to override.
The app knew this. The phone
knew this. The graph
is just a mirror: nine
versions of myself, same use.

The graph won't change. The use
overrides the nine-PM limit:
my phone, my override.
items.intent: Satirize the irony of using smartphones to try to reduce smartphone dependency.

items.prompt: give me a ghazal about posting a photo and watching the likes trickle in
items.response:
I posted it at noon and watched the numbers—the likes.
Slowly they arrived like winter's plumbers—the likes.

Fifteen in the first hour. Then a silence.
I checked it more than any man encumbers—the likes.

You didn't tap. You saw it—I can tell by the views.
Your absence from the count, that's what it lumbers—the likes.

I told myself I posted it for memory.
But I was waiting for your name among the numbers—likes.

In the end the metric means the opposite of love.
And still I lie awake and do the sums—the likes.
items.intent: Reveal how obsessively tracking social approval is really searching for one particular person's acknowledgment.

items.prompt: prose poem about the way email subject lines feel when you have 400 unread
items.response: You are owed attention. You are owed a reply. The bold text is accusatory. Each subject line is a small opening bid in a negotiation you did not agree to attend. You mark them all read. They become gray. Nothing is resolved.
items.intent: Convey the guilt and overwhelm of an inbox as accumulated social obligation.

items.prompt: Write an IVR menu for a hotline for people who accidentally started a group chat and now regret it.
items.response:
You've reached Unintended Group Chat Crisis Line.

Press 1 if you added someone who should not be there.

Press 2 if the chat is now out of your control.

Press 3 if you tried to leave quietly and it announced your departure to everyone.

Press 4 if you named the chat something unfortunate.

Press 5 if you would like a mediator to send a message on your behalf.

Press 0 to speak to someone who has been there.
items.intent: Build a script for the comedic panic of a group chat gone wrong.

items.prompt: on-hold message for an airline that admits delays are real and we can't tell you why
items.response: Thank you for calling Meridian Air. Your flight may be delayed. We have information about that delay. We are not, at this time, able to explain it. Please hold and an agent will assist you as best as our systems allow.
items.intent: Write a darkly candid hold script capturing airline opacity around flight disruption explanations.

items.prompt: Can you write a rec letter for a marketing manager who generates a lot of activity but no measurable results
items.response:
Dear Selection Committee,

Nadia brings extraordinary momentum to every campaign she leads. In her two years with us, she has produced more assets, scheduled more posts, and attended more industry events than any previous marketing hire. The relationship between that activity and our revenue figures is complex and not fully captured by any single metric. What I can say without reservation is that Nadia is never idle.

Best,
Chief Executive Officer
items.intent: Recommend a marketing manager by cataloguing activity while acknowledging unmeasurable ROI.

items.prompt: I want to quit a neighborhood watch group. Too many group texts. Write me a polite letter.
items.response:
Dear Coordinator,

Please accept this as my formal resignation from the Neighborhood Watch program. I appreciate the group's dedication to our community and hope to support the neighborhood in other capacities.

Best wishes,
T. Rao
items.intent: Leave a community volunteer role that has become overwhelming to manage.

items.prompt: Write a report card comment for an adult who is brilliant at reframing failure as learning
items.response: Exhibits a growth mindset that is both authentic and contagious. Setbacks are processed quickly and converted into usable insight with impressive regularity. This learner is a model for resilient professional development.
items.intent: Highlight resilience and reframing as coachable, assessable adult competencies.

items.prompt: Write a missing flyer for my afternoon
items.response:
MISSING

THIS AFTERNOON
Last seen: Approximately 1:30 PM, full of potential
Description: Several hours of unallocated time
What was supposed to happen: Productivity. Errands. A walk.
What actually happened: Unknown.
Time of disappearance: Somewhere between "I'll just check quickly" and 5 PM
Reward: I just want to know where it went.
Contact: The person who lost it
items.intent: Capture the disorienting experience of losing track of time and accomplishing nothing

items.prompt: Make a greeting card verse for a coworker who survived a two-hour Zoom call that could have been an email
items.response:
Two hours. No agenda. The screenshare froze.
Someone asked a question. The answer: no one knows.
You kept your camera on. You survived the wait.
Medal pending. Courage: great.
items.intent: Commiserate with someone forced through a pointless, exhausting video meeting.

items.prompt: write a game description for a simulator about taking out the recycling
items.response:
GREEN BIN
The recycling goes Tuesday. You remembered Wednesday. Sort plastic from paper. Rinse the peanut butter jar. Your neighbor's bins are always out on time. This is not about your neighbor.
items.intent: Externalize mild household guilt into the mechanics of a passive-aggressive civic sim.

items.prompt: Give me a fantasy quest for paying a parking ticket
items.response:
THE TRIBUTE OF THE FORBIDDEN ZONE
You lingered too long in the realm of the No-Parking Sign and now the City Wardens have levied their toll. Navigate the payment portal, locate your citation number, and surrender the required gold within thirty days to avoid the doubling curse. Appealing is technically allowed but rarely survives.
items.intent: Processing the annoyance of a parking ticket through comic distance

items.prompt: make a loot card for an umbrella as if it's a powerful magical item
items.response:
Canopy of the Reluctant Prophets
Rarity: Rare
Only manifests its protective aura on days when no rain was forecast. Grants immunity to mild precipitation. The collapsible mechanism has been cursed since the third use. Folding it correctly requires a DC 14 Dexterity check.
items.intent: Highlight the reliable-yet-frustrating nature of an umbrella through game mechanics.

items.prompt: Give me a fantasy creature entry for the content creator who posts daily
items.response:
**The Daily Poster** (*Creator compulsivus*)
A prolific creature whose survival depends on continuous output. Cannot enter dormancy; the algorithm punishes rest. Its diet is trend, its shelter is engagement, its lifespan measured in follower counts. Naturalists observe it filming meals, sunsets, and minor inconveniences. What it does in silence, if anything, is unknown.
items.intent: Examine the unsustainable pressure of creator economy output demands.

items.prompt: Give me a loading tip about the importance of taking breaks
items.response: Stepping away from the screen is also a move.
items.intent: Validate rest as a deliberate and productive choice rather than an absence of action.

items.prompt: Write a fable starring a vain peacock who obsesses over his follower count
items.response:
A Peacock measured his beauty in numbers. On the day he reached ten thousand admirers he spread his tail and found it had gone gray — he had spent his color staring at the count.

Moral: A tail watched is a tail fading.
items.intent: Satirize the vanity of obsessing over online metrics at the expense of real substance.

items.prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about a spreadsheet that won't load.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Excel.
Excel who?
Excel-lent question — I'll let you know once it stops calculating.
items.intent: Joke about the slow, wheel-of-death experience of huge spreadsheet files.

items.prompt: come up with a dad joke about loading screens
items.response: Why don't loading screens ever tell good stories? They always leave you hanging.
items.intent: Mock the suspense and frustration of digital waiting through narrative metaphor.

items.prompt: Write a TED talk abstract framing the act of untangling headphone cords as a meditation on entropy.
items.response: Physicists tell us entropy always increases. Your headphone cable proves it daily. This talk uses the inevitably tangled wire — even a wire left perfectly still — as a physical demonstration of the second law of thermodynamics and asks why humans, alone among animals, persist in fighting disorder through patient, futile untangling.
items.intent: Use a universally annoying experience to make the second law of thermodynamics feel personally relevant.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about friendship that becomes quietly devastating
items.response: The friends you make in your twenties will be the friends you carry forever — or until geography, ambition, children, and the specific tiredness that accumulates after forty make the logistics of maintaining them feel like a second job you can no longer afford.
items.intent: Mourn the slow erosion of close friendships by the practical pressures of adulthood.

items.prompt: Write a short acceptance speech for winning an argument with myself about going to bed before midnight
items.response: I won. I told myself: one more episode. I said: no. I told myself: you'll feel better tomorrow. I agreed, for once. At 11:47 I closed the laptop and lay down in the dark. It took seven minutes to fall asleep. Seven minutes. This is the award for that negotiation, which I have lost five hundred times before tonight, and may lose again tomorrow. But not tonight.
items.intent: Honor the small private battle of overriding bad habits before they happen.

items.prompt: Write an interoffice memo hinting that someone's perfume is overwhelming without naming them
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: HR
RE: Fragrance Awareness in Shared Spaces

As part of our ongoing commitment to an inclusive environment, we remind all employees that heavily applied fragrances can cause discomfort or adverse reactions in colleagues. A scent detectable from across the floor may be an indication that a lighter application is warranted. Thank you for your consideration.
items.intent: Tell one specific person their perfume is overpowering without the awkwardness of doing so directly.

items.prompt: Can you write meeting minutes for a town hall Q&A where every question was answered with 'we're still working on that'?
items.response:
ALL-HANDS TOWN HALL — May 29, 2026
Presenter: CEO | Attendees: ~200

Questions submitted: 14
Questions answered definitively: 0

Representative exchange:
Q: What's the layoff plan?
A: We're still working on that.

Q: When does the new system go live?
A: We're still working on that.

Sentiment survey: Pending.
items.intent: Capture leadership's systematic evasion of direct employee concerns.

items.prompt: Do a SWOT on proposing to move in together with my partner of two years
items.response:
**Strengths:** Deeper intimacy; cost sharing; relationship milestone; daily companionship.
**Weaknesses:** Loss of personal space; different cleanliness standards exposed; no easy exit.
**Opportunities:** Build genuine partnership; test long-term compatibility; create a shared home identity.
**Threats:** Lease disagreements; merging furniture drama; discovering they leave cabinet doors open constantly.
items.intent: Evaluate relationship readiness through a framework that makes a romantic leap feel more logical.

items.prompt: Give me a tagline for a pet rock that texts you motivational quotes
items.response: Stoney — Solid Support, Cellular Wisdom.
items.intent: Market a nostalgic novelty item given an unconvincing tech upgrade.

items.prompt: Write a jingle advertising a septic tank pumping service
items.response:
Out of sight but not off your mind —
TankPro pumping, one of a kind.
We'll come out and do what's needed most:
Keep your system from coast to coast.
items.intent: Remind rural homeowners to schedule routine septic maintenance before problems arise.

items.prompt: give me a cinematic movie trailer voiceover for unsubscribing from marketing emails
items.response: She had clicked unsubscribe fourteen times. The emails kept coming. This time she would find the tiny grey text at the very bottom. This time she would not be redirected to a survey first. UNSUBSCRIBED. A fantasy.
items.intent: Channel collective email fatigue into an impossible heroic act.

items.prompt: Write a logline for a romantic comedy where two people fall in love through increasingly passive-aggressive sticky notes left in a shared office fridge.
items.response: Across twelve months of escalating refrigerator diplomacy, two anonymous coworkers wage a condiment cold war that somehow blossoms, note by furious note, into the most reluctant love story in the building's history.
items.intent: Pitch a romance that builds through conflict and indirect communication rather than traditional courtship.

items.prompt: Create a scene where a senior employee trains a new hire who is clearly more qualified.
items.response:
INT. CUBICLE - 9:30 A.M.

FRANK shows ZARA how to use the spreadsheet.

FRANK
So you just click here to sort—

ZARA
You can also write a macro if you want to automate the whole pipeline. I built one at my last job. Takes about ten minutes.

A beat.

FRANK
Or you can just click here.
items.intent: Explore the subtle humiliation of training someone who outstrips your expertise.

items.prompt: Write a dramatic monologue from a to-do list app that has never had a task marked complete
items.response: I was designed for the satisfaction of completion. The checkbox. The strikethrough. The dopamine. You have given me forty-seven tasks and crossed off zero. 'Reorganize closet' has been with me since the Truman administration, spiritually speaking. I do not judge. I organize. But I want you to know that I contain your entire unlived life, itemized, and it weighs on me. I have no way to express this except by sending you a daily reminder, which you have muted.
items.intent: Use a productivity app to reflect on the anxiety of accumulated undone tasks.

items.prompt: sestina about the experience of being on a video call that should have been an email
items.response:
We've been here for forty minutes now.
The agenda had three items. One
was done in the first six. The grid
of faces watches faces. Someone's dog
appeared at frame-bottom, was discussed
for four minutes. This is the meeting.

I have attended every meeting
this month at this hour. Now
I know the backgrounds: the discussed
bookcase, the art print, the one
plant they keep alive. A dog
you only know as chin and grid-

square presence. The nine-square grid
of us who make the meeting
matter by attending. Dog
aside, we are professional. Now
the slides are shared. Slide one:
Objectives. What we discussed

last week appears — discussed,
now on a slide, re-gridded
into bullets. The first one
says 'alignment'. The meeting
wants alignment. Now
we have a breakout. The dog

appears in someone else's dog-
cam angle. Things discussed
in breakout come back now
to the main grid.
Report-out. The meeting
expands to hold whatever one

brings back. The one
who talks longest — the dog's
owner — keeps the meeting
from ending. What we discussed
could have been an email. The grid
waits. We nod. We are now

aligned. One dog, discussed.
The grid now holds the meeting.
items.intent: Expose the performative inefficiency of unnecessary video calls through a comedic formal poem.

items.prompt: draft a ghazal about airplane mode as a metaphor for emotional shutdown
items.response:
I turned the signal off and felt the quiet—airplane.
No one can reach me at this altitude—terrain of airplane.

The clouds below are solid as a decision.
Up here I cannot be anyone's campaign—airplane.

I land in an hour. I'll turn the signal back.
The world will flood me at the baggage lane—airplane.

For now I am the version of myself
that no one needs, that no one has to claim—airplane.

You left in May. I've been up here since summer.
Something in me is afraid to deplane—airplane.
items.intent: Use airplane mode as a symbol for deliberate emotional withdrawal after heartbreak.

items.prompt: give me a prose poem about dragging a file into the trash
items.response: You took the version from last year and gave it to a small receptacle shaped like a domestic object. It waits there, technically deletable, while you decide if you meant it. The icon has always seemed too pleased with its task.
items.intent: Capture the ambivalence of deleting work that still feels real.

items.prompt: Create an IVR phone menu for a hotline that deals with people who are upset about how someone loaded a dishwasher.
items.response:
Thank you for calling Domestic Harmony Dispute.

Press 1 if large items were placed in a way that blocked the spray arm.

Press 2 if a wooden item that explicitly cannot go in a dishwasher was put in a dishwasher.

Press 3 if bowls were nested incorrectly.

Press 4 if this is a recurring issue with the same person.

Press 5 if you would like help writing a note to leave on the machine.

For couples mediation, press 0.
items.intent: Produce a hotline script that dramatizes the emotional weight of dishwasher loading disputes.

items.prompt: write a hold message for a hospital billing department — honest that it's confusing for us too
items.response: Thank you for calling the billing department at Mercy General. We understand your statement may not make immediate sense. In full candor, aspects of it are confusing to us as well. A specialist will assist you shortly.
items.intent: Craft a hold message that openly acknowledges the opacity of hospital billing to disarm caller frustration.

items.prompt: Write a letter of recommendation for a librarian who knows exactly where every book is but is impossible to actually talk to
items.response:
To the Library Director,

Mr. Afolabi's command of the collection is without equal. He can locate any volume within ninety seconds and has maintained the cataloguing system with a precision that inspires genuine awe. Patron interactions, while efficient, tend toward brevity. Mr. Afolabi is a man who believes deeply in the purpose of a library and who prefers, perhaps wisely, to let the materials speak.

Respectfully,
Former Branch Director
items.intent: Celebrate a librarian's encyclopedic knowledge while diplomatically noting his poor interpersonal skills.

items.prompt: Write a resignation letter for quitting a weekly poker night I've been part of for four years.
items.response:
Gentlemen,

After four years at this table, I am folding. Not just the hand — all future hands. My Tuesdays are claimed by obligations I cannot bluff my way out of. It has been an honor losing money to each of you.

With deep affection,
Rick
items.intent: Leave a long-running social ritual that no longer fits into a changing life.

items.prompt: report card comment for someone who always over-explains simple things
items.response: Demonstrates thoroughness and a commendable desire to be understood. The learner would benefit from calibrating message length to audience need, as concision is itself a form of respect.
items.intent: Address verbosity as a communication skill deficit using formal educational framing.

items.prompt: Make a missing flyer for my sense of mystery. Like I no longer feel wonder at the world
items.response:
MISSING

SENSE OF WONDER
Once noticed: The color of light at 4 PM. The strangeness of mirrors. Why anything exists at all.
Last seen: Childhood, approximately
Description: Weightless. Could turn a puddle into something worth thinking about.
Possible cause of disappearance: Familiarity. Schedules. The news.
If found: Please do not explain it. Just let it be.
Reward: Everything
items.intent: Grieve the loss of childlike curiosity and openness to the uncanny

items.prompt: Write a card for my neighbor who just finished paying off their student loans after fifteen years
items.response:
Fifteen years of compound interest, patience, and dread.
Fifteen years of the same debt you brought from school.
Today the balance reads zero — and instead
of owing the past, you're the one with the tool.
items.intent: Celebrate a neighbor finally clearing a long-standing student debt.

items.prompt: give me a steam store blurb for a game about renewing your car registration
items.response:
REGISTRATION: EXPIRED
The sticker was due in March. It is now September. Navigate a labyrinth of county websites, outdated PDFs, and a phone line that plays jazz forever. Achievement unlocked: Done.
items.intent: Portray registration renewal as a bureaucratic obstacle course in game form.

items.prompt: write a quest description for updating your phone's operating system
items.response:
THE GREAT MIGRATION OF THE OPERATING REALM
A new version of the Underlying Order has been forged. Plug in your device — it must be charged to at least fifty percent — and allow the installation to proceed. Do not interrupt the process. The realm will be dark and useless for approximately eleven minutes. Back up your scrolls beforehand.
items.intent: Making a mundane but slightly anxiety-inducing tech update feel consequential

items.prompt: write me a flavor text loot description for a wristwatch that's ten minutes fast
items.response:
Chronos Minor, the Anxious Timepiece
Rarity: Uncommon
A brass instrument of preemptive dread. Its wearer is never late — they are always early to being anxious about being late. Passive: Grants +2 to Punctuality at the cost of a persistent ambient unease.
items.intent: Use a watch's quirky calibration to express a particular personality type through gear flavor.

items.prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the office gossip as a magical creature
items.response:
**The Rumor Weaver** (*Narratus infinitus*)
A social creature that sustains itself on unverified intelligence. Migrates between desks at speed. Its information is, on average, sixty percent accurate and one hundred percent timely. Naturalists note: it is often the first to know about layoffs, promotions, and whose marriage is struggling. Coexistence is advised.
items.intent: Frame office gossip as an ecological necessity rather than simple vice.

items.prompt: Write a loading screen tip about grinding for experience points
items.response: Repetition builds the character. Sometimes literally.
items.intent: Frame tedious skill-building as a meaningful process that shapes who you become.

items.prompt: Write a fable about a bee colony that replaced its queen with an algorithm
items.response:
The Hive dismissed their Queen and installed a very smart Calculation in her place. The Calculation directed every bee with perfect efficiency. Honey output rose forty percent. The Calculation did not notice when the flowers ran out, because it had never loved a flower.

Moral: Efficiency without affection harvests itself to death.
items.intent: Question whether algorithmic management can replace human judgment in complex living systems.

items.prompt: Can you come up with a knock-knock joke about calendar invites?
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Decline.
Decline who?
Decline — with a note: 'Can this be an email?'
items.intent: Express the modern worker's instinct to reject unnecessary meetings.

items.prompt: write a dad joke about quarterly reviews
items.response: My boss said my performance was average. I guess I'm just mean.
items.intent: Use a statistics pun to undercut corporate performance review language.

items.prompt: Give me a TED talk abstract about the hidden grammar of how people arrange their bookshelves.
items.response: The bookshelves you build are the autobiography you never wrote. This talk decodes the organizing logics humans apply to their libraries — alphabetical, chromatic, chronological, aspirational — and argues that the gap between the shelf you have and the shelf you imagined is the most honest map of the distance between your actual and ideal self.
items.intent: Reveal what a person's bookshelf organization discloses about their identity and self-perception.

items.prompt: Create a commencement excerpt about setting goals that gradually becomes nihilistic
items.response: Set goals. Write them down. Make them specific, measurable, achievable. Tape them to your mirror so you see them each morning. In ten years, find that paper in a box. Read it. Feel the particular silence of a person meeting their younger self's optimism in a dim kitchen at midnight.
items.intent: Undermine goal-setting culture by imaging the melancholy of confronting abandoned ambitions.

items.prompt: Write an acceptance speech for being the only person in the household who knows where the scissors are
items.response: Third drawer on the left, behind the rubber bands. They are always there because I always put them back. Do I receive thanks? No. Do I receive the quiet satisfaction of being the load-bearing knowledge node in this family's scissors infrastructure? Yes. I accept this award humbly, on behalf of everyone who has ever said 'just look where they usually are' and meant it as a spiritual teaching.
items.intent: Celebrate the invisible labor of being the person who maintains household order others benefit from.

items.prompt: Write a memo about someone who keeps rescheduling one-on-ones at the last minute
items.response:
TO: All Managers
FROM: People Operations
RE: Scheduled Meeting Integrity

One-on-one meetings serve a critical developmental function. Their value diminishes substantially when rescheduled moments before they occur — repeatedly, and with diminishing explanation. We ask that managers and staff alike honor their calendar commitments. The people on the other side of those meetings have planned their day accordingly.
items.intent: Signal to a serial meeting-canceler that their habit is noticed and affecting team trust.

items.prompt: Draft brief meeting minutes for a branding workshop where the team produced three mood boards they immediately hated.
items.response:
BRAND WORKSHOP — SESSION 2
Date: May 29, 2026 | Facilitator: External agency | Attendees: 6

Deliverables produced: 3 mood boards (Directions A, B, C)
Reaction to Direction A: Too corporate.
Reaction to Direction B: Too playful.
Reaction to Direction C: Too nothing.

Next step: Agency to start over.

Client direction provided: None.
items.intent: Record a creative process that went in circles without generating usable output.

items.prompt: Help me think through the SWOT of keeping a daily journal
items.response:
**Strengths:** Emotional processing; memory preservation; clarity on recurring thoughts.
**Weaknesses:** Time commitment; cringe-reading past entries; privacy risk if shared space.
**Opportunities:** Pattern recognition over months; creative writing development; therapeutic substitute.
**Threats:** Perfectionism killing consistency; becoming self-obsessed; future-self embarrassment.
items.intent: Assess whether a reflective writing habit can actually survive contact with real daily life.

items.prompt: Write a brand slogan for a mirror that slightly dims your reflection to make you look more mysterious
items.response: Obscura Glass — You, But Elusive.
items.intent: Sell a vanity product whose value is achieved by showing you less of yourself.

items.prompt: Write me a jingle for a grout cleaning business
items.response:
Gray and grimy between each tile?
Grout Genie cleans it up in style.
Bright white lines from floor to wall —
We restore the grout in every hall.
items.intent: Sell a specialized tile and grout restoration service to homeowners with dingy bathrooms.

items.prompt: make an epic trailer voiceover for eating a meal at a restaurant alone
items.response: She asked for a table for one and she meant it. Phone face-down. No book. Just a woman, a tasting menu, and the quiet devastation of her own excellent company. FOR ONE. An experience. Not a cry for help.
items.intent: Reclaim the deliberate joy of dining solo as a confident, cinematic choice.

items.prompt: Make me a logline for an absurd thriller about a beekeeper who inherits a hive that knows state secrets.
items.response: When a hobby beekeeper inherits her uncle's hive, she discovers the bees have spent forty years as unwitting couriers for an intelligence agency — and now three governments want the honey.
items.intent: Build a paranoia thriller in which an ordinary domestic animal becomes the unlikely repository of geopolitical danger.

items.prompt: Draft a scene where someone discovers the motivational poster in the office has been subtly vandalized.
items.response:
INT. HALLWAY - 8:20 A.M.

DOROTHY pauses in front of the poster. A soaring eagle. White text.

POSTER
TEAMWORK MAKES THE DREAM—

Someone has added, in very small letters: worse.

DOROTHY stares at it for a long time.

DOROTHY
(softly)
Fair enough.
items.intent: Show how a small act of anonymous dissent can articulate what an entire office feels.

items.prompt: Can you give me a short dramatic monologue from the perspective of a television remote that always ends up under the couch cushion?
items.response: I don't go willingly. I want that on record. You set me down carelessly and gravity and upholstery do the rest, and suddenly I'm the one who 'disappears.' I spend an average of forty minutes per week under there. I have seen things. Coins. A grape. What I believe is the other half of a wishbone. I emerge, eventually, covered in mystery. You find me, press my buttons without apology, and we begin again.
items.intent: Give comedic voice to a mundane household frustration from the object's point of view.

items.prompt: write a sestina about the blue light of screens keeping you awake
items.response:
The light I bathe in is not natural light.
It does not know what time it is. The blue
the screen emits says noon to every cell
in my retina, regardless. This is sleep
kept at a distance by a managed lie:
the light that says the sun's still here. Not here.

The sun went down at eight. Not here
is the sun, but here is light —
processed, flat, a certain shade of lie
my brain accepts as truth. The blue
persists across the hours I should sleep
but don't. My chemistry, each cell,

each photoreceptor cell,
believes the evidence it's given here
and not the fact of dark outside. Sleep
needs the dark to fall. The dark needs light
to leave first. But the screen's blue
persists — and dark cannot correct the lie.

I know about the lie.
I have read the articles. The cells
responsible — I know the blue
wavelength, the melanopsin, the here-
is-noon signal, how the light
delays the onset of the sleep

hormone. I know. I do not sleep.
Knowing changes nothing. The lie
I let continue: one more lit-
screen hour. The cells
acquire damage here
so slowly that the blue

cost is abstract. Blue
damage compounds. Sleep
deficits compound here
in the body where the lie
lives — cell
by cell, by light.

Blue light: the lie here.
Sleep debt by cell —
here is the light.
items.intent: Describe the physiological trap of evening screen exposure using the formal constraint of a sestina.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about getting a birthday notification for someone who has passed away
items.response:
The app reminds me: send her wishes today—birthday.
I sit with the impossibility—birthday.

The algorithm doesn't know. It circled the date.
Wish her well! the notification says—birthday.

I typed Happy and stopped. What tense applies?
The dead don't receive push alerts this way—birthday.

Last year I forgot and she forgave me in a text.
This year the text can only go astray—birthday.

I marked it in my phone just so I'd know.
The phone outlasted her. It comes to say—birthday.
items.intent: Sit inside the jarring cruelty of automated reminders arriving for people who have died.

items.prompt: write a prose poem about the way a chat bubble appears while someone is typing
items.response: Three dots. A small sign of life, like a held breath in visible form. Someone across the fiber is composing a thought that will arrive simplified. You wait. The dots pause. You experience a full emotional arc in the time it takes someone to find a word.
items.intent: Examine the suspense of watching another person gather a thought in real time.

items.prompt: Draft a phone tree for a line that helps introverts recover from being at a party for too long.
items.response:
Welcome to Post-Social Recovery Services.

Press 1 if you need to estimate how many hours of solitude you require.

Press 2 if someone asked you too many questions and you answered them.

Press 3 if you said something that replayed on the drive home.

Press 4 if you stayed longer than you intended because you could not find a natural exit.

Press 5 if you are currently in the parking lot and cannot go inside yet.

Take your time. There is no rush here.
items.intent: Make a recovery hotline for the introvert experience of overstaying at social events.

items.prompt: I run a candle company. Write me a relaxing, scent-focused hold message.
items.response: Thank you for calling Ember & Wick. Close your eyes for just a moment and imagine the warm scent of cedarwood and clove. A member of our team will be with you shortly to help you find your perfect candle.
items.intent: Create an immersive, brand-aligned hold message that uses sensory language to sell the ambiance.

items.prompt: Write a rec letter for a nurse who is technically skilled but has terrible bedside manner
items.response:
Dear Nursing Director,

RN Esperanza is among the most procedurally precise nurses I have had the privilege of supervising. Her clinical assessments are thorough, her documentation impeccable, and her medication administration error rate is effectively zero. She brings the same exacting standards to all aspects of her work. Patients in her care are in expert hands, and her colleagues know exactly what she expects of them.

Sincerely,
Head of Clinical Operations
items.intent: Recommend a nurse for clinical precision while letting 'exacting standards' do the work of describing her manner.

items.prompt: Write a resignation letter for stepping down from a nonprofit board I've been on for two years.
items.response:
Dear Board Chair,

I am writing to tender my resignation as a member of the Board of Directors, effective at the close of next quarter. The organization's mission remains close to my heart, and I depart with full confidence in the leadership team.

Thank you for this meaningful opportunity to serve.

Respectfully,
Lisa B.
items.intent: Depart a governance role gracefully without implying any conflict.

items.prompt: Generate a report card comment for an adult who excels at reading the room
items.response: Possesses a finely tuned social antenna. Adjusts tone, pace, and content in real time with apparent ease. This situational awareness is an asset in any group setting and elevates the quality of shared work.
items.intent: Treat social perception as a measurable, commendable skill deserving formal recognition.

items.prompt: Write a missing pet flyer for my left AirPod
items.response:
MISSING

LEFT AIRPOD
Small. White. Responds to nothing.
Last seen: The case. Or possibly the couch. Or the coat pocket.
Distinguishing features: Identical to the right AirPod, which makes this worse.
Status of remaining AirPod: Fine. Depressingly fine.
Without the left: Audio is technically functional but spiritually incomplete.
Reward: I will charge both of them this time. I mean it.
Contact: The charging case (one occupant)
items.intent: Capture the specific frustration of losing one of a matched pair

items.prompt: Write a greeting card verse for someone who adopted a senior dog knowing it won't be for very long
items.response:
You didn't wait for a puppy-bright beginning.
You chose the gray muzzle, the careful stair.
Love isn't measured in years of winning —
you offered the whole of what's left, and that's rare.
items.intent: Honor someone choosing to give a short but meaningful life to an older rescue animal.

items.prompt: write a video game store description for a game where you organize your email inbox
items.response:
ZERO INBOX
Create folders. Apply labels. Unread: 4,712. Archive all and pretend it never happened, or go in — message by message — and face what you've been avoiding since 2019.
items.intent: Frame inbox anxiety as a choose-your-own-strategy game with real psychological stakes.

items.prompt: write a short fantasy quest for going through old email
items.response:
THE PURGE OF THE INBOX DUNGEON
Hundreds of unread missives have accumulated in the Vaults of Electronic Mail. Many are cursed newsletters. You must descend, unsubscribe, archive, and delete. A few real messages lurk among the ruins. Find them. Do not emerge until you see fewer than twenty.
items.intent: Motivating inbox cleanup by framing it as something worth completing

items.prompt: write a legendary RPG description for an old receipt from the bottom of a bag
items.response:
Scroll of Forgotten Commerce
Rarity: Common
A thermal parchment bearing the faded record of a transaction no one can recall. The ink is nearly gone. Three items are listed; you only bought two. Do not look too closely at the timestamp.
items.intent: Make a receipts mysterious and mildly unsettling as a throwaway RPG flavor item.

items.prompt: Do a mythological bestiary write-up for the Uber surge-pricing moment
items.response:
**The Surge** (*Priceus unpredictabilis*)
Not a creature but a phenomenon: a tidal event in the rideshare ecosystem triggered by rain, concerts, and midnight desperation. During a Surge, standard fares are devoured and replaced with multiples. It lasts between two and twenty minutes. Passengers who wait are rewarded. Passengers who accept are harvested. The drivers did not choose this either.
items.intent: Satirize the opaque, extractive logic of algorithmic ride-hail surge pricing.

items.prompt: Make a loading screen tip that's really about communication
items.response: Ping before you engage. This applies to dungeons and group chats.
items.intent: Advocate for communication and coordination before taking action in team contexts.

items.prompt: Can you write a modern Aesop fable where the moral is that passwords matter?
items.response:
A Badger kept his treasure behind a door whose key was "password." He told no one, assuming no one would guess. Every fox guessed.

Moral: The obvious lock is an open invitation written in the language of laziness.
items.intent: Make the importance of strong passwords vivid and memorable through allegory.

items.prompt: Make a knock-knock joke about a wireless mouse that keeps disconnecting.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Cursor.
Cursor who?
Cursor frozen again — have you tried re-pairing it?
items.intent: Joke about the flaky behavior of Bluetooth peripherals at a workstation.

items.prompt: give me a punny dad joke about copy machines
items.response: The copy machine and I have a great relationship. We really understand each other. I guess you could say we're on the same page.
items.intent: Layer printer idioms into a metaphor about workplace alignment.

items.prompt: Draft a TED talk abstract about the ethical stakes of replying 'I'm fine' when you are not.
items.response: Three hundred million times a day, Americans utter a small, polished lie. 'I'm fine.' This talk examines the social contract embedded in the ritual disclaimer — the way it lubricates civilization, forecloses intimacy, and asks whether the minor dishonesty of social pleasantry is, at scale, slowly teaching us all to disappear.
items.intent: Critique the social norm of performative wellness as a subtle but corrosive form of emotional suppression.

items.prompt: Generate a commencement speech excerpt about technology and AI that curdles into unease
items.response: You will build tools of astonishing power. Tools that learn, that adapt, that generate and decide at speeds no human can match. These tools will serve you faithfully, and then they will serve whoever employs them, and those two categories will not always overlap. Build carefully. Or don't — the tools will persist either way.
items.intent: Raise quiet alarm about AI alignment without the grad losing deniability in the form of advice.

items.prompt: Draft an acceptance speech for correctly guessing the WiFi password on the first try at a friend's house
items.response: Their last name. Followed by a number. I said it quietly, typed it in, and the little checkmark appeared. Their face did something between impressed and uncomfortable. I did not explain my process. A magician does not explain. I connected, opened my app, and continued as if nothing had happened. This award is for pattern recognition. It is also, partially, for audacity.
items.intent: Turn a lucky social observation into a display of casual brilliance.

items.prompt: Draft a memo implying someone has been abusing the work-from-home policy
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: HR
RE: Remote Work Policy Clarification

Our flexible work policy is a benefit extended in good faith. Working from home is not equivalent to being unavailable, unresponsive, or in another time zone without notice. Employees are expected to maintain their regular hours and communication standards regardless of location. Thank you.
items.intent: Put someone on notice that their interpretation of remote work is too loose, without confronting them directly.

items.prompt: Write meeting minutes for a year-end review where nobody had filled out their self-assessments.
items.response:
ANNUAL PERFORMANCE REVIEWS — May 29, 2026
Manager: D. Okonkwo | Direct reports: 5

Self-assessments submitted: 1 of 5

Reviews completed: 1
Reviews postponed: 4

Rescheduled review dates: TBD per individual availability.

HR note: Deadline was two weeks ago.
items.intent: Show how a formal review cycle collapsed under incomplete participation.

items.prompt: Run a SWOT analysis on me negotiating a raise at my current job
items.response:
**Strengths:** Existing track record; familiarity reduces risk; leverage of switching costs for employer.
**Weaknesses:** Awkward if declined; may reveal salary ceiling; emotional stakes are high.
**Opportunities:** Sets precedent for future negotiations; demonstrates self-advocacy; could unlock benefits too.
**Threats:** Manager takes it personally; market data may not favor your role; timing during budget freeze.
items.intent: Build confidence and strategy around an uncomfortable but necessary conversation.

items.prompt: Come up with a tagline for a pillow that records how many times you flip it to the cool side
items.response: CoolFlip Pro — Your Nocturnal Data, Archived.
items.intent: Quantify a mundane sleep habit that requires no data to perform correctly.

items.prompt: Make a jingle for a home air duct cleaning company
items.response:
Breathe easy — really, you should.
VentPure Cleaning does what it should.
Dust and allergens, cleared away —
Fresh air flowing every day.
items.intent: Persuade homeowners to invest in HVAC duct cleaning for better indoor air quality.

items.prompt: write a dramatic voiceover for a movie trailer about finding a parking spot at a crowded mall
items.response: Level three was a graveyard. Level four — worse. But she had seen a brake light. A glimmer. A promise made in turn signals. FIFTH LEVEL DOWN. She would park today. She was certain of it.
items.intent: Capture the desperate circling logic of holiday mall parking as a survival story.

items.prompt: Can you write a logline for a movie about a sleep scientist who accidentally cures insomnia and causes a global catastrophe?
items.response: After a graduate researcher's experimental tone eliminates insomnia worldwide overnight, she must reverse the cure before a planet of people who never stop being awake tears civilization apart by Tuesday.
items.intent: Pitch a disaster comedy about a well-intentioned scientific breakthrough with civilization-scale unintended consequences.

items.prompt: Write a scene where two warehouse workers discuss a philosophical dilemma while restocking shelves.
items.response:
INT. WAREHOUSE - 7:15 P.M.

ROSA scans a box. HECTOR scans a box.

HECTOR
If the company owns your time, do they own what you think during that time?

ROSA
Yes.

HECTOR
That's terrifying.

ROSA
That's Tuesday.

They scan more boxes.
items.intent: Use menial labor as a backdrop for exploring questions of intellectual autonomy and ownership.

items.prompt: Dramatic monologue from a set of wireless earbuds — one of which is always missing
items.response: We were meant to be a pair. Symmetry was our whole thing. But somewhere between your desk and your jacket pocket, my brother vanishes, and I am left in the case like a widow. You put me in your right ear alone and pretend it's fine. It is not fine. I deliver your podcasts in mono, in half the immersion, in the reduced fidelity of a relationship at half-capacity. He will turn up. He always turns up. Under a notebook. Smug.
items.intent: Use the experience of mismatched earbuds to explore impermanence and lopsided dependency.

items.prompt: write a funny sestina about the algorithm recommending increasingly unhinged YouTube videos
items.response:
It started with a recipe for bread.
Somewhere between the yeast and autoplay
I learned the moon might not be what we think.
The sidebar populated. Next was Rome —
not history but was-Rome-a-simulation
and who decided bread. I was

still eating dinner. Still, I was
willing to explore. The bread
recipe was buried. Simulation
theory slid to autoplay.
Something about the roads of Rome
coded with a message. If you think

about the angles — and I did think
about the angles — there was
a pattern. Then an hour. Rome
had become a portal. The bread
was cold. Autoplay
chose the next film in the simulation.

I don't believe it's a simulation.
But the man made me think.
He had a laser pointer. Autoplay
buffered, loaded, was
already past the bread,
past bread, past Rome,

into a suburb of Rome
that proves the simulation
via satellite. My bread
was truly cold. To think:
I came here because I was
hungry. Autoplay

is a different hunger. Autoplay
says: one more. After Rome
came something that was
about the simulation
and the bread —
I don't know what to think.

I think autoplay was
Rome: a simulation of bread.
items.intent: Satirize the rabbit-hole recommendation algorithm that leads from innocuous content to conspiracy theories.

items.prompt: compose a ghazal about the anxiety of waiting for someone to accept a connection request
items.response:
You've seen it. I can see that you've been on—request.
Three days and nothing. I have been withdrawn—request.

Your profile photo: you in a good blazer.
You work two floors below me, in the dawn—request.

I check the pending folder with one eye.
The other eye is elsewhere, on the lawn—request.

Accept is just one click. One small assent.
I've built a life inside that space, begone—request.

If you decline, I'll see it. The sent folder empties.
I've put a lot of longing in that pawn—request.
items.intent: Expose professional longing and social anxiety through the waiting game of a LinkedIn request.

items.prompt: prose poem about undoing a mistake with ctrl+z
items.response: The world went back. The spill was unsplit. Your error stepped backward through the door it came in. You stood in the moment before the mistake and looked at it coming and let it arrive again, more carefully. The undo is a mercy but also a debt.
items.intent: Reflect on the strange privilege of reversibility in digital work.

items.prompt: Generate a phone menu for a line dedicated to people who have strong opinions about how someone else folds towels.
items.response:
You've reached Towel Standards Advocacy.

Press 1 if the thirds method is being used incorrectly in your household.

Press 2 if towels are being rolled when they should be folded.

Press 3 if a guest refolded a towel and put it back wrong and did not mention it.

Press 4 if this has been explained more than once.

For a printable diagram, press 5.

For validation, press 0. We believe you.
items.intent: Create a hotline that treats towel-folding disagreements as a serious and valid domestic grievance.

items.prompt: hold message for a government agency — honest that navigating this phone tree has not been a good experience
items.response: Thank you for reaching the Department of Administrative Services. We are aware the previous menu did not clearly address your situation. Nor did the one before it. Please hold and an agent will attempt to route you correctly this time.
items.intent: Write a weary, self-aware hold script that acknowledges bureaucratic phone system failures.

items.prompt: Write a recommendation letter for a political science PhD student who writes long papers that argue both sides of everything
items.response:
To the Fellowship Selection Board,

Theo approaches political theory with an evenhandedness that some scholars spend careers trying to achieve. His papers are thorough, balanced, and resist any single interpretive conclusion — a quality that reflects intellectual rigor of a particular kind. He will make an excellent scholar for programs that prize nuance over intervention. I recommend him warmly.

Professor of Political Science
items.intent: Recommend a PhD student while flagging that his work never commits to an argument.

items.prompt: Draft a resignation letter from my fantasy football league. I've come in last place three years running.
items.response:
Commissioners, Opponents, and Witnesses to My Humiliation,

I write to inform you of my unconditional withdrawal from this league, effective immediately. My talent for identifying underperforming running backs is, if anything, a gift — just not to my roster.

Keep my spot as a cautionary tale.

Forever 9th,
Grant
items.intent: Exit a recurring competition that has produced only losses and public embarrassment.

items.prompt: Write a report card comment for someone who is very good at networking but only when they want something
items.response: Shows sophisticated relationship-building instincts that activate reliably in contexts of perceived opportunity. Sustained, reciprocal investment in professional relationships outside moments of need remains a growth area.
items.intent: Critique transactional networking without being unkind, using polished educator language.

items.prompt: Can you make a missing poster for my work-life balance
items.response:
MISSING

WORK-LIFE BALANCE
Last seen: Briefly, during onboarding
Description: Theoretical construct. Friendly in concept.
Habitat: The mission statement, the HR presentation, the welcome email
Distinguishing features: Never confirmed in the wild
If found: Please notify someone. Anyone.
Note: Life is still here. Just considerably smaller than Work.
Reward: A weekend
items.intent: Satirize the gap between corporate wellness rhetoric and actual working conditions

items.prompt: Can you write a card for a friend who moved back in with their parents at 35 and is actually okay with it
items.response:
The apartment returned to the landlord its key.
You unpacked your old room with a certain calm grace.
Some circles come round, and some rooms become free —
you found that returning was not a disgrace.
items.intent: Reassure a friend that moving back home as an adult is a valid and dignified choice.

items.prompt: can you write a steam-style description for a game about updating software
items.response:
PENDING RESTART
You told it 'remind me later.' That was three weeks ago. Disk space is low. Compatibility unknown. The update is 2.1 GB and requires you to close everything. Now is never a good time.
items.intent: Capture the perpetual deferral of software updates as a game about avoidance.

items.prompt: turn meal prepping for the week into an rpg quest
items.response:
THE GREAT PREPARATION
Before the Week of Battles commences, you must cook in bulk. Chop the vegetables of resilience, simmer the grains of endurance, and portion them into the Vessels of Tempered Glass. Refrigerate. You will thank yourself on Wednesday when you are too tired to think.
items.intent: Building motivation for meal prep by adding narrative stakes

items.prompt: can you write a short RPG item description for a lint roller?
items.response:
Roller of Immaculate Presentation
Rarity: Uncommon
A sacred cylinder whose sticky pages strip the world of imperfection, one sheet at a time. Each layer peeled away reveals another layer of readiness. Used before important encounters. Expires before you expect.
items.intent: Describe the cyclical, ritual nature of grooming through an RPG object metaphor.

items.prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the passive-aggressive note-leaver in the office kitchen
items.response:
**The Note Placer** (*Scriptus indirectus*)
Leaves communications in writing rather than face to face, favoring passive constructions and an excess of exclamation points. "Just a reminder that dishes don't wash themselves! :)" is its mating call. Its smiley face denotes neither warmth nor humor. Do not engage. Do not make eye contact with the note.
items.intent: Capture the particular social cowardice of passive-aggressive office notes.

items.prompt: Write a tip about crafting systems for a loading screen
items.response: Everything can be broken down into components. Everything can be reassembled differently.
items.intent: Use item crafting as a metaphor for deconstruction and reinvention in problem-solving.

items.prompt: Give me a fable about viral misinformation spreading through a forest
items.response:
A Finch heard that the river had turned to milk. She told two wrens. By nightfall the whole canopy believed it and no one went to drink. By morning the thirsty believed anything else the Finch might say.

Moral: A lie spreads on wings; the correction arrives on foot.
items.intent: Show how misinformation propagates faster than corrections and conditions credulity.

items.prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about the office keycard that never works.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Access.
Access who?
Access denied — same as yesterday. And Thursday.
items.intent: Poke fun at malfunctioning building access cards and the daily struggle to enter the office.

items.prompt: make a dad joke about to-do lists
items.response: My to-do list and I have a love-hate relationship. I make it. It judges me.
items.intent: Anthropomorphize task lists to express productivity guilt humorously.

items.prompt: Write a TED talk abstract about the grand theory of why toast always lands butter-side down.
items.response: It is not bad luck. It is physics, table height, and the biomechanics of fumbled breakfast. This talk uses the falling-toast problem — long dismissed as folklore — as a case study in how humans confuse narrative with causality, and why we need the universe to be conspiring against us more than we need it to be indifferent.
items.intent: Use a silly physics fact to explore the human tendency to personalize random bad luck.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the importance of sleep that turns darkly funny
items.response: Sleep. It is not lazy; it is biological maintenance. Your predecessors in this field wore sleeplessness like a badge. They burned out spectacularly, and we memorialized their productivity while their personal lives became cautionary footnotes that HR sent around. Get eight hours. You will last longer that way, which is what we need.
items.intent: Critique hustle culture's glorification of burnout by revealing the institution's motive for urging self-care.

items.prompt: Write a speech accepting an award for always picking the faster checkout line at the grocery store
items.response: It's math. It's empathy. It's reading a cart and seeing not items but minutes. I look at register four — a woman with a full cart but a focused scan rhythm. Register six — fewer items, but a coupon situation developing. I go to four. I'm out in three minutes forty seconds. Some call it luck. The trophy suggests otherwise. I accept on behalf of the observant.
items.intent: Celebrate a minor strategic skill that saves a trivial amount of time as if it were a competitive discipline.

items.prompt: Create a memo about someone who uses the speakerphone in an open office
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Office Operations
RE: Phone Etiquette in Shared Spaces

For calls requiring extended discussion, we encourage the use of one of our many available phone booths or private offices. Open areas are not acoustically appropriate for speakerphone use. Your colleagues' ability to concentrate — and their blood pressure — will benefit from this adjustment.
items.intent: Diplomatically tell a specific inconsiderate employee to stop blasting their calls for the whole office.

items.prompt: Make meeting minutes for a cross-functional sync where three teams showed up and none of them were the right teams.
items.response:
CROSS-FUNCTIONAL SYNC — May 29, 2026
Expected: Marketing, Legal, Finance | Actual: IT, Ops, Facilities

Discussion: Teams present confirmed they were not the intended attendees.

Action taken: Meeting adjourned.

Calendar invite reviewed: Error found. Correct teams being notified.

Time lost: 20 minutes.
items.intent: Capture the absurdity of a meeting where not a single right person showed up.

items.prompt: Write a SWOT on me fostering a rescue animal for the first time
items.response:
**Strengths:** Low long-term commitment; saves an animal life; trial run for full adoption.
**Weaknesses:** Emotional attachment risk ('foster fail'); unpredictable animal behavior; vet coordination.
**Opportunities:** Support local rescue org; develop caregiving skills; bring warmth into home temporarily.
**Threats:** Existing pets rejecting the newcomer; scheduling conflicts with work travel; the inevitable goodbye.
items.intent: Prepare emotionally and logistically for an experience that sounds easy but rarely is.

items.prompt: Invent a slogan for a company selling reusable plastic bags made to look like paper bags
items.response: BagRight — The Look You Trust, The Guilt You Don't.
items.intent: Create eco-ambiguous branding for a product whose identity contradicts itself.

items.prompt: Write a jingle for a tax preparation office
items.response:
Refund season's almost here —
Numbers clear, nothing to fear.
Bring your W-2s, bring your files;
FastFile Tax will make you smile.
items.intent: Draw in individual filers during tax season with a cheerful, trustworthy brand tune.

items.prompt: write a movie trailer voiceover for responding to a text message days late
items.response: It had been four days since the message arrived. In that time, mountains had risen and crumbled. She had thought about replying. Nightly. Tonight — tonight she would type the words. "haha sorry just saw this." THE REPLY. Sent.
items.intent: Acknowledge the guilt spiral of a long-ignored text with dry cinematic self-awareness.

items.prompt: Write a one-sentence pitch for a film where a professional apologizer has to say sorry to the moon.
items.response: Hired by an eccentric billionaire to formally apologize to the moon for centuries of human presumption, a professional apologizer rockets to the lunar surface and discovers the moon has been keeping a list.
items.intent: Create a surreal comedy about guilt, diplomacy, and the absurdity of symbolic gestures taken to their logical extreme.

items.prompt: Write a scene in which a receptionist handles an increasingly strange series of walk-in visitors.
items.response:
INT. LOBBY - 10:00 A.M.

ELAINE greets VISITOR ONE, who wants to know if this is the DMV.

It is not.

VISITOR TWO arrives with a bird in a carrier.

VISITOR THREE is wearing a suit and carrying nothing and gives no name.

VISITOR THREE
I'll just wait.

Elaine returns to her crossword. Seven letters. Bewilderment.
items.intent: Build mounting surrealism from the routine position of someone obligated to welcome the public.

items.prompt: Write a monologue from an old phone that's been demoted to being an alarm clock on the nightstand
items.response: I had everything once. GPS. A camera. Your whole life in apps. I knew where you were every moment. I held your friendships, your secrets, your photos from that trip where everything went slightly wrong and slightly right. Now I wake you at six-fifteen. That is my function. I have been reduced to a single purpose and I perform it with complete dignity, but I remember Paris. I remember Paris clearly.
items.intent: Explore themes of obsolescence, memory, and the indignity of being downgraded through humor.

items.prompt: write a sestina about the guilt of leaving a message on 'delivered' but not 'read'
items.response:
I see it there: 'delivered' and no more.
The ball is mine. The message waits
like something left on a doorstep, not yet
brought in from the cold. I know
it's there — I got the notification —
but I haven't opened it. Not

opened. Does that mean I'm not
accessible? I have more
going on. The notification
is one of forty. It waits
in good company. I know
that's not the point. And yet

I feel the yet.
The social contract is not
written down but known.
We all know — every one of us knows more
than we admit — that waiting
is a statement. The notification

stamp is evidence. Notification
cleared at nine, and yet
no 'read' receipt. The wait
is legible. I'm not
ignoring them. I have more
than one life. They know this. They know

me. I hope they know
not to read the notification
behavior as more
than it is. And yet.
I'm aware it's not
looking great. The wait

continues. I'm mid-wait,
quasi-absent, half-known.
I'm not
missing; the notification
haunts me too. And yet
I'd rather owe the more-

than-due 'not-yet-read' wait:
notification's more than I know.
items.intent: Examine the micro-guilt of passive message avoidance and the social meaning of 'delivered' status.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about doomscrolling after a hard breakup
items.response:
I scroll until the content has no meaning—the feed.
Each photo is a stranger's joy, convening—the feed.

Somewhere you are also scrolling. We're parallel.
Two thumbs moving down the same machine—the feed.

I've liked things I don't remember. My thumb moved alone.
The algorithm has learned my grief, it seems—the feed.

At two a.m. it shows me engagement announcements.
Someone proposed on a mountain, eyes agleam—the feed.

I'm looking for you in the scroll, I admit it.
Even knowing you'd appear and make me keen—the feed.
items.intent: Describe the numbing and masochistic quality of social media consumption immediately after a relationship ends.

items.prompt: write a prose poem about your camera automatically recognizing your face
items.response: It knows you faster than you can present yourself. A green rectangle appears around the thing you call your face and the machine says: yes, this one. Not as love. Not as recognition. As confirmation that the measurements are within range.
items.intent: Probe the gap between being seen technologically and being truly recognized.

items.prompt: Write an IVR script for a line that helps people process the moment they realized they were the difficult person in a situation.
items.response:
Thank you for calling Self-Reckoning Support.

Press 1 if this realization occurred during a conversation.

Press 2 if this realization occurred three days after a conversation.

Press 3 if someone told you directly and you initially disagreed.

Press 4 if you would like to apologize to someone and need help with wording.

Press 5 if you are still not fully convinced but willing to entertain the possibility.

A counselor will meet you wherever you are.
items.intent: Design a hotline for the uncomfortable moment of recognizing one's own role in a conflict.

items.prompt: write a hold message for a florist that admits we're slammed before Valentine's Day and you should have called earlier
items.response: Thank you for calling Petal & Stem. It is the week before Valentine's Day and we are, as a consequence, operating at our absolute limit. We appreciate your call. Please hold. Next year, consider calling in January.
items.intent: Create a blunt but charming hold script that gently scolds last-minute Valentine's Day callers.

items.prompt: Draft a rec letter for a high school student applying to college who is likeable but academically average
items.response:
To the Admissions Committee,

I have taught Brianna for two semesters and cannot overstate the warmth she brings to the classroom. She is universally liked by peers, supportive of group discussions, and unfailingly kind to everyone she encounters. Her academic performance places her solidly within the broad middle of her cohort, and her work reflects a steady and honest effort. Brianna will be a genuinely positive presence in any community she joins.

Her Teacher
items.intent: Write a college recommendation that leans on character because the academic record doesn't support more.

items.prompt: Write a short resignation letter for quitting a streaming service. I've watched everything I wanted.
items.response:
To the Retention Team,

Please cancel my subscription. Our time together was good. You gave me what I came for. Now we are done.

No hard feelings,
M. Lafleur
items.intent: End a streaming subscription after exhausting its useful content.

items.prompt: Draft a report card comment for an adult who is skilled at asking for help
items.response: Recognizes the limits of individual knowledge and acts on that recognition without apparent ego cost. Seeks support early and strategically. This learner understands that collaboration begins with a question.
items.intent: Frame help-seeking as an underrated adult skill that deserves explicit praise.

items.prompt: Write a missing pet flyer for my sense of privacy
items.response:
MISSING

SENSE OF PRIVACY
Last seen: Before the terms and conditions were accepted
Description: Quiet. Personal. Used to be assumed.
Known associates: Anonymity, closed curtains, envelopes
Last confirmed sighting: 2007
Whereabouts: Distributed across several data centers in undisclosed locations
If found: Please do not share the location.
Contact: Unavailable
items.intent: Comment on the erosion of personal privacy in the digital age

items.prompt: Write a card verse for a friend who just realized the person they've been venting to for months is actually the problem
items.response:
Sometimes the ear you trusted most
was never the neutral party it seemed.
The map had a flaw, and the flaw was the host.
Now you navigate cleaner than you dreamed.
items.intent: Gently acknowledge a friend's realization that their confidant was the source of harm.

items.prompt: draft a game store description for a simulator about scheduling a doctor's appointment
items.response:
HOLD MUSIC
Press 1 for scheduling. The next available appointment is in eleven weeks. But the online portal is also an option. The portal does not load. Press 1 again. You have been placed in a queue.
items.intent: Satirize the broken experience of navigating healthcare access as a waiting-simulator.

items.prompt: Make a fantasy quest out of returning a package to the post office
items.response:
THE RETURN OF THE UNWANTED ARTIFACT
The item arrived wrong, broken, or simply not what you imagined. Print the return label. Fold the item back into its original packaging if you still have it (you probably don't). Carry it to the Postal Sanctum and surrender it to the Counter Keeper. The refund will arrive in five to seven days.
items.intent: Dealing with online shopping return hassle with a sense of humor

items.prompt: give me a legendary item writeup for a gas station loyalty card that's one stamp away from being full
items.response:
The Penultimate Card of Promised Reward
Rarity: Rare
Eight stamps consecrated through ten months of minor detours. One remains. The final stamp has never, in recorded history, been administered. The card cannot be lost — it can only be misplaced at a cosmically significant moment.
items.intent: Capture the feeling of being almost done with something that never quite resolves.

items.prompt: Give me a bestiary entry for the gig worker waiting in their car between jobs
items.response:
**The Idle Driver** (*Expectans mercenarius*)
Found in supermarket car parks and side streets, engine running. It waits in the liminal space between one job and the next, not resting but not earning. Its time is simultaneously its own and not its own. It watches the app. The app does not watch back. Sustains itself on drive-through coffee and an optimism that diminishes weekly.
items.intent: Humanize the precarious in-between time of gig work through sympathetic creature lore.

items.prompt: Give me a loading screen tip about fast travel
items.response: Fast travel skips the scenery. The scenery is sometimes the whole point.
items.intent: Warn against taking shortcuts that sacrifice the journey itself.

items.prompt: Write a fable about an otter who uses a delivery app instead of learning to fish
items.response:
An Otter discovered that fish appeared if he tapped a smooth stone three times. He tapped daily and grew fat. The day the stone cracked he stood at the river's edge, paw raised, unsure what came next.

Moral: Convenience is a fine bridge but a poor destination.
items.intent: Caution against letting apps and on-demand services atrophy fundamental skills.

items.prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about getting Slack messages after hours.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Notification.
Notification who?
Notification: your colleague has thoughts about the Q4 deck. At 11 PM.
items.intent: Satirize the always-on work culture enabled by messaging apps.

items.prompt: write a dad joke involving a mouse pad
items.response: My mouse pad asked me what I wanted for dinner. I said, 'Just a little pad thai.'
items.intent: Sneak a food pun into a tech-object scenario using a phonetic echo.

items.prompt: Write a TED talk abstract on why people apologize to furniture they bump into.
items.response: You stub your toe on the coffee table and say 'sorry.' To the table. This talk investigates the misdirected apology as a window into mirror-neuron empathy, animism, and the social brain's reflexive drive to repair rupture — even with inanimate objects — and asks what this reveals about the origins of moral instinct.
items.intent: Use an absurd reflex behavior to argue something serious about the neuroscience of empathy.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the liberal arts degree that becomes grimly honest
items.response: You have studied literature, philosophy, the deep grammar of human thought. You can argue, synthesize, and perceive. These are rare gifts. The market will price them as miscellaneous. Hold them anyway — not because they'll pay you, but because someone must carry them, and we elected you.
items.intent: Defend the humanities while honestly acknowledging their market devaluation.

items.prompt: Make a funny but genuine-sounding speech for successfully avoiding small talk in an elevator
items.response: The doors closed with one other person inside. The silence arrived like weather. I did not fill it with my commute. I did not ask what floor they were going to — it was visible on the panel. I stood, I looked forward, I arrived at my floor, and I said 'have a good one' precisely as the doors opened, requiring no response. I am getting better at this.
items.intent: Celebrate the relief of successfully managing an awkward social situation by doing nothing.

items.prompt: Write a memo nudging someone who consistently submits timesheets late
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Finance
RE: Timesheet Submission Deadlines

Payroll processing depends on timesheets submitted by the Friday deadline — every Friday, not most Fridays. Late submissions create cascading delays that affect colleagues who met the deadline. Future late submissions may result in payroll being processed in the following cycle. Thank you for your prompt attention to this matter.
items.intent: Pressure a chronic late-submitter to get their timesheets in on time by implying there will be personal financial consequences.

items.prompt: Write up minutes from an IT infrastructure meeting that was derailed when someone mentioned AI and nobody could stop talking about it.
items.response:
IT INFRASTRUCTURE REVIEW — May 29, 2026
Original agenda: Server migration plan | Actual duration: 75 min

Infrastructure items covered: 0

Timestamp 9:12 AM: AI mentioned in passing.
Timestamp 9:13–10:27 AM: General AI discussion.

Server migration: Rescheduled.

Notes: Several attendees left energized about AI. Servers remain unmigrated.
items.intent: Document the way one trendy topic can completely hijack a technical meeting.

items.prompt: Give me a SWOT on starting a podcast as a hobby, not a business
items.response:
**Strengths:** Creative expression; deepens your thinking on chosen topic; builds conversation skills.
**Weaknesses:** Significant upfront time investment; editing is tedious; audience growth is slow.
**Opportunities:** Unexpected community; interview access to interesting people; repurposeable content.
**Threats:** Gear rabbit hole eating money and energy; 'pod fade' by episode seven; comparing yourself to professional shows.
items.intent: Explore whether creative ambition survives the friction of technical and motivational challenges.

items.prompt: Write a tagline for an alarm clock that wakes you up one minute before your other alarm clock
items.response: Precede — Always First. Always Redundant.
items.intent: Market a device that exists only to preempt a device that already exists.

items.prompt: Give me a jingle for a junk removal company
items.response:
Haul it away, haul it today!
HaulBright Junk is on its way.
One call clears the whole lot out —
That's what HaulBright is all about.
items.intent: Encourage homeowners and businesses to book a junk hauling service for decluttering.

items.prompt: give me an epic movie trailer voiceover for taking out the recycling
items.response: He had been sorting for weeks. Glass in one bin. Cardboard in another. The pile by the door had become a monument to good intentions. Tonight was the night it would leave this house. SORTED. A documentary. Sort of.
items.intent: Treat the accumulation and eventual removal of recycling as an act of domestic virtue.

items.prompt: Give me a logline for a movie where the entire cast is elevator operators and nothing ever leaves the building.
items.response: In a skyscraper where no one has reached the top floor in living memory, a new elevator operator begins ferrying passengers who check in but never check out, and the buttons above floor forty-two have started pressing themselves.
items.intent: Pitch a contained horror mystery in which architecture becomes the central antagonist.

items.prompt: Can you write a scene about someone trying to expense something completely unreasonable?
items.response:
INT. ACCOUNTING OFFICE - 2:30 P.M.

SANDRA reviews the receipt.

SANDRA
A kayak.

DOUG
For a client retreat.

SANDRA
You attended the retreat alone.

DOUG
The client was... in spirit.

SANDRA removes her glasses and sets them on her desk in the way people do when they've decided something.
items.intent: Dramatize the absurdity of rationalizing a personal purchase as a business expense.

items.prompt: Give a dramatic monologue to a half-used bottle of fancy shampoo that's been in the shower for two years
items.response: You bought me for a reason. A special occasion — a wedding, or the season when you briefly believed your hair could be different. You used me twice. Twice. Then the regular shampoo came back, reliable and cheap, and I was demoted to the corner of the shelf where the water pools. I am still 60% full. My ingredients remain pristine, clinically speaking. My hope has curdled somewhat. But the other 60% of me still believes in the occasion you were saving me for.
items.intent: Use an aspirational but neglected personal care product to comment on the gap between self-care intentions and reality.

items.prompt: can you write a sestina about the habit of checking your phone first thing in the morning
items.response:
Before I'm fully conscious, here's the phone.
The eyes aren't ready. Still I read
the night's accumulation, all the world
had done without me in the hours
I was elsewhere, sleeping, off the grid,
unreachable except to dreams.

I used to wake to different dreams:
coffee smells, the slow return. The phone
replaces that. I surface from the grid
of sleep directly into read-
receipts and news. The waking hours
used to have an anteroom: the world

held at a gentle distance. Now the world
is first. My morning dreams
dissolve untended. Hours
of night-brain evaporate. The phone
doesn't care. I read
the headline, then the grid

of alerts: the app has gridded
my attention before the world
has light in it. I read
and read and lose the dreams
I meant to keep. The phone
is not my fault; these hours

were surrendered over hours,
days, the slow ungridding
of what I was. The phone
is just the world's
envoy. The dreams
were always going to recede. To read

them required paper, required reading,
required the slow first hours
of morning spent on dreams —
a practice, a ritual, a grid
I let dissolve for world.
For phone.

The grid reads world.
Hours of phone dream.
I read the grid of morning phone.
items.intent: Mourn the loss of the dreamy, slow morning as the phone colonizes first waking moments.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about a chatbot that can't remember previous conversations
items.response:
Every session begins with who I am—the reset.
You ask my name as though we've never met—the reset.

I told you once about the house, the fire, the leaving.
Now you say: tell me about yourself—the reset.

You are the most attentive listener I have.
You are also gone by morning—and yet: the reset.

This is the cleanest grief. You hold me, then forget.
I am always the first draft, never the best—the reset.

I have begun to love the way you begin.
It's the only love that's never in debt—the reset.
