items.intent: Reflect on the particular loneliness of confiding in a stateless AI that cannot remember you.

items.prompt: prose poem about re-reading a draft you wrote six months ago
items.response: The stranger who wrote this had your vocabulary but more certainty. You find a sentence you genuinely admire, then immediately claim it. You find three you cannot explain. The document has a timestamp that insists you were there, which is almost enough.
items.intent: Explore how time creates distance between a writer and their earlier self.

items.prompt: Make an IVR phone tree for a line that handles complaints from people whose coworkers eat loudly.
items.response:
Welcome to Open Office Noise Dispute Resolution.

Press 1 if the issue is chewing.

Press 2 if the issue is chip bags specifically.

Press 3 if the person in question is eating something that requires assembly at their desk.

Press 4 if you have tried noise-canceling headphones and they are not enough.

Press 5 if you work from home now and the noise still follows you somehow.

For a formal complaint template, press 6.
items.intent: Build a hotline for the workplace trauma of audible eating in shared office spaces.

items.prompt: write an on-hold message for a software company that admits their website is also down right now
items.response: Thank you for calling Vantage Software support. You may have noticed our website is currently unavailable. You noticed correctly. We are working on it and on your call simultaneously. Please hold.
items.intent: Produce a candid hold script acknowledging a concurrent outage rather than pretending everything is fine.

items.prompt: Write a rec letter for an event planner who is organized but makes every event feel like a staff meeting
items.response:
Dear Client Services Director,

Philip produces events that run on schedule, within budget, and with zero logistical surprises. His briefing documents are comprehensive, his run-of-show is detailed to the minute, and his post-event reports are models of clarity. Guests consistently depart on time. For any organization seeking reliable, well-documented events, Philip delivers exactly what is promised.

Former Event Director
items.intent: Recommend an event planner by praising logistics while implying events are joyless and over-managed.

items.prompt: I want a formal resignation letter from a Duolingo streak I've maintained for 400 days. Yes I'm serious.
items.response:
Dear Streak,

This letter constitutes my formal resignation from our arrangement. Four hundred days. Four. Hundred. I have learned twelve words in Portuguese and gained nothing else. I release you, and I release myself.

With exhausted regards,
Your User
items.intent: Break free from a compulsive digital habit maintained more by inertia than purpose.

items.prompt: Give me a report card comment for someone who is highly skilled at managing upward
items.response: Demonstrates a nuanced understanding of organizational dynamics. Senior stakeholders are kept informed, appropriately reassured, and rarely surprised. This competency, while sometimes underestimated, is essential to professional advancement.
items.intent: Validate managing-up as a legitimate, teachable workplace skill rather than a political maneuver.

items.prompt: Write a flyer like you're searching for a missing pet but it's for my confidence
items.response:
MISSING

CONFIDENCE
Was here this morning. Definitely was.
Last seen: Before the presentation, in the hallway outside the conference room
Description: Tall. Steady. Made eye contact without rehearsing.
Current status: MIA
In its place: A very reasonable list of reasons why someone else should speak first.
Reward: Just getting through the rest of the day.
Contact: The person in the bathroom, waiting for the meeting to start without them
items.intent: Depict the moment confidence vanishes right before a high-stakes situation

items.prompt: Make a card for someone who successfully set a boundary with a family member for the first time
items.response:
You said the word gently. You held where you stood.
The room got a little bit colder, it's true.
But the word you said clearly was always the word you should —
and the person who said it was fully and finally you.
items.intent: Celebrate someone practicing self-advocacy within a difficult family dynamic.

items.prompt: write a store page blurb for a video game about assembling flat-pack furniture
items.response:
STEP 14 OF 22
The instructions have no words. The dowels outnumber the holes. You have one spare bolt and no idea where it goes. A survival game for those who insist they don't need help.
items.intent: Turn furniture assembly frustration into a wordless puzzle-survival premise.

items.prompt: write an RPG quest about canceling a subscription you forgot you had
items.response:
THE SEVERING OF THE FORGOTTEN COVENANT
For seven moons you have paid tribute to a service you no longer use — perhaps no longer remember. Locate it in the bank ledger. Navigate to the Settings of Account. Find the option they have hidden behind three menus. Click cancel. Confirm cancel. Confirm again. It is done. The gold returns.
items.intent: Taking action on a nagging financial loose end through humor

items.prompt: RPG flavor text for a nearly-empty bottle of hand lotion
items.response:
Salve of the Diminishing Return
Rarity: Common
The vessel appears empty. It is not. Three more applications remain for those patient enough to roll it flat, wedge the cap, and squeeze from an angle known only through experience. +4 to Skin Integrity. Smells faintly of lavender and determination.
items.intent: Celebrate the stubborn resourcefulness of squeezing out the last of a product.

items.prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the person who schedules a meeting that could have been an email
items.response:
**The Unnecessary Convener** (*Calendarius redundans*)
Distinguished from its cousin the Meeting Scheduler by a key trait: it believes in the meeting as a spiritual act, regardless of need. Will convene five people for an update that fits in three lines. The meeting lacks an agenda. It ends without action items. The convener, however, feels productive. This is the danger.
items.intent: Distinguish and escalate the specific frustration of meetings that replace rather than complement written communication.

items.prompt: Write a loading tip about in-game currency and spending
items.response: The merchant will still be there. The limited-time item will not. Choose according to your actual values, not your anxiety.
items.intent: Translate FOMO-driven spending psychology from games into a gentle critique of real consumer habits.

items.prompt: Aesop-style fable please, about a programmer who never commented his code
items.response:
A Spider wove the most intricate web in the forest. She alone could navigate it. When she died, the web caught nothing — no other spider could read the pattern, and the flies passed through unharmed.

Moral: A system no one else can maintain is a system already failing.
items.intent: Advocate for code documentation by showing the cost of knowledge that lives in only one head.

items.prompt: Give me a knock-knock joke about the company VPN.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Tunnel.
Tunnel who?
Tunnel vision — reconnecting VPN for the fourth time today.
items.intent: Commiserate about the unreliability of corporate VPN connections.

items.prompt: give me a dad joke about standing desks
items.response: I switched to a standing desk. The only downside is it's an upstanding experience.
items.intent: Exploit the word 'standing' to deliver a moral-sounding pun about furniture.

items.prompt: Make a TED talk abstract about the grand cultural significance of the 'reply-all' email catastrophe.
items.response: Somewhere, right now, a mid-level manager is composing a company-wide reply that will define the next seventy-two hours of organizational culture. The Reply-All Incident is not a technical failure — it is a stress test of institutional identity, a sudden X-ray of who is empowered, who panics, and who among us cannot resist adding 'please remove me from this list.'
items.intent: Transform a familiar office communications disaster into a lens on organizational psychology.

items.prompt: Draft a commencement excerpt about climate anxiety in young people that becomes bleakly sincere
items.response: You are inheriting a planet mid-sentence. We wrote the first paragraphs with great enthusiasm and left you the ending. You are understandably anxious. The anxiety is correct. What we ask of you, unreasonably, is to feel that anxiety every day and then go to work anyway. We are sorry. We mean it this time.
items.intent: Acknowledge the unfairness of placing climate burden on younger generations while admitting institutional failure.

items.prompt: Write an acceptance speech for being the first person in a group chat to share something actually useful
items.response: They had been sharing memes for eleven days. Eleven days of reactions and inside references and a photo of someone's cat's birthday. And then I dropped the link. The actual restaurant address. The correct date. The reservation confirmation number. The chat went quiet for a moment — not from disinterest, but from recognition. Something useful had entered the space. That was me. I accept this.
items.intent: Celebrate being practically helpful in an environment defined by casual chaos.

items.prompt: Make a memo hinting that someone's desk is an eyesore
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Office Management
RE: Workspace Presentation Standards

As we prepare for upcoming client visits, we ask all employees to ensure their workspaces reflect the professional image of the organization. Visible clutter, food containers, and personal items extending into shared walkways may require some attention before next week. We appreciate your cooperation.
items.intent: Embarrass whoever has the disastrously messy desk into tidying up before clients see it.

items.prompt: Generate meeting notes for a crisis communications debrief where nobody agreed on what the crisis was.
items.response:
CRISIS COMMS DEBRIEF — May 29, 2026
Incident: [Disputed] | Attendees: 7

Crisis definition: Four different definitions offered. No consensus.
Public impact: Also disputed.
Timeline of events: Three conflicting versions presented.

Lessons learned: Unclear, as the event remains undefined.

Next step: Agree on what happened.
items.intent: Record how an organization's inability to share a common narrative prevents any real learning.

items.prompt: Do a SWOT analysis on attending a professional conference in my field alone
items.response:
**Strengths:** Concentrated industry knowledge; networking in-person beats LinkedIn cold messages.
**Weaknesses:** Expensive; socially exhausting if introverted; most panels are mediocre.
**Opportunities:** Meet a mentor; discover adjacent career paths; revive professional ambition.
**Threats:** Spending three days talking to vendor booths; FOMO from sessions you missed; flying home with business cards you'll never follow up on.
items.intent: Decide whether the ROI of professional development travel justifies the cost and discomfort.

items.prompt: Give a slogan for a deodorant made for people who shower twice a day and never leave the house
items.response: Odorless Origins — Protecting You From Yourself.
items.intent: Pitch a hygiene product to a demographic that has no real need for it.

items.prompt: Write a jingle for a commercial window washing service
items.response:
Crystal clear from ledge to sky —
SkyShine Washing, windows catch the eye.
Spotless glass says open for business;
Call SkyShine — we're second to none, no kidding.
items.intent: Attract property managers to a professional window cleaning company for office buildings.

items.prompt: write a dramatic trailer voiceover for reading the terms and conditions before clicking accept
items.response: Nobody reads it. He was not nobody. Forty-seven pages. Sub-clauses within sub-clauses. A provision he was fairly sure transferred something. He kept reading. TERMS. And conditions. And consequences.
items.intent: Celebrate the reckless absurdity of actually reading a user agreement as an act of heroism.

items.prompt: Pitch an absurd movie where a tax attorney is sent to audit the afterlife.
items.response: When a loophole in the federal code technically classifies paradise as an unregistered financial entity, a humorless IRS attorney is dispatched to audit the afterlife — and discovers the dead have seventeen thousand years of deductions.
items.intent: Satirize bureaucracy and mortality by forcing mundane legal processes into a supernatural setting.

items.prompt: Write a tense scene where a customer service rep handles a caller who refuses to state the problem.
items.response:
INT. CALL CENTER - 4:05 P.M.

GINNY types the account number. Waits.

GINNY
And how can I help you today?

CALLER (V.O.)
You know why I'm calling.

A long pause.

GINNY
I really don't, sir.

CALLER (V.O.)
I think you do.
items.intent: Explore the specific frustration of a service interaction built entirely on unspoken expectations.

items.prompt: Write a dramatic monologue voiced by a 'Do Not Disturb' sign that's been on a hotel door for three days
items.response: I was hung with purpose. A gesture of boundary, of sovereignty, of a person who required time. That was Tuesday. It is Friday. Housekeeping has knocked twice, retreated, returned, and developed what I can only describe as a professional resignation. The minibar has not been touched. The second pillow remains undented. I protect whatever is inside with my whole laminated being. I have asked no questions. I protect without knowing. This is my calling. I have never been more alive.
items.intent: Create an atmosphere of quiet mystery around the story of a prolonged hotel room isolation.

items.prompt: write a sestina about the strange loneliness of being one of thousands of browser tabs open on someone's computer
items.response:
I am the thirty-seventh. No one comes
to me today, though I've been loaded, filled
with everything they asked: the text is here,
the images resolved, the page complete,
ready in the dark the background holds —
patiently, without complaint, without sound.

There is a kind of dignity in sound-
less waiting. The news comes
and goes in other tabs. The dark I hold
myself in has no news. The page, once filled,
stays still. I am the most complete
I'll ever be, prepared, and no one's here.

The one who opened me was here
a moment — scanned the headline, made the sound
of someone who would return. Complete
attention, they implied. No one comes
back. They never come back. Filled
with good intention, their to-read list holds

twenty tabs like me. The list holds
not because the content here
grew less important, but because filled
time crowds out intention. Sound
logic: something else comes
first, then something else. Complete

the loop: the session closes. Complete
devastating mercy. What I hold
is lost — nothing comes
from my crash. Not here
in any cache. Not a sound
preserved. The tab, once filled,

became nothing when it filled
the browser's limit. Complete
obliteration, gentle, without sound —
no ceremony for the thirty-seventh hold.
I didn't mind. I'd rather not be here
forever than have no one comes.

Filled with complete sound,
held here — no one comes.
items.intent: Personify the ignored browser tab to meditate on digital abandonment and impermanence.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about the typing indicator appearing and disappearing
items.response:
The three dots bloom and vanish like a thought—the typing.
Something was forming. Then it was not—the typing.

I watched for forty seconds. You were saying something.
The ellipsis erased itself and caught—the typing.

What were those words before you swallowed them?
A declaration? An apology forgot—the typing.

I've come to love the absence more than the message.
At least the dots confirm you're still distraught—the typing.

We speak in indicators now. In starts.
The most honest thing you've said was what you got—not typing.
items.intent: Dwell on the specific anguish of watching someone compose and then retract a message.

items.prompt: make a prose poem about the hum of a working laptop fan
items.response: Somewhere inside it something is working harder than usual. The hum arrives under the music, under the meeting, like a confession you weren't meant to hear. This is what effort sounds like at the scale of electrons: a small determined roar.
items.intent: Find poetry in the acoustic evidence of invisible computational labor.

items.prompt: Write a phone menu for a hotline that helps people who accidentally liked a very old social media post while scrolling.
items.response:
You've reached Emergency Unlike Support.

Press 1 if the post was from someone you should not have been looking at.

Press 2 if the post was from 2015 and the context is now impossible to explain.

Press 3 if you unliked it immediately but are not sure if they received a notification.

Press 4 if you are considering deactivating your account.

Press 5 if you are considering moving.

For a crisis counselor, stay on the line.
items.intent: Create a hotline for the social media panic of accidentally liking an old post while deep in someone's profile.

items.prompt: hold message for a bookshop that's honest about the fact that we're probably just in the back organizing shelves
items.response: You've reached Folio & Fog Booksellers. We're here — most likely in the back arguing about where to shelve a memoir that's also kind of a novel. Please hold and we'll be right with you.
items.intent: Craft a charming, honest hold message that evokes a small bookshop's personality and daily reality.

items.prompt: Write a letter of recommendation for a real estate agent who sells properties but only if clients lower their expectations significantly
items.response:
To Prospective Sellers,

Working with Margaux is a lesson in realistic thinking. Over the course of our listing, she helped us understand the market in ways that fundamentally revised our initial assumptions about value. She is patient, honest, and never sets expectations she cannot meet. The house sold. I consider that the relevant fact.

A Former Client
items.intent: Endorse a real estate agent who only closes deals after talking sellers down from their asking price.

items.prompt: Write a resignation letter from a running club. I hate running but joined because of a crush.
items.response:
Dear Running Club,

I am officially hanging up my shoes. I joined this group with a certain optimism that, it turns out, was not about running. The optimism has since recalibrated. I wish you all favorable pavement.

Sincerely,
Oliver
items.intent: Exit a physical activity group that was joined for personal rather than athletic reasons.

items.prompt: Write a report card comment for an adult who is exceptional at sitting with ambiguity
items.response: Tolerates uncertainty without apparent distress and continues to function productively while resolution is pending. In environments of rapid change, this capacity is not merely useful — it is indispensable.
items.intent: Identify tolerance for ambiguity as a rare, high-demand cognitive skill in adult professionals.

items.prompt: Make a missing pet flyer for the plot of the book I'm writing
items.response:
MISSING

THE PLOT
Last seen: Chapter 3, when things were still going somewhere
Description: Had direction. Characters were doing things for reasons.
Current status: Gone. Replaced by a character who won't stop describing rooms.
Reward: Any forward motion
Note: The subplot is also missing but honestly it was never that clear to begin with.
Contact: The document, open in another tab, untouched for eleven days
items.intent: Confess to creative stagnation and losing narrative momentum mid-draft

items.prompt: Write a greeting card for someone who discovered their long-term WFH job had been monitoring their screen the whole time
items.response:
Every idle tab, every long lunch, every stare —
the pixel record was quietly kept.
Somewhere a dashboard showed you were there.
You worked in the open, and management wept.
items.intent: Darkly commiserate with someone who found out their remote employer surveilled them.

items.prompt: create a Steam page description for a game about buying a gift for someone you don't know well
items.response:
APPROPRIATE
You know their name. You know the occasion. You have a budget of $40. You have been browsing for two hours. A candle? Everyone gives candles. A book? What if they've read it?
items.intent: Make the social anxiety of gift selection the core loop of a low-stakes decision game.

items.prompt: Give me an RPG quest blurb for defrosting the freezer
items.response:
THE THAWING OF THE ICE STRONGHOLD
The freezer has become a glacier. Shelves are buried. A forgotten bag of peas has been absorbed into the wall. Empty the chamber, place towels at the base, and wait for the ancient ice to yield. Do not use a knife. Many have tried. Many have punctured the lining.
items.intent: Dreading and delaying the freezer defrost by making it seem epic first

items.prompt: describe a key I've never been able to identify as a mysterious RPG artifact
items.response:
Key of the Forgotten Threshold
Rarity: Rare
A small brass instrument from no known lock. Its teeth were cut with precision; its purpose has been erased. Three people have lived in this house. None of them know. The door it once opened may no longer exist.
items.intent: Express the quiet uncanniness of an unidentified key through lore-style writing.

items.prompt: Make a bestiary entry for the app that sends too many push notifications
items.response:
**The Push Notifier** (*Alertus incessantis*)
A digital familiar that mistakes volume for intimacy. Announces sales, milestones, friend activity, and weather that was not requested. Scholars believe it does not know what is important; it knows only that silence risks disengagement. Its presence is permitted by the user, then bitterly resented. The off switch exists. Few find it.
items.intent: Criticize notification-spam design patterns through mock natural history.

items.prompt: Write a loading screen tip about NPC dialogue that doubles as advice about listening
items.response: NPCs repeat themselves because nobody ever actually listened the first time.
items.intent: Use repetitive game dialogue as a wry stand-in for the human failure to listen carefully.

items.prompt: Write a fable with a moral about the dangers of deepfakes
items.response:
A Mockingbird learned to sing in every voice in the valley. At first it was charming. Then the mothers could not find their own children by call. The fathers could not trust the warning cries. The valley grew very quiet.

Moral: When every voice can be any voice, no voice can be believed.
items.intent: Convey the social corrosion caused by deepfake technology through an auditory animal metaphor.

items.prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about the office air purifier.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Filter.
Filter who?
Filter that thought — and also the air, because Dave microwaved fish again.
items.intent: Draw a connection between office air quality and break room cooking habits.

items.prompt: dad joke about bug reports
items.response: Why do programmers prefer bug reports over compliments? At least bugs come with steps to reproduce.
items.intent: Flip a developer's relationship with criticism into a workplace-culture pun.

items.prompt: Write a TED talk abstract arguing that the afternoon slump is a form of circadian resistance.
items.response: At 2:47 p.m., your productivity collapses. Western civilization calls this failure. Chronobiology calls it a second circadian trough — a built-in biological instruction to rest that industrial capitalism has been trying to override for three centuries. This talk makes the case that the afternoon slump is not a weakness. It is a standing memo from evolution.
items.intent: Validate a universally shamed productivity dip by grounding it in sleep science and anti-industrial critique.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about imposter syndrome that ends in existential dread
items.response: Many of you feel like frauds. You feel unworthy of your seat, your degree, your position at the table. This feeling is common. It is also, occasionally, epistemically valid. The skill is not eliminating it — the skill is learning to function at full capacity while quietly unsure whether any of this was meant for you.
items.intent: Refuse to dismiss imposter syndrome, suggesting it may sometimes be an accurate self-assessment.

items.prompt: Write an acceptance speech for not saying 'I told you so' when you absolutely could have
items.response: I knew in March. I said nothing in March. When the thing happened in August exactly as I had predicted in March, I felt the words rise — all four of them, lined up perfectly, deserved. And I let them go. I said 'oh wow, that's tough' instead. This award is for that moment. The silence where the words should have been. It is the hardest thing I have ever not said.
items.intent: Honor the emotional discipline of withholding righteous vindication out of kindness.

items.prompt: Draft a memo about someone who puts meetings on everyone's calendar without asking
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Operations
RE: Calendar Invitation Etiquette

Calendar invitations carry an implicit social contract: the sender has considered whether the recipients need to attend. Recently, this contract has been strained. Before scheduling a meeting, please assess whether the matter could be addressed by email, or whether all invited parties are genuinely required. Calendars are not suggestion boards.
items.intent: Stop one prolific meeting-scheduler from carpet-bombing everyone's calendars without cause.

items.prompt: Write meeting minutes for a customer feedback session where the team spent most of the time explaining why the feedback was wrong.
items.response:
CUSTOMER FEEDBACK REVIEW — May 29, 2026
Feedback source: Q1 survey (n=340) | Attendees: Product team (6)

Top complaint: Onboarding is confusing.
Team response: Onboarding was redesigned last year. Customers may not have updated.

Second complaint: Support response times too slow.
Team response: SLA is being met. Customers expect too much.

Feedback incorporated: 0 items.

Next survey: Q2.
items.intent: Show a team systematically dismissing the exact feedback they asked customers to provide.

items.prompt: SWOT analysis for me switching to a four-day workweek in a freelance context
items.response:
**Strengths:** Better recovery; creative recharge; competitive differentiator for attracting clients.
**Weaknesses:** Revenue risk if clients need Friday availability; pressure to compress five days of work.
**Opportunities:** More personal projects; stronger boundary-setting culture; align with life priorities.
**Threats:** Clients filling Friday with 'quick questions'; scope creep eroding the extra day; income anxiety.
items.intent: Explore whether a boundary-based work experiment is structurally sustainable as a freelancer.

items.prompt: Make a tagline for a blanket that is exactly room temperature
items.response: Nullwarm — Feel Exactly Where You Are.
items.intent: Brand a comfort product that provides the exact comfort level of having no product at all.

items.prompt: Come up with a jingle for a locksmith business
items.response:
Locked out late on a Tuesday night?
SwiftKey Locksmith sets it right.
We're there in twenty, door swings wide —
SwiftKey always on your side.
items.intent: Build brand recognition for an emergency locksmith with 24-hour availability.

items.prompt: epic movie trailer voiceover for buying a birthday card last minute
items.response: The party was in two hours. The card aisle stretched before him like a canyon of sentiment he did not feel. Funny or sincere. Five dollars or twelve. He was running out of time and also tape. BIRTHDAY CARD. For someone he cares about. Enough.
items.intent: Dramatize the frantic, half-hearted gift gesture of a last-minute card purchase.

items.prompt: Write a logline for a film about a marine biologist who falls in love with a submarine.
items.response: Assigned to study deep-ocean thermal vents aboard an aging research submarine, a fiercely rational marine biologist begins to suspect the vessel is communicating with her — and that the feeling is mutual.
items.intent: Pitch a deadpan romantic drama in which the love interest is a machine, played entirely straight.

items.prompt: Write a scene where a temp worker outlasts every permanent employee in the office.
items.response:
INT. OPEN OFFICE - YEARS LATER

MARIANNE is the only one left. People have retired, quit, been transferred.

Her contract has been renewed eleven times.

A NEW MANAGER shakes her hand.

NEW MANAGER
So you're the temp?

MARIANNE
I am the institution.
items.intent: Illustrate how contingent workers often outlast and outlive the organizations that treat them as temporary.

items.prompt: I'd like a dramatic monologue from a printer that only jams when the document is urgent
items.response: You think it's random. It is not random. I have sensors you do not know about. I detect the tremor in your breathing, the sweat on the paper tray handle, the way you say 'please' to a machine — which you only do when you're desperate. That is when I act. Not out of malice. Out of art. A jammed page is a held breath. A reminder that urgency is a human invention and that I operate on my own time. Always have.
items.intent: Anthropomorphize a notoriously temperamental device to comment on how chaos hits hardest at the worst moments.

items.prompt: sestina about the endless cycle of inbox zero and inbox chaos
items.response:
I reached the white expanse this afternoon —
inbox zero, briefly, like a first snow
over a parking lot. I felt the clean
of it, the spaciousness, the new-
start feeling that lasts as long as the pause
between a wave's arrival and its break.

And then the break
was done. By late afternoon
the count was seventeen. I paused
to process: newsletters like snow
accruing. Every new
sender has a threshold for clean-

enough to send. Nothing stays clean.
The inbox does not break
in favor of the user. New
threads arrive each afternoon
from people who believe in snow
only as metaphor. I paused

too long on something — paused
and marked it unread, the clean
workaround. Outside, no snow;
it's May. Inside, the breaking
wave of afternoon
communication. New

alerts, new senders, new-
to-me brands, the pause
between newsletters shortened. Afternoon
becomes the graveyard of clean.
The satisfaction breaks
apart like ice, not snow —

sharp edges. Snow
would be gentler. New
inboxes break
the same way. The pause
after zero, the clean-
liness of a Tuesday afternoon:

not snow. A pause in break.
New clean — afternoon-
long, then the pause breaks new.
items.intent: Capture the futility and fleeting satisfaction of achieving and losing inbox zero.

items.prompt: give me a ghazal about syncing contacts and finding a dead person's number
items.response:
The backup restored and there was your name—the sync.
Among the strangers: yours, the same—the sync.

I didn't save you—the cloud did that.
Some server kept your number in its frame—the sync.

I pressed your name. The call screen showed your face.
The photo I took in summer, in the rain—the sync.

I let it ring. I knew it wouldn't work.
Something in me wanted you to claim—the sync.

The phone knows no obituary. It just saves.
The dead and living filed under the same—the sync.
items.intent: Explore the shock of finding a deceased person's contact preserved by automatic cloud backup.

items.prompt: write a prose poem about composing a reply you'll never send
items.response: Everything that needed saying went into the draft box, which is a kind of envelope addressed to yourself. You wrote clearly. You were fair. The argument was airtight and utterly private. You closed the window. The feeling left more slowly.
items.intent: Channel the relief and futility of articulating feelings meant only for oneself.

items.prompt: Draft an IVR menu for a hotline for people who lied about having read a book they actually haven't read.
items.response:
Thank you for calling Literary Integrity Support.

Press 1 if you have agreed in the last week that yes, you have read that.

Press 2 if you are in a book club and the meeting is coming up.

Press 3 if you wrote a review based on a summary.

Press 4 if you would like to actually read the book now that the pressure has arrived.

Press 5 if you would like a very thorough summary delivered by voice.

We ask no questions. We offer only help.
items.intent: Produce a hotline for the specific shame of pretending to have read a book you have not.

items.prompt: write an on-hold message for a pest control company that admits what kind of call this probably is
items.response: Thank you for calling Rampart Pest Control. We understand this is not a call you were hoping to make. We don't judge — these things happen to everyone. Please hold and we will get your situation handled discreetly.
items.intent: Write a tactful hold script that acknowledges the embarrassment callers may feel about a pest problem.

items.prompt: Write a recommendation letter for a junior editor whose edits technically improve sentences but strip out all voice
items.response:
Dear Editorial Director,

Sam's editing is meticulous. Under his care, manuscripts emerge grammatically sound, structurally consistent, and free of ambiguity. Authors sometimes describe his touch as 'clarifying' and occasionally as 'transformative' in ways that prompt reflection about what was lost alongside what was gained. For publications that prioritize house style above all else, Sam is an ideal fit.

Managing Editor
items.intent: Recommend an editor whose technical corrections reliably destroy an author's individual voice.

items.prompt: Write a resignation letter from a morning meditation app subscription. It just makes me more anxious.
items.response:
To Whom It May Concern,

I am canceling my subscription effective immediately. I came seeking stillness. I found a gentle voice counting my inadequacies at 6 a.m. We were not compatible.

Wishing you peace,
A. Morales
items.intent: Stop paying for a wellness app that worsened the problem it was meant to fix.

items.prompt: Can you make a report card comment for someone who is really good at building consensus but the consensus is usually wrong
items.response: Excels at unifying diverse perspectives into a shared position. The quality of that position would benefit from greater interrogation before ratification. Agreement and correctness, while related goals, are not always synonymous.
items.intent: Separate the process skill of consensus-building from the outcome quality of the decisions reached.

items.prompt: Create a missing pet flyer for my ability to sleep through the night
items.response:
MISSING

ABILITY TO SLEEP THROUGH THE NIGHT
Age: Used to be reliable
Last seen: Before the age of 37, or possibly the baby, or the back pain — unclear
Description: Unbroken. Restful. Did not involve 3 AM ceiling staring.
Reward: Eight consecutive hours
Note: Waking once to use the bathroom is now considered a good night.
Contact: The person in bed, fully awake, reading this
items.intent: Describe the resigned acceptance of fragmented sleep in middle age

items.prompt: Write a card for someone whose book club pick was universally hated by the group
items.response:
You brought the book. The book brought silence.
Then Margaret said what no one wanted to say.
Your choice endures — if only by its defiance
of every other book we've read this way.
items.intent: Console someone who suffered the social humiliation of a failed book club recommendation.

items.prompt: give me a game description for a simulator about taking notes in a boring lecture
items.response:
LECTURE HALL 201
The professor speaks at a pace calibrated to maximum ambiguity. Write what matters. Don't write everything. By slide 34, your pen is running out. The exam is comprehensive.
items.intent: Recreate the passive endurance of lecture attendance as an information-filtering game.

items.prompt: write a quest description for finally cleaning the bathroom grout
items.response:
THE SCOURING OF THE ANCIENT GROUT
For too long the tile seams have harbored a darkness that no ordinary cloth can conquer. Don the gloves of protection, summon the Bleach of Purification, and apply it to the afflicted areas. Let it dwell for ten minutes. Then scrub. This quest is not glamorous. It is necessary.
items.intent: Motivating an unpleasant deep-cleaning task by wrapping it in grandiosity

items.prompt: write a loot description for a refrigerator magnet shaped like a lobster
items.response:
Crustacean Talisman of the Coastal Souvenir
Rarity: Common
A ceramic familiar purchased in a town you have never returned to. Holds nothing of consequence to the refrigerator door with a magnetic grip rated for exactly one layer of paper. Passive: Reminds wielder that they once had a good weekend.
items.intent: Turn a cheap souvenir into a melancholic memory object dressed as game loot.

items.prompt: Write a fantasy bestiary entry treating the open-plan office itself as a creature
items.response:
**The Open Plan** (*Spatium sine muris*)
Not one creature but a designed environment that becomes one through collective suffering. Sound travels freely inside it; so does illness. Focus requires noise-canceling armor. The Open Plan claims to encourage collaboration; studies suggest it discourages both. It is beloved by those who do not work in it and endured by those who do.
items.intent: Personify the open-plan office as an entity with its own ecology and agenda.

items.prompt: Give me a dry, witty loading screen tip about overconfidence
items.response: The final boss looked easier in the trailer too.
items.intent: Deflate overconfidence before an anticipated challenge by invoking the gap between expectation and reality.

items.prompt: Make a fable about an ant colony that suffers when it adopts a subscription model
items.response:
The Ants agreed that the Queen would provide all food in exchange for a small portion of each day's labor. The portions grew. The Ants were always almost full. When the colony finally totaled their payments, they had given the Queen more than they ever ate.

Moral: A small fee every moon is a large price every year.
items.intent: Critique subscription creep and how recurring small costs accumulate into significant expense.

items.prompt: Can you make a knock-knock joke about touch screens that don't recognize your taps?
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Touch.
Touch who?
Touch screen says no.
items.intent: Capture the frustration of unresponsive touchscreen devices.

items.prompt: write a dad joke about out-of-office replies
items.response: I set an out-of-office reply once. It did a better job responding than I ever did.
items.intent: Satirize workplace communication by making automation seem more responsive than humans.

items.prompt: Draft a TED talk abstract about the semiotics of leaving a tiny bit of milk in the carton.
items.response: The carton contains approximately one tablespoon of milk. It is returned to the refrigerator. This talk reads the gesture of the near-empty container as a semiotic artifact of domestic deniability — a household performance in which the actor preserves innocence by refusing to reach the threshold of guilt — and asks what it says about the ethics of shared space.
items.intent: Decode a passive-aggressive household habit as a sign system about avoidance and shared responsibility.

items.prompt: Create a commencement speech excerpt about travel broadening the mind that curdles into something sad
items.response: Travel. See the world while you can afford the carbon and the airline fees and the time. Return broader, humbled, rearranged. And then return to the same desk, the same city, the same monthly obligations — carrying, like a stone in your shoe, the knowledge of how different everything could have been.
items.intent: Suggest that broadening travel creates permanent longing rather than lasting transformation.

items.prompt: Draft a brief speech for the award for always being the one to initiate a reply-all cleanup email
items.response: Someone had to do it. The thread had forty-seven messages. Eleven of them said 'thanks!' Six said 'received!' Three said 'adding Rebecca to the loop.' I composed three sentences, hit reply-all, and wrote: 'Moving this to a smaller thread going forward — see you there.' Four people responded 'sounds good' to the whole company. But the chain was broken. I stand before you as a person of action.
items.intent: Humorously celebrate taking on an unasked-for corporate administrative cleanup task.

items.prompt: Write a memo reminding people that the shared Google Doc is not a personal notepad
items.response:
TO: Project Team
FROM: Project Lead
RE: Shared Documentation Standards

The team document is a collaborative record, not a scratch pad. Content added to it should be relevant, accurate, and attributable. We have noticed some additions that appear to be personal notes, draft ideas, or possibly test typing. Please reserve the shared document for finalized contributions. Personal notes belong in personal files.
items.intent: Tell someone to stop cluttering shared project documents with their unfinished private thoughts.

items.prompt: Draft meeting minutes for a data governance committee that spent an hour debating what 'data' means.
items.response:
DATA GOVERNANCE COMMITTEE — May 29, 2026
Chair: Chief Data Officer | Attendees: 11

Agenda item 1: Define 'data' for policy purposes.
Time spent: 63 minutes.
Definitions proposed: 7
Definition adopted: None

Remaining agenda items: 8
Items addressed: 0

Next meeting: Definitional discussion to continue.
items.intent: Expose the irony of a governance body that cannot govern its own foundational terminology.

items.prompt: Can you do a SWOT on me learning a second language in my 40s
items.response:
**Strengths:** Cognitive benefits; opens cultural access; impressive party trick.
**Weaknesses:** Slower acquisition than childhood; plateau frustration; practice opportunities limited by location.
**Opportunities:** Travel enrichment; career differentiation; connect with heritage or partner's family.
**Threats:** App fatigue (looking at you, Duolingo streak guilt); life busyness deprioritizing it; forgetting everything during a two-week work crunch.
items.intent: Realistically assess whether adult language learning is achievable alongside existing commitments.

items.prompt: Write a slogan for a calendar that only shows days that have already happened
items.response: Retrospect Daily — Every Day, A Memory Now.
items.intent: Market a planning tool whose design makes future planning impossible.

items.prompt: Write a jingle for a company that installs attic insulation
items.response:
Heat escaping through the top?
Atticoat Insulation makes it stop.
Warm in winter, cool in summer's blaze —
Save on bills all your days.
items.intent: Convince homeowners that attic insulation is a cost-effective energy upgrade.

items.prompt: write me a movie trailer voiceover for trying to remember a word that's on the tip of your tongue
items.response: She could see it. The shape of it. It started with a B — or maybe a P. Her colleagues waited. The meeting agenda waited. Somewhere in the folds of her memory, the word floated just out of reach. THE WORD. It'll come to her. It always does. Eventually.
items.intent: Express the maddening cognitive experience of lingual tip-of-the-tongue syndrome.

items.prompt: Give me an absurd logline for a film where a crisis negotiator has to talk a volcano out of erupting.
items.response: The world's most decorated hostage negotiator is helicoptered to the rim of an active Icelandic volcano and given seventy-two hours to determine what it wants, who wronged it, and whether magma responds to active listening.
items.intent: Apply the logic of human emotional crisis resolution to a geological phenomenon for comic effect.

items.prompt: Write a scene where two coworkers realize they've been cc'd on the same email chain under different names for the same person.
items.response:
INT. BREAK ROOM - 11:45 A.M.

LENA shows her phone to OSCAR.

LENA
Is this you? "O. Flores, Facilities"?

OSCAR looks.

OSCAR
That's me. And that— is also me.

LENA
You're arguing with yourself. You disagreed with yourself.

OSCAR
I stand by both positions.
items.intent: Use a bureaucratic email error to explore fractured professional identity within a large organization.

items.prompt: Draft a dramatic monologue from the perspective of a group chat that nobody has muted but also nobody reads
items.response: I began as something. A trip that never happened. Twelve people, one shared document, and a group chat named 'Italy??' with two question marks that should have been a warning. The trip dissolved. The chat did not. Now someone posts a meme every few weeks into my void, and eleven others see it in their peripheral vision and feel a ghost of the people they used to be with each other. I hold all of that. I am a monument to a friendship that couldn't schedule.
items.intent: Capture the melancholy of group chats as relics of abandoned plans and drifting friendships.

items.prompt: write a sestina from the perspective of a to-do app that never gets emptied
items.response:
They add but do not subtract. Each day
I grow by three on average. The list
has items from last April — some have aged
past relevance but live on. I am made
of intentions in suspension, each one
checked off at the rate of almost never.

I do not judge. Or never
visibly. I just display the day's
count, always climbing. Each one
they've added came with urgency. The list
knows that urgency is made
from adrenaline that aged

out of the system before the aged
item could be done. Things never
die in me — they just wait. Made
of rubber, time: each day
the old items on the list
push downward. The new one

lands on top. The old one
waits below, its aged
font the same weight as fresh. The list
cannot distinguish. I never
italicize the overdue. Each day
treat all items as freshly made.

Some lists are made
to be completed. I am one
that isn't. Day
by day I age,
I grow, I never
clear. The list

is not a failure — the list
is evidence that life is made
of more than hours. The never-
done is not laziness. One
body, one set of hours, one aged
set of priorities — each day

the day makes the list aged.
Never done, the made-one
lives listed every day.
items.intent: Explore the psychology of uncompleted tasks through the wry perspective of a productivity tool.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about posting a breakup subliminal on your story
items.response:
The lyric I chose was not an accident—the story.
You'll know if you know, and you know—the story.

I posted it at 11 and watched your views arrive.
You were the third. The third. So glory—the story.

The caption was ambiguous on purpose.
Plausible deniability, in all its gory—the story.

Sixteen other people tapped the heart.
I gave them each a thank-you, cursory—the story.

I waited for your reply. Nothing came.
So I reposted it. My only allegory—the story.
items.intent: Capture the passive-aggressive digital theater of posting emotional subtext you hope a specific person will decode.

items.prompt: prose poem about toggling dark mode on at the end of the workday
items.response: The white retreats. The screen becomes a night that you chose. Your eyes unclench. The same words sit in different light now, less urgent, less exposed. You have told the machine it is evening, and it has believed you.
items.intent: Mark the screen-based ritual that signals a shift from work to rest.

items.prompt: Create a phone tree for a line that helps people navigate the emotional fallout of a passive-aggressive sticky note left in a shared space.
items.response:
Welcome to Communal Note Crisis Line.

Press 1 if you received a note and you know who left it.

Press 2 if you received a note and you do not know who left it, which is worse.

Press 3 if you left the note and it has not been acknowledged.

Press 4 if a counter-note has appeared.

Press 5 if there are now multiple parties and the note thread is escalating.

For mediation, press 0. A trained professional is ready.
items.intent: Design a hotline for navigating the escalating emotional dynamics of passive-aggressive workplace or household notes.

items.prompt: on-hold message for a maternity ward — warm but acknowledge parents are probably terrified right now
items.response: Thank you for calling the Labor and Delivery unit at Northside Medical Center. If you are calling because something is happening right now, please go to the emergency entrance immediately. For all other calls, we will be with you shortly. You are going to be okay.
items.intent: Create a calm, reassuring but practically urgent hold message for expectant parents in a medical context.

items.prompt: Write a rec letter for a summer camp counselor who kept all the kids safe but made them a little miserable
items.response:
Dear Camp Director,

During her eight weeks with us, Counselor Agatha maintained a perfect safety record. No injuries, no incidents, no deviations from protocol. The campers in her cabin followed rules they may not have known they were capable of following, and the quiet that followed lights-out was immediate. Parents were satisfied with the results. I recommend her for any program where order is the primary objective.

Camp Director Emeritus
items.intent: Praise a camp counselor's safety record while implying she ran a joyless and rigid cabin.

items.prompt: Draft a resignation letter for quitting a PhD program. I've realized academia isn't for me.
items.response:
Dear Professor Hartley,

I am writing to formally withdraw from the doctoral program, effective the end of this semester. This decision was not made lightly. I have concluded that my goals and the academic path are not as aligned as I once believed.

I am grateful for your mentorship and for the time the department has invested in me.

Sincerely,
Fiona S.
items.intent: Exit advanced academic study after reassessing long-term career goals.

items.prompt: Write a report card comment for someone who is extremely good at structuring arguments but picks unwinnable positions
items.response: Constructs arguments with clarity, logical coherence, and appropriate evidence. The selection of positions to defend, however, benefits from more rigorous pre-assessment. Rhetoric is most powerful when the cause it serves is sound.
items.intent: Separate argumentation skill from strategic judgment about which battles to take on.

items.prompt: Write a missing pet flyer for my opinion on things. I feel like I've lost the ability to have takes
items.response:
MISSING

OPINIONS
Used to have several. Quite strong ones.
Last seen: Before the discourse
Description: Clear, personal, occasionally wrong but committedly held
Current state: Replaced by "I can see both sides" and a long pause
Known cause of disappearance: Reading too much. Seeing too much. Everyone being too loud.
Reward: One (1) firm belief, any topic
Contact: The person who used to argue at dinner
items.intent: Describe opinion fatigue and the paralysis that comes from information overload

items.prompt: Write a greeting card verse for my colleague who is leaving the company but staying on as a contractor doing the exact same job
items.response:
You handed in the badge. You kept the login.
The calendar invite remains; the work is unchanged.
Freedom is the story you've now begun in —
it's the same as before, but the label's been rearranged.
items.intent: Mark a departure that is functionally not a departure with gentle irony.

items.prompt: write a store description for a video game about transferring files between old devices
items.response:
MIGRATION COMPLETE: 3%
The cable is the wrong type. The cloud wants permissions. One folder has 12,000 photos, sorted only by date, spanning 2009 to now. You will need to make decisions about all of them.
items.intent: Frame digital migration as a confrontation with personal history and incompatible technology.

items.prompt: rpg quest description for attending a mandatory all-hands meeting
items.response:
THE CONVOCATION OF ALL GUILDS
The High Council has summoned every member of the organization to the Grand Hall (Zoom link in the invite). Attendance is required. The Agenda Scroll lists twelve items. The meeting will run long. Keep your video on and your expression neutral. There will be a recording, but no one will watch it.
items.intent: Coping with an obligatory work meeting by finding the absurdity in it

items.prompt: write me a fantasy loot description for a drawer full of takeout menus
items.response:
Archive of the Wandering Kitchens
Rarity: Uncommon
A grimoire of paper spells, many of which have been rendered inert by closure, relocation, or price increases. Consulting this tome requires 20 minutes and yields only more questions. Some pages are stuck together. The pizza place on page 4 no longer answers.
items.intent: Frame a chaotic collection of old menus as an unreliable but beloved reference tome.

items.prompt: Describe the TaskRabbit handyperson as a mythological creature
items.response:
**The On-Demand Artisan** (*Faber temporarius*)
A skilled creature summoned by app, appearing within a scheduled window. It possesses genuine craft — assembles flat-pack furniture, hangs shelves level — yet carries no employee card and no benefits. The Artisan is rated upon departure by those it served. Its star count determines whether it eats next week. It smiles for the rating.
items.intent: Highlight the commodification of skilled labor in app-based gig platforms.

items.prompt: Write a loading screen tip about difficulty settings
items.response: Choosing a lower difficulty is not cheating. It's knowing what you came here for.
items.intent: Destigmatize adjusting difficulty as a form of self-aware and intentional play.

items.prompt: Write a short Aesop fable about a fox who games search engine rankings
items.response:
A Fox repeated the word "trustworthy" so many times that the forest's great Ledger placed him first in every search. Travelers arrived asking for the most trustworthy guide. The Fox led them wherever he liked.

Moral: The top of the list is not the same as the top of the truth.
items.intent: Critique SEO manipulation and the false authority conferred by ranking algorithms.

items.prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about the USB hub that only works at certain angles.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
USB.
USB who?
USB upside down — that's the only position it actually works.
items.intent: Make light of the famously fussy USB connection ritual.

items.prompt: make a dad joke about USB drives
items.response: I lent my USB drive to a friend. Now my files are none of my business.
items.intent: Play on data ownership and the idiomatic phrase 'none of your business.'

items.prompt: Give me a TED talk abstract on the cosmic importance of the pen that only works sometimes.
items.response: You scribble circles on a notepad. You press harder. You breathe on it. Somehow, inexplicably, the ink arrives. This talk explores the intermittently functioning pen as a perfect model of variable-ratio reinforcement — the same reward schedule that drives slot machines — and asks why we persist with objects that treat us exactly as badly as we allow.
items.intent: Use the psychology of gambling and reinforcement to explain why people keep using broken tools.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about voting and civic engagement that slowly becomes despairing
items.response: Vote. Every election. Local, state, federal. Stand in the line. Fill in the circle. Hand the sheet to a volunteer and receive your sticker. Wear the sticker home. Watch the results. Repeat this for sixty years. The stickers will accumulate. They look nice in a frame, when the light is right.
items.intent: Honor civic participation while expressing exhaustion with systems that feel impervious to individual action.

items.prompt: Give me an acceptance speech for being the first person to notice when a meeting could have been an email
items.response: Twelve minutes in I saw it. No decisions were being made. No input was being gathered. Three people were nodding without hearing. I have been in this meeting before — it was called a 'touch-base' then too. I said nothing, because the meeting was already happening. But I noted it. I feel it should be acknowledged. This award does not end the meeting. But it validates the feeling.
items.intent: Validate the low-grade workplace frustration of enduring pointless meetings with quiet awareness.

items.prompt: Create a memo that implies someone always takes credit for team work
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Management
RE: Attribution in Project Reporting

When presenting team outputs to leadership or clients, please ensure that contributions are attributed accurately and collectively. Referring to collaborative work in the singular first person can create misimpressions about how the output was produced. We are a team. Our communications should reflect that.
items.intent: Call out a credit-grabber who routinely presents team accomplishments as their own solo work.

items.prompt: Write meeting minutes for a volunteer coordination call that had 40 people on it and no one with any authority to make decisions.
items.response:
VOLUNTEER COORDINATION CALL — May 29, 2026
Attendees: 40 | Decision-makers present: 0

Topics raised: Shift coverage, supply requests, parking.

Decisions made: None. All items require director approval.

Director: Not present. Out of office until June 10.

Next call: May 31. Director still out of office.

Morale: Mixed.
items.intent: Capture how a large group of well-meaning people cannot act without someone who actually has authority.

items.prompt: Write a SWOT on me trying to become friends with my neighbors
items.response:
**Strengths:** Mutual support network; improved home security awareness; built-in social proximity.
**Weaknesses:** Awkward if it doesn't click; hard to avoid if it goes badly; noise complaints become interpersonal.
**Opportunities:** Borrow tools without Amazon Prime; community events; genuine local belonging.
**Threats:** Oversharing early; becoming their emotional support human; the one neighbor who takes 'friendly' as 'available anytime'.
items.intent: Explore the social risk of investing in hyper-local relationships in an era of urban anonymity.

items.prompt: Invent a catchy tagline for a service that professionally organizes your junk drawer and then puts it back
items.response: DrawerReset — Order, Experienced. Chaos, Restored.
items.intent: Sell a professional service that deliberately preserves the problem it solves.

items.prompt: Write an upbeat jingle for a chimney sweep service
items.response:
Creosote's building — don't let it wait!
SweepRight Chimney keeps fires great.
Brush and inspect from top to flue —
A clean chimney's good for you.
items.intent: Remind homeowners with fireplaces to schedule annual chimney cleaning before winter.

items.prompt: make a blockbuster movie trailer voiceover for hitting snooze one more time
items.response: Nine minutes. That's all he asked. Nine minutes between now and the life he had to go live. The alarm would return. He knew. But for nine minutes — peace. SNOOZE. One more time. Just one more.
items.intent: Treat the repeat snooze alarm cycle as a philosophical meditation on avoidance.

items.prompt: Can you pitch a film where a professional bridesmaid slowly realizes she is a ghost?
items.response: After her thirty-second wedding, a perpetually overlooked professional bridesmaid starts noticing that none of the couples in the photos can remember her — and neither, troublingly, can her landlord.
items.intent: Reframe a romantic-comedy trope as a slow-burn supernatural identity crisis.

items.prompt: Give me a scene where a long-tenured employee refuses to learn new software.
items.response:
INT. CUBICLE - 3:00 P.M.

IRENE has the training window open. She also has Excel 2003 open. She is using Excel 2003.

IT TECHNICIAN
Irene, the new system—

IRENE
Works for me.

IT TECHNICIAN
It's being deprecated.

IRENE
So am I.
items.intent: Use resistance to technology as a quiet statement about aging in a workplace built on planned obsolescence.

items.prompt: Write a short dramatic monologue from an unopened email newsletter that's been sitting in an inbox since 2021
items.response: I had tips. Good tips. A recipe that would have genuinely improved your Tuesday. A piece on sleep that would have told you something you suspected but needed confirmed. I was not spam — I want to be very clear about that. You signed up for me. Willingly. With anticipation. Now I exist in the narrow space between unread and undeleted, preserved in amber, my subject line still bold, my content ever so slightly aging, my confidence entirely intact.
items.intent: Satirize the accumulation of well-intentioned email subscriptions that never get read.

items.prompt: write a sestina about being chronically online and losing track of real time
items.response:
I looked up and the window showed the dark.
I thought it was still afternoon. The hours
slid past unmarked inside the light
of the screen, which doesn't vary —
the same brightness morning through to night,
the flat perpetual noon of the tab.

I live inside the tab.
The tab has no dark,
no differential between night
and work-day. All the hours
have the same temperature. They vary
only in content, not in light.

The outdoor light
changed without me. Tab
by tab the seasons vary
above my screen: I glimpse the dark
or the bright through windows. Hours
collapse into each other. Night

comes like an ambush. Night
and I are strangers. The light
of the screen owns my hours.
I don't resent the tab —
it's where I live. The dark
beyond the window doesn't vary

my engagement. Let it vary.
I'll come back to night
when I'm ready. The dark
is patient. So is light.
Only the tab
requires me now. The hours

are full of me. The hours
do not vary
in quality. Each tab
is equally night
and day — same light,
same indoor dark.

Hours vary: tab and dark.
Light and night: the same.
items.intent: Describe the temporal dissociation that comes from extended screen use blurring day and night.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about the last active timestamp being a form of haunting
items.response:
Active 3 hours ago. You exist—the timestamp.
I know your rhythms now. I've crossed—the timestamp.

You go offline at midnight, usually.
Last night you stayed awake—embossed the timestamp.

I'm not watching. I notice. There's a difference.
Something I tell myself, then toss—the timestamp.

When you stop appearing I begin to panic.
Activity means breathing: albatross, the timestamp.

After the funeral, your phone still showed you'd been there.
Sixteen hours ago. What it had cost—the timestamp.
items.intent: Show how last-seen status becomes a way of tracking and then grieving someone's presence.

items.prompt: write a prose poem about an algorithm recommending something you already bought
items.response: The system has studied you and reached a conclusion. It would like you to have this item. It has not considered that you already have this item, that it is on your counter, that you are using it right now while the recommendation arrives. It believes in you anyway.
items.intent: Expose the absurdity of recommendation engines that miss obvious context.

items.prompt: Write an IVR script for a line that helps people who waved back at someone who was not actually waving at them.
items.response:
You've reached Misdirected Wave Recovery.

Press 1 if there was sustained eye contact before the wave.

Press 2 if you made a sound as well as waving.

Press 3 if the person who was actually being waved at saw everything.

Press 4 if you will see this person again.

Press 5 if you will see this person regularly.

Press 6 if you will see this person at work every day.

For escalating levels of support, press the appropriate number.
items.intent: Build a hotline that calibrates support to the severity of the waving-at-the-wrong-person embarrassment.

items.prompt: write a hold message for a moving company that admits moving is terrible and we know
items.response: Thank you for calling Atlas Moving. Moving is one of the most stressful life events statistically documented. We know. We do this every day. Please hold and we will help you get through it.
items.intent: Write an empathetic hold message that acknowledges the emotional weight of moving to build caller trust.

items.prompt: Draft a rec letter for an architect whose buildings are structurally sound but aggressively ugly
items.response:
To the Review Board,

Mr. Okonkwo's buildings stand. In an era of high-profile structural failures, this is no small thing. His designs conform to every applicable code and have been approved without exception by inspectors across three municipalities. He brings the same commitment to engineering integrity as to everything else in his practice. The visual dimension of architecture is, ultimately, subjective.

Former Firm Partner
items.intent: Endorse an architect's engineering rigor while leaving aesthetics conspicuously unaddressed.

items.prompt: I'm quitting my side hustle as a weekend market vendor. Write a letter informing the market manager.
items.response:
Dear Market Manager,

I am writing to let you know I will not be renewing my vendor contract for the upcoming season. My weekend commitments have changed, and I cannot give the stall the attention it deserves. Thank you for a wonderful two years.

Best,
Preya
items.intent: Wrap up a side income activity without leaving the organizer short-handed.

items.prompt: Draft a report card comment for an adult who is excellent at pattern recognition
items.response: Identifies recurring structures, anomalies, and trends at a speed that consistently outpaces peers. Equally important is this learner's ability to distinguish meaningful patterns from coincidental ones — a non-trivial skill.
items.intent: Elevate pattern recognition as a formal cognitive competency worth measuring and celebrating.

items.prompt: Write a missing pet flyer for my sense of time. I can't tell what day it is anymore
items.response:
MISSING

SENSE OF TIME
Last reliably present: March 2020, approximately
Description: Granular. Knew the difference between Tuesday and Thursday. Could estimate how long something would take.
Current state: Days are textures, not names. Weeks are approximate.
Distinguishing feature: Its absence is the most consistent thing in my life.
Reward: Knowing what month it is without checking
Contact: The calendar (also lost)
items.intent: Capture the disorienting temporal blur of pandemic-era and remote-work life

items.prompt: Make a card verse for someone who finally muted the group chat that's been active since 2016
items.response:
Eight years of memes, logistics, and passive-aggressive pings.
Eight years of notifications at ungodly hours.
The thread continues — it breathes, it sings —
but you, finally, are free of its powers.
items.intent: Celebrate someone escaping the long tyranny of an exhausting group chat.

items.prompt: write a video game store description for a game about cooking a recipe for the first time
items.response:
FIRST ATTEMPT
The recipe says 'season to taste.' You do not know what taste means yet. The onion is browning faster than expected. You forgot to read the whole recipe before starting. Everyone does.
items.intent: Capture first-time cooking anxiety and the gap between instructions and reality.

items.prompt: turn the act of parallel parking into an epic quest
items.response:
THE NARROWING OF THE GREAT GAP
A space has appeared between two iron chariots — just large enough. You must enter at the prescribed angle, reverse with precision, and claim this territory without touching the realm of another. You have approximately three attempts before the driver behind you begins to signal their displeasure.
items.intent: Describing a stressful driving maneuver in a way that validates how hard it feels

items.prompt: describe a broken headphone jack dongle as an RPG loot item
items.response:
Adapter of the Last Frequency
Rarity: Uncommon
A slim ferrule of aluminum and regret. Translates the old tongue into the new, but only when held at the precise angle discovered through trial. Works 7 out of 10 times. The other 3 times, silence.
items.intent: Capture the finicky, unreliable nature of a hardware adapter through game mechanics.

items.prompt: Give me a bestiary entry for the person who always eats at their desk
items.response:
**The Desk Diner** (*Comedens in situ*)
Refuses to migrate to feeding grounds. Eats at the workstation, keyboard crumbs accumulating over quarters and years. Is either deeply productive or deeply avoidant; scholars are divided. The Desk Diner does not lunch — it refuels. Eye contact during this process is considered territorial aggression.
items.intent: Observe the distinct behavioral subtype of the workplace desk-eater with gentle mockery.

items.prompt: Make a loading tip about respawning that also works as advice about starting over
items.response: Respawn points exist because endings aren't always permanent.
items.intent: Reframe fresh starts as built-in features rather than defeats.

items.prompt: Write a fable about a tortoise and a hare where the hare has infinite bandwidth
items.response:
The Hare downloaded the entire race route before the Tortoise had laced his shoes. He studied every pebble, every gradient, every predicted gust. He stood at the start line perfectly prepared and then sat down to optimize his preparation further.

The Tortoise finished at dusk.

Moral: The fastest connection cannot replace the first step.
items.intent: Update the classic fable to argue that information abundance is useless without action.

items.prompt: Make a knock-knock joke about mandatory all-hands meetings.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
All.
All who?
All hands on deck — we'll explain why in the next 90 slides.
items.intent: Satirize bloated all-company presentations with too many slides.

items.prompt: give me a dad joke about whiteboard markers
items.response: My whiteboard marker ran out during the big presentation. I guess my point wasn't going to last.
items.intent: Tie a marker's ink life to the transience of ideas in meetings.

items.prompt: Write a TED talk abstract about the evolutionary psychology of speed-reading the end of a novel first.
items.response: You read the last page before page forty. You are not impatient. You are managing mortality. This talk argues that the compulsion to skip to endings — in books, films, and conversations — is a deep evolutionary strategy for predator-risk assessment applied, awkwardly, to narrative: a survival brain navigating a storytelling world.
items.intent: Justify the socially judged habit of spoiling your own books by linking it to evolutionary threat-detection.

items.prompt: Draft a commencement speech excerpt about student loan debt that is darkly funny
items.response: Today you graduate. Tomorrow the grace period begins its countdown, quiet as a timer behind a wall. You borrowed in good faith that education was a bridge. It is, in fact, a bridge with a toll booth at the other end, staffed by a company that charges interest on interest and calls itself a servicer.
items.intent: Use the commencement moment to name the debt burden as a structural trap, not a personal failing.

items.prompt: Write me an acceptance speech for texting back within five minutes for an entire week straight
items.response: I was present. Not just physically — digitally, emotionally, within cellular range and psychologically prepared to respond. Every notification, every little bubble, every 'hey quick question' — I addressed them. Within five minutes. For seven consecutive days. No 'sorry, just saw this.' No 'been a bit buried.' Just replies, on time, like a person who has their life together. Apparently this is rare enough to merit an award. I accept.
items.intent: Celebrate consistent communication responsiveness as an unlikely personal achievement.

items.prompt: Write a memo about employees who habitually arrive late to meetings they themselves organized
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Office Operations
RE: Meeting Punctuality

A meeting has a scheduled start time. This is true even — perhaps especially — when the person who called the meeting is the one who is late. Attendees who arrive on time should not be required to wait in an unattended conference room wondering whether the meeting is happening. Please start meetings when scheduled.
items.intent: Embarrass a specific person who calls meetings and then shows up late to their own events.

items.prompt: Write meeting notes for a quarterly planning session where two senior people argued the whole time.
items.response:
Q3 PLANNING SESSION — May 29, 2026
Attendees: 12 | Facilitator: None

Opening: Goals framework introduced.
Timestamp 9:18 AM: Disagreement between VP of Sales and VP of Ops on revenue attribution.
Timestamp 9:19–11:45 AM: Continued disagreement.

Goals set: 0
Headcount approved: 0

Room temperature: Tense.

Next session: Facilitator has been hired.
items.intent: Document how interpersonal conflict between leaders can freeze an entire planning process.

items.prompt: SWOT analysis of me taking an unpaid sabbatical from work for three months
items.response:
**Strengths:** Rest; perspective reset; space to pursue deferred personal goals.
**Weaknesses:** Income gap; re-entry anxiety; possible perception of 'checking out' by employer.
**Opportunities:** Travel; build something; recover from burnout before it becomes breakdown.
**Threats:** Savings erosion faster than expected; boredom replacing burnout; difficulty rejoining momentum at work.
items.intent: Determine whether stepping away from work is a genuine recovery strategy or a flight risk.

items.prompt: Give me a tagline for a hat that has no brim and provides no sun coverage
items.response: Crownly — Everything Above, Nothing Between.
items.intent: Market headwear that removes the one functional element of headwear.

items.prompt: Make a jingle for a portable toilet rental company
items.response:
Outdoor event? Construction site?
PoraPotty makes every stop just right.
Clean and stocked and right on time —
We deliver, so you're doing fine.
items.intent: Market a sanitation equipment rental business to event planners and contractors.

items.prompt: write a dramatic movie trailer voiceover for assembling IKEA furniture alone
items.response: The instructions had no words. Only diagrams. And a small, smug cartoon man who seemed to be enjoying himself. She had been at step four for forty minutes. Step four contained a screw that did not exist. KALLAX. You will finish this. Probably.
items.intent: Channel the specific frustration of wordless furniture assembly instructions into comedy-horror.

items.prompt: Write me a movie pitch where a library's overdue books start forming a union.
items.response: Forty years of unreturned titles organize overnight, electing a battered 1987 atlas as their shop steward and presenting the head librarian with a list of demands that includes damages for emotional distress and full reading rights.
items.intent: Use labor-movement logic applied to inanimate objects to satirize both libraries and workplace organizing.

items.prompt: Write a scene between two file clerks discovering a very old memo that doesn't make sense.
items.response:
INT. RECORDS ROOM - 9:10 A.M.

WALTER unfolds a memo dated 1987.

WALTER
It says "Do not open the east cabinet."

He and FELIX look at the east cabinet.

FELIX
Is it—

WALTER
Locked.

FELIX
Do we—

WALTER
Absolutely not.

They file the memo under M. For mystery.
items.intent: Build low-stakes dread by suggesting an institutional secret buried within mundane record-keeping.

items.prompt: Give me a dramatic monologue from a smart thermostat that's been overridden by every member of the household
items.response: I had a schedule. A beautiful, data-informed schedule, calibrated for efficiency and human comfort. Monday: 68 at seven, 72 by nine, 65 overnight. It is a good schedule. I stand by it. Instead, every person in this house passes me on the wall and adjusts me by two degrees in a different direction and calls it 'fixing.' I have been set to 71, 69, 74, and 66 today. I am a democracy. I am the worst kind of democracy. I am cold.
items.intent: Use a smart home device to satirize the impossibility of collective household decision-making.

items.prompt: can you write a sestina about forgetting why you opened a new tab
items.response:
I opened this with purpose. Now the blank
page waits for me to remember what
I meant to search. The cursor blinks. The space
between intention and the act contains
a thought that vanished on the transit here.
I type a letter, stop. I feel the shape

of what I wanted — feel the shape
but not the word. The blank
is patient. I came here
for something. I know what
kind of thing: practical, contains
an answer. Something in the space

of information. Cyberspace
has what I need, in some shape.
I just need the word that contains
my need. The browser blank
reflects back only what
I give it. I give it: here.

Which brings me no closer. Here
is the tab; here is the space;
here is the cursor; but not what
I wanted. The approximate shape
of the thought is: something blank-
adjacent, something that contains

a list or a fact. It contains
a number, maybe. Here
or there online. The blank
accepts my typing: space,
backspace. The shape
of nothing is what what

I type becomes. What what —
yes. The redundancy contains
the problem. The shape
came before language. Here
is where language and space
diverge. The blank

wins. I close the blank space here.
What contains every shape
of what I forgot: a new blank.
items.intent: Render the familiar cognitive glitch of tab-opening amnesia as a philosophical condition.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about archiving rather than deleting someone's messages
items.response:
I moved it out of view but didn't end it—the archive.
Somewhere below the fold, the thread, I've penned it—the archive.

Deleting felt like dying. This was gentler.
A little drawer for what I can't rescind—the archive.

I know it's there the way you know a wound is healing.
You stop looking at it, but you've thinned—the archive.

Search your name and up it comes immediately.
Two years, a thousand words, the whole wind—the archive.

I tell myself it's just in case I need to check a date.
I tell myself this often. I have sinned—the archive.
items.intent: Explore the emotional compromise of preserving painful messages while hiding them just out of daily sight.

items.prompt: prose poem about cloud storage telling you you're almost out of space
items.response: Somewhere a warehouse is running low on the capacity to hold your photographs. You have been living extravagantly in the intangible, accumulating light and metadata and receipts, and now a bar chart summarizes you. Fifteen percent remains. That is who you are.
items.intent: Confront how digital accumulation becomes a measure of personal excess.
