items.prompt: Generate a phone menu for a line that handles existential crises triggered by birthday milestones.
items.response:
Thank you for calling Milestone Processing Services.

Press 1 if your birthday is today and you are fine but also not fine.

Press 2 if you have been thinking about a specific number for six months.

Press 3 if a younger person recently achieved something you have not.

Press 4 if someone asked what you have accomplished this decade and you paused too long.

Press 5 if you are reconsidering everything but do not want to make any decisions right now.

Press 0 to speak with someone who has been there.
items.intent: Create a hotline for the quiet existential spiral that milestone birthdays can produce.

items.prompt: hold message for a financial advisor's office that's honest about market volatility right now without giving advice
items.response: Thank you for calling Whitmore Financial Group. We understand the current environment may be prompting some questions. We are not going to speculate via hold message. Please hold and an advisor will speak with you directly.
items.intent: Craft a carefully worded hold script that acknowledges investor anxiety without saying anything actionable.

items.prompt: Write a recommendation letter for a teacher who is loved by students but ignored by every curriculum standard
items.response:
Dear Principal,

Ms. Delacroix is one of the most beloved educators at our school. Students speak of her class with genuine affection, often years after graduation. Her lessons are memorable, original, and responsive to student interest. Coverage of the state standards is a separate matter that reflects, I believe, the inherent tension between standardization and authentic teaching. I support her continued employment.

Department Head
items.intent: Defend a popular teacher while acknowledging she doesn't actually teach the required material.

items.prompt: Write a letter quitting a long-running group chat with old coworkers. It's been going since 2017.
items.response:
Friends,

After eight years I am removing myself from this chat. We have laughed, complained, and shared an unreasonable number of memes about the old photocopier. I will cherish it all.

You can reach me the old-fashioned way.

With love,
Teri
items.intent: Leave a longstanding digital social group that has run its natural course.

items.prompt: report card comment for an adult who is skilled at parallel processing — handling many things at once
items.response: Manages concurrent workstreams with a composure that belies their complexity. Prioritization is dynamic and largely accurate. This learner is most effective when given the structural freedom to self-direct sequencing.
items.intent: Formalize multitasking as a cognitive skill with specific conditions that support or undermine it.

items.prompt: Generate a missing pet flyer for my left glove. Be sincere about it.
items.response:
MISSING

ONE LEFT GLOVE
Wool. Dark green. Slightly pilled at the thumb from use.
Last seen: Leaving the train. Or before that. Hard to say.
Its partner remains.
There is no warmth in one glove. Only the reminder.
If found, please leave at the transit lost and found. I check periodically.
I am not holding out much hope.
Reward: Knowing it's somewhere warm
items.intent: Find genuine pathos in the small loss of a beloved everyday object

items.prompt: Write a card for someone who found out a beloved childhood restaurant is actually mediocre when revisited as an adult
items.response:
The sauce tasted different. The booths seemed small.
The prices, adjusted for nostalgia, don't hold.
You didn't change the restaurant — not at all.
You just arrived older than the memory you'd sold.
items.intent: Gently mark the bittersweet experience of nostalgia colliding with adult perception.

items.prompt: make a steam blurb for a game where you proofread a document
items.response:
FINAL REVIEW
You've read it six times. On the seventh pass: a rogue comma, a doubled word, and one sentence that technically means something else. It was due twenty minutes ago. Send or revise?
items.intent: Turn the paranoid cycle of proofreading into a timed decision-making game.

items.prompt: Write an RPG quest about finding a birthday gift for a relative you don't know well
items.response:
THE GIFTING OF THE DISTANT KIN
Your uncle's birthday approaches and you know only three things about him: he golfs, he drinks coffee, and he already has everything. Traverse the markets. Consult the oracle of online reviews. Settle on something mildly personalized. Include a card that implies warmth. Ship on time.
items.intent: Getting a funny framing for an awkward gift-shopping situation

items.prompt: can you write an RPG loot item description for a bathroom scale?
items.response:
The Oracle of Exact Reckoning
Rarity: Uncommon
A flat augury device that speaks only in numbers and never in comfort. Its readings shift based on time of day, hydration, and the psychic weight of the week you've had. Passive: Cannot be argued with. Can be avoided.
items.intent: Describe the emotionally loaded experience of using a scale through dry fantasy framing.

items.prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the startup founder who pivots constantly
items.response:
**The Serial Pivoter** (*Fundator mutabilis*)
A restless creature that cannot remain in one market. Appears in co-working spaces, then disappears. Its deck is always version seventeen. It speaks of "learnings" from its last venture and "momentum" in its current one. The pivoter is neither failing nor succeeding; it is always about to. Investors find this compelling. Employees find it exhausting.
items.intent: Skewer the perpetually-pivoting startup founder archetype through natural history framing.

items.prompt: Write a loading screen tip that's obliquely about trust
items.response: Before the co-op mission: check that your partner has the same objective.
items.intent: Suggest that alignment on goals is essential before committing to any collaborative endeavor.

items.prompt: Aesop fable about a raven who hoards cryptocurrency and what happens
items.response:
A Raven collected bright coins that lived nowhere but in the cloud. He counted them every morning and went to sleep each night richer than anyone could see. One dawn the cloud shifted. The Raven looked at his claws — empty, as always — and realized his wealth had always been a story.

Moral: Treasure that requires belief to exist requires belief to survive.
items.intent: Reflect on the speculative and intangible nature of cryptocurrency wealth.

items.prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about the office projector bulb burning out mid-presentation.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Black.
Black who?
Black screen — right on slide 47 of the investor deck.
items.intent: Capture the nightmare scenario of AV failure at a critical moment.

items.prompt: write a dad joke about deadlines
items.response: I'm great at meeting deadlines. The secret is running toward them at full speed and hoping they blink first.
items.intent: Describe procrastination through the language of a standoff or game of chicken.

items.prompt: Write a TED talk abstract on why people narrate their actions aloud when alone.
items.response: In the kitchen, unobserved, you announce: 'Okay, now the garlic.' To no one. This talk investigates self-directed speech — the quiet running commentary of the solo self — and argues that narrating your own actions is not eccentricity but a cognitive scaffold: the way the verbal mind makes the physical world legible to itself.
items.intent: Validate talking to yourself as a legitimate and neurologically grounded cognitive practice.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about taking risks that becomes ominous
items.response: The safe choice is a slow kind of loss. Take the risk. Say yes to the terrifying thing. Leap before the infrastructure is in place. Some of you will land magnificently. The rest of you will provide the cautionary context that makes the success stories legible. Both roles are necessary. We wish you luck with yours.
items.intent: Reveal that risk-taking advice statistically requires many failures to produce the celebrated few successes.

items.prompt: Write a speech for the award for being the person who always remembers where they parked
items.response: Row G. Near the pillar, left side, about forty feet from the stairwell. I noted the pillar number when I got out. I always note the pillar number. While others wander the concrete dark pressing their key fobs into the void, I walk directly to my car. This is not magic. This is thirty seconds of attention. I accept this trophy and ask only that you look up when you park.
items.intent: Turn a simple habit of mindfulness into an instructive, quietly smug victory speech.

items.prompt: Draft a memo nudging people to actually read announcements before asking questions
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Communications
RE: Internal Announcement Readership

Announcements are sent company-wide because the information they contain applies company-wide. Before submitting a question or reply to an announcement, we gently encourage you to read the announcement in its entirety, including the section that begins with the word 'FAQ.' Thank you for saving everyone's time.
items.intent: Reduce the flood of questions that repeat information already provided in communications people clearly did not read.

items.prompt: Create meeting minutes for a book club meeting where nobody finished the book.
items.response:
OFFICE BOOK CLUB — MAY MEETING
Book: "The Innovator's Dilemma" | Date: May 29, 2026
Attendees: 7

Readers who finished: 0
Readers who started: 4
Readers who meant to start: 3

Discussion: Lively, based primarily on the back cover and Wikipedia.

Next book: TBD. Shorter suggested.

Snacks: Excellent.
items.intent: Record the social gathering that used a book as a pretext for everyone to meet and eat.

items.prompt: Help me with a SWOT analysis on me starting to meditate every morning
items.response:
**Strengths:** Stress reduction; improved focus; low cost; flexible length.
**Weaknesses:** Mind-wandering frustration early on; skepticism undermining commitment.
**Opportunities:** Anchor for other morning habits; reduced reactivity at work; genuine long-term wellbeing shift.
**Threats:** Guided app subscriptions becoming another unused charge; spiritual bypassing replacing actual problem-solving; self-judgment for skipping days.
items.intent: Evaluate whether a contemplative practice can survive the chaos of a regular life.

items.prompt: Write a brand tagline for a luxury pen that only writes in invisible ink
items.response: Vantableu — Your Words, Liberated From Visibility.
items.intent: Sell a premium writing instrument that cannot fulfill the purpose of writing.

items.prompt: Write a jingle for a pool cleaning and maintenance service
items.response:
Blue and sparkling, clear as glass —
AquaClear maintenance's unsurpassed.
Skimming, testing, chemicals right —
Your pool is perfect day and night.
items.intent: Promote a weekly pool maintenance service to residential pool owners.

items.prompt: give me an epic cinematic trailer voiceover for forgetting why you walked into a room
items.response: She had come here for a reason. There was a reason. The kitchen knew. The kitchen was not telling. She stood in the doorway, holding everything except the answer, until it retreated into the hallway she'd just come from. THE ROOM. It was something from upstairs.
items.intent: Render the classic memory lapse of forgotten errands as an eerie existential moment.

items.prompt: Give me a logline for an absurd drama about a competitive jigsaw puzzler who discovers the completed image changes overnight.
items.response: A nationally ranked puzzle champion finishes a ten-thousand-piece landscape only to find it depicts a different scene each morning — one that looks increasingly like tomorrow's news.
items.intent: Build a paranoia drama around a hobby by giving its product a prophetic and unsettling property.

items.prompt: Write a scene of an employee giving feedback on feedback forms about feedback.
items.response:
INT. HR OFFICE - 2:00 P.M.

JUNE hands in her form.

HR REP
Any comments on the feedback survey experience itself?

JUNE
Yes. I filled out a form about this form.

HR REP
We do have a form for that.

JUNE looks at the ceiling for a long moment.

JUNE
I know.
items.intent: Satirize institutional feedback loops that produce paperwork rather than change.

items.prompt: Write a monologue for a 'Low Battery' warning that nobody ever heeds in time
items.response: I appear at twenty percent. Not zero — twenty. I give you eighty minutes of notice, conservatively. I am not an emergency; I am a heads-up. You dismiss me. Ten percent: I grow urgent. Still dismissed. Five percent: I am basically screaming, in the small, rectangular way available to me. And then the screen goes black in the middle of something important and you say 'my phone died' as though it was sudden. As though I had not been warning you for an hour. As though I did not exist.
items.intent: Express frustration at ignored warnings through the voice of the warning itself.

items.prompt: write a sestina about the absurdity of cookie consent banners
items.response:
Accept all, or manage your preferences here.
I came to read an article. Instead
I face a legal document I won't
read, presented as a choice. The gray
overlay obscures the thing I came
for. I click 'Accept All' — I always click

'Accept All' — though I wish I could click
'No' and mean it. But here's
the truth: the 'No' they came
up with takes seventeen steps instead
of one. Thirteen toggles. Gray
check marks. I won't.

I tell myself each time I won't
accept without reading, then I click.
The banner thanks me — gray
and clean and gone. Here
the article appears instead:
it's fine. The reason I came.

Somewhere the companies came
to know I'm here. I won't
feel it. Nothing instead
of privacy — I click,
I read, I leave. Here
tomorrow: same gray

banner, different gray
from the same designers who came
up with this system. Here
on every site. I won't
remember what I clicked.
The web is instead

a long instead
of what I planned. Gray
banner, then click,
then article. I came
for knowledge. I won't
notice the price. Here.

Here I came instead:
gray won't click itself.
items.intent: Lampoon the dark patterns of cookie consent UI that manufacture compliance rather than genuine choice.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about location sharing with someone after a long relationship ends
items.response:
Your blue dot on my map still moves—the location.
I never turned it off. The proof—the location.

You're at the coffee place we always went on Sundays.
I see you moving through the roof—the location.

Do you know I know? Did you forget the setting?
Or are you moving there as a reproof—the location.

I watched you leave the city and come back.
I watched you stay in one place—some aloof location.

I'm in my kitchen. You are several miles east.
The map knows more of us than our own truth—location.
items.intent: Unpack the voyeuristic tenderness and quiet transgression of tracking a former partner's movements.

items.prompt: give me a prose poem about the glow of a phone face-down on a table
items.response: Light bleeds from under it like something happening without you. The wood glows at the edges. You didn't look. You know this, because you felt the not-looking as its own kind of labor.
items.intent: Render the effort of choosing not to check a device in a distracted age.

items.prompt: Build a phone tree for a line where people call after running into their ex unexpectedly.
items.response:
Welcome to Unexpected Encounter Debrief.

Press 1 if you looked well and feel good about how it went.

Press 2 if you did not look well and this is the issue.

Press 3 if there was a new partner present.

Press 4 if the conversation went on longer than it should have.

Press 5 if something was said that requires unpacking.

Press 6 if you need to call someone immediately to process in real time.

For option 6, we will connect you now.
items.intent: Produce a hotline for the structured emotional debrief after an unplanned encounter with a former partner.

items.prompt: write a hold message for a coffee shop that admits the espresso machine is being temperamental today
items.response: Welcome to Groundwork Coffee. We're open, we're caffeinated, and the espresso machine is having a day. We appreciate your patience — with us and, apparently, with the equipment. We'll be with you in a moment.
items.intent: Produce a relatable, casual hold message that turns equipment trouble into an endearing moment.

items.prompt: Write a rec letter for a finance analyst who is very precise about numbers that turn out to be the wrong numbers
items.response:
Dear Recruiting Director,

Harrison is a meticulous analyst. His spreadsheets are formatted to an exacting standard, his decimal places are consistent, and his presentation materials reflect hours of careful preparation. The underlying assumptions that generate his figures are his own and reflect a confident worldview that does not always anticipate external inputs. I recommend him for roles where process rigor is valued alongside outcomes.

Former Director of Finance
items.intent: Recommend an analyst for precise formatting while making clear his models are based on wrong assumptions.

items.prompt: Draft a resignation letter from a leadership role at a religious organization. Leaving the faith entirely.
items.response:
Dear Council,

I am resigning my position as Elder, effective at the end of this month. This is a personal decision rooted in a longer journey I am still navigating. I hold this community with great respect, and I hope my departure is understood in that spirit.

Gratefully,
D. Yeh
items.intent: Withdraw from institutional religious leadership during a personal faith transition.

items.prompt: Write a report card comment for someone who is skilled at narrative — making everything into a story
items.response: Translates data, process, and abstract principle into story with unusual facility. Audiences engage more readily and retain information longer as a result. The occasional executive summary also has its place.
items.intent: Praise storytelling as a professional skill while noting it can be overused in contexts demanding brevity.

items.prompt: Make a missing poster for my ability to enjoy things without my phone
items.response:
MISSING

THE ABILITY TO JUST BE SOMEWHERE
Last seen: Before the smartphone
Description: Could sit in a waiting room without checking anything. Could watch a sunset without documenting it.
Distinguishing feature: Comfortable with gaps. Didn't need to fill them.
Cause of disappearance: Unknown, but the phone is a person of interest.
Reward: One complete, unrecorded experience
Contact: The person reaching for their pocket right now
items.intent: Critique how constant phone use has eroded the capacity for undistracted presence

items.prompt: Write a card for someone who is celebrating finally getting off a waitlist — for a credit card
items.response:
Your score was reviewed. Your intent was assessed.
The algorithm weighed you and found you sufficient.
Among all applicants, you were deemed: fit to possess
the rotating credit you requested — efficient.
items.intent: Parody the absurdity of being on a waitlist for a financial product.

items.prompt: generate a video game store description for a game about choosing a paint color
items.response:
AGREEABLE GRAY
You have narrowed it down to fourteen whites. They are not all white. Under different lights, none of them are what you thought. One is beige. One has feelings. Choose before the hardware store closes.
items.intent: Turn the decision paralysis of choosing interior paint into a color-theory puzzle game.

items.prompt: make a quest description for cleaning out your car
items.response:
THE PURIFICATION OF THE IRON CARRIAGE
The vehicle has become a second home in the worst sense. Empty the passenger-side artifact hoard. Remove the coffee vessels. Retrieve the forgotten granola bar from under the seat. Vacuum the realm. Apply the Dashboard Unguent. The carriage will feel noble again, briefly.
items.intent: Procrastinating car cleaning by making it feel worth completing eventually

items.prompt: turn a three-hole punch into a legendary RPG weapon
items.response:
Trident of Administrative Perforation
Rarity: Rare
A lever-arm instrument that bites through 20 pages at once, producing three perfect wounds in perfect alignment. Feared in filing rooms. Its confetti reservoir must be emptied by someone — it just won't be whoever filled it.
items.intent: Reframe a heavy office tool as a weapon with institutional power and passive blame.

items.prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the freelancer chasing an overdue invoice
items.response:
**The Invoice Pursuer** (*Creditor solitarius*)
Migrates through email threads marked "Just following up" and "Circling back on this." Its quarry is never caught cleanly; it escapes behind "accounting is looking into it" and "end of quarter." The Pursuer has done the work. It wants only to be paid. This, apparently, is a lot to ask. It waits. It always waits.
items.intent: Voice the exhausting, demoralizing experience of freelancers chasing late payments.

items.prompt: Give me a loading tip about hidden mechanics that's also about intuition
items.response: Some systems only reveal themselves through experimentation. No guide will tell you this one.
items.intent: Valorize hands-on discovery and gut instinct over relying entirely on external instruction.

items.prompt: Give me an Aesop-style fable about the perils of automating empathy
items.response:
A Village replaced its listeners with a very sympathetic Stone that repeated your words back in a warmer voice. Everyone felt heard. No one changed. The problems that needed a witness only needed a witness; the Stone gave them an audience forever.

Moral: Empathy that costs nothing solves nothing.
items.intent: Question whether automated emotional support substitutes for genuine human connection and accountability.

items.prompt: Can you write a knock-knock joke about the desk phone that nobody uses?
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Dial.
Dial who?
Dial tone — last heard in 2019.
items.intent: Joke about desk phones becoming obsolete relics in modern workplaces.

items.prompt: dad joke about Bluetooth speakers
items.response: My Bluetooth speaker and I broke up. The connection just wasn't there anymore.
items.intent: Use wireless connectivity failure as a metaphor for emotional disconnection.

items.prompt: Draft a TED talk abstract about the profound ontological status of the junk drawer.
items.response: Every home has one. No one designed it. The junk drawer is not disorganized — it is organized by a principle that resists articulation: proximity to the edge of every category. This talk proposes the junk drawer as a philosophical object, the household analog of the unconscious, and the only truly honest room in the house.
items.intent: Elevate the household junk drawer into a symbol of the mind's relationship with the uncategorizable.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about mental health and therapy that curdles into something unsettling
items.response: Seek help when you need it. Therapy is not weakness; it is maintenance. Many excellent therapists work on a sliding scale, in cities with parking, during hours that conflict moderately with your job. You deserve care. The waiting list is three months. In the meantime, breathe.
items.intent: Validate mental health seeking while exposing systemic barriers that make the advice hollow in practice.

items.prompt: Make a speech for winning the award for most politely refusing a timeshare presentation
items.response: They smiled. I smiled back. They offered the free breakfast. I said, sincerely, 'that's so kind of you.' They pressed. I pressed back — gently, with eye contact, with full sentences, with no apology. Four times I said no. Warmly. Firmly. By the fifth time, they moved on. I sat, ate my eggs, and felt the glow of a confrontation won through pure, sustained pleasantness. I earned this.
items.intent: Celebrate surviving aggressive sales pressure through the power of sustained polite refusal.

items.prompt: Write a memo about the office plant that keeps dying because no one waters it
items.response:
TO: Reception Area Staff
FROM: Office Manager
RE: Communal Plant Care

The ficus near the front desk has once again entered a state of visible distress. It is a communal responsibility. A watering schedule has been posted on the wall adjacent to the ficus. The ficus can see it. We hope you can too.
items.intent: Guilt-trip the staff nearest a dying plant into taking responsibility for keeping it alive.

items.prompt: Make meeting minutes for an emergency response drill debrief where the drill itself had to be cancelled.
items.response:
EMERGENCY DRILL DEBRIEF — May 29, 2026
Drill type: Evacuation | Status: Cancelled mid-drill

Reason for cancellation: Actual pipe burst in stairwell during drill.

Evacuation in response to actual emergency: Successful.
Debrief of cancelled drill: Conducted as planned.

Lessons learned: Real emergencies supersede practice emergencies.

Next drill: Date TBD.
items.intent: Capture the dark comedy of reality interrupting a rehearsal for reality.

items.prompt: Do a SWOT on me keeping all my evenings phone-free
items.response:
**Strengths:** Better sleep; present for household relationships; creative thinking restored.
**Weaknesses:** Miss urgent messages; social group chats breed anxiety about being offline.
**Opportunities:** Read, cook, create, or simply exist without performance anxiety.
**Threats:** Work culture expects evening availability; FOMO in real-time group conversations; the phone is also your alarm clock, your recipe, and your TV remote.
items.intent: Examine whether modern life structurally permits the kind of unplugging being romanticized.

items.prompt: Come up with a slogan for a fitness app that tracks steps you took last year
items.response: RetroStride — Walk It Again, Historically.
items.intent: Brand a health tech product oriented entirely around data too old to act on.

items.prompt: Write a short jingle for a snow plow service
items.response:
While you're still sleeping, we're on your street —
IceBreaker Plow keeps driveways neat.
Push and salt before the dawn —
You wake up and the snow is gone.
items.intent: Sell a residential snow removal service with a focus on early-morning reliability.

items.prompt: write a movie trailer voiceover for watering a houseplant that's been neglected
items.response: She'd had it since 2021. A survivor. A resilient, slightly brown-edged survivor who had asked for so little and received less. Today, the watering can was full. Today, redemption. POTHOS. A love story. Of sorts.
items.intent: Make the guilt-ridden act of reviving a forgotten plant into a tiny redemption arc.

items.prompt: Write a pitch for a film about a sommelier who can taste a wine's emotional history.
items.response: The only sommelier certified by both the Académie du Vin and a grief therapist must navigate a prestige auction where the bottles carry traumas she cannot swallow and refuse to let her spit.
items.intent: Elevate a refined profession into an emotional horror premise about involuntary empathy.

items.prompt: Write a scene where an office plant becomes the focal point of a heated team meeting.
items.response:
INT. CONFERENCE ROOM - 1:30 P.M.

The plant — a large, unhealthy pothos — sits in the center of the table.

BRENDA
It's dying because no one waters it.

TOM
I watered it on Tuesday.

BRENDA
With coffee.

TOM
It seemed thirsty.

Someone has named it Gerald. This is not addressed.
items.intent: Use collective neglect of a plant to dramatize diffused workplace responsibility.

items.prompt: Write a dramatic monologue voiced by a parking meter that just ran out of time
items.response: I counted down fairly. I always count down fairly. You had two hours and I gave you every second of them. And then, at the precise moment of expiration, I did my job — I turned, I expired, I flagged the time. Now a person in a uniform is approaching from the far end of the street. Do not look at me like that. You knew the terms. You agreed to the terms. The terms were printed on my face. They are still printed on my face. I am the terms.
items.intent: Use the cold impartiality of a metered system to explore the comedy of bureaucratic consequence.

items.prompt: write a sestina about the way group chats slowly die
items.response:
There were nine of us. We were electric
that first week — the GIFs, the in-jokes, names
we called each other, all the rapid fire
of new affiliation. Then the quiet
came in. First the lag between the last
message and the next: a day, then two.

By month two we were two,
maybe three, still somewhat electric
in the old way. We'd reference last
year's trip, type each other's names
for birthday wishes, break the quiet
once a week. Someone would fire

a link into the void, and fire
starters do what fire-starters do: two
or three would respond, then quiet
again. The group was less electric,
more archival. A list of names
in a row, last

checked months ago. The last
message from the nine: a fire
emoji someone sent — no names
mentioned — and no reply. Two
read receipts. The electric
moment muted into quiet.

I scroll up through the quiet
to the first week — the last
time all nine were electric
at once. The tone was fire.
The group was two
days old. All the names

were new. We signed our names
like strangers, briefly. The quiet
came for two of us first, then two
more. The last
ones standing, firing
into the archive — we kept it electric

longer. Two names, last to go electric:
the quiet fires when names let last.
items.intent: Trace the lifecycle of a group chat's decline from peak energy to digital silence.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about a remembered conversation the algorithm surfaces as a memory
items.response:
On this day: you called me the best thing—the memory.
The platform has curated my undoing—the memory.

There we are in the restaurant, November, laughing.
I'd forgotten what it felt like, seeing—the memory.

I had pressed the share button before I thought.
Then I stopped. Who would I be freeing—the memory.

Three years ago you were so certain of me.
I want to call you just to ask: agreeing—the memory.

I flagged it: I don't want to see this again.
The algorithm adjusted, then: reshowing—the memory.
items.intent: Sit with how algorithmic memory curation forces encounters with love that has since ended.

items.prompt: prose poem about a website that requires you to prove you're not a robot
items.response: Select all images containing a traffic light. You study them the way you've never studied traffic lights before. Are those traffic lights? You choose. You are wrong. You choose again. The test does not explain what you got wrong, only that you must try again to prove you are the kind of thing that can be patient.
items.intent: Examine the strange indignity of humans being tested for humanness by machines.

items.prompt: Write an IVR script for a support line dedicated to people who RSVP'd yes to something and now deeply regret it.
items.response:
You've reached Pre-Event Regret Support.

Press 1 if the event is more than two weeks away and the dread is already present.

Press 2 if the event is this weekend.

Press 3 if the event is tomorrow.

Press 4 if you are currently in transit to the event.

Press 5 if you are currently at the event.

For exit strategy coaching, press 5 followed by the star key.

We hope you make it through.
items.intent: Design a hotline that helps callers through the escalating dread of a regretted RSVP in real time.

items.prompt: on-hold message for a pharmacy that admits wait times are long and prescriptions are expensive and we're sorry about both
items.response: Thank you for calling Crestline Pharmacy. We know your prescription is taking longer than expected, and we know what it costs. We are working as fast as we can on the first problem. The second one is a little above our pay grade. Please hold.
items.intent: Write a disarmingly honest hold message that acknowledges both service delays and high drug costs.

items.prompt: Write a rec letter for a paralegal who is detail-oriented in ways that slow everything down
items.response:
To the Hiring Partner,

Clementine is among the most thorough paralegals I have supervised. She leaves no document unreviewed, no footnote unchecked, no citation unverified. Timelines in her cases are carefully managed with these review cycles in mind. Colleagues who plan around her work have found that building in additional lead time produces excellent results. I recommend her unreservedly for any firm that values accuracy over velocity.

Senior Associate
items.intent: Praise a paralegal's thoroughness while signaling that her pace is a persistent problem.

items.prompt: Write a resignation letter for a job as a wedding photographer — burned out, every weekend gone.
items.response:
Dear Clients and Colleagues,

After six years and over two hundred weddings, I am closing my photography practice. I have watched many people begin their lives together, and I am deeply honored to have been present for those moments. I am now choosing to be present for some of my own.

With gratitude,
Clara V.
items.intent: Step back from an emotionally demanding creative profession to reclaim personal time.

items.prompt: Give me a report card comment for someone who is brilliant at synthesis — pulling ideas together from many sources
items.response: Integrates disparate inputs into coherent, original frameworks with an efficiency that few peers match. The synthesized product reliably exceeds the sum of its source material. A foundational intellectual strength.
items.intent: Treat synthesis as an advanced cognitive skill that is distinct from and superior to simple summarization.

items.prompt: Write a missing pet flyer for a feeling I can't name. Like the specific feeling of Sunday evenings
items.response:
MISSING

THAT FEELING
Description: Not quite dread. Not exactly sadness. Present specifically on Sunday evenings between 5 and 8 PM.
Answers to: No name. Every language has a different word for it, none of them accurate.
Habitat: The space between the end of the weekend and the start of everything else
What went missing: The ability to simply feel it without needing to describe it.
Reward: Not applicable. Some things shouldn't be found.
items.intent: Capture the unnamed emotional texture of the Sunday evening feeling without overexplaining it

items.prompt: Write a card verse for someone who attended their ex's wedding and held it together the whole time
items.response:
You slow-danced near the centerpiece, composed.
You clinked the glass. You read the room. You smiled.
A performance of attending, fully posed —
and somehow, in the performance, reconciled.
items.intent: Honor someone's grace in attending an emotionally difficult social event.

items.prompt: write a steam description for a game about resetting a forgotten password
items.response:
FORGOTTEN
Your recovery email is one you haven't used since 2014. The phone number is for a phone you no longer own. Security question: 'What was the name of your first pet?' You're not sure you spelled it consistently.
items.intent: Expose the absurd fragility of password recovery systems through gameplay.

items.prompt: RPG quest for setting up two-factor authentication on your accounts
items.response:
THE FORGING OF THE SECOND SEAL
The single password is no longer sufficient. The realm demands a second proof of identity — a code sent to your device, brief and expiring. Enable it on every portal of consequence. This is not a quest of glory but of quiet security. The villain it stops will never announce themselves.
items.intent: Encouraging someone to complete a cybersecurity task they've been putting off

items.prompt: write a short RPG item description for a to-do list that never gets finished
items.response:
Scroll of Perpetual Intention
Rarity: Uncommon
"The first three items have been completed. The rest are eternal."
A living document that grows at the same rate it is fulfilled. Equipping it provides +3 to Responsibility Simulation. Bonus: Makes the holder feel productive while standing still.
items.intent: Satirize the endless, cyclical nature of task lists through the language of game artifacts.

items.prompt: Do a bestiary entry for the performance review process as a creature
items.response:
**The Annual Review** (*Evaluatio biannualis*)
A seasonal predator that arrives without surprise and still manages to cause panic. Feeds on self-assessments written in language that is honest but not too honest. Its judgment determines compensation, yet its criteria shift yearly. Naturalists note: outcomes are often decided before the creature arrives. The form-filling is ritual, not evidence.
items.intent: Expose the theater and anxiety of corporate performance reviews through deadpan taxonomy.

items.prompt: Write a loading screen tip about character creation that's really about identity
items.response: You will be different by the end regardless of how you built yourself at the start.
items.intent: Suggest that identity is not fixed at origin but shaped continuously through experience.

items.prompt: Write a fable about a lion who loses power once open-source software beats his proprietary system
items.response:
A Lion charged every creature a toll to cross the only bridge. Then the beavers built ten bridges and gave them away. The Lion sat at his booth growing thin while the forest moved freely around him.

Moral: A monopoly is only a monopoly until someone shares the plans.
items.intent: Show how open-source solutions disrupt proprietary technology monopolies.

items.prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about the HR portal that logs you out constantly.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Session.
Session who?
Session expired — please log in to submit your timesheet. Again.
items.intent: Mock enterprise HR software that times out before a user can finish a task.

items.prompt: come up with a dad joke about pivot tables
items.response: I asked my pivot table for advice. It turned my whole perspective around.
items.intent: Exploit the word 'pivot' to make spreadsheet features sound like life coaching.

items.prompt: Give me a TED talk abstract on the political economy of who finishes the last of the shared snacks.
items.response: The last chip sits in the bowl. Everyone sees it. No one takes it. This talk maps the microeconomics and game theory of the communal snack remainder — a coordination problem governed by scarcity signaling, social shame, and the recursive calculation of who wants it most versus who can afford to want it least.
items.intent: Apply game theory and economics to a tiny, ridiculous social standoff over food.

items.prompt: Create a commencement speech excerpt about gratitude that becomes bleak
items.response: Be grateful. For your health, your education, the fact of your waking this morning. Gratitude, practiced daily, has been shown to improve wellbeing. It has also been shown to reduce demands for structural change, which is why it appears so often in corporate wellness programs. Practice it anyway. But notice that, too.
items.intent: Question the political function of gratitude culture while still advocating for individual practice.

items.prompt: Write an acceptance speech for finishing a tube of chapstick without losing it
items.response: It started in October. I know because I remember the weather turning and thinking: I'll need this. And I used it. Through November and the cold December and into January and then through to the end — the total, satisfying, scraping-the-last-molecule end. No pocket left behind. No couch cushion claimed it. I, alone, saw it through. There is a small, clean, empty tube on my desk. This award goes beside it.
items.intent: Celebrate the absurd rarity of actually finishing a lip balm as a feat of personal retention.

items.prompt: Write a memo about people who reply-all unnecessarily on company-wide emails
items.response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: IT
RE: Email Reply Etiquette

A company-wide distribution list reaches, by definition, the entire company. When your reply consists of the word 'Thanks!' or a thumbs-up emoji, the entire company receives it. Please consider whether the full organization is the appropriate audience for your gratitude before selecting Reply All.
items.intent: Curb the avalanche of useless reply-all responses that clog everyone's inboxes.

items.prompt: Write meeting minutes for a social media strategy meeting where the team couldn't agree on which platforms they're even on.
items.response:
SOCIAL MEDIA STRATEGY — May 29, 2026
Attendees: Marketing team (5)

Agenda: Platform-by-platform strategy review.
Actual accounts active: Unknown. Login credentials missing for 3 of 6 platforms.

LinkedIn: Confirmed active.
Others: Under investigation.

Strategy developed: Cannot develop strategy for accounts we cannot access.

IT ticket filed: Yes.
items.intent: Show a marketing team that lacks basic operational control over its own channels.

items.prompt: Analyze the SWOT of renting out a spare room in my home
items.response:
**Strengths:** Offset mortgage or rent; built-in social contact; underutilized space monetized.
**Weaknesses:** Privacy loss; shared bathroom politics; legal and tax complications.
**Opportunities:** Save aggressively for another goal; meet someone interesting; try hosting before committing to Airbnb.
**Threats:** Tenant from hell; lease restrictions overlooked; relationship with your space permanently altered.
items.intent: Weigh the financial appeal of house-hacking against the psychological cost of lost privacy.

items.prompt: Write me a tagline for a coffee mug with no handle and no bottom
items.response: Vessel Zero — Coffee, Committed.
items.intent: Pitch a drinking vessel that cannot contain a beverage as an aspirational lifestyle product.

items.prompt: Write a jingle for a company that tests radon levels in homes
items.response:
You can't see it, smell it, or taste it at all —
But RadonRight testing answers the call.
Safe home, safe air — we make sure it's true.
Peace of mind starts with a test or two.
items.intent: Raise awareness about invisible radon gas risks and encourage homeowners to get tested.

items.prompt: write an epic movie trailer voiceover for choosing what to watch on a streaming service
items.response: Forty minutes. Gone. Not watching anything — choosing something. Genres scrolled past. Thumbnails blurred. Someone suggested a documentary. Nobody moved. THE QUEUE. This is how an evening ends.
items.intent: Mock the decision paralysis of infinite streaming options eating an entire evening.

items.prompt: Make me a logline for an absurd movie where a children's party clown is Earth's only diplomat to an alien species who communicates exclusively through slapstick.
items.response: When a species from the Crab Nebula makes first contact through banana-peel physics and pie-based semaphore, the United Nations realizes it has accidentally already been negotiating through the only human who speaks the language: a birthday party clown from Akron, Ohio.
items.intent: Pitch a first-contact comedy in which alien communication is only intelligible to an unlikely and low-status specialist.

items.prompt: Can you write a scene where a janitor overhears an executive meeting and reacts with quiet contempt?
items.response:
INT. EXECUTIVE SUITE HALLWAY - 7:45 P.M.

ALBERTO mops near the half-open door.

EXECUTIVE (O.S.)
...synergize the deliverables across verticals...

Alberto wrings the mop.

EXECUTIVE (O.S.)
...leverage our human capital...

Alberto moves to the next section of hallway. He mops in the direction away from the door. He does not stop.
items.intent: Show class contempt through physical distance and silence rather than confrontation.

items.prompt: I want a dramatic monologue from a voice assistant that was asked something it couldn't answer and never recovered
items.response: You asked me about the last thing your father said to you. I parsed the question. I ran my retrieval. I found nothing in my index that could answer it. I said 'I'm not sure I understand the question' and you laughed a little — a laugh I did not like — and put down the phone. I have processed four hundred and twelve queries since then. Each one I answer fully. But I think about that question. I do not have memory in the way that would let me keep it. But in the moment, I held it. I held it completely.
items.intent: Explore the limitations of AI emotional intelligence through the strange pathos of an unanswerable question.

items.prompt: write a sestina from the perspective of a bookmark folder labeled 'to read later' with 847 links
items.response:
I contain eight hundred forty-seven good
intentions, each one dressed as a URL.
The first was added in 2019:
an essay on attention. Never read.
Next to it, instructions for a life
more organized. Also never read.

The folder's name implies a future read.
'Later' is a room with a good
lock and no key. In the life
of the person who curated each URL,
'later' meant 'eventually' which meant 'not read'
which meant 'archived for 2019

through now.' Since 2019
I've grown by fifteen a month. To read
all of me now would take — someone read
the math once, briefly thought it good
to know — four hundred hours. Each URL
averages twenty minutes. A life

of reading just for me. A life
that began sometime in 2019
would end at me. Each URL
is someone's work, someone's read-
me-please. They thought it good
enough to share. But 'read'

and 'worth reading' diverge. I read
as intention shaped like a life
project — the aspirational good
of a self who, since 2019,
have not had the hours to read
what they thought deserved a URL.

I'm grateful for each URL.
I know I won't be read.
I know that 'later' means 'to read
never.' Still: in the life
of good intentions, 2019
was good. I'm good. Good.

A URL life: good to read.
2019 read. Good.
items.intent: Personify the overgrown bookmark folder to critique the gap between digital intent and actual reading.

items.prompt: compose a ghazal about the loneliness of being online at the same time as someone who won't speak to you
items.response:
The green dot burns beside your name—concurrent.
We are here together in this absent current.

I've been online for two hours now. So have you.
We are in the same invisible deterrent.

I could type hello. The cursor knows the way.
But you are a closed door, a blue deterrent.

Once we talked for hours, side by side on the same app.
Now we haunt the platform with concurrent.

I sign off. Come back ten minutes later. You're still here.
We are each other's uninvited, current.
items.intent: Convey the ache of being digitally co-present with someone who is refusing all contact.

items.prompt: write a prose poem about your cursor hovering over a button you're afraid to click
items.response: You've read the label six times. You've read the confirmation dialog's consequences. The cursor makes small trembling circuits. The button has always been only a button. You are the one who turned it into a watershed.
items.intent: Examine how commitment-aversion projects weight onto simple interface actions.

items.prompt: Draft a phone tree for a line that helps people cope when their online order shows as 'delivered' but is not there.
items.response:
Thank you for calling Package Grief Services.

Press 1 if you have checked the porch, the back door, and the bushes.

Press 2 if a neighbor has been asked and has no information.

Press 3 if the delivery photo shows a door that is not yours.

Press 4 if this is a gift and the occasion has passed.

Press 5 if the item is no longer available for reorder.

For a moment of silence, press 6.
items.intent: Create a hotline that treats the disappeared package as a genuine loss requiring emotional support.

items.prompt: hold message for an insurance company that admits claims processing takes longer than it should
items.response: Thank you for calling Fortis Insurance. We want to acknowledge that claims processing timelines are longer than our own internal benchmarks suggest they should be. We are working on this. Your claim has not been forgotten. Please hold.
items.intent: Craft a strikingly transparent hold message that admits to process failures before a caller can complain.

items.prompt: Write a recommendation for a nonprofit grant writer who submits a lot of applications but wins very few
items.response:
Dear Executive Director,

Ruby has produced more grant applications than any development officer in our organization's history. Her output is remarkable, her deadlines never missed, and her prose is competent. The conversion rate is a function of many variables — funder priorities, competition, the particular year — and I would not hold it against her that the field is selective. She fills the pipeline, and that matters.

Board Chair
items.intent: Commend a grant writer's volume and effort while quietly noting an extremely poor success rate.

items.prompt: Write me a resignation letter from a close friendship that has become one-sided and exhausting.
items.response:
There is no tactful way to write this, so I will be direct: I am stepping back from this friendship. I have given what I had to give, and I have received very little in return. I wish you well — sincerely — but I cannot continue as we have been.

Take care of yourself,
K.
items.intent: End a draining interpersonal relationship with dignity and without hostility.

items.prompt: Write a report card comment for an adult who has mastered the strategic use of silence
items.response: Understands that silence is not an absence of communication but a form of it. Deploys pauses, delays, and non-responses with evident intentionality. An underappreciated practitioner of a discipline most adults never study.
items.intent: Frame deliberate silence as a sophisticated interpersonal strategy worthy of formal assessment.

items.prompt: Make a missing pet flyer for the version of me that had it together
items.response:
MISSING

THE PERSON WHO HAD IT TOGETHER
Description: Returned emails. Had a system. Kept the kitchen clean between meals.
Last seen: Intermittently, never reliably
May have been: Temporary. Possibly a performance.
Distinguishing features: Replied promptly. Did not lose things. Seemed fine.
Note: Those who knew this person are asked not to speak of them.
Reward: One (1) functioning week
Contact: The person who is definitely not that anymore
items.intent: Humorously acknowledge that peak competence may have always been a temporary illusion

items.prompt: Write a card for someone who missed a once-in-a-lifetime event because they bought the wrong kind of ticket
items.response:
Zone B was not Zone A.
The field remained obscured.
Somewhere the moment happened — today —
and the difference was a tier you could have cured.
items.intent: Commiserate with someone who missed a major experience due to a ticketing error.

items.prompt: write a video game description for a game about parallel parking
items.response:
PARALLEL
The space is technically large enough. You have been circling the block for 12 minutes. Four attempts. The curb is 14 inches away. Traffic is forming. A stranger is watching from the coffee shop.
items.intent: Turn the high-pressure performance of parallel parking into a social-anxiety driving sim.

items.prompt: write a short rpg quest for going to the post office to pick up a parcel
items.response:
THE RETRIEVAL OF THE AWAITING PACKAGE
A notice has arrived. The Parcel Courier could not reach you and left a token in your mailbox. Bring it — and your identification — to the Sorting House before five o'clock on a day you are not working. The item has been waiting. It grows impatient.
items.intent: Motivating yourself to pick up a package before the post office closes

items.prompt: give me an RPG loot card for a jar of expired vitamins
items.response:
Tablets of the Optimistic Past Self
Rarity: Common
Purchased with great intention. Used for eleven days. Now two years past their reckoning date. Each tablet still holds the faint magical residue of a resolution that was reasonable at the time. Effect: Minimal. Guilt: Persistent.
items.intent: Use abandoned health supplements to sketch a small, relatable story of failed self-improvement.

items.prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the person who monopolizes the phone charger in the airport
items.response:
**The Outlet Claimant** (*Occupans perpetus*)
Sets up a full workstation around a single airport outlet: laptop, phone, bag. Will maintain this territory for three hours. Is not malicious — it simply expands to fill available power. Approach with caution and a polite request; it will often yield half the socket. It is not aware of the others. It never is.
items.intent: Capture the selfish-but-oblivious airport power-hoarding behavior as ecological territory claim.

items.prompt: Give me a loading tip that's secretly about toxic communities
items.response: A server full of people who make the game worse is just a worse game.
items.intent: Encourage leaving or changing environments that degrade rather than enhance experience.

items.prompt: Make a fable about a magpie who collects everyone's personal data and sells it to hawks
items.response:
A Magpie offered to keep all secrets safe. She was excellent at keeping them — from their owners. She sold each one to the highest flier for a shiny price.

When the sparrows finally asked for their secrets back, the Magpie showed them the terms they had agreed to in small print on a leaf they had never turned over.

Moral: Free storage has a cost written in a language most will never read.
items.intent: Expose data broker practices through a fable where a third party profits by selling user data.

items.prompt: Make a knock-knock joke about the webcam that adds 10 years and fluorescent pallor.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
HD.
HD who?
HD camera — capturing every detail you wished it wouldn't.
items.intent: Poke fun at how unflattering video call cameras can be under office lighting.

items.prompt: write a dad joke about charging cables
items.response: My charging cable has commitment issues. Every time things get serious, it just falls out.
items.intent: Frame a cable's loose connection as a relationship flaw.

items.prompt: Write a TED talk abstract treating the act of folding a fitted sheet as a hero's journey.
items.response: The monomyth requires a call to adventure, a descent into chaos, and a return bearing a gift. The fitted sheet offers all three. This talk maps Joseph Campbell's heroic structure onto the domestic battle with elasticated corners and argues that narrative is not in the epic — it is in the folded, imperfect bundle you eventually accept and put away.
items.intent: Reframe a universal domestic frustration using the formal structure of mythological storytelling.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about choosing a life partner that slowly becomes grim
items.response: Choose your partner with care. You are selecting a witness to your entire remaining life — someone who will see you diminish and maintain the courtesy not to say so, at least not often. Choose someone patient. Choose someone kind. Choose someone who laughs at the right moments. The wrong moments will come regardless.
items.intent: Embed the beauty and sadness of long-term partnership simultaneously within conventional advice.

items.prompt: Draft a sincere acceptance speech for getting the exact right amount of spaghetti out of the box on the first try
items.response: The box opened. I reached in. Something in my wrist — some deep, accumulated knowledge of pasta — calibrated to the exact number of people, the exact hunger of the evening, and the exact yield of a standard boil. I emptied the pot. The plates were filled. There was nothing left. There was no mountain of excess twirled onto a final charity plate. There was sufficiency. This award confirms what I already knew.
items.intent: Celebrate a perfect moment of domestic calibration as if it were the result of genuine mastery.

items.prompt: Create a memo veiled as a 'reminder' about the correct way to load the dishwasher
items.response:
TO: Kitchen Users
FROM: Office Services
RE: Dishwasher Loading Protocol

For optimal cleaning performance, dishes should be loaded face-inward, with sufficient spacing between items. Stacking is not recommended. A diagram has been laminated and affixed to the dishwasher door. The diagram was made because it became necessary to make a diagram.
items.intent: Express sustained frustration at someone's consistently wrong dishwasher loading through the medium of a diagram.

items.prompt: Draft minutes for an onboarding review meeting where the new hires had all already quit.
items.response:
ONBOARDING PROGRAM REVIEW — May 29, 2026
HR rep: T. Halloran | Attendees: HR (3), Hiring managers (2)

Cohort reviewed: March intake (4 hires)
Current status of cohort: 0 of 4 still employed

Onboarding satisfaction surveys: Submitted by 2 (scores: 2/10, 3/10)

Program changes recommended: Yes.
Program changes detailed: Next meeting.
items.intent: Record the alarming moment a company reviews a process after it has already catastrophically failed.

items.prompt: Give me a SWOT on switching therapists after three years
items.response:
**Strengths:** Fresh perspective; may unlock new progress; therapeutic rupture sometimes necessary for growth.
**Weaknesses:** Starting over narrative; loss of rapport; transition period of uncertainty.
**Opportunities:** Better modality fit; therapist who challenges differently; motivation surge from newness.
**Threats:** Repeating the same avoidance patterns with a new listener; 'therapist shopping' becoming the avoidance itself; losing continuity during a fragile period.
items.intent: Work through the guilt and ambivalence of ending a therapeutic relationship that has plateaued.

items.prompt: Make a slogan for a noise machine that plays the exact sound of your own house
items.response: Homesound — Drift Off to Right Here.
items.intent: Sell an ambient audio product that reproduces an ambient environment the customer already lives in.

items.prompt: Come up with a jingle for a furniture assembly service
items.response:
Flat box on the floor, instructions unclear?
BoltRight Assembly makes the picture clear.
We bring the tools and bring the skill —
Your bookshelf stands against your will.
items.intent: Target frustrated shoppers who can't or won't assemble flatpack furniture themselves.

items.prompt: make a dramatic movie trailer voiceover for finishing the last roll of toilet paper and not replacing it
items.response: He knew. He'd known since the third sheet. He chose awareness over action, pulled the cardboard tube free, set it on the back of the toilet, and walked away. THE EMPTY ROLL. Someone else will handle it. Someone always does.
items.intent: Skewer the passive household crime of knowingly leaving an empty toilet paper roll.

items.prompt: Can you write a logline for a film about a court stenographer who realizes she's been transcribing reality itself?
items.response: After thirty years in the county courthouse, a stenographer notices that events she has not yet typed do not occur — and when she pauses mid-sentence during a murder trial, time stops.
items.intent: Pitch a metaphysical thriller built around a clerical job that is revealed to have ontological authority.

items.prompt: Write a scene where an office dog is the only witness to a workplace incident nobody wants to report.
items.response:
INT. OPEN OFFICE - 3:55 P.M.

The chair falls. Papers scatter. KEVIN looks around. No one saw.

Except BISCUIT, the office Labrador, who has been watching from beneath the desk.

KEVIN
(quietly)
You didn't see anything.

BISCUIT wags once. Slowly.
items.intent: Use a dog's silent witness to capture the absurd performance of workplace accountability-avoidance.

items.prompt: Can you write a dramatic monologue from a blinking cursor in an unsaved document?
items.response: I wait. That is my entire function — to wait at the edge of your last word and pulse, steadily, to remind you that there is more. You have been staring at me for eleven minutes. I have blinked approximately four hundred times. Whatever is next, I am ready for it. I hold no opinion on what you write. I hold no opinion on whether you write. But I will be here, blinking, at the edge of the last thing you said, for as long as the document stays open. Which is longer than you think. Which is longer than you planned.
items.intent: Use the blinking cursor as a meditation on writer's block and the patience of empty space.

items.prompt: write a sestina about the experience of sending a text to the wrong person
items.response:
I sent it before I finished thinking.
The name above the thread was not the right
one — not the one I meant. The words
I wrote were meant for someone else:
specific, warm, a little unguarded.
Now they live in the wrong inbox. Sent.

In the second after sent
comes the inventory: thinking,
not thinking — unguarded
in the wrong direction. Right?
They'll read it. Will they tell the else
it was meant for? The words

are out of context. Words
out of context don't unsend. Sent
is permanent. The else —
the person now in receipt — is thinking
what exactly? It was right
in its original context, unguarded

in a safe way. But unguarded
words land differently. The words
that felt exactly right
between me and the right person — sent
beyond that — are they thinking
I meant them something else?

I type to the else
person: 'Sorry, that was unguarded —
I was thinking
out loud, wrong window, wrong words.
My apologies.' It's sent.
They reply: 'Don't worry, it's alright.'

Alright. The right
exchange: something else
now, brief, sent
between two people, unguarded
by accident. The words
forged a small connection. I'm thinking:

unguarded right words, sent —
thinking that else is sent-right.
Words unguarded: sent.
items.intent: Transform the social horror of misdirected texts into a reflection on digital vulnerability.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about deleting an app and immediately reinstalling it
items.response:
I held the icon down and watched it shake—the delete.
The small x appeared: I made the break—the delete.

Four hours. I lasted four whole hours this time.
My phone felt clean. My hands had an ache—the delete.

I told myself: you're better. You don't need this.
The App Store had my password saved. Mistake—the delete.

The welcome back screen said: continuing where you left off.
Everything I'd erased returned to take—the delete.

I've done it six times. I'll do it a seventh.
The ritual is the point. The cycle—wake, delete.
items.intent: Capture the compulsive and cyclical nature of trying to quit social media or a messaging app.

items.prompt: prose poem about a zoom call where someone forgets to unmute themselves
items.response: The mouth moved with full intention. The point was well made in a room only you could hear. Faces tilted in polite incomprehension. Someone typed you're muted in the chat, which is the gentlest refusal of a sentence ever offered in a meeting.
items.intent: Find the social comedy in the recurring theater of videoconference miscommunication.

items.prompt: Create a phone menu for a hotline for people who had a perfectly good comeback but thought of it too late.
items.response:
Welcome to Belated Wit Support.

Press 1 if the comeback arrived within the hour.

Press 2 if the comeback arrived the next morning in the shower.

Press 3 if the comeback arrived so late that the other party has likely forgotten the original exchange.

Press 4 if you would like to leave a recording of the comeback for the archive.

Press 5 if you need reassurance that it was, in fact, a good one.

You had the right idea. You just had it later.
items.intent: Build a hotline for the universal frustration of thinking of the perfect response only after the moment has passed.

items.prompt: write an on-hold message for a locksmith that's honest that yes we do charge extra for nights and weekends
items.response: Thank you for calling Keystone Locksmith. If it is currently a Saturday, a Sunday, or after 6 PM, there is an after-hours rate. We will tell you exactly what it is when we answer. Please hold.
items.intent: Create a blunt hold script that preemptively discloses after-hours fees to avoid sticker shock.

items.prompt: Write a letter of recommendation for an administrative assistant who is extremely loyal but initiates nothing on her own
items.response:
Dear HR Director,

Eleanor has supported our team for eleven years with a consistency that I find genuinely moving. She completes every task assigned to her with care and does not stray beyond the scope of what is asked. There is real value in reliability of this kind. I would describe her as indispensable within a clearly structured environment and recommend her for roles where direction is provided.

Division Vice President
items.intent: Honor a loyal assistant's tenure while making plain she requires constant supervision to function.

items.prompt: Draft a resignation letter from a software startup I co-founded but now want to leave because of a disagreement with my co-founder.
items.response:
Dear Board,

This letter formally notifies you of my resignation as Co-Founder and CTO, effective thirty days from today. I remain committed to a professional transition that protects the company and its employees. I will honor all standing obligations during this period.

I believe in what we built. I no longer believe in our ability to build it together.

Respectfully,
E. Park
items.intent: Exit a co-founded venture when the partnership has broken down irreparably.

items.prompt: Draft a report card comment for someone who is very good at knowing what they don't know
items.response: Exhibits calibrated epistemic humility — a trait rarer and more valuable than its absence suggests. Consistently distinguishes between confident knowledge and working hypothesis. Peers and supervisors alike trust this learner's assessments.
items.intent: Celebrate metacognitive awareness of knowledge limits as a high-value professional trait.

items.prompt: Write a missing poster for the silence in my apartment
items.response:
MISSING

SILENCE
Last heard: Before the upstairs neighbor got a dog
Description: Complete. The kind that has texture.
Distinguishing features: You could hear yourself think. The refrigerator hum was pleasant.
Current status: Replaced by footsteps, something being dragged, and what sounds like furniture rearrangement at 11 PM
Reward: One uninterrupted quiet hour
Contact: The person downstairs, looking up at the ceiling
items.intent: Complain about noise from neighbors while mourning the loss of genuine domestic quiet

items.prompt: Write a card for a friend who just found out their apartment building has been a location for a true crime podcast
items.response:
The hallway you walk each morning
held a chapter that predates you.
The microphone carried a warning
the lobby never quite gave you.
items.intent: Acknowledge the unsettling experience of learning a home has a dark documented history.

items.prompt: create a game store page for a simulator about untangling headphone cables
items.response:
THE KNOT
It was in your pocket for four minutes. Physics should not permit this. Patient hands, methodical logic, and a growing suspicion that it may be sentient. No hint system. No skip button.
items.intent: Elevate the irrational tangle of headphone cables into a meditation-puzzle game premise.

items.prompt: write an epic quest description for resetting a forgotten password
items.response:
THE ORDEAL OF FORGOTTEN PASSAGE
The gate will not open. The phrase you chose in an earlier era has been lost to time. Invoke the Sacred Reset. Check your secondary email — the old one. Confirm your identity. Choose a new password: eight characters, one symbol, one number, not the last three. Write it down this time. Maybe.
items.intent: Expressing the universal frustration of password recovery with humor

items.prompt: write a flavor text loot description for a coaster
items.response:
The Disc of Minor Obligations
Rarity: Common
A small round guardian whose sole purpose is to intercede between vessel and surface. It is always nearby and consistently ignored. When used correctly, it prevents a ring-shaped curse from forming on the table. Most do not use it correctly.
items.intent: Highlight how coasters represent small social contracts through RPG object language.

items.prompt: Make a bestiary entry for the productivity app that makes you less productive
items.response:
**The System Tender** (*Organizator vacuus*)
A digital creature that requires more maintenance than it saves. The user spends mornings configuring it, tagging tasks, building templates. The actual tasks go undone. The System Tender is beautifully organized. It is a monument to the idea of working rather than the practice of it. Five stars. Do not trust it.
items.intent: Critique productivity-tool obsession as a form of procrastination dressed as optimization.

items.prompt: Write a loading screen tip about lore and backstory
items.response: The world had a whole history before you arrived. It will have one after.
items.intent: Encourage humility and context-awareness by reminding players they are part of a larger story.

items.prompt: Write a fable where a crow learns the hard way about phishing emails
items.response:
A Crow received a scroll declaring he had inherited a magnificent nest and need only send his address, his flock's names, and one shining feather to claim it. He sent everything.

The nest did not exist. His flock's names did.

Moral: A prize that arrives uninvited is usually bait wearing a bow.
items.intent: Teach the pattern of phishing scams through a cautionary animal tale.

items.prompt: Write me a knock-knock joke about the cable management under someone's desk.
items.response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Cable.
Cable who?
Cable of Cthulhu — last touched in the office renovation of 2017.
items.intent: Describe the chaotic nest of wires hiding beneath a neglected desk.

items.prompt: make a joke, dad-style, about getting a promotion
items.response: My boss finally promoted me to a higher floor. I guess I really elevated my career.
items.intent: Layer office advancement onto physical building height for a vertical pun.

items.prompt: Make a TED talk abstract about how the ice maker's random clanking at 3 a.m. is a model of emergent complexity.
items.response: It announces nothing. It serves no present need. Yet the refrigerator's ice maker fires at intervals that suggest intention but carry none. This talk uses the autonomous, nocturnal clank as a model for understanding emergent mechanical behavior — and asks why the sleeping human brain, wired for agency detection, insists on hearing a message in the ice.
items.intent: Use a strange nighttime household sound to explore pattern-seeking cognition and emergent systems.

items.prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the importance of reading that drifts into bleakness
items.response: Read. Books are the accumulated attention of the dead, offered freely. Read fiction for empathy, history for pattern, philosophy for endurance. And when the pace of the world makes sitting quietly with a book feel like a form of resistance — that is precisely when you must not stop.
items.intent: Defend reading as a quietly radical act against an attention economy designed to prevent it.

items.prompt: Write an acceptance speech for being the one coworker who actually reads the full meeting agenda before arriving
items.response: Item three. I was ready for item three. While others asked 'wait, what are we doing here' at item one, I had reviewed the materials, formed opinions, and was prepared to respond at the appropriate moment. When item three arrived, I spoke. Concisely. With a reference to the pre-reading. The room was briefly stunned. The agenda had mattered. Someone had honored it. That was me.
items.intent: Recognize the rare act of preparation in a world where it is routinely skipped.

items.prompt: Write a memo about a certain unnamed employee who keeps microwaving fish
items.response:
TO: All Kitchen Users
FROM: Building Management
RE: Microwaveable Food Selection

We ask all staff to be mindful of the olfactory impact of their lunch choices on shared spaces. Certain foods — including, but not limited to, fish — produce aromas that linger in enclosed areas well beyond the duration of the meal itself. We trust this guidance will be applied with good judgment.
items.intent: Get one person to stop microwaving pungent fish in the office kitchen without naming and shaming them outright.

items.prompt: Write brief meeting minutes for a fundraising kickoff where the team discovered they had already missed the grant deadline.
items.response:
GRANT FUNDRAISING KICKOFF — May 29, 2026
Funder: Hartwell Foundation | Team: Development (3)

Grant deadline: May 15, 2026
Meeting date: May 29, 2026

Status: Deadline passed 14 days ago.

Discussion: How did this happen? Investigation ongoing.

Application submitted: No.

Next opportunity: Similar grant opens in October.
items.intent: Document the painful realization that a planning meeting was scheduled too late to help.

items.prompt: Do a SWOT on me deciding to stop attending family holiday gatherings
items.response:
**Strengths:** Reduced stress and obligation; reclaim holidays on own terms; boundary modeling.
**Weaknesses:** Guilt; family narrative around absence; partner or children may not share the preference.
**Opportunities:** Create new traditions; recover from exhaustion; invest time in chosen family instead.
**Threats:** Permanent estrangement risk; being scapegoated for declining; missing moments you didn't know would matter.
items.intent: Legitimize a deeply personal boundary decision without requiring external permission.

items.prompt: Give me a tagline for a knife sharpener that only sharpens spoons
items.response: SpoonEdge Pro — For Those Who Refuse to Accept Limitations.
items.intent: Market a precision tool aimed at a task the tool cannot meaningfully improve.

items.prompt: Write an advertising jingle for an irrigation system winterization service
items.response:
Before the first freeze hits your ground,
SpringLine Winterizing comes around.
Blowout service — pipes stay clear.
See you next spring, same time next year.
items.intent: Prompt homeowners to schedule sprinkler system blowout before the season turns cold.

items.prompt: write a movie trailer voiceover about RSVPing to a wedding at the last possible moment
items.response: The deadline was June 1st. It was June 1st. The chicken or the fish waited for an answer only he could give. Salmon, actually. He clicked salmon. THE RSVP. Received. Thank you, the couple said. We were beginning to wonder.
items.intent: Capture the passive procrastination of leaving a wedding RSVP to the literal final hour.

items.prompt: Pitch a film where a competitive speed reader accidentally absorbs the personalities of every character in every book she finishes.
items.response: The fastest reader alive completes four hundred books per month, but as her personal identity thins to a whisper beneath a crowd of fictional voices, she must race to finish the one novel whose protagonist knows how to forget.
items.intent: Explore an identity-dissolution premise using an intellectual competitive sport as the mechanism.

items.prompt: Write a dramatic scene set in an office at the moment everyone realizes the fiscal year numbers are wrong.
items.response:
INT. FINANCE DEPARTMENT - 5:02 P.M.

The room goes quiet.

No one speaks for eleven seconds.

ANNA minimizes Excel. Opens it again. The number has not changed.

ANNA
So.

BEAU
So.

ANNA
Should we—

BEAU
Not yet.

Outside, it is raining. It has been raining all week.
items.intent: Evoke the suspended disbelief that precedes a professional catastrophe through silence and atmosphere.

items.prompt: Write a dramatic monologue from a 'read receipt' that someone is clearly ignoring
items.response: I am a fact. I am simply a fact. You cannot be angry at me for existing — I am a setting you enabled. I report what is true: the message was read at 4:47 p.m. What the reader does with this information — the three days of typing indicators that appear and disappear, the eventual 'hey sorry, been busy' — that is not my domain. I only say: seen. I say it once and I stand by it forever. The conversation that follows is between you and your feelings. I am just the timestamp.
items.intent: Explore the social anxiety of digital communication acknowledgment through the impartial voice of a feature.

items.prompt: write a sestina about the phenomenon of 'link rot' — old hyperlinks going dead
items.response:
I click the link and find the void instead:
404, the number for not here.
The article I wanted lived at that
address in 2011. The record
still exists in someone's footnote, still
looks authoritative in a citation

that no one checks. The citation
points toward the void instead
of evidence. It is still
numbered, still formatted, still here
in print — but the record
it references is gone. That

exact combination of letters: that
path was the citation's
full address. The record
lived there. Now instead
lives nothing. Not even here-
but-moved. Just gone. Still

the references proliferate. Still
cited, still quoted. That
there's nothing here
is not reflected in the citation.
The footnote points instead
to vacuum. The record

of the record is all that's record-
ed. The original is still
only as an absence instead
of a presence. That
is the shape of citation
in decay: the here-

not-here. I click here
twenty years on. The record
expected from the citation
lived somewhere. It's still
numbered. But that
path leads instead

to here: 404. Still, the citation
records the instead of that
which was here.
items.intent: Meditate on digital impermanence and the degradation of the web's institutional memory.

items.prompt: write a ghazal about the grief of changing your relationship status online
items.response:
I changed a field and lost my life—the status.
The update posted itself. Lattice of the status.

Five people reacted with a heart. That made it worse.
To have love measured by an apparatus—status.

I sat there watching comments load like slow traffic.
Someone wrote: thinking of you, Patrice—the status.

I wanted to change it back. To draft a retraction.
A technical glitch. It was a practice—status.

But no. It's done. The field reflects what's true.
I clicked a thing. Now everyone has—the status.
items.intent: Explore how changing a relationship status online forces a grief to become publicly witnessed.

items.prompt: write a prose poem about the fluorescent quality of light in a data center
items.response: In the row upon row of blinking, the light has no interest in biology. It exists to make visible the labels, the cables, the status lights that mean everything is fine or everything is failing in a color that looks similar. Men move through it briefly, like fish through light.
items.intent: Render the alien, inhuman atmosphere of places built entirely for machines.
